Memory Requirement Best For Broadcast Hash Join No (large side) No Small table fits in executor RAM Small + large table joins Sort-Merge Join Both sides Both sides Moderate (sort buffers) Two large tables Shuffle Hash Join Both sides No One side fits in partition RAM Medium + large table Broadcast Nested Loop No (large side) No Small table in RAM Non-equi joins only
Verifying the Join Strategy
Always verify which strategy Spark selected:
result.explain("formatted")

# Look for:
# BroadcastHashJoin    -> best case (broadcast)
# SortMergeJoin        -> default for large tables
# ShuffledHashJoin     -> less common middle ground
# BroadcastNestedLoop  -> non-equi join (expensive)
If you see SortMergeJoin where BroadcastHashJoin would be appropriate, add a broadcast() hint.

sections.title: Lesson 4: Shuffle Joins in Practice — Controlling the Cost

blocks.content: Most production joins involve two large tables and result in a Sort-Merge Join. Since you cannot avoid the shuffle, your goal is to minimize its cost.
The Real Cost of a Shuffle Join
Consider joining two large tables:

items: meter_readings → 1 TB (1 billion rows)
weather_history → 500 GB (5 billion hourly records)

blocks.content: In a Sort-Merge Join, Spark must:

items: Shuffle 1 TB of readings across the network
Shuffle 500 GB of weather data across the network
Sort both sides within each partition
Merge and produce the result

blocks.content: Total network transfer: up to 1.5 TB. This can take 20–60 minutes depending on network bandwidth and cluster size.
Strategy 1: Filter Before Joining
The single most impactful optimization. Every row eliminated before the join is a row that does not need to be shuffled.

# Bad: join first, filter later (shuffles entire 1.5 TB)
result = readings.join(weather, ["region", "date"]) \
    .filter(col("date") >= "2026-01-01") \
    .filter(col("daily_kw") > 0)

# Good: filter first, join smaller data
filtered_readings = readings \
    .filter(col("date") >= "2026-01-01") \
    .filter(col("daily_kw") > 0)

filtered_weather = weather \
    .filter(col("date") >= "2026-01-01")

result = filtered_readings.join(filtered_weather, ["region", "date"])
If January represents 1/12th of the data, filtering first reduces shuffle from 1.5 TB to ~125 GB — a 12x improvement.

Catalyst can push some filters automatically (predicate pushdown), but filters that reference only one side of a join are most reliably pushed down when you place them explicitly before the join in your code.
Strategy 2: Select Only Needed Columns Before Joining
Every column you carry through a join must be shuffled. If readings has 50 columns but you only need 5 downstream, dropping the other 45 before the join reduces shuffle volume by ~90%.

# Bad: shuffle all 50 columns
result = readings.join(weather, ["region", "date"])

# Good: shuffle only 5 columns
result = readings.select("meter_id", "daily_kw", "date", "region", "customer_type") \
    .join(
        weather.select("region", "date", "temperature", "humidity"),
        ["region", "date"]
    )
Strategy 3: Pre-Partitioning for Repeated Joins

If you join the same large table with multiple smaller tables in a pipeline, each join triggers a new shuffle of the large table. You can eliminate repeated shuffles by pre-partitioning the large table by the join key:

# Pre-partition readings by meter_id
readings_partitioned = readings.repartition(500, "meter_id")
readings_partitioned.cache()  # Cache the partitioned version

# First join: no shuffle needed for readings (already partitioned by meter_id)
result1 = readings_partitioned.join(metadata, "meter_id")

# Second join: still no shuffle for readings
result2 = readings_partitioned.join(calibration, "meter_id")

The initial repartition costs one shuffle. But all subsequent joins on meter_id can skip the shuffle for the readings side because the data is already partitioned by the join key. This technique is called bucketed joins in more advanced Spark configurations, where tables are pre-sorted and pre-bucketed during write time.
Strategy 4: Handling Skewed Keys in Joins
Skew in joins is more dangerous than skew in aggregations because join output can explode in size.

Consider: meter_id "M_UNKNOWN" appears in 50 million reading rows (faulty meters defaulting to a placeholder ID) and 10 metadata rows. The join partition for "M_UNKNOWN" produces 50M × 10 = 500 million output rows. That single partition may exhaust executor memory.
Mitigation approaches:

items: Filter out known problematic keys before the join — handle them separately
Salted joins — salt the skewed key on both sides (more complex than salting for aggregation)
AQE skew join optimization — Spark 3.0+ can detect skewed partitions during shuffle and split them automatically

blocks.content:
# Enable AQE skew join handling
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled", "true")

# AQE will detect partitions > 5x median and split them
# Threshold is configurable:
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor", "5")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes", "256m")

sections.title: Lesson 5: Broadcast Joins — Eliminating Shuffle at Scale

blocks.content: We covered broadcast joins in Module 4. Here we go deeper into the mechanics and edge cases.
The Broadcast Lifecycle
Broadcast Join Lifecycle:
1. Driver collects the small table from executors [Exec A: Part 1] + [Exec B: Part 2] --> [Driver: full table]
2. Driver builds hash table in Driver memory [Driver: hash_table(meter_id -> row)]
3. Driver serializes and broadcasts to all executors [Driver] --> [Exec A] + [Exec B] + [Exec C] + ...
4. Each executor stores hash table in execution memory
5. Each executor scans its local partition of the large table For each row: probe hash table by key --> emit joined row

Critical insight: Step 1 and 2 happen on the Driver. The entire small table must fit in Driver memory during the broadcast assembly phase. If the small table is 500 MB and your Driver has 1 GB, you risk Driver OOM — even if your executors have plenty of memory.
When Auto-Broadcast Fails
Spark auto-broadcasts tables estimated to be under 10 MB. But these estimates can be wrong:

items: Statistics not collected — Without ANALYZE TABLE or Delta Lake statistics, Spark may not know a table’s size
After filtering — Spark may not realize a 10 GB table becomes 5 MB after filtering
Complex subqueries — Size estimation is difficult for derived DataFrames

blocks.content: In these cases, explicitly use broadcast():

# Spark doesn't know this subquery produces only 5 MB
small_result = large_table.filter(col("flag") == True) \
    .select("meter_id", "flag_reason")

# Force broadcast
enriched = readings.join(broadcast(small_result), "meter_id")
Broadcast Join Limitations

Limitation Why Workaround Cannot broadcast for full outer join Need unmatched rows from both sides Use Sort-Merge Join Small table must fit in Driver RAM Assembled on Driver before broadcast Increase spark.driver.memory or use shuffle join Replicated on every executor Memory multiplied by executor count Keep broadcast tables as small as possible No benefit if both tables are large Broadcasting 500 GB is worse than shuffling Use Sort-Merge Join; filter first

sections.title: Lesson 6: Window Functions — The Shuffle-Free Alternative

blocks.content: Many patterns that appear to require a self-join — comparing a row to its neighbors, computing running totals, ranking within groups — can be solved more efficiently with window functions. Window functions operate within partitions without requiring a full reshuffle of the dataset. They are one of the most powerful and underused tools in PySpark.
Self-Join vs Window Function: The Core Trade-off
Consider the problem: for each meter reading, compute the difference from the previous reading.
Self-join approach (expensive):

# Join the table with itself, offset by one time step
prev = readings.select(
    col("meter_id"),
    col("timestamp").alias("prev_ts"),
    col("daily_kw").alias("prev_kw")
)

# This triggers a full shuffle of the entire table
result = readings.join(prev, ...complex_condition...)
Self-Join:

readings (1 TB)  --SHUFFLE-->  +------+
   readings (1 TB)  --SHUFFLE-->  | JOIN |  --> Result
                                  +------+
   Network transfer: up to 2 TB
Window function approach (efficient):

from pyspark.sql.window import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import lag, col

window_spec = Window.partitionBy("meter_id").orderBy("timestamp")

result = readings.withColumn(
    "prev_kw",
    lag("daily_kw", 1).over(window_spec)
).withColumn(
    "kw_change",
    col("daily_kw") - col("prev_kw")
)
Window Function:
Partition by meter_id (one shuffle to group by meter)
Exec A: meter_001 readings (ordered by timestamp) lag() applied locally within this partition
Exec B: meter_002 readings (ordered by timestamp) lag() applied locally within this partition
Exec C: meter_003 readings (ordered by timestamp) lag() applied locally within this partition
Network transfer: 1 shuffle (partition by meter_id) vs self-join: 2 shuffles of the full table
The window function requires one shuffle (to partition by meter_id) instead of two. And it operates within each partition — no cross-partition data movement during the actual computation.
Defining Window Specifications
A window specification has two parts:

items: partitionBy() — defines how data is grouped (similar to groupBy, but rows are not collapsed)
orderBy() — defines the sort order within each partition

blocks.content:
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

# Partition by meter, order by time
w = Window.partitionBy("meter_id").orderBy("timestamp")

# Partition by customer type and date, order by consumption
w2 = Window.partitionBy("customer_type", "date").orderBy(desc("daily_kw"))

# No partition (entire dataset is one window) - use with caution
w3 = Window.orderBy("daily_kw")

Performance note: partitionBy() triggers a shuffle (data must be grouped by the partition key). If you omit partitionBy() and use only orderBy(), the entire dataset is treated as one partition — this forces all data to a single executor and is extremely expensive. Always partition your windows.
Essential Window Functions
lag() and lead() — Accessing Adjacent Rows

from pyspark.sql.functions import lag, lead

w = Window.partitionBy("meter_id").orderBy("timestamp")

# Previous row's value
df.withColumn("prev_kw", lag("daily_kw", 1).over(w))

# Two rows back
df.withColumn("two_back_kw", lag("daily_kw", 2).over(w))

# Next row's value
df.withColumn("next_kw", lead("daily_kw", 1).over(w))

# Default value when no adjacent row exists
df.withColumn("prev_kw", lag("daily_kw", 1, 0.0).over(w))
Smart grid use case: Detect consumption spikes by comparing each reading to its predecessor. A sudden jump of 10x might indicate a faulty meter or theft.
row_number(), rank(), dense_rank() — Ranking Within Groups

from pyspark.sql.functions import row_number, rank, dense_rank, desc

w = Window.partitionBy("customer_type").orderBy(desc("daily_kw"))

# Unique sequential number (1, 2, 3, ...)
df.withColumn("rn", row_number().over(w))

# Rank with gaps for ties (1, 2, 2, 4)
df.withColumn("rnk", rank().over(w))

# Dense rank without gaps (1, 2, 2, 3)
df.withColumn("d_rnk", dense_rank().over(w))
Common pattern — Top N per group:

# Top 5 highest-consuming meters per customer type
w = Window.partitionBy("customer_type").orderBy(desc("daily_kw"))

ranked = df.withColumn("rank", row_number().over(w))
top5 = ranked.filter(col("rank") <= 5)

This is dramatically more efficient than the self-join or subquery equivalent in traditional SQL, because it requires only one shuffle (partition by customer_type) and one sort (by daily_kw within each partition).
Running Aggregates — sum(), avg(), count() Over Windows

from pyspark.sql.functions import sum, avg, count

# Running total of consumption per meter
w = Window.partitionBy("meter_id").orderBy("date") \
    .rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)
df.withColumn("running_total_kw", sum("daily_kw").over(w))

# 7-day moving average
w7 = Window.partitionBy("meter_id").orderBy("date") \
    .rowsBetween(-6, Window.currentRow)
df.withColumn("moving_avg_7d", avg("daily_kw").over(w7))

# Count readings in the last 30 days
w30 = Window.partitionBy("meter_id").orderBy("date") \
    .rowsBetween(-29, Window.currentRow)
df.withColumn("readings_30d", count("daily_kw").over(w30))
first(), last() — Accessing Boundary Values

from pyspark.sql.functions import first, last

# First reading of the day for each meter
w = Window.partitionBy("meter_id", "date").orderBy("timestamp")
df.withColumn("first_reading", first("daily_kw").over(w))

# Latest reading for each meter (across all dates)
w_all = Window.partitionBy("meter_id").orderBy(desc("timestamp"))
df.withColumn("latest_kw", first("daily_kw").over(w_all))
Window Function Summary

Function Returns Typical Use Case lag(col, n) Value n rows before current row Compare to previous reading lead(col, n) Value n rows after current row Look ahead for gaps row_number() Sequential number within partition Top N per group, deduplication rank() Rank with gaps for ties Competitive ranking dense_rank() Rank without gaps Dense ranking sum() over window Running or windowed sum Running totals, cumulative metrics avg() over window Running or windowed average Moving averages, smoothing first() First value in window frame Access earliest value last() Last value in window frame Access latest value ntile(n) Divides rows into n equal buckets Percentile/quartile assignment

sections.title: Lesson 7: Anti-Joins and Semi-Joins for Data Quality

blocks.content: Data quality is not an afterthought — it is a core engineering responsibility. Anti-joins and semi-joins are precision tools for detecting data gaps, validating referential integrity, and ensuring pipeline correctness.
Anti-Joins: Finding What’s Missing
A left_anti join returns rows from the left table that have no match in the right table. It answers the question: "What’s in A but not in B?"

# Find meter readings with no corresponding metadata
unregistered = readings.join(metadata, "meter_id", "left_anti")

# Find customers who have metadata but never sent a reading
silent_meters = metadata.join(readings, "meter_id", "left_anti")
Smart grid data quality pipeline:

# Step 1: Find unregistered meters
unregistered = readings.join(metadata, "meter_id", "left_anti")
unregistered_count = unregistered.select("meter_id").distinct().count()
print(f"Unregistered meters sending data: {unregistered_count}")

# Step 2: Find registered meters with no readings (possible faults)
silent = metadata.join(readings, "meter_id", "left_anti")
silent_count = silent.count()
print(f"Registered meters with no readings: {silent_count}")

# Step 3: Find readings without matching billing records
unbilled = readings.join(billing, ["meter_id", "date"], "left_anti")
Semi-Joins: Filtering by Existence
A left_semi join returns rows from the left table that have a match in the right table, without including any right-side columns.

# Get all readings from meters that are registered
registered_readings = readings.join(metadata, "meter_id", "left_semi")

# Get all readings from meters in a specific region list
regional_readings = readings.join(target_regions, "region_code", "left_semi")
Semi-joins are more efficient than inner joins when you only need the left columns, because Spark can stop scanning the right side after finding the first match for each key.
Anti-Join vs NOT IN vs NOT EXISTS
Anti-joins handle NULLs correctly, unlike NOT IN:

Method NULL-Safe? Performance Recommendation left_anti join Yes Optimized by Catalyst Preferred NOT IN (subquery) No (NULL makes entire result empty) Can be slower Avoid NOT EXISTS (correlated) Yes Depends on optimizer Acceptable in SQL syntax
Always prefer left_anti join over NOT IN, especially when the right side may contain NULLs.

sections.title: Lesson 8: Common Join Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them

blocks.content: Pitfall 1: Duplicate Columns After Join
When you join on a column that exists in both DataFrames, the result may contain duplicate column names:

# This can create ambiguous columns
result = readings.join(metadata, readings.meter_id == metadata.meter_id)

# Fix: join using a string (Spark deduplicates automatically)
result = readings.join(metadata, "meter_id")

# Fix for multiple join keys:
result = readings.join(metadata, ["meter_id", "region_code"])

When you pass a string or list of strings as the join condition, Spark produces only one copy of the join column. When you use an expression (df1.col == df2.col), both columns survive, causing ambiguity in downstream operations.
Pitfall 2: NULL Keys in Joins
In standard SQL and Spark, NULL != NULL. This means rows with NULL join keys never match in an inner join.

# Rows where meter_id is NULL will be dropped in an inner join
# This is standard SQL behavior, but it can cause silent data loss

# Check for NULLs in join keys before joining
null_count = readings.filter(col("meter_id").isNull()).count()
print(f"Readings with NULL meter_id: {null_count}")

# If you need to match NULLs, use eqNullSafe (equivalent to <=>)
result = readings.join(
    metadata,
    readings.meter_id.eqNullSafe(metadata.meter_id)
)
Pitfall 3: Join Explosions (Many-to-Many)
If a join key has duplicates on both sides, the result contains the Cartesian product of those duplicates.

# If meter_id M001 appears 1000 times in readings
# and 5 times in metadata (unlikely but possible with errors)
# The join produces 1000 x 5 = 5000 rows for M001 alone

# Detect potential explosions before joining:
left_dups = readings.groupBy("meter_id").count() \
    .filter(col("count") > 1).count()
right_dups = metadata.groupBy("meter_id").count() \
    .filter(col("count") > 1).count()
print(f"Duplicate keys - left: {left_dups}, right: {right_dups}")
If both sides have duplicates on the same key, investigate the data. Many-to-many joins on large datasets are a common cause of OOM errors and unexpectedly large results.
Pitfall 4: Type Mismatches in Join Keys
If the join key is StringType in one table and IntegerType in the other, Spark will cast one side. This cast may prevent predicate pushdown and disable certain join optimizations.

# Check types before joining
readings.printSchema()  # meter_id: string
metadata.printSchema()  # meter_id: integer  <-- mismatch!

# Fix: cast to the same type explicitly
metadata = metadata.withColumn("meter_id", col("meter_id").cast("string"))
Always verify that join keys have matching types. A type mismatch can silently degrade performance or produce incorrect results.
Production pipelines often join three or more tables. The order in which joins execute can dramatically affect performance.
Join Order Matters
Consider a three-table join:

result = readings \           # 1 TB
    .join(metadata, "meter_id") \  # 5 MB
    .join(weather, ["region", "date"])  # 500 GB
If Spark executes left to right:

items: Join 1: readings (1 TB) shuffle-join metadata (5 MB) → should be broadcast
Join 2: intermediate result (1 TB+) shuffle-join weather (500 GB)

blocks.content: Optimized version:

result = readings \
    .join(broadcast(metadata), "meter_id") \  # No shuffle!
    .join(weather, ["region", "date"])
Broadcasting metadata eliminates one full shuffle of the 1 TB readings table.
General rule for multi-table joins:

items: Broadcast all small tables first (eliminates their shuffles entirely)
Filter large tables as aggressively as possible before any shuffle join
Put the most selective join first (the join that eliminates the most rows)
Use explain() to verify the execution plan before running

blocks.content: Star Schema Joins
In data warehouse workloads, the star schema pattern is common: one large fact table joined with multiple small dimension tables.
Star Schema Join Pattern:

[dim_customer]  (broadcast)
                        |
  [dim_region]  --- [fact_readings] --- [dim_time]
  (broadcast)        (1 TB)             (broadcast)
                        |
                   [dim_tariff]  (broadcast)
All dimension tables broadcast. Zero shuffles of the fact table. Massive network savings.
For star-schema workloads, broadcast joins turn expensive multi-shuffle pipelines into zero-shuffle operations on the fact table.

sections.title: Lesson 10: The Complete Performance Checklist for Distributed Joins

blocks.content: Before running any join in production, walk through this checklist:
1. Filter early Eliminate rows on both sides before the join. Every row removed is a row not shuffled.
2. Select only needed columns Drop unused columns before the join. Fewer columns = less shuffle volume.
3. Broadcast small tables If one side fits in memory (<200 MB typically), broadcast it. This eliminates shuffle on the large side.
4. Check for data skew Profile key distributions before joining. If one key has 100x more data than average, apply salting or use AQE skew handling.
5. Verify join key types match Mismatched types (string vs int) cause implicit casts that can degrade performance.
6. Check for NULLs in join keys NULLs never match in standard joins. Ensure this is expected behavior, or use eqNullSafe.
7. Watch for join explosions If both sides have duplicate keys, the output can be vastly larger than either input. Profile duplicate counts before joining.
8. Inspect the execution plan Run explain() and look for BroadcastHashJoin vs SortMergeJoin. Count Exchange nodes. Every Exchange is a shuffle.
9. Use window functions instead of self-joins If you are joining a table with itself to compare adjacent rows, window functions are almost always cheaper.
10. Use anti-joins for data quality Detect orphan records, missing foreign keys, and data gaps with left_anti joins.
Quick Reference: Join Strategy Decision Tree

Scenario Strategy Expected Cost Small table (≤10 MB) + large table Broadcast Hash Join (auto) Very low (no large-side shuffle) Medium table (10–200 MB) + large table Broadcast Hash Join (explicit hint) Low Two large tables, equi-join Sort-Merge Join High (both sides shuffled + sorted) Two large tables, skewed key Sort-Merge + AQE skew handling High, but no straggler Non-equi join Broadcast Nested Loop Very high (N×M comparison) Self-join for row comparison Window function instead Medium (one shuffle, no join) Detect orphans / missing data Left Anti Join Same as inner join cost Filter by existence in another table Left Semi Join Same as inner, but more efficient
You now understand:

items: All seven logical join types (inner, left, right, full, semi, anti, cross) and when to use each
All four physical join strategies (Broadcast Hash, Sort-Merge, Shuffle Hash, Broadcast Nested Loop) and their cost profiles
How shuffle joins redistribute both tables and why filtering and projecting before joins is critical
How broadcast joins eliminate large-scale shuffle and their lifecycle from Driver to executors
How to handle skewed keys in joins using AQE, salting, or pre-filtering
How window functions (lag, lead, row_number, rank, running aggregates) replace expensive self-joins
How anti-joins and semi-joins enable data quality engineering
Common pitfalls: duplicate columns, NULL keys, join explosions, type mismatches
Multi-table join optimization: broadcast dimensions, filter facts, verify plans

blocks.content: You are not writing SQL. You are engineering distributed data relationships.

This completes the core PySpark engineering curriculum. From Module 1’s history through Module 5’s advanced joins, you have built a mental model that spans the full stack: from why distributed systems exist, through the architecture that powers them, to the practical engineering skills that make them performant.
You now think like a distributed systems engineer.

chapters.title: Module 6: Spark SQL
chapters.description: Two Interfaces, One Engine Master Spark SQL as a first-class interface to the distributed engine, understand how SQL and DataFrames share the same Catalyst optimizer, and learn to build hybrid pipelines that leverage the strengths of both.

blocks.content: Everything you have learned so far — Catalyst, DAG Scheduler, Tungsten, shuffle, partitions, broadcast joins — applies equally to Spark SQL.
That sentence is the single most important insight in this module.

Spark SQL is not a separate system. It is a second interface to the same distributed engine. When you write a SQL query and when you write a DataFrame chain, both paths converge at the Catalyst Optimizer. Both produce the same physical plan. Both execute on the same executors.
Two Interfaces, One Engine:

DataFrame API              Spark SQL
  df.filter(...)             SELECT ... WHERE ...
  df.groupBy(...)            GROUP BY ...
  df.join(...)               JOIN ... ON ...
       \                       /
        \                     /
         v                   v
    +---------------------------+
    |    Catalyst Optimizer     |
    |    (same for both)       |
    +---------------------------+
              |
              v
    +---------------------------+
    |    Physical Plan          |
    |    DAG Scheduler          |
    |    Tungsten Execution     |
    +---------------------------+
              |
              v
    [Executor] [Executor] [Executor]

This means every performance concept you have learned — predicate pushdown, projection pruning, shuffle minimization, broadcast joins, partition tuning — applies identically to SQL queries. There is no performance penalty for choosing SQL over DataFrames or vice versa.
So why learn Spark SQL if we already have DataFrames?

items: Enterprise adoption — Most organizations have SQL-literate analysts who may not know Python. Spark SQL lets them query distributed data directly.
Readability — Complex analytical queries (CTEs, subqueries, CASE expressions) are often clearer in SQL.
Interoperability — SQL is the lingua franca of data. Dashboards, BI tools, and reporting systems speak SQL.
Hybrid pipelines — The most effective PySpark code mixes DataFrame operations with SQL queries, choosing whichever is clearest for each step.
Catalog management — Databases, tables, and views are managed through SQL-style metadata operations.

sections.title: Lesson 2: Temporary Views — The Bridge Between DataFrames and SQL

blocks.content: To query a DataFrame with SQL, you must first register it as a temporary view. A view is a named reference that the SQL engine can resolve — think of it as giving your DataFrame a table name.
Creating Temporary Views

# Create a local temporary view
df.createOrReplaceTempView("readings")

# Now you can query it with SQL
result = spark.sql("SELECT * FROM readings WHERE daily_kw > 1000")
result.show()
createOrReplaceTempView() registers the DataFrame as a view that is visible within the current SparkSession. If a view with the same name already exists, it is replaced.
The view does not copy or materialize data. It is a reference to the DataFrame’s logical plan. When you query the view, Spark executes the underlying transformations.
View Creation Methods

Method Scope Behavior If Exists createOrReplaceTempView(name) Current SparkSession Replaces silently createTempView(name) Current SparkSession Throws error if exists createOrReplaceGlobalTempView(name) All SparkSessions in the application Replaces silently createGlobalTempView(name) All SparkSessions in the application Throws error if exists
Local temp views are by far the most common. Global temp views are rarely used because most applications have a single SparkSession.
Important: Global temp views live in a special database called global_temp. You must prefix queries with it:
df.createOrReplaceGlobalTempView("readings")

# Must use global_temp prefix
spark.sql("SELECT * FROM global_temp.readings")
View Lifecycle

Temporary views exist only for the duration of the SparkSession. When the session ends (or the notebook kernel restarts), all views are gone. This is fundamentally different from persistent tables, which survive across sessions. We will cover persistent tables later in this module.
View Lifecycle:

SparkSession starts
       |
  df.createOrReplaceTempView('readings')  --> view created
       |
  spark.sql('SELECT ... FROM readings')   --> works
  spark.sql('SELECT ... FROM readings')   --> works
       |
  spark.stop()  or  kernel restart
       |
  spark.sql('SELECT ... FROM readings')   --> ERROR: not found
Registering Multiple Views
In a typical pipeline, you register multiple DataFrames as views and then write SQL that joins them:

# Register both DataFrames
readings_df.createOrReplaceTempView("readings")
metadata_df.createOrReplaceTempView("metadata")

# Join them in SQL
result = spark.sql("""
    SELECT r.meter_id, r.daily_kw, m.customer_type
    FROM readings r
    JOIN metadata m ON r.meter_id = m.meter_id
    WHERE r.daily_kw > 500
""")
result.show()
The SQL query returns a DataFrame. You can chain further DataFrame operations on it, register it as another view, or write it to storage. SQL and DataFrames are fully interchangeable.

Spark SQL supports ANSI SQL with extensions. If you know SQL from any relational database, you already know most of Spark SQL. This section covers the core operations and highlights Spark-specific behavior.

SELECT and Filtering

# Basic SELECT with WHERE
spark.sql("""
    SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date
    FROM readings
    WHERE daily_kw > 1000
      AND date >= '2026-01-01'
""").show()

# Column aliases
spark.sql("""
    SELECT
        meter_id,
        daily_kw / 1000 AS daily_mw,
        CASE
            WHEN daily_kw < 500 THEN 'Low'
            WHEN daily_kw < 2000 THEN 'Medium'
            ELSE 'High'
        END AS usage_tier
    FROM readings
""").show()
The CASE expression in SQL is equivalent to when().otherwise() in the DataFrame API. Both produce the same Catalyst plan.
Aggregations

# GROUP BY with multiple aggregations
spark.sql("""
    SELECT
        customer_type,
        COUNT(*) AS meter_count,
        SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw,
        AVG(daily_kw) AS avg_kw,
        MIN(daily_kw) AS min_kw,
        MAX(daily_kw) AS max_kw
    FROM readings
    JOIN metadata USING (meter_id)
    GROUP BY customer_type
""").show()

# HAVING clause (filter after aggregation)
spark.sql("""
    SELECT customer_type, AVG(daily_kw) AS avg_kw
    FROM readings
    JOIN metadata USING (meter_id)
    GROUP BY customer_type
    HAVING AVG(daily_kw) > 1000
""").show()
Distributed note: GROUP BY triggers shuffle in SQL exactly as groupBy() does in DataFrames. The same skew, partition, and performance considerations apply. Catalyst optimizes both identically.
Joins in SQL

# Inner join (default)
spark.sql("""
    SELECT r.*, m.customer_type
    FROM readings r
    JOIN metadata m ON r.meter_id = m.meter_id
""")

# Left join
spark.sql("""
    SELECT r.*, m.customer_type
    FROM readings r
    LEFT JOIN metadata m ON r.meter_id = m.meter_id
""")

# Anti join (find orphans)
spark.sql("""
    SELECT r.*
    FROM readings r
    LEFT ANTI JOIN metadata m ON r.meter_id = m.meter_id
""")

# Broadcast hint in SQL
spark.sql("""
    SELECT /*+ BROADCAST(m) */ r.*, m.customer_type
    FROM readings r
    JOIN metadata m ON r.meter_id = m.meter_id
""")
The /*+ BROADCAST(m) */ syntax is a SQL hint. It is equivalent to broadcast() in the DataFrame API. Catalyst respects these hints when choosing the physical join strategy.
Sorting and Limiting

# ORDER BY (triggers full shuffle — same cost as DataFrame orderBy)
spark.sql("""
    SELECT meter_id, daily_kw
    FROM readings
    ORDER BY daily_kw DESC
    LIMIT 10
""")
LIMIT optimizes execution. Spark does not sort the entire dataset — it uses a TopK algorithm to find the top N rows without a full global sort. ORDER BY without LIMIT, however, triggers a full sort.
DISTINCT and Set Operations

# DISTINCT (triggers shuffle)
spark.sql("SELECT DISTINCT customer_type FROM metadata")

# UNION ALL (no shuffle — concatenates partitions)
spark.sql("""
    SELECT meter_id, daily_kw FROM readings_jan
    UNION ALL
    SELECT meter_id, daily_kw FROM readings_feb
""")

# UNION (removes duplicates — triggers shuffle)
spark.sql("""
    SELECT meter_id FROM readings_jan
    UNION
    SELECT meter_id FROM readings_feb
""")

# EXCEPT (anti-join equivalent)
spark.sql("""
    SELECT meter_id FROM readings
    EXCEPT
    SELECT meter_id FROM metadata
""")

sections.title: Lesson 4: Common Table Expressions (CTEs) — Readable Complex Queries

blocks.content: CTEs are the most powerful readability tool in SQL. They let you break a complex query into named, sequential steps — similar to how you chain DataFrame transformations with intermediate variables.
Basic CTE Syntax

result = spark.sql("""
    WITH clean_readings AS (
        SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date
        FROM readings
        WHERE daily_kw > 0 AND daily_kw < 10000
    ),
    enriched AS (
        SELECT c.*, m.customer_type, m.zip_code
        FROM clean_readings c
        JOIN metadata m ON c.meter_id = m.meter_id
    ),
    daily_summary AS (
        SELECT
            customer_type,
            date,
            SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw,
            COUNT(*) AS meter_count
        FROM enriched
        GROUP BY customer_type, date
    )
    SELECT *
    FROM daily_summary
    WHERE total_kw > 100000
    ORDER BY date, customer_type
""")
Each CTE is a named step. Later CTEs can reference earlier ones. The final SELECT produces the result.
This is equivalent to the following DataFrame chain:

# Equivalent DataFrame pipeline
clean = readings.filter((col('daily_kw') > 0) & (col('daily_kw') < 10000)) \
    .select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date')

enriched = clean.join(metadata, 'meter_id')

daily_summary = enriched.groupBy('customer_type', 'date') \
    .agg(sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'), count('*').alias('meter_count'))

result = daily_summary.filter(col('total_kw') > 100000) \
    .orderBy('date', 'customer_type')
Both produce the same Catalyst plan. The choice between them is purely about readability and team preference.
When CTEs Shine

items: Multi-step analytics — Clean, enrich, aggregate, filter. Each step is named and self-documenting.
Reusing intermediate results — If a CTE is referenced multiple times in the final query, Catalyst may optimize it (though CTEs are not guaranteed to be materialized — see below).
Complex reporting queries — Queries with multiple aggregation levels, pivots, or ranked results are much clearer with CTEs.
Team collaboration — SQL-literate analysts can review and modify CTE-based queries without Python knowledge.

blocks.content: CTE Materialization: A Common Misconception

In some databases (e.g., PostgreSQL), CTEs act as optimization fences — each CTE is materialized independently, preventing the optimizer from pushing predicates across CTE boundaries. In Spark SQL, CTEs are inlined. Catalyst treats them as subqueries and optimizes the entire plan holistically. Filters from the final SELECT can be pushed into CTE definitions.
This means:

items: CTEs in Spark have no performance penalty compared to equivalent inline subqueries or DataFrame chains.
CTEs referenced multiple times may be computed multiple times (unlike materialized views). If you need to reuse an expensive intermediate result, cache it as a DataFrame.

sections.title: Lesson 5: Subqueries — Scalar, Correlated, and EXISTS

blocks.content: Subqueries embed one query inside another. Spark SQL supports three types, each with different performance characteristics.
Scalar Subqueries
A scalar subquery returns a single value and can be used anywhere a constant is expected.

# Compare each reading to the overall average
spark.sql("""
    SELECT
        meter_id,
        daily_kw,
        daily_kw - (SELECT AVG(daily_kw) FROM readings) AS diff_from_avg
    FROM readings
""")
Catalyst optimizes scalar subqueries by computing them once and broadcasting the result. The subquery does not re-execute for each row.
IN / NOT IN Subqueries

# Filter readings to registered meters only
spark.sql("""
    SELECT *
    FROM readings
    WHERE meter_id IN (SELECT meter_id FROM metadata)
""")

# Find readings from unregistered meters
spark.sql("""
    SELECT *
    FROM readings
    WHERE meter_id NOT IN (
        SELECT meter_id FROM metadata WHERE meter_id IS NOT NULL
    )
""")

Warning: NOT IN with NULLs is dangerous. If the subquery returns any NULL value, the entire NOT IN condition evaluates to UNKNOWN, and no rows are returned. Always add a WHERE ... IS NOT NULL filter inside NOT IN subqueries, or use NOT EXISTS instead.
EXISTS / NOT EXISTS Subqueries

# Equivalent to left_semi join (more readable for some queries)
spark.sql("""
    SELECT *
    FROM readings r
    WHERE EXISTS (
        SELECT 1 FROM metadata m
        WHERE m.meter_id = r.meter_id
    )
""")

# Equivalent to left_anti join (NULL-safe, unlike NOT IN)
spark.sql("""
    SELECT *
    FROM readings r
    WHERE NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT 1 FROM metadata m
        WHERE m.meter_id = r.meter_id
    )
""")
Catalyst typically rewrites EXISTS and NOT EXISTS into semi-joins and anti-joins internally. The physical execution is identical to the DataFrame join equivalents.
Subquery Strategy Comparison

Pattern NULL-Safe? Catalyst Rewrites To Recommendation IN (subquery) Yes Left Semi Join Good for positive filtering NOT IN (subquery) NO — NULLs break it Anti Join (if no NULLs) Avoid; use NOT EXISTS EXISTS (correlated) Yes Left Semi Join Preferred for positive filtering NOT EXISTS (correlated) Yes Left Anti Join Preferred for negative filtering Scalar subquery N/A Broadcast scalar Good for single-value comparisons

sections.title: Lesson 6: Window Functions in SQL Syntax

blocks.content: You learned window functions with the DataFrame API in Module 5. Here we show the SQL syntax, which many analysts find more natural.
The OVER Clause

# lag() / lead() in SQL
spark.sql("""
    SELECT
        meter_id,
        timestamp,
        daily_kw,
        LAG(daily_kw, 1) OVER (
            PARTITION BY meter_id ORDER BY timestamp
        ) AS prev_kw,
        daily_kw - LAG(daily_kw, 1) OVER (
            PARTITION BY meter_id ORDER BY timestamp
        ) AS kw_change
    FROM readings
""")
Ranking in SQL

# Top 5 highest-consuming meters per customer type
spark.sql("""
    WITH ranked AS (
        SELECT
            meter_id,
            customer_type,
            daily_kw,
            ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
                PARTITION BY customer_type
                ORDER BY daily_kw DESC
            ) AS rank
        FROM readings
        JOIN metadata USING (meter_id)
    )
    SELECT * FROM ranked WHERE rank <= 5
""")
Running Aggregates in SQL

# Running total and 7-day moving average
spark.sql("""
    SELECT
        meter_id,
        date,
        daily_kw,
        SUM(daily_kw) OVER (
            PARTITION BY meter_id
            ORDER BY date
            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
        ) AS running_total,
        AVG(daily_kw) OVER (
            PARTITION BY meter_id
            ORDER BY date
            ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
        ) AS moving_avg_7d
    FROM readings
""")
Named Window Definitions
When multiple columns use the same window specification, you can define it once with the WINDOW clause:

spark.sql("""
    SELECT
        meter_id,
        date,
        daily_kw,
        LAG(daily_kw, 1) OVER w AS prev_kw,
        LEAD(daily_kw, 1) OVER w AS next_kw,
        AVG(daily_kw) OVER w AS moving_avg
    FROM readings
    WINDOW w AS (
        PARTITION BY meter_id ORDER BY date
        ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND 3 FOLLOWING
    )
""")
This eliminates repetition and makes the query easier to maintain.

sections.title: Lesson 7: SQL vs DataFrame API — When to Use Which

blocks.content: Both interfaces produce identical physical plans. The choice is about readability, maintainability, and team capability, not performance.

Criterion SQL Preferred DataFrame API Preferred Team skills SQL-literate analysts, mixed teams Python-heavy engineering teams Query complexity Multi-step analytics, CTEs, complex CASE Dynamic column logic, programmatic joins Readability Declarative intent is clear Chaining logic with variables Dynamic queries Difficult (string interpolation is fragile) Natural (Python variables, loops, conditionals) Testing Harder to unit test SQL strings Easier to test DataFrame functions IDE support Limited (SQL in strings) Full autocomplete and type checking BI tool integration Direct connection via JDBC/ODBC Not applicable Schema evolution Manual ALTER TABLE Programmatic schema manipulation
The Hybrid Pattern — Best of Both Worlds
The most effective PySpark code uses both interfaces, choosing whichever is clearest for each step:

# Step 1: Read and register (DataFrame)
readings = spark.read.parquet('/data/readings/')
metadata = spark.read.parquet('/data/metadata/')
readings.createOrReplaceTempView('readings')
metadata.createOrReplaceTempView('metadata')

# Step 2: Complex analytics (SQL — more readable for this)
daily_stats = spark.sql("""
    WITH enriched AS (
        SELECT r.*, m.customer_type, m.zip_code
        FROM readings r
        JOIN metadata m ON r.meter_id = m.meter_id
    )
    SELECT
        customer_type,
        date,
        SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw,
        PERCENTILE_APPROX(daily_kw, 0.95) AS p95_kw
    FROM enriched
    GROUP BY customer_type, date
""")

# Step 3: Programmatic output (DataFrame — more flexible)
daily_stats \
    .coalesce(20) \
    .write.mode('overwrite') \
    .partitionBy('customer_type') \
    .parquet('/output/daily_stats/')
Read with DataFrames (programmatic control over formats and schemas). Analyze with SQL (declarative, readable). Write with DataFrames (control over partitioning and output).

sections.title: Lesson 8: The Catalog — Databases, Tables, and Metadata

blocks.content: Spark SQL includes a catalog — a metadata store that tracks databases, tables, views, and functions. In enterprise environments (especially Databricks), the catalog is the central registry for all data assets.
Exploring the Catalog

# List all databases
spark.catalog.listDatabases()

# List all tables in the current database
spark.catalog.listTables()

# List tables in a specific database
spark.catalog.listTables("analytics")

# List columns of a table
spark.catalog.listColumns("readings")

# Check if a table exists
spark.catalog.tableExists("readings")

# Get current database
spark.catalog.currentDatabase()
Creating Databases

# Create a database
spark.sql("CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS smart_grid")

# Switch to it
spark.sql("USE smart_grid")

# All subsequent table operations apply to smart_grid
spark.sql("CREATE TABLE readings AS SELECT * FROM ...")
Managed vs External Tables
Spark distinguishes between two types of persistent tables:

Type Data Ownership DROP Behavior Use When Managed Table Spark owns the data Drops metadata AND data files Spark controls the full lifecycle External Table You own the data Drops metadata only; data files remain Data is shared with other systems

# Managed table: Spark controls data location
spark.sql("""
    CREATE TABLE smart_grid.readings (
        meter_id STRING,
        daily_kw DOUBLE,
        date DATE
    )
    USING PARQUET
""")

# External table: you specify the data location
spark.sql("""
    CREATE TABLE smart_grid.readings_ext (
        meter_id STRING,
        daily_kw DOUBLE,
        date DATE
    )
    USING PARQUET
    LOCATION '/data/external/readings/'
""")

In Databricks with Delta Lake, managed tables use the workspace’s default storage. External tables point to a path you control. In either case, the catalog stores the table’s schema, location, and partition information.
Saving DataFrames as Tables

# Save as a managed table
df.write.mode("overwrite").saveAsTable("smart_grid.readings")

# Save as a partitioned table
df.write \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .partitionBy("date") \
    .saveAsTable("smart_grid.readings_partitioned")

# Read back from the catalog
readings = spark.table("smart_grid.readings")

spark.table() reads a table registered in the catalog. It is equivalent to spark.sql("SELECT * FROM smart_grid.readings") but more concise.
Table Operations

# Describe table structure
spark.sql("DESCRIBE TABLE readings")

# Describe table with detailed metadata
spark.sql("DESCRIBE TABLE EXTENDED readings")

# Show table creation statement
spark.sql("SHOW CREATE TABLE readings")

# Drop a table
spark.sql("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS readings")

# Rename a table
spark.sql("ALTER TABLE readings RENAME TO meter_readings")

# Add a column
spark.sql("ALTER TABLE readings ADD COLUMNS (region STRING)")
Persisted Views
Unlike temporary views that vanish with the session, persisted views are stored in the catalog and survive across sessions:

# Create a persisted view
spark.sql("""
    CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW smart_grid.high_usage AS
    SELECT * FROM smart_grid.readings WHERE daily_kw > 5000
""")

# This view persists across sessions
# Future sessions can query it directly:
spark.sql("SELECT * FROM smart_grid.high_usage")
Persisted views store the query definition, not the data. They are recomputed each time they are queried. For materialized (pre-computed) results, use tables.

sections.title: Lesson 9: SQL Hints and Performance Control

blocks.content: Catalyst makes intelligent optimization decisions, but sometimes you know more than the optimizer. SQL hints let you override Catalyst’s choices.
Join Hints

# Force broadcast join
spark.sql("""
    SELECT /*+ BROADCAST(m) */
        r.meter_id, r.daily_kw, m.customer_type
    FROM readings r
    JOIN metadata m ON r.meter_id = m.meter_id
""")

# Force sort-merge join (disable broadcast)
spark.sql("""
    SELECT /*+ MERGE(r, m) */
        r.meter_id, r.daily_kw, m.customer_type
    FROM readings r
    JOIN metadata m ON r.meter_id = m.meter_id
""")

# Force shuffle hash join
spark.sql("""
    SELECT /*+ SHUFFLE_HASH(m) */
        r.meter_id, r.daily_kw, m.customer_type
    FROM readings r
    JOIN metadata m ON r.meter_id = m.meter_id
""")

Hint Forces When to Use BROADCAST(table) Broadcast Hash Join When Catalyst misestimates table size (filtered subquery, missing stats) MERGE(t1, t2) Sort-Merge Join When you know broadcast would be too expensive SHUFFLE_HASH(table) Shuffle Hash Join When one side is moderately sized, avoiding sort cost SHUFFLE_REPLICATE_NL(table) Broadcast Nested Loop Non-equi joins with small table (rare)
Coalesce and Repartition Hints

# Repartition output of a SQL query
spark.sql("""
    SELECT /*+ REPARTITION(200) */
        meter_id, daily_kw
    FROM readings
""")

# Repartition by a specific column
spark.sql("""
    SELECT /*+ REPARTITION_BY_RANGE(10, meter_id) */
        meter_id, daily_kw
    FROM readings
""")

# Coalesce to reduce output partitions
spark.sql("""
    SELECT /*+ COALESCE(5) */
        meter_id, daily_kw
    FROM readings
""")
These hints give you the same partition control in SQL that repartition() and coalesce() provide in the DataFrame API.
Verifying Plan Optimization in SQL
You can inspect SQL query plans the same way as DataFrames:

# Method 1: explain() on the result DataFrame
result = spark.sql("SELECT ... FROM readings JOIN metadata ...")
result.explain("formatted")

# Method 2: EXPLAIN directly in SQL
spark.sql("EXPLAIN FORMATTED SELECT ... FROM readings JOIN metadata ...")
Look for the same red flags: Exchange nodes (shuffle), SortMergeJoin where BroadcastHashJoin would work, missing PushedFilters, and full ReadSchema.

sections.title: Lesson 10: The Spark SQL Built-in Functions Library

blocks.content: Spark SQL includes hundreds of built-in functions. These are the same functions available through pyspark.sql.functions, accessible via SQL syntax.
String Functions

spark.sql("""
    SELECT
        UPPER(customer_type) AS upper_type,
        LOWER(customer_type) AS lower_type,
        LENGTH(meter_id) AS id_length,
        TRIM(meter_id) AS trimmed_id,
        CONCAT(meter_id, '_', date) AS composite_key,
        SUBSTRING(meter_id, 1, 3) AS prefix,
        REGEXP_REPLACE(meter_id, '[^0-9]', '') AS digits_only
    FROM readings JOIN metadata USING (meter_id)
""")
Date and Timestamp Functions

spark.sql("""
    SELECT
        date,
        YEAR(date) AS year,
        MONTH(date) AS month,
        DAY(date) AS day,
        DAYOFWEEK(date) AS dow,
        DATE_ADD(date, 30) AS plus_30_days,
        DATEDIFF(CURRENT_DATE(), date) AS days_ago,
        DATE_TRUNC('month', date) AS month_start,
        DATE_FORMAT(date, 'yyyy-MM') AS year_month
    FROM readings
""")
Conditional and NULL Functions

spark.sql("""
    SELECT
        meter_id,
        COALESCE(daily_kw, 0) AS kw_filled,
        IFNULL(daily_kw, 0) AS kw_filled_alt,
        NULLIF(daily_kw, 0) AS kw_null_if_zero,
        CASE
            WHEN daily_kw IS NULL THEN 'Missing'
            WHEN daily_kw < 500 THEN 'Low'
            ELSE 'High'
        END AS status
    FROM readings
""")
Aggregate and Statistical Functions

spark.sql("""
    SELECT
        customer_type,
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(daily_kw) AS total,
        AVG(daily_kw) AS mean,
        STDDEV(daily_kw) AS std_dev,
        PERCENTILE_APPROX(daily_kw, 0.5) AS median,
        PERCENTILE_APPROX(daily_kw, ARRAY(0.25, 0.5, 0.75)) AS quartiles,
        COLLECT_LIST(meter_id) AS meter_list,
        COUNT(DISTINCT meter_id) AS unique_meters
    FROM readings JOIN metadata USING (meter_id)
    GROUP BY customer_type
""")
Functions Reference by Category

Category Key Functions Notes String UPPER, LOWER, LENGTH, TRIM, CONCAT, SUBSTRING, REGEXP_REPLACE, SPLIT, LPAD, RPAD Identical to pyspark.sql.functions equivalents Date/Time YEAR, MONTH, DAY, DATE_ADD, DATE_SUB, DATEDIFF, DATE_TRUNC, DATE_FORMAT, CURRENT_DATE, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP Spark uses Java date format patterns Math ROUND, CEIL, FLOOR, ABS, SQRT, LOG, POW, GREATEST, LEAST

NULL handling COALESCE, IFNULL, NULLIF, NVL, NVL2 COALESCE takes any number of arguments Conditional CASE/WHEN/ELSE, IF, IIF IF(condition, true_val, false_val) Aggregate SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX, STDDEV, VARIANCE, COLLECT_LIST, COLLECT_SET, PERCENTILE_APPROX COLLECT_LIST/SET returns arrays Window ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, LAG, LEAD, FIRST_VALUE, LAST_VALUE, NTILE, CUME_DIST, PERCENT_RANK Require OVER clause Type Conversion CAST, TRY_CAST, INT, DOUBLE, STRING, DATE, TIMESTAMP TRY_CAST returns NULL instead of error
To see all available functions: spark.sql("SHOW FUNCTIONS").show(500, truncate=False)
Pattern 1: Incremental Processing with Merge Logic
A common ETL pattern: merge new data into an existing table, inserting new records and updating existing ones.

# Register new batch and existing table
new_batch.createOrReplaceTempView("new_data")
existing.createOrReplaceTempView("existing_data")

# Find truly new records (anti-join)
inserts = spark.sql("""
    SELECT n.*
    FROM new_data n
    LEFT ANTI JOIN existing_data e ON n.meter_id = e.meter_id AND n.date = e.date
""")

# Find records to update (inner join, take new values)
updates = spark.sql("""
    SELECT n.*
    FROM new_data n
    JOIN existing_data e ON n.meter_id = e.meter_id AND n.date = e.date
""")

# Find unchanged records
unchanged = spark.sql("""
    SELECT e.*
    FROM existing_data e
    LEFT ANTI JOIN new_data n ON e.meter_id = n.meter_id AND e.date = n.date
""")

# Combine for the final table state
final = inserts.union(updates).union(unchanged)
In Databricks with Delta Lake, this is simplified to a single MERGE INTO statement. But understanding the underlying logic is essential.
Pattern 2: Deduplication with ROW_NUMBER

# Keep only the latest reading per meter per day
deduped = spark.sql("""
    WITH ranked AS (
        SELECT *,
            ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
                PARTITION BY meter_id, date
                ORDER BY timestamp DESC
            ) AS rn
        FROM readings
    )
    SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, timestamp
    FROM ranked
    WHERE rn = 1
""")
This is the standard deduplication pattern in distributed systems. It works regardless of how many duplicates exist and scales to any data size.
Pattern 3: Pivot Tables

# Pivot: one row per meter, columns for each customer_type's total
# Note: PIVOT is a Spark SQL extension, not standard ANSI SQL
spark.sql("""
    SELECT *
    FROM (
        SELECT date, customer_type, daily_kw
        FROM readings JOIN metadata USING (meter_id)
    )
    PIVOT (
        SUM(daily_kw)
        FOR customer_type IN ('Residential', 'Commercial', 'Industrial')
    )
""")
Pivot transforms row-based data into columnar layout. Specify exact values in the IN clause — Spark must know the output columns at plan time.
Pattern 4: Gap Detection in Time-Series

# Detect missing readings (gaps > 1 day) per meter
gaps = spark.sql("""
    WITH ordered AS (
        SELECT
            meter_id,
            date,
            LAG(date) OVER (PARTITION BY meter_id ORDER BY date) AS prev_date
        FROM readings
    )
    SELECT
        meter_id,
        prev_date AS gap_start,
        date AS gap_end,
        DATEDIFF(date, prev_date) AS gap_days
    FROM ordered
    WHERE DATEDIFF(date, prev_date) > 1
    ORDER BY gap_days DESC
""")

This pattern uses a window function to compare each reading’s date to its predecessor. Gaps larger than 1 day indicate missing data — critical for data quality monitoring in IoT and smart grid systems.
You now understand:

items: That SQL and DataFrames are two interfaces to the same Catalyst engine — with identical performance
How to create, use, and manage temporary views as the bridge between DataFrames and SQL
Core SQL operations in Spark: SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, JOIN, ORDER BY, DISTINCT, UNION
How to write CTEs for readable, multi-step analytical queries
Subquery types (scalar, IN, EXISTS) and why NOT EXISTS is safer than NOT IN
Window functions in SQL syntax (OVER, PARTITION BY, frame specifications, WINDOW clause)
When to use SQL vs DataFrames, and the hybrid pattern that combines both
Catalog management: databases, managed vs external tables, persisted views
SQL hints for controlling join strategy, partitioning, and coalescing
The Spark SQL built-in functions library across all categories
Production patterns: incremental merge, deduplication, pivots, time-series gap detection

blocks.content: You are not just writing SQL. You are issuing declarative instructions to a distributed optimizer that executes across hundreds of machines.

In Module 7, we turn to data cleaning and transformation patterns — the practical engineering of turning raw, messy, real-world data into reliable analytical assets using everything you have learned so far.

chapters.title: Module 7: Data Cleaning & Transformation Patterns at Scale
chapters.description: Master the practical engineering of transforming raw, messy, real-world data into reliable analytical assets using PySpark, from NULL handling and deduplication through outlier detection, type enforcement, and the medallion architecture.

blocks.content: You can now build distributed execution graphs, read execution plans, tune performance, engineer joins, and write SQL. You have mastered the engine.
Now the engine meets reality.

Real-world data is not clean. It arrives with NULLs where values should exist, duplicates where records should be unique, timestamps in six different formats, negative numbers in columns that should be positive, and string fields filled with invisible whitespace characters.

A model trained on dirty data produces wrong predictions. A dashboard built on duplicate records shows inflated metrics. An alert system fed by outlier-contaminated data fires false alarms or — worse — misses real ones.
Data cleaning is not a preliminary chore. It is the engineering that makes everything downstream trustworthy.

In this module, you will learn the systematic patterns that production PySpark engineers use to transform raw data into reliable assets. Every pattern uses the distributed primitives you have already mastered: filters, window functions, joins, aggregations, and Catalyst-optimized execution plans.
The Cost of Dirty Data

Consider our smart grid domain. A utility company ingests 96 million meter readings per day. If just 0.1% of those readings contain errors — duplicate transmissions, NULL values from network dropouts, impossible spikes from faulty sensors — that is 96,000 bad records per day. Over a year, 35 million corrupted records accumulate in the data lake. If the demand forecasting model trains on this data without cleaning, it systematically overestimates consumption in regions with high duplication rates and underestimates in regions with frequent NULLs. The result: incorrect power allocation, wasted generation capacity, and potential blackouts.
Cleaning is not optional. It is infrastructure.

sections.title: Lesson 2: The Medallion Architecture — Bronze, Silver, Gold

blocks.content: Before diving into individual cleaning techniques, you need a framework for organizing the work. The medallion architecture (also called multi-hop architecture) is the industry standard for structuring data pipelines.
The Medallion Architecture:

RAW DATA
  (CSV, JSON, API, IoT, Kafka)
       |
       v
  +------------------+
  |    BRONZE        |  Raw ingestion. Exact copy of source.
  |    (Raw)         |  Append-only. Schema-on-read.
  +------------------+  No transformations. Full history.
       |
       v
  +------------------+
  |    SILVER        |  Cleaned and conformed.
  |    (Cleaned)     |  Deduplicated. NULLs handled.
  +------------------+  Types enforced. Validated.
       |
       v
  +------------------+
  |    GOLD          |  Business-level aggregations.
  |    (Aggregated)  |  Feature tables. Report tables.
  +------------------+  Ready for ML, dashboards, APIs.
Bronze Layer: Preserve Everything

The bronze layer is a faithful copy of the source data. No cleaning, no deduplication, no type casting. If the source sends a NULL, bronze stores a NULL. If the source sends a duplicate, bronze stores the duplicate.
Why preserve raw data?

items: Reproducibility — You can always re-derive silver and gold from bronze if cleaning logic changes.
Auditability — Regulators or auditors can verify what the source actually sent.
Debugging — When a downstream anomaly appears, you can trace it back to the raw input.

blocks.content:
# Bronze ingestion: raw, untouched, with ingestion metadata
raw_df = spark.read \
    .option('header', True) \
    .option('inferSchema', False)  # Keep everything as strings at bronze \
    .csv('/data/incoming/meters_2026-02-25/')

bronze = raw_df \
    .withColumn('_ingestion_timestamp', current_timestamp()) \
    .withColumn('_source_file', input_file_name())

bronze.write \
    .mode('append') \
    .partitionBy('_ingestion_date') \
    .parquet('/data/bronze/meter_readings/')

Adding ingestion metadata (_ingestion_timestamp, _source_file) is a best practice. It enables lineage tracking: for any record in silver or gold, you can trace back to exactly when and from where it was ingested.
Silver Layer: Clean and Conform
Silver is where all the cleaning happens. This module teaches the patterns you apply here:

items: NULL handling
Deduplication
Type casting and schema enforcement
String standardization
Outlier detection and removal
Date/timestamp normalization
Referential integrity validation

blocks.content: The output of silver is a reliable, typed, deduplicated dataset that downstream consumers can trust.
Gold Layer: Business Aggregations
Gold tables are purpose-built for specific consumers:

items: Feature tables for ML models (daily consumption features per meter)
Report tables for dashboards (regional daily summaries)
API tables for applications (current meter status)

blocks.content: Gold tables are aggregated, joined, and shaped for their consumer. They should be fast to query, with appropriate partitioning and column pruning already applied.
Why This Structure Matters for Distributed Systems
The medallion architecture is not just organizational. It has direct performance implications:

Layer Read Pattern Write Pattern Format Bronze Full scans (reprocessing) Append-only, high throughput Parquet or Delta (append) Silver Filtered reads by date/key Overwrite or merge Delta or Parquet (partitioned) Gold Targeted queries by analysts/models Overwrite on schedule Delta or Parquet (optimized)
Each layer is optimized for its access pattern. Bronze maximizes write throughput. Silver maximizes cleaning efficiency. Gold maximizes query speed.

sections.title: Lesson 3: NULL Handling — The Most Common Data Problem

blocks.content: NULLs are the most pervasive data quality issue. In our smart grid data, NULLs appear when meters lose network connectivity, when sensors malfunction, when fields are optional in the source schema, or when upstream ETL jobs fail silently.
Detecting NULLs

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, count, when, sum as spark_sum, lit

# Count NULLs per column
null_counts = df.select(
    [spark_sum(when(col(c).isNull(), 1).otherwise(0)).alias(c)
     for c in df.columns]
)
null_counts.show()

# NULL percentage per column
total = df.count()
null_pct = df.select(
    [(spark_sum(when(col(c).isNull(), 1).otherwise(0)) / lit(total) * 100)
     .alias(c) for c in df.columns]
)
null_pct.show()
Always profile NULLs before choosing a strategy. A column with 0.1% NULLs requires a different approach than a column with 40% NULLs.
Strategy 1: Drop Rows with NULLs

# Drop rows where ANY column is NULL
df.dropna()

# Drop rows where specific columns are NULL
df.dropna(subset=['meter_id', 'daily_kw'])

# Drop rows where ALL specified columns are NULL
df.dropna(how='all', subset=['daily_kw', 'voltage', 'current'])

# Drop rows with fewer than N non-null values
df.dropna(thresh=3)  # Keep rows with at least 3 non-null values

When to drop: When the NULL rate is very low (<1%) and the missing data is random. Dropping is safe when losing a small fraction of rows does not bias the dataset. When NOT to drop: When NULLs are systematic (all readings from region X are NULL during storms), dropping introduces survivorship bias. The missing data carries information.
Strategy 2: Fill with Constants

# Fill all NULLs with a single value (type must match)
df.fillna(0)

# Fill specific columns with different values
df.fillna({
    'daily_kw': 0.0,
    'customer_type': 'Unknown',
    'region_code': 'UNASSIGNED'
})

# Fill with column-specific logic using coalesce
from pyspark.sql.functions import coalesce, lit
df.withColumn('daily_kw', coalesce(col('daily_kw'), lit(0.0)))

When to use: When a sensible default exists. Zero is reasonable for "missing readings that probably mean zero consumption." But zero is dangerous for "missing temperature readings" where the actual temperature matters for analysis.
Strategy 3: Forward/Backward Fill (Time-Series)
For time-series data, the best fill strategy is often to carry the last known value forward. This is called forward-fill and requires a window function.

from pyspark.sql.window import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import last

# Forward-fill: carry last non-null value forward
w = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('timestamp') \
    .rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)

filled = df.withColumn(
    'daily_kw_filled',
    coalesce(col('daily_kw'), last('daily_kw', ignorenulls=True).over(w))
)
How it works: The window looks at all rows from the start of the partition to the current row, finds the last non-null value for daily_kw, and uses it when the current value is NULL.

Smart grid example: A meter loses connectivity at 2:00 AM and reconnects at 6:00 AM. The readings at 2:15, 2:30, ... 5:45 are NULL. Forward-fill carries the 2:00 AM reading through the gap. This is reasonable for slow-changing values like baseline consumption, but would be wrong for rapidly changing values like real-time voltage.
Strategy 4: Statistical Imputation

from pyspark.sql.functions import avg, median

# Fill NULLs with the group mean
group_avg = df.groupBy('customer_type').agg(
    avg('daily_kw').alias('avg_kw')
)

filled = df.join(group_avg, 'customer_type', 'left') \
    .withColumn('daily_kw',
        coalesce(col('daily_kw'), col('avg_kw'))
    ) \
    .drop('avg_kw')
When to use: When NULLs are random within groups and the group average is a reasonable estimate. Common in feature engineering for ML pipelines.
NULL Handling Strategy Decision Guide

Scenario
Strategy
Example
Critical key column is NULL
Drop the row
meter_id is NULL — record is useless
Low NULL rate, random missingness
Drop rows
<1% of readings missing randomly
Sensible default exists
Fill with constant
Missing customer_type → 'Unknown'
Time-series with connectivity gaps
Forward-fill
Meter offline for 4 hours
Nullable feature for ML model
Group mean/median
Missing daily_kw → segment average
High NULL rate, systematic pattern
Investigate root cause first
All Region X readings NULL during storms
NULL Propagation: The Silent Bug
NULLs propagate silently through expressions. Any arithmetic or comparison involving NULL produces NULL:

# These all produce NULL if daily_kw is NULL:
# daily_kw + 100      --> NULL
# daily_kw * 2        --> NULL
# daily_kw > 1000     --> NULL (not False!)
# daily_kw == NULL    --> NULL (never use == for NULL checks)

# Correct NULL check:
df.filter(col('daily_kw').isNull())    # rows where kw IS null
df.filter(col('daily_kw').isNotNull()) # rows where kw is NOT null
This means a filter like col('daily_kw') > 0 silently drops NULL rows — the comparison returns NULL, which is treated as False. If you want to keep NULLs, handle them explicitly.

Duplicates are the second most common data quality problem. They arise from network retransmissions (a meter sends the same reading twice), ETL re-runs (a pipeline processes the same batch twice), source system bugs, or merging overlapping data sources.
Exact Deduplication
Remove rows that are identical across all columns or a set of key columns.

# Remove fully identical rows
df.dropDuplicates()

# Remove duplicates based on natural key
df.dropDuplicates(['meter_id', 'date'])

Performance note: dropDuplicates() triggers a shuffle — Spark must compare rows across partitions. On large datasets, this is a significant operation. It is equivalent to a groupBy on the deduplicated columns followed by picking one row per group.
Keep-Last Deduplication with ROW_NUMBER

When duplicates have different timestamps or versions, you typically want to keep the latest record. dropDuplicates() does not guarantee which duplicate survives. Use ROW_NUMBER for deterministic control.

from pyspark.sql.functions import row_number, desc
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

# Define window: partition by natural key, order by timestamp descending
w = Window.partitionBy('meter_id', 'date').orderBy(desc('timestamp'))

# Assign row numbers: 1 = most recent per (meter_id, date)
ranked = df.withColumn('rn', row_number().over(w))

# Keep only the latest record per key
deduped = ranked.filter(col('rn') == 1).drop('rn')

This pattern is the standard deduplication technique in production PySpark. It is deterministic (the latest timestamp always wins), flexible (you control the ordering), and scales to any dataset size.
Before Deduplication:
After filter(rn == 1):
Quantifying Duplication Before Cleaning
Always measure the duplication rate before removing duplicates. This serves as both a diagnostic and a validation checkpoint.

total = df.count()
distinct = df.dropDuplicates(['meter_id', 'date']).count()
dup_rate = (total - distinct) / total * 100
print(f'Total rows: {total}')
print(f'Distinct keys: {distinct}')
print(f'Duplication rate: {dup_rate:.2f}%')

# Find the most duplicated keys
df.groupBy('meter_id', 'date').count() \
    .filter(col('count') > 1) \
    .orderBy(desc('count')) \
    .show(20)
If the duplication rate is unexpectedly high (>5%), investigate the root cause before blindly deduplicating. High duplication often signals an upstream pipeline bug.

Bronze data often arrives as strings. CSV files have no type metadata. JSON values may be strings that look like numbers. API responses embed dates as formatted strings. Silver data must have correct, enforced types. Type mismatches cause silent bugs: string comparison of "9" > "10" returns True (lexicographic), while numeric comparison of 9 > 10 returns False.
Safe Casting with try_cast
Standard cast() throws an error or produces NULL silently when a value cannot be converted. In Spark 3.4+, try_cast provides explicit control:

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, try_to_number, to_date, to_timestamp

# Cast string to double (NULL if unparseable)
df = df.withColumn('daily_kw', col('daily_kw_str').cast('double'))

# Parse dates from strings
df = df.withColumn('date', to_date(col('date_str'), 'yyyy-MM-dd'))

# Parse timestamps
df = df.withColumn('ts', to_timestamp(col('ts_str'), 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss'))
When cast fails (e.g., casting "N/A" to double), Spark produces NULL. This is correct behavior — but you must detect and handle these NULLs downstream.
Detecting Cast Failures

# Detect rows where casting failed (original non-null, cast result null)
cast_failures = df.filter(
    col('daily_kw_str').isNotNull() & col('daily_kw').isNull()
)

failure_count = cast_failures.count()
print(f'Cast failures: {failure_count}')

# Inspect the unparseable values
cast_failures.select('daily_kw_str').distinct().show(20, truncate=False)
Common cast failures: "N/A", "NULL", "---", empty strings, numbers with commas ("1,200.50"), numbers with units ("450 kW"). Each requires a specific pre-cleaning step before casting.
Pre-Cleaning Before Casting

from pyspark.sql.functions import regexp_replace, trim, when, upper

# Clean numeric strings before casting
df = df.withColumn('daily_kw_clean',
    regexp_replace(col('daily_kw_str'), '[^0-9.\\-]', '')  # Remove non-numeric chars
)

# Handle known sentinel values
df = df.withColumn('daily_kw_clean',
    when(upper(trim(col('daily_kw_str'))).isin('N/A', 'NULL', 'NA', '', '-'),
         None)
    .otherwise(col('daily_kw_clean'))
)

# Now cast safely
df = df.withColumn('daily_kw', col('daily_kw_clean').cast('double'))
Enforcing a Schema at Read Time
The most robust approach is to enforce a schema when reading, rather than casting afterward:

from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType

silver_schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id', StringType(), nullable=False),
    StructField('customer_type', StringType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('daily_kw', DoubleType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('date', DateType(), nullable=True),
])

# Read with enforced schema (non-conforming values become NULL)
df = spark.read \
    .schema(silver_schema) \
    .option('mode', 'PERMISSIVE') \
    .option('columnNameOfCorruptRecord', '_corrupt') \
    .csv('/data/bronze/meter_readings/')

Read Mode Behavior on Bad Records Use When PERMISSIVE (default) Sets non-conforming fields to NULL; optionally captures raw line Production pipelines (don't lose data) DROPMALFORMED Silently drops non-conforming rows Exploratory analysis (acceptable data loss) FAILFAST Throws exception on first bad record Strict validation jobs (fail loudly)

String fields are the most inconsistent data type. The same concept can be encoded dozens of ways: "Residential", "residential", "RESIDENTIAL", " Residential ", "Res.", "RES". If your pipeline does not standardize strings, aggregations silently split into phantom groups.
Essential String Operations

from pyspark.sql.functions import trim, ltrim, rtrim, lower, upper, initcap
from pyspark.sql.functions import regexp_replace, regexp_extract, split, length

# Remove leading/trailing whitespace (including invisible characters)
df = df.withColumn('customer_type', trim(col('customer_type')))

# Standardize case
df = df.withColumn('customer_type', upper(col('customer_type')))

# Replace multiple spaces with single space
df = df.withColumn('address',
    regexp_replace(col('address'), '\\s+', ' ')
)

# Remove non-printable characters
df = df.withColumn('notes',
    regexp_replace(col('notes'), '[^\\x20-\\x7E]', '')
)
Mapping Inconsistent Categories
When source systems use inconsistent category names, create a mapping:

from pyspark.sql.functions import when

# Map variant spellings to canonical values
df = df.withColumn('customer_type_clean',
    when(upper(trim(col('customer_type'))).isin(
        'RESIDENTIAL', 'RES', 'RES.', 'R'), 'RESIDENTIAL')
    .when(upper(trim(col('customer_type'))).isin(
        'COMMERCIAL', 'COM', 'COM.', 'C', 'COMM'), 'COMMERCIAL')
    .when(upper(trim(col('customer_type'))).isin(
        'INDUSTRIAL', 'IND', 'IND.', 'I'), 'INDUSTRIAL')
    .otherwise('UNKNOWN')
)
For large mapping tables, use a broadcast join against a reference table instead of chaining when() expressions:

# Reference mapping table
mapping_data = [
    ('residential', 'RESIDENTIAL'), ('res', 'RESIDENTIAL'),
    ('res.', 'RESIDENTIAL'), ('r', 'RESIDENTIAL'),
    ('commercial', 'COMMERCIAL'), ('com', 'COMMERCIAL'),
    ('comm', 'COMMERCIAL'), ('c', 'COMMERCIAL'),
    ('industrial', 'INDUSTRIAL'), ('ind', 'INDUSTRIAL'),
]
mapping = spark.createDataFrame(mapping_data, ['raw_type', 'clean_type'])

# Broadcast join for standardization
df = df.withColumn('type_lower', lower(trim(col('customer_type')))) \
    .join(broadcast(mapping),
          col('type_lower') == col('raw_type'),
          'left') \
    .withColumn('customer_type',
        coalesce(col('clean_type'), lit('UNKNOWN'))) \
    .drop('type_lower', 'raw_type', 'clean_type')
The broadcast join approach is more maintainable (the mapping table can be stored as a file and updated without changing code) and often faster for many categories.
Extracting Structured Data from Strings

# Extract meter model and version from a description field
# Input: 'SmartMeter-v3.2-Industrial'
df = df.withColumn('model',
    regexp_extract(col('description'), '(\\w+)-v', 1)
)
df = df.withColumn('version',
    regexp_extract(col('description'), 'v([0-9.]+)', 1)
)

# Split a composite key
# Input: 'US-CA-94105'
df = df.withColumn('country', split(col('location_code'), '-').getItem(0))
df = df.withColumn('state', split(col('location_code'), '-').getItem(1))
df = df.withColumn('zip', split(col('location_code'), '-').getItem(2))

Date and timestamp inconsistencies are pervasive in multi-source pipelines. Different systems use different formats, timezones, and conventions. Getting dates wrong can shift entire days of data into wrong partitions.
Parsing Multiple Date Formats
When different sources use different formats, parse them conditionally:

from pyspark.sql.functions import to_date, coalesce

# Try multiple formats, take the first successful parse
df = df.withColumn('date_parsed',
    coalesce(
        to_date(col('date_str'), 'yyyy-MM-dd'),        # 2026-02-25
        to_date(col('date_str'), 'MM/dd/yyyy'),         # 02/25/2026
        to_date(col('date_str'), 'dd-MMM-yyyy'),        # 25-Feb-2026
        to_date(col('date_str'), 'yyyyMMdd'),            # 20260225
    )
)

# Detect unparseable dates
bad_dates = df.filter(
    col('date_str').isNotNull() & col('date_parsed').isNull()
)
bad_dates.select('date_str').distinct().show(20, truncate=False)
Timezone Handling
Timestamp data from multiple regions may arrive in different timezones. For consistent analysis, normalize everything to a single timezone (typically UTC).

from pyspark.sql.functions import from_utc_timestamp, to_utc_timestamp

# Convert local timestamps to UTC
df = df.withColumn('timestamp_utc',
    to_utc_timestamp(col('local_timestamp'), col('timezone'))
)

# Convert UTC to a specific local timezone for display
df = df.withColumn('timestamp_eastern',
    from_utc_timestamp(col('timestamp_utc'), 'America/New_York')
)
Warning: Spark uses Java timezone IDs (e.g., 'America/New_York', not 'EST'). Abbreviations like 'EST' are ambiguous and should be avoided.
Filling Time-Series Gaps
After detecting missing timestamps (using the gap detection pattern from Module 6), you may need to generate the missing rows and fill them:

from pyspark.sql.functions import explode, sequence, col, lit

# Generate expected timestamps for each meter (every 15 min for one day)
expected = df.select('meter_id').distinct() \
    .crossJoin(
        spark.sql("""
            SELECT explode(sequence(
                timestamp('2026-02-25 00:00:00'),
                timestamp('2026-02-25 23:45:00'),
                interval 15 minutes
            )) AS expected_ts
        """)
    )

# Left join actual readings against expected timestamps
complete = expected.join(
    df,
    (expected.meter_id == df.meter_id) &
    (expected.expected_ts == df.timestamp),
    'left'
)

# NULLs in daily_kw now represent genuine gaps
# Apply forward-fill or interpolation as needed
This pattern is essential for time-series analysis where regularity is required — ML models, resampling, and aggregation all assume consistent intervals.

Outliers are values that deviate significantly from the expected range. In smart grid data, a residential meter reporting 50,000 kW (enough to power a small factory) is clearly wrong. Detecting and handling these values is critical for trustworthy analytics.
Domain-Specific Validation Rules
The most reliable outlier detection uses domain knowledge. You know the physical constraints of your system:

# Physical impossibility checks for smart meters
validated = df.withColumn('is_valid',
    (col('daily_kw') >= 0) &                    # No negative consumption
    (col('daily_kw') < 50000) &                 # Physical max for residential
    (col('voltage').between(100, 260)) &         # Valid voltage range
    (col('date') <= current_date()) &            # No future dates
    (col('date') >= lit('2020-01-01'))            # No absurdly old dates
)

# Separate valid from invalid
clean = validated.filter(col('is_valid'))
quarantine = validated.filter(~col('is_valid'))

# Write quarantined records for investigation
quarantine.write.mode('append').parquet('/data/quarantine/meter_readings/')

The quarantine pattern is a best practice: instead of dropping outliers, write them to a separate table for human review. This preserves data lineage and may reveal systematic issues (a faulty sensor, a misconfigured meter, or actual anomalous events).
Statistical Outlier Detection: IQR Method
When domain-specific rules are insufficient, statistical methods identify values that are far from the central distribution.

from pyspark.sql.functions import percentile_approx

# Compute IQR per customer_type
stats = df.groupBy('customer_type').agg(
    percentile_approx('daily_kw', 0.25).alias('q1'),
    percentile_approx('daily_kw', 0.75).alias('q3'),
)
stats = stats.withColumn('iqr', col('q3') - col('q1'))
stats = stats.withColumn('lower_bound', col('q1') - 1.5 * col('iqr'))
stats = stats.withColumn('upper_bound', col('q3') + 1.5 * col('iqr'))

# Join bounds back and filter
df_with_bounds = df.join(broadcast(stats), 'customer_type')

clean = df_with_bounds.filter(
    (col('daily_kw') >= col('lower_bound')) &
    (col('daily_kw') <= col('upper_bound'))
).drop('q1', 'q3', 'iqr', 'lower_bound', 'upper_bound')

The IQR method is robust to the outliers themselves (unlike mean ± standard deviation, which outliers can inflate). Computing IQR per group ensures that Industrial meters (naturally high consumption) are not flagged by Residential thresholds.
Z-Score Method

from pyspark.sql.functions import avg, stddev, abs as spark_abs

# Compute mean and stddev per group
stats = df.groupBy('customer_type').agg(
    avg('daily_kw').alias('mean_kw'),
