    stddev('daily_kw').alias('std_kw'),
)

# Compute Z-score
df_z = df.join(broadcast(stats), 'customer_type') \
    .withColumn('z_score',
        (col('daily_kw') - col('mean_kw')) / col('std_kw')
    )

# Filter outliers (Z > 3 means more than 3 standard deviations from mean)
clean = df_z.filter(spark_abs(col('z_score')) <= 3) \
    .drop('mean_kw', 'std_kw', 'z_score')
Outlier Detection Strategy Comparison

Method Robust to Outliers? Requires Domain Knowledge? Best For Domain rules N/A (rule-based) Yes Known physical constraints, data contracts IQR (1.5x) Yes No Skewed distributions, unknown ranges Z-score (3σ) No (outliers inflate mean/std) No Approximately normal distributions Percentile caps Yes No Capping extreme values rather than removing

Distributed data lakes do not enforce foreign keys like relational databases. A readings table can reference meter_ids that do not exist in the metadata table. It is your responsibility to detect and handle these orphan records.
Anti-Join Validation

# Find readings with no matching metadata (orphan readings)
orphans = readings.join(metadata, 'meter_id', 'left_anti')
orphan_count = orphans.select('meter_id').distinct().count()
print(f'Meters with readings but no metadata: {orphan_count}')

# Find metadata with no readings (silent meters)
silent = metadata.join(readings, 'meter_id', 'left_anti')
silent_count = silent.count()
print(f'Registered meters with no readings: {silent_count}')

# Find readings referencing non-existent regions
bad_regions = readings.join(
    region_lookup, 'region_code', 'left_anti'
)
Building a Data Quality Report
A comprehensive quality report runs all checks and produces a summary:

from pyspark.sql.functions import lit

def quality_report(df, key_cols, metadata_df, key_col):
    total = df.count()
    results = []

    # NULL counts per column
    for c in df.columns:
        nulls = df.filter(col(c).isNull()).count()
        results.append(('null_count', c, nulls, nulls/total*100))

    # Duplicate count
    distinct = df.dropDuplicates(key_cols).count()
    dups = total - distinct
    results.append(('duplicates', str(key_cols), dups, dups/total*100))

    # Orphan count
    orphans = df.join(metadata_df, key_col, 'left_anti') \
        .select(key_col).distinct().count()
    results.append(('orphan_keys', key_col, orphans, None))

    report = spark.createDataFrame(
        results,
        ['check_type', 'column', 'count', 'percentage']
    )
    return report

report = quality_report(readings, ['meter_id', 'date'], metadata, 'meter_id')
report.show(truncate=False)

Run this report at each layer transition: bronze → silver (before cleaning) and silver → gold (after cleaning). The before/after comparison validates that your cleaning pipeline actually improved data quality.

sections.title: Lesson 10: Putting It All Together — A Complete Cleaning Pipeline

blocks.content: Here is a complete bronze-to-silver pipeline that applies all the patterns from this module:

from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col, trim, upper, when, coalesce, lit, current_timestamp,
    to_date, row_number, desc, broadcast, percentile_approx
)
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

# ─── READ BRONZE ─────────────────────────────────────────────
bronze = spark.read.parquet('/data/bronze/meter_readings/')

# ─── STEP 1: TYPE CASTING ───────────────────────────────────
typed = bronze \
    .withColumn('daily_kw', col('daily_kw_str').cast('double')) \
    .withColumn('date', to_date(col('date_str'), 'yyyy-MM-dd')) \
    .withColumn('meter_id', trim(col('meter_id')))

# ─── STEP 2: NULL HANDLING ──────────────────────────────────
# Drop records with NULL keys (cannot be processed)
no_nulls = typed.filter(
    col('meter_id').isNotNull() &
    col('date').isNotNull()
)

# ─── STEP 3: STRING STANDARDIZATION ─────────────────────────
standardized = no_nulls \
    .withColumn('customer_type', upper(trim(col('customer_type')))) \
    .withColumn('customer_type',
        when(col('customer_type').isin('RES', 'RES.', 'R'), 'RESIDENTIAL')
        .when(col('customer_type').isin('COM', 'COM.', 'C'), 'COMMERCIAL')
        .when(col('customer_type').isin('IND', 'IND.', 'I'), 'INDUSTRIAL')
        .otherwise(col('customer_type'))
    )

# ─── STEP 4: DOMAIN VALIDATION ──────────────────────────────
validated = standardized.withColumn('is_valid',
    (col('daily_kw') >= 0) &
    (col('daily_kw') < 50000) &
    (col('date') <= current_date())
)
clean = validated.filter(col('is_valid')).drop('is_valid')
quarantine = validated.filter(~col('is_valid'))

# ─── STEP 5: DEDUPLICATION ──────────────────────────────────
w = Window.partitionBy('meter_id', 'date').orderBy(desc('_ingestion_timestamp'))
ranked = clean.withColumn('rn', row_number().over(w))
deduped = ranked.filter(col('rn') == 1).drop('rn')

# ─── STEP 6: SELECT FINAL SCHEMA ───────────────────────────
silver = deduped.select(
    'meter_id', 'customer_type', 'daily_kw', 'date'
)

# ─── WRITE SILVER ───────────────────────────────────────────
silver.coalesce(50) \
    .write.mode('overwrite') \
    .partitionBy('date') \
    .parquet('/data/silver/meter_readings/')

# ─── WRITE QUARANTINE ──────────────────────────────────────
quarantine.write.mode('append') \
    .parquet('/data/quarantine/meter_readings/')

This pipeline applies every technique from this module in a logical sequence: cast types first (so subsequent filters work correctly), remove NULL keys, standardize strings, validate domain rules, deduplicate, and write clean output.
Pipeline Execution: Distributed Reality
Despite looking like a sequential script, Spark executes this as a single distributed plan. Catalyst sees the entire chain and optimizes it holistically:

items: Predicate pushdown moves the NULL filters into the Parquet scan
Projection pruning reads only the columns that survive to the final select
The deduplication window triggers one shuffle (partition by meter_id + date)
The coalesce before write reduces output files without an additional shuffle

blocks.content: One logical pipeline. One optimized physical plan. Distributed across the cluster.
The Cleaning Order Matters
Always clean in this order:

Step What Why This Order 1 Type casting All subsequent operations depend on correct types 2 NULL handling (key columns) Cannot join, deduplicate, or validate records without keys 3 String standardization Aggregation groupings must be consistent before validation 4 Domain validation / outlier removal Requires correct types and clean strings 5 Deduplication Run last so you deduplicate clean data, not dirty data 6 Schema selection Drop working columns, keep only the final schema
Always Quarantine, Never Silently Drop
Write rejected records to a quarantine table instead of discarding them. Quarantine enables:

items: Root cause analysis of recurring quality issues
Recovery if cleaning rules are too aggressive
Compliance audit trails
Quality trend monitoring over time

blocks.content: Measure Before and After
For every cleaning pipeline, log metrics:

Metric Before Cleaning After Cleaning Total row count 96,000,000 95,750,000 NULL rate (daily_kw) 0.15% 0.00% Duplicate rate 0.26% 0.00% Orphan meter_ids 1,247 0 Outlier rate (daily_kw) 0.03% 0.00% Quarantined rows — 250,000
These metrics are your proof that the pipeline works. Store them as a time-series — degrading quality metrics signal upstream source problems.
Idempotency
A cleaning pipeline should produce the same output whether run once or ten times on the same input. This means:

items: Use overwrite mode for silver writes (not append)
Deduplication handles re-ingested records gracefully
No pipeline state leaks between runs

blocks.content: Idempotent pipelines are safe to re-run after failures, schedule changes, or backfills. Non-idempotent pipelines accumulate errors over time.
You now understand:

items: The medallion architecture (bronze, silver, gold) and why each layer exists
How to detect and handle NULLs with five strategies: drop, fill, forward-fill, impute, and investigate
NULL propagation and why it causes silent bugs in filters and expressions
Exact deduplication with dropDuplicates and deterministic keep-last with ROW_NUMBER
Type casting, cast failure detection, and pre-cleaning for safe conversion
Schema enforcement at read time with PERMISSIVE, DROPMALFORMED, and FAILFAST modes
String standardization: trim, case, regex, category mapping with broadcast joins
Date parsing across multiple formats, timezone normalization, and time-series gap filling
Outlier detection with domain rules, IQR, and Z-score methods
The quarantine pattern for preserving rejected records
Referential integrity validation with anti-joins
Building data quality reports
A complete bronze-to-silver pipeline applying all techniques in order
Best practices: cleaning order, quarantine over drop, measure before/after, idempotency

blocks.content: You are no longer writing code that assumes clean data. You are engineering the systems that make data clean.

In Module 8, we turn to complex data types — arrays, maps, structs, and nested JSON — the messy structures that arrive from APIs, IoT devices, and modern application databases, and how to flatten, explode, and transform them at distributed scale.

chapters.title: Module 8: Complex Data Types
chapters.description: Arrays, Maps, Structs & Nested JSON at Scale Master Spark’s complex data types — arrays, maps, and structs — and learn to read, flatten, transform, and reconstruct nested data structures that arrive from APIs, IoT devices, NoSQL databases, and modern application logs.

blocks.content: In Modules 1 through 7, every DataFrame column held a simple scalar value: a string, a number, a date. Clean rows, clean columns, clean types.
Real-world data is not that polite.

A single IoT message from a smart meter may contain an array of 96 quarter-hourly readings. A customer record from a NoSQL database may embed an entire address as a nested struct. An API response may return a map of dynamic key-value pairs whose keys change from row to row.
These are complex data types — columns that contain collections and nested structures rather than single values. They are the native language of JSON, Avro, Protobuf, and most modern data sources.
If you cannot work with complex types, you cannot work with modern data.
Simple vs Complex Column Values:
SIMPLE (Modules 1-7): COMPLEX (Module 8):
This module teaches you to navigate, flatten, transform, and reconstruct complex types using Spark’s built-in functions — all within the distributed, Catalyst-optimized engine you already understand.
Spark supports three complex data types. Understanding their structure is the foundation for everything that follows.
ArrayType — Ordered Collections
An array is an ordered list of elements, all of the same type. It is the Spark equivalent of a Python list.

from pyspark.sql.types import ArrayType, StringType, DoubleType, StructType, StructField

# Schema definition
schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id', StringType()),
    StructField('hourly_readings', ArrayType(DoubleType())),  # array of doubles
    StructField('alert_codes', ArrayType(StringType())),       # array of strings
])
ArrayType in memory:

meter_id: 'M001'
  hourly_readings: [112.5, 118.3, 95.0, 140.2, 108.7, ...]
                    ^0     ^1     ^2    ^3     ^4
                    Elements accessed by index
alert_codes: ['LOW_VOLTAGE', 'SPIKE_DETECTED']
Where arrays appear: IoT devices sending batched readings, log entries with multiple tags, customer records with multiple phone numbers, any source that packs multiple values into a single field.
MapType — Key-Value Pairs
A map is an unordered collection of key-value pairs. Keys must be of one type; values must be of one type. It is the Spark equivalent of a Python dictionary.

from pyspark.sql.types import MapType

schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id', StringType()),
    StructField('properties', MapType(StringType(), StringType())),  # str -> str
    StructField('daily_by_tariff', MapType(StringType(), DoubleType())), # str -> double
])
MapType in memory:
meter_id: 'M001' properties: {'firmware': '3.2.1', 'manufacturer': 'SiemensAG', 'install_year': '2022'} Keys are strings. Values are strings. Access by key name, not by index.
daily_by_tariff: {'peak': 280.5, 'off_peak': 170.0}
Where maps appear: API responses with dynamic fields, configuration objects, event properties with variable keys, NoSQL document exports where different records have different attributes.
StructType — Named, Typed Fields
A struct is a fixed collection of named, typed fields. It is like a row within a row — a nested record with a known schema.

schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id', StringType()),
    StructField('location', StructType([
        StructField('latitude', DoubleType()),
        StructField('longitude', DoubleType()),
        StructField('address', StringType()),
        StructField('zip_code', StringType()),
    ])),
    StructField('owner', StructType([
        StructField('name', StringType()),
        StructField('contact_email', StringType()),
    ])),
])
StructType in memory:
meter_id: 'M001' location: { latitude: 37.7749, longitude: -122.4194, address: '123 Grid St', zip_code: '94105' } owner: { name: 'Jane Doe', contact_email: 'jane@utility.com' }
Where structs appear: Any nested JSON object, database records with embedded sub-documents, Avro/Protobuf messages with nested message types.
Complex Type Comparison

Type
Structure
Access Pattern
Schema
Analogy
ArrayType
Ordered list of same-type elements
By index: col[0], col[1]
Fixed element type
Python list
MapType
Unordered key-value pairs
By key: col['key_name']
Fixed key + value types
Python dict
StructType
Named, typed fields
By field name: col.field
Fixed field names + types
Python namedtuple
Nesting: Types Within Types
Complex types can be nested arbitrarily. An array can contain structs. A struct can contain arrays of maps. Real-world JSON often nests 3–5 levels deep.
Deeply nested schema example:

root
   |-- meter_id: string
   |-- readings: array                    <-- ArrayType
   |    |-- element: struct               <-- StructType inside array
   |         |-- timestamp: timestamp
   |         |-- kw: double
   |         |-- alerts: array            <-- Array inside struct inside array
   |              |-- element: string
   |-- metadata: map                      <-- MapType
        |-- key: string
        |-- value: string
By the end of this module, you will be able to navigate, flatten, and transform structures of any depth.
Reading JSON
JSON is the most common source of complex types. Spark infers nested schemas automatically:

df = spark.read.json('/data/meters_nested.json')
df.printSchema()
Example output:

root
   |-- meter_id: string
   |-- location: struct
   |    |-- lat: double
   |    |-- lon: double
   |    |-- city: string
   |-- hourly_readings: array
   |    |-- element: double
   |-- properties: map
   |    |-- key: string
   |    |-- value: string
Performance consideration: JSON schema inference requires a full scan of the data. For large files, provide an explicit schema:

from pyspark.sql.types import *

json_schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id', StringType()),
    StructField('location', StructType([
        StructField('lat', DoubleType()),
        StructField('lon', DoubleType()),
        StructField('city', StringType()),
    ])),
    StructField('hourly_readings', ArrayType(DoubleType())),
    StructField('properties', MapType(StringType(), StringType())),
])

df = spark.read.schema(json_schema).json('/data/meters_nested.json')
Reading Nested Parquet and Avro
Parquet and Avro natively support complex types. Schemas are embedded in the files, so no inference is needed:

# Parquet with nested structs and arrays
df = spark.read.parquet('/data/meters_nested.parquet')

# Avro (requires spark-avro package)
df = spark.read.format('avro').load('/data/meters.avro')
Parquet supports nested column pruning: if a struct has 20 fields but you select only 2, Spark reads only those 2 fields from disk. This is a major performance advantage over JSON for nested data.
Parsing JSON Strings Within Columns
Sometimes JSON arrives not as a file format but as a string column inside a CSV or database:

# CSV where 'payload' column contains JSON strings
# meter_id, payload
# M001, '{"kw": 450.5, "alerts": ["LOW_V"]}'

from pyspark.sql.functions import from_json, col
from pyspark.sql.types import *

payload_schema = StructType([
    StructField('kw', DoubleType()),
    StructField('alerts', ArrayType(StringType())),
])

df = spark.read.csv('/data/meters_with_json.csv', header=True)

# Parse the JSON string column into a struct
parsed = df.withColumn('payload_parsed',
    from_json(col('payload'), payload_schema)
)

# Now access nested fields
parsed.select(
    col('meter_id'),
    col('payload_parsed.kw').alias('kw'),
    col('payload_parsed.alerts').alias('alerts'),
).show()
from_json() is the bridge between string columns and structured Spark types. It requires a schema because Spark cannot infer types from a column of strings at plan time.
The Reverse: Converting Structs to JSON Strings

from pyspark.sql.functions import to_json, struct

# Convert struct columns to JSON strings
df.withColumn('location_json',
    to_json(col('location'))
).show(truncate=False)

# Build a JSON string from multiple columns
df.withColumn('metadata_json',
    to_json(struct('meter_id', 'customer_type', 'daily_kw'))
).show(truncate=False)
to_json() is useful when writing data to systems that expect JSON strings (Kafka producers, REST APIs, certain database columns).
Before you can transform complex types, you must know how to reach into them.
Struct Fields: Dot Notation

# Access struct fields with dot notation
df.select(
    col('meter_id'),
    col('location.lat'),
    col('location.lon'),
    col('location.city'),
).show()

# Rename to flat columns
df.select(
    col('meter_id'),
    col('location.lat').alias('latitude'),
    col('location.lon').alias('longitude'),
).show()

# Filter on nested struct fields
df.filter(col('location.city') == 'San Francisco').show()
Dot notation works at any depth: col('location.address.zip_code') accesses a field three levels deep.
Array Elements: Index Access

# Access by index (0-based)
df.select(
    col('meter_id'),
    col('hourly_readings')[0].alias('first_reading'),
    col('hourly_readings')[23].alias('last_reading'),
).show()

# Alternative syntax using getItem()
df.select(
    col('hourly_readings').getItem(0).alias('first'),
).show()
Index access is useful when you know the position of the element you need. For processing all elements, use explode() (covered in Part 4).
Map Values: Key Access

# Access by key name
df.select(
    col('meter_id'),
    col('properties')['firmware'].alias('firmware_version'),
    col('properties')['manufacturer'].alias('manufacturer'),
).show()

# Alternative syntax
df.select(
    col('properties').getItem('firmware').alias('fw'),
).show()

# Filter on map values
df.filter(col('properties')['firmware'] == '3.2.1').show()
If a key does not exist in a particular row’s map, the access returns NULL. This is safe — no error is thrown.
Access Pattern Summary

Type
Access Syntax
Example
Returns
Struct
col('struct.field')
col('location.city')
The field value
Array
col('array')[index]
col('readings')[0]
Element at that index
Map
col('map')['key']
col('props')['firmware']
Value for that key
Nested
Chain accessors
col('loc.coords')[0]
Deep nested value

sections.title: Lesson 5: Explode — Turning One Row into Many

blocks.content: explode() is the most important function for working with arrays and maps. It transforms a single row containing a collection into multiple rows — one per element.
Exploding Arrays

from pyspark.sql.functions import explode, posexplode

# Each meter has an array of 24 hourly readings
# Explode creates one row per reading
exploded = df.select(
    col('meter_id'),
    explode(col('hourly_readings')).alias('kw'),
)
Before explode:
After explode:
3 elements + 2 elements = 5 rows total
Explode with Position: posexplode()
When the element’s position within the array carries meaning (e.g., hour 0, hour 1, ..., hour 23), use posexplode() to get the index:

# posexplode gives both position and value
exploded = df.select(
    col('meter_id'),
    posexplode(col('hourly_readings')).alias('hour', 'kw'),
)
After posexplode:
Smart grid example: A meter sends 96 quarter-hourly readings in a single array. posexplode() gives each reading its time-slot index (0–95), which you can convert to an actual timestamp.
Exploding Maps
Exploding a map produces two columns: one for the key and one for the value.

exploded = df.select(
    col('meter_id'),
    explode(col('properties')).alias('prop_key', 'prop_value'),
)
Before explode (map):
After explode:

meter_id | prop_key     | prop_value
  ---------|--------------|----------
  M001     | firmware     | 3.2.1
  M001     | manufacturer | Siemens
explode_outer() — Preserving NULLs and Empty Collections
Standard explode() drops rows where the array is NULL or empty. If a meter has no readings, that meter disappears entirely from the result. explode_outer() preserves those rows with NULL values:

from pyspark.sql.functions import explode_outer

# Preserves rows where hourly_readings is NULL or empty
exploded = df.select(
    col('meter_id'),
    explode_outer(col('hourly_readings')).alias('kw'),
)

Function NULL/Empty Array Use When explode() Row is DROPPED You only want rows that have data explode_outer() Row is KEPT with NULL value You need to preserve all rows (e.g., for auditing, counts) posexplode() Row is DROPPED You need both index and value posexplode_outer() Row is KEPT with NULL Index + value, preserving empty rows
Performance: The Data Amplification Problem

Explode can massively increase the number of rows. If you have 1 million meters, each with 96 readings, explode produces 96 million rows. If each of those rows also contains an array of 5 alert codes, a second explode produces 480 million rows.
This is called data amplification and it can overwhelm memory and trigger shuffle spills.
Rules for safe explode:

items: Filter before explode — Reduce the number of rows before amplifying them
Select only needed columns before explode — Each amplified row carries all columns
Never explode twice unless necessary — Each explode multiplies row count
Consider array functions instead — Many operations (sum, avg, size) can be done without exploding

sections.title: Lesson 6: Array Functions — Operating Without Exploding

blocks.content: Spark provides a rich library of functions that operate on arrays in place, without creating additional rows. These are almost always more efficient than exploding.
Aggregate Functions on Arrays

from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    size, array_contains, array_distinct, array_sort,
    array_min, array_max, array_position, element_at,
    slice, flatten, array_union, array_intersect, array_except,
    array_join, transform, filter as array_filter, aggregate, exists,
    col
)

# Size (number of elements)
df.withColumn('num_readings', size(col('hourly_readings')))

# Min and max of array
df.withColumn('min_kw', array_min(col('hourly_readings')))
df.withColumn('max_kw', array_max(col('hourly_readings')))

# Check if array contains a value
df.withColumn('has_spike',
    array_contains(col('alert_codes'), 'SPIKE_DETECTED')
)

# Sort elements
df.withColumn('sorted_readings', array_sort(col('hourly_readings')))

# Remove duplicates
df.withColumn('unique_alerts', array_distinct(col('alert_codes')))
Higher-Order Functions: transform, filter, aggregate
Higher-order functions let you apply custom logic to every element of an array without exploding. They are the distributed equivalent of Python’s map(), filter(), and reduce().
transform() — Apply a Function to Every Element

# Convert all readings from kW to MW
df.withColumn('hourly_mw',
    transform(col('hourly_readings'), lambda x: x / 1000)
)

# Round all readings to 1 decimal place
from pyspark.sql.functions import round as spark_round
df.withColumn('rounded',
    transform(col('hourly_readings'), lambda x: spark_round(x, 1))
)
filter() — Keep Only Matching Elements

# Keep only readings above 200 kW
df.withColumn('high_readings',
    array_filter(col('hourly_readings'), lambda x: x > 200)
)

# Keep only non-null readings
df.withColumn('valid_readings',
    array_filter(col('hourly_readings'), lambda x: x.isNotNull())
)
Note: We imported filter as array_filter to avoid clashing with the DataFrame filter() method.
aggregate() — Reduce an Array to a Single Value

# Sum all readings in the array
df.withColumn('total_kw',
    aggregate(
        col('hourly_readings'),
        lit(0.0),                          # initial value
        lambda acc, x: acc + x             # accumulator function
    )
)

# Count readings above threshold
df.withColumn('spike_count',
    aggregate(
        col('hourly_readings'),
        lit(0),
        lambda acc, x: acc + when(x > 500, 1).otherwise(0)
    )
)
exists() — Test if Any Element Matches

# Does any reading exceed 1000 kW?
df.withColumn('has_extreme_reading',
    exists(col('hourly_readings'), lambda x: x > 1000)
)
Array Set Operations

# Union of two arrays (combine alerts from two sources)
df.withColumn('all_alerts',
    array_union(col('alerts_primary'), col('alerts_secondary'))
)

# Intersection (alerts in both sources)
df.withColumn('common_alerts',
    array_intersect(col('alerts_primary'), col('alerts_secondary'))
)

# Difference (alerts in primary but not secondary)
df.withColumn('unique_primary',
    array_except(col('alerts_primary'), col('alerts_secondary'))
)
When to Explode vs Use Array Functions

Need Approach Why Sum, min, max of array Array functions (aggregate, array_min) No row amplification Check if value exists in array array_contains() or exists() Returns boolean, no amplification Transform every element transform() Returns new array, no amplification Filter elements within array Higher-order filter() Returns filtered array, no amplification Join array elements to another table explode() then join Need one row per element for the join Group by array element values explode() then groupBy Need flat rows for aggregation keys Complex per-element logic with other columns explode() then operations Need access to both element and row context
General rule: If you can do it without exploding, do it without exploding. Array functions are cheaper because they avoid row amplification and the potential shuffle that follows.
Creating Maps

from pyspark.sql.functions import create_map, map_from_arrays, col, lit

# Create a map from column pairs
df.withColumn('summary',
    create_map(
        lit('type'), col('customer_type'),
        lit('region'), col('region_code'),
        lit('kw'), col('daily_kw').cast('string'),
    )
)

# Create a map from two arrays (keys array + values array)
df.withColumn('readings_map',
    map_from_arrays(col('hour_labels'), col('hourly_readings'))
)
Querying Maps

from pyspark.sql.functions import map_keys, map_values, map_entries, size

# Get all keys as an array
df.withColumn('all_keys', map_keys(col('properties')))

# Get all values as an array
df.withColumn('all_values', map_values(col('properties')))

# Get number of entries
df.withColumn('num_props', size(col('properties')))

# Check if a key exists
df.filter(col('properties')['firmware'].isNotNull())

# Access with default value
from pyspark.sql.functions import coalesce
df.withColumn('fw',
    coalesce(col('properties')['firmware'], lit('unknown'))
)
Transforming Maps

from pyspark.sql.functions import transform_keys, transform_values, map_filter

# Uppercase all keys
df.withColumn('props_upper',
    transform_keys(col('properties'), lambda k, v: upper(k))
)

# Add prefix to all values
df.withColumn('props_prefixed',
    transform_values(col('properties'), lambda k, v: concat(lit('val_'), v))
)

# Filter map entries (keep only entries where value is not 'N/A')
df.withColumn('clean_props',
    map_filter(col('properties'), lambda k, v: v != 'N/A')
)
Merging and Combining Maps

from pyspark.sql.functions import map_concat

# Merge two map columns (later keys overwrite earlier ones)
df.withColumn('all_props',
    map_concat(col('static_props'), col('dynamic_props'))
)
Pivoting Maps to Columns
When map keys are consistent across rows, you often want to pivot them into separate columns:

# If every row has keys 'firmware', 'manufacturer', 'model':
df.select(
    col('meter_id'),
    col('properties')['firmware'].alias('firmware'),
    col('properties')['manufacturer'].alias('manufacturer'),
    col('properties')['model'].alias('model'),
).show()
When keys vary across rows and you want a dynamic pivot, explode the map first:

# Explode map to key-value rows, then pivot
exploded = df.select('meter_id', explode(col('properties')).alias('key', 'value'))

pivoted = exploded.groupBy('meter_id').pivot('key').agg(first('value'))
Many downstream consumers — dashboards, ML feature stores, JDBC databases — require flat tables. Flattening converts nested structs into top-level columns.
Manual Flattening

# Flatten a one-level struct
flat = df.select(
    col('meter_id'),
    col('location.lat').alias('latitude'),
    col('location.lon').alias('longitude'),
    col('location.city').alias('city'),
    col('owner.name').alias('owner_name'),
    col('owner.contact_email').alias('owner_email'),
)
Automatic Recursive Flattening
For deeply nested schemas with dozens of fields, manual flattening is tedious and error-prone. Here is a reusable function that recursively flattens any schema:
Before flatten: After flatten:

root                            root
   |-- meter_id: string            |-- meter_id: string
   |-- location: struct             |-- location_lat: double
   |    |-- lat: double             |-- location_lon: double
   |    |-- lon: double             |-- location_city: string
   |    |-- city: string            |-- owner_name: string
   |-- owner: struct                |-- owner_email: string
   |    |-- name: string
   |    |-- contact_email: string
Selective Flattening
Sometimes you only need a few fields from a deeply nested struct. There is no need to flatten everything:

# Extract only what you need
df.select(
    col('meter_id'),
    col('location.city'),           # Just the city
    col('owner.name'),              # Just the name
    size(col('hourly_readings')),   # Count of readings
).show()

Spark’s projection pruning works on nested fields. In Parquet, selecting col('location.city') reads only the city column from the nested struct, not lat or lon. This is a significant performance advantage.

sections.title: Lesson 9: Collecting & Reconstructing — From Flat to Nested

blocks.content: Flattening is only half the picture. You often need to go the other direction: collect flat rows back into arrays, build structs from columns, or assemble maps from key-value pairs.
Building Structs from Columns

from pyspark.sql.functions import struct

# Combine columns into a struct
df.withColumn('location',
    struct(
        col('latitude').alias('lat'),
        col('longitude').alias('lon'),
        col('city'),
    )
).show()
Collecting Rows into Arrays: collect_list and collect_set

from pyspark.sql.functions import collect_list, collect_set, sort_array

# Collect all readings for each meter into an array
nested = flat_df.groupBy('meter_id').agg(
    sort_array(collect_list('kw')).alias('all_readings'),
    collect_set('alert_code').alias('unique_alerts'),
    collect_list(struct('timestamp', 'kw')).alias('reading_records'),
)
Before collect_list: After collect_list:
collect_list preserves duplicates and order is not guaranteed (use sort_array to order). collect_set removes duplicates.
Performance warning: collect_list triggers a shuffle (groupBy) and can produce very large arrays if a key has millions of associated rows. Use it intentionally, and monitor partition sizes.
Building Maps from Grouped Data

from pyspark.sql.functions import map_from_entries, collect_list, struct

# Convert key-value rows into a map per meter
# Input: meter_id | prop_key     | prop_value
#        M001     | firmware     | 3.2.1
#        M001     | manufacturer | Siemens

maps = exploded_props.groupBy('meter_id').agg(
    map_from_entries(
        collect_list(struct('prop_key', 'prop_value'))
    ).alias('properties')
)
The Roundtrip: Flatten → Transform → Reconstruct
A common production pattern:

1. READ nested JSON/Avro | 2. EXPLODE arrays to flat rows | 3. CLEAN, FILTER, TRANSFORM (all Module 7 techniques) | 4. COLLECT back into arrays/structs | 5. WRITE as nested Parquet/JSON for downstream consumers

# Example: clean hourly readings and reconstruct

# Step 1-2: Explode
flat = df.select('meter_id', posexplode('hourly_readings').alias('hour', 'kw'))

# Step 3: Clean (remove negatives, cap outliers)
clean = flat.filter(col('kw') >= 0) \
    .withColumn('kw', when(col('kw') > 10000, 10000).otherwise(col('kw')))

# Step 4: Reconstruct
nested_clean = clean.groupBy('meter_id').agg(
    sort_array(collect_list(struct('hour', 'kw'))).alias('hourly_readings'),
)

# Step 5: Write
nested_clean.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('/data/silver/meters_nested/')
Pattern 1: Parsing IoT Device Payloads
IoT devices often send batched readings as JSON arrays. Each message contains a meter ID and an array of timestamped readings.

# Sample IoT JSON message:
# {"meter_id": "M001", "batch": [
#   {"ts": "2026-02-25T00:00:00Z", "kw": 112.5, "v": 238.1},
#   {"ts": "2026-02-25T00:15:00Z", "kw": 118.3, "v": 237.8}
# ]}

# Read and parse
raw = spark.read.json('/data/iot_messages/')

# Explode batch array into individual readings
readings = raw.select(
    col('meter_id'),
    explode(col('batch')).alias('reading'),
).select(
    col('meter_id'),
    col('reading.ts').alias('timestamp'),
    col('reading.kw').alias('kw'),
    col('reading.v').alias('voltage'),
)

# Now apply all Module 7 cleaning patterns on flat data
Pattern 2: Extracting Features from Event Arrays
ML feature engineering often requires deriving scalar features from arrays without exploding.

features = df.select(
    col('meter_id'),

    # Size features
    size(col('hourly_readings')).alias('num_readings'),
    size(col('alert_codes')).alias('num_alerts'),

    # Statistical features from arrays
    array_min(col('hourly_readings')).alias('min_kw'),
    array_max(col('hourly_readings')).alias('max_kw'),
    aggregate(col('hourly_readings'), lit(0.0),
        lambda acc, x: acc + x).alias('total_kw'),

    # Computed features
    (array_max(col('hourly_readings')) -
     array_min(col('hourly_readings'))).alias('kw_range'),

    # Boolean features
    array_contains(col('alert_codes'), 'SPIKE_DETECTED').alias('had_spike'),
    exists(col('hourly_readings'), lambda x: x > 500).alias('exceeded_500'),
)
All these features are computed without explode() — no row amplification, no extra shuffle.
Pattern 3: Denormalizing Relational Data to Nested JSON
APIs and frontend applications often consume nested JSON rather than flat tables. You can construct nested output from relational data:

# Start with flat relational tables
# meters: meter_id, customer_type, lat, lon, city
# readings: meter_id, date, kw

# Collect readings into an array per meter
readings_nested = readings.groupBy('meter_id').agg(
    collect_list(struct('date', 'kw')).alias('readings')
)

# Join with meter info and build nested output
api_output = meters.join(readings_nested, 'meter_id') \
    .select(
        col('meter_id'),
        struct(
            col('customer_type'),
            col('lat'), col('lon'), col('city')
        ).alias('metadata'),
        col('readings'),
    )

# Write as nested JSON for API consumption
api_output.write.mode('overwrite').json('/data/api/meters/')
Pattern 4: Dynamic Schema Discovery
When JSON schemas vary across records (different devices send different fields), you may need to discover and merge schemas:

# Read with mergeSchema for Parquet (discovers all columns across files)
df = spark.read \
    .option('mergeSchema', True) \
    .parquet('/data/multi_schema_meters/')

# For JSON with varying schemas, use schema inference on a sample
sample = spark.read.json('/data/varying_schema/', samplingRatio=0.1)
inferred_schema = sample.schema

# Then read with the merged schema
full = spark.read.schema(inferred_schema).json('/data/varying_schema/')

sections.title: Lesson 11: Performance Considerations for Complex Types

blocks.content: Parquet vs JSON for Nested Data

Feature JSON Parquet Nested column pruning No (reads entire record) Yes (reads only referenced fields) Predicate pushdown on nested fields No Yes (min/max stats on nested columns) Compression Moderate (text-based) Excellent (columnar, per-type encoding) Schema Inferred (expensive scan) Embedded (instant) Read speed for wide nested schemas Slow Fast (read only needed columns) Human readability Yes No (binary)
Rule: Ingest as JSON when sources provide it. Convert to Parquet immediately. Do all analytical work on Parquet.
Avoiding Common Performance Traps

Trap Problem Solution Double explode Row count multiplied twice Explode one array, process, collect, then explode the next Explode then groupBy same key Shuffle after amplification Use array functions instead when possible collect_list on skewed key One group has millions of elements Cap array size or salt the key schema inference on large JSON Full scan just to read schema Define schema explicitly Selecting all nested fields Reads entire struct from Parquet Select only needed nested fields Large arrays in shuffle Each row carries a huge array Explode before shuffle, reconstruct after
Memory Impact of Complex Types
Arrays and maps within each row increase the memory footprint per partition. A row with a 1,000-element array takes ~1,000x more memory than a scalar row. When processing nested data:

items: Increase spark.executor.memory to accommodate large rows
Increase spark.sql.shuffle.partitions to keep per-partition sizes manageable
Monitor spill metrics closely — nested data is the most common trigger for unexpected spills

blocks.content: You now understand:

items: The three complex types: ArrayType (ordered list), MapType (key-value pairs), StructType (named fields)
How to read nested data from JSON, Parquet, and Avro, including from_json() for JSON string columns
How to access nested fields with dot notation (structs), index (arrays), and key (maps)
How explode() turns one row into many, and when to use explode_outer() and posexplode()
The data amplification problem and rules for safe explode
Array functions that avoid exploding: size, array_min, array_max, array_contains, array_sort, array_distinct
Higher-order functions: transform, filter, aggregate, exists — the distributed map/filter/reduce for arrays
Map operations: create_map, map_keys, map_values, map_filter, transform_keys, transform_values, map_concat
How to flatten nested structs into flat tables (manual and recursive)
How to reconstruct nested data from flat rows using collect_list, collect_set, struct, map_from_entries
Real-world patterns: IoT payload parsing, ML feature extraction without explode, denormalization, dynamic schema discovery
Performance: Parquet nested column pruning, avoiding double-explode traps, memory impact of complex types

blocks.content: You are no longer limited to flat tables. You can now navigate, transform, and engineer data of any shape or depth.

In Module 9, we turn to User-Defined Functions (UDFs) — when Spark’s built-in functions are not enough, how to extend the engine with custom Python logic while understanding the performance trade-offs, and how Pandas UDFs bridge the gap between flexibility and speed.

chapters.title: Module 9: User-Defined Functions
chapters.description: Extending the Engine with Custom Logic Understand when and how to extend Spark with custom Python logic through UDFs, master the critical performance differences between row-at-a-time UDFs and vectorized Pandas UDFs, and learn to make the right trade-off between built-in functions, UDFs, and Pandas UDFs in production pipelines.

blocks.content: Spark’s built-in function library is vast. Between Modules 2 through 8, you have used dozens of functions: filters, aggregations, window operations, string manipulations, date functions, array operations, and higher-order transforms.
But eventually you will encounter a transformation that no built-in function can express.

Perhaps you need to apply a proprietary energy tariff formula that varies by state and season. Perhaps you need to call a custom validation library written by your engineering team. Perhaps you need to run a pre-trained scikit-learn model on every row of a 500-million-row table.
When built-in functions are not enough, Spark lets you define your own: User-Defined Functions (UDFs).
But UDFs come with a cost. A profound cost. Understanding that cost — and knowing when to pay it — is what separates competent engineers from production-grade ones.
This module teaches three things:

items: Why UDFs are expensive — the architectural reason, not just a rule of thumb
How to write UDFs correctly — row-at-a-time, Pandas UDFs, and SQL UDFs
When to use each — the decision framework that prevents unnecessary performance penalties

sections.title: Lesson 2: Why UDFs Are Expensive — The Architectural Reality

blocks.content: To understand UDF performance, you must understand how PySpark actually executes code on executors.
The Normal Path: Built-in Functions
When you call a built-in function like col('daily_kw') * 2, here is what happens:

items: Catalyst adds the operation to the logical plan.
Tungsten generates optimized JVM bytecode for the operation.
The operation executes entirely inside the JVM on each executor.
Data stays in Tungsten’s compact binary format (off-heap memory).
No Python process is involved on executors.

blocks.content: This is the fast path. The entire computation happens in optimized JVM code. Python was only involved on the Driver to build the plan.
Built-in Function Execution:

Driver (Python) | | Build plan: col('daily_kw') * 2 v Catalyst Optimizer (JVM) | | Generate optimized bytecode v Executor (JVM only) | | Process data in Tungsten binary format | No Python. No serialization. v Result (stays in JVM memory)
The UDF Path: Python on Every Executor
When you call a Python UDF, the execution path is fundamentally different:

items: Spark cannot optimize the UDF — Catalyst treats it as a black box.
For each row, Spark must serialize the data from JVM binary format into Python-readable format.
The data is sent to a separate Python worker process running alongside each executor.
The Python worker deserializes the data, runs your function, and serializes the result back.
Spark deserializes the result back into JVM format.

blocks.content: Python UDF Execution (per row):

Executor (JVM)
    |
    | 1. Serialize row data to Python format
    | 2. Send to Python worker process via socket
    |
    +-------> Python Worker Process
    |           |
    |           | 3. Deserialize data
    |           | 4. Execute your_function(value)
    |           | 5. Serialize result
    |           |
    | <---------+
    |
    | 6. Deserialize result back to JVM format
    v
  Result (back in JVM memory)
Steps 1-6 happen FOR EVERY ROW.
The cost is not the Python function itself. A simple lambda x: x * 2 executes in nanoseconds. The cost is the serialization and inter-process communication that wraps every single invocation.
The Five Costs of a Python UDF

Cost
What Happens
Impact
1. Catalyst blindness
Optimizer cannot see inside the UDF; no pushdown, no pruning, no reordering
Eliminates most optimizations
2. Serialization (JVM → Python)
Every input value is converted from Tungsten binary to Python objects
CPU overhead per row
3. Inter-process communication
Data crosses process boundary via socket between JVM and Python worker
Latency per batch
4. Python execution
Your function runs in CPython (no JIT, GIL-bound)
Slower than JVM for numeric ops
5. Deserialization (Python → JVM)
Every result is converted back to Tungsten binary format
CPU overhead per row
On a 1-billion-row dataset, a row-at-a-time Python UDF can be 10–100x slower than an equivalent built-in function. This is not a minor overhead. It can turn a 5-minute job into an 8-hour job.

sections.title: Lesson 3: Row-at-a-Time UDFs — The Basic Approach

blocks.content: Despite their cost, row-at-a-time UDFs are sometimes necessary. Here is how to write them correctly.
Defining and Registering a UDF
df.withColumn('usage_class', classify_udf(col('daily_kw'))).show()
Registering UDFs for SQL

# Register for use in spark.sql() queries
spark.udf.register('classify_usage', classify_usage, StringType())

# Now usable in SQL
spark.sql("""
    SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, classify_usage(daily_kw) AS usage_class
    FROM readings
""").show()
Return Type Rules
Every UDF must declare its return type. Spark cannot infer it because the function is a black box.

Python Return
Spark ReturnType
Notes
int
IntegerType() or LongType()
Use LongType for large numbers
float
DoubleType() or FloatType()
DoubleType is safer default
str
StringType()
Most common
bool
BooleanType()
True/False
None
Any nullable type
Always handle None in your function
list
ArrayType(element_type)
Element type must match
dict
MapType(key_type, value_type)
Types must match
tuple
StructType([fields...])
Each position maps to a field
Critical: Always Handle NULLs
Rule: Every UDF must begin with a None check on every input parameter. There are no exceptions.
Returning Complex Types

sections.title: Lesson 4: Pandas UDFs — The Vectorized Solution

blocks.content: Pandas UDFs (also called vectorized UDFs) solve the primary performance problem of row-at-a-time UDFs. Instead of processing one row at a time with serialization for each, Pandas UDFs process data in batches using Apache Arrow as a zero-copy transfer format.
The Architecture: Why Pandas UDFs Are Faster
Row-at-a-Time UDF: Pandas UDF (Vectorized):

JVM                                JVM
   |                                  |
   | serialize row 1                  | serialize BATCH of 10,000 rows
   | --> Python: f(row1)              | via Apache Arrow (zero-copy)
   | <-- result 1                     | --> Python: f(pandas_series)
   |                                  | <-- result_series
   | serialize row 2                  |
   | --> Python: f(row2)              | serialize next batch...
   | <-- result 2                     |
   |                                  |
   | ... 1 billion times              | ... 100,000 batches
   |                                  |
   | 1B serialization round-trips     | 100K batch transfers
   | Python loop overhead             | NumPy vectorized operations
The improvements are threefold:

items: Batch processing — Instead of 1 billion round-trips, a few hundred thousand batches are transferred. Each batch contains thousands of rows.
Apache Arrow — Arrow provides a columnar memory format that both JVM and Python can read without conversion. This eliminates most serialization cost.
Vectorized operations — Inside your function, you operate on pandas Series or DataFrames, which use NumPy’s C-optimized operations instead of Python loops.

blocks.content: The result: Pandas UDFs are typically 3–10x faster than row-at-a-time UDFs, and sometimes up to 100x faster for numeric operations where NumPy shines.
Series to Series: The Most Common Type
A Series-to-Series Pandas UDF takes one or more pandas Series as input and returns a pandas Series of the same length. It is the vectorized equivalent of a scalar UDF.

import pandas as pd
from pyspark.sql.functions import pandas_udf
from pyspark.sql.types import DoubleType

# Define a vectorized UDF
@pandas_udf(DoubleType())
def tariff_cost_v(kw: pd.Series, type_code: pd.Series, month: pd.Series) -> pd.Series:
    """Vectorized tariff calculation."""
    # Map customer types to rates using pandas vectorized operations
    rate_map = {'RESIDENTIAL': 0.12, 'COMMERCIAL': 0.10, 'INDUSTRIAL': 0.08}
    rate = type_code.map(rate_map).fillna(0.15)

    # Winter surcharge (vectorized boolean indexing)
    winter_mask = month.isin([12, 1, 2])
    rate = rate.where(~winter_mask, rate * 1.25)

    return kw * rate

# Apply exactly like a regular UDF
df.withColumn('cost',
    tariff_cost_v(col('daily_kw'), col('customer_type'), col('month'))
).show()

Key insight: Inside the function, you write pandas code, not row-level Python. Use vectorized operations (Series arithmetic, .map(), .where(), boolean indexing) instead of for-loops. A for-loop inside a Pandas UDF defeats the purpose.
Iterator of Series to Iterator of Series
When your UDF needs expensive one-time initialization (loading a model, establishing a connection, compiling a regex), use the Iterator variant. It lets you initialize once and reuse across batches:

from typing import Iterator

@pandas_udf(DoubleType())
def score_with_model(iterator: Iterator[pd.Series]) -> Iterator[pd.Series]:
    # INITIALIZE ONCE (not per batch)
    import pickle
    with open('/models/consumption_model.pkl', 'rb') as f:
        model = pickle.load(f)

    # Process each batch
    for batch in iterator:
        predictions = model.predict(batch.values.reshape(-1, 1))
        yield pd.Series(predictions)
df.withColumn('predicted_kw', score_with_model(col('daily_kw')))

The model is loaded once per executor partition, not once per row or once per batch. For expensive initialization (ML models, database connections, configuration loading), this is a major improvement.
Multiple Input Columns with Iterator
df.withColumn('risk_score', multi_feature_score(col('daily_kw'), col('voltage'), col('temperature')) )

sections.title: Lesson 5: Grouped Map UDFs — Custom Logic Per Group

blocks.content: Sometimes you need to apply custom logic to entire groups of rows, not individual rows. For example: fitting a separate regression model per meter, or applying a custom normalization per customer type.
Grouped Map UDFs (also called applyInPandas) split the DataFrame by a key, convert each group to a pandas DataFrame, apply your function, and return a pandas DataFrame.
The Architecture
Grouped Map UDF Execution:

Spark DataFrame
    |
    | groupBy('meter_id')
    v
  +-------+  +-------+  +-------+
  | M001  |  | M002  |  | M003  |
  | group |  | group |  | group |
  +---+---+  +---+---+  +---+---+
      |          |          |
      v          v          v
  [pandas DF] [pandas DF] [pandas DF]
      |          |          |
  your_func() your_func() your_func()
      |          |          |
      v          v          v
  [result DF] [result DF] [result DF]
      |          |          |
      +-----+----+-----+---+
            |
            v
     Combined Spark DataFrame
applyInPandas Example: Per-Meter Z-Score Normalization

from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType

# Define the output schema (MUST match what your function returns)
output_schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id', StringType()),
    StructField('date', DateType()),
    StructField('daily_kw', DoubleType()),
    StructField('kw_zscore', DoubleType()),
])

def normalize_per_meter(pdf: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Apply Z-score normalization within each meter's readings."""
    mean_kw = pdf['daily_kw'].mean()
    std_kw = pdf['daily_kw'].std()
    if std_kw is None or std_kw == 0:
        pdf['kw_zscore'] = 0.0
    else:
        pdf['kw_zscore'] = (pdf['daily_kw'] - mean_kw) / std_kw
    return pdf[['meter_id', 'date', 'daily_kw', 'kw_zscore']]

# Apply to each group
normalized = df.groupBy('meter_id').applyInPandas(
    normalize_per_meter, schema=output_schema
)
Each group is converted to a pandas DataFrame, processed independently, and the results are stitched back into a Spark DataFrame.
applyInPandas: Per-Meter Trend Detection

import numpy as np

trend_schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id', StringType()),
    StructField('slope', DoubleType()),
    StructField('intercept', DoubleType()),
    StructField('trend_direction', StringType()),
])

def fit_trend(pdf: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Fit a linear trend to each meter's consumption."""
    pdf = pdf.sort_values('date')
    x = np.arange(len(pdf))
    y = pdf['daily_kw'].values

# numpy polyfit for linear regression
    mask = ~np.isnan(y)
    slope, intercept = np.polyfit(x[mask], y[mask], 1)

    direction = 'INCREASING' if slope > 0.5 else 'DECREASING' if slope < -0.5 else 'STABLE'

    return pd.DataFrame({
        'meter_id': [pdf['meter_id'].iloc[0]],
        'slope': [float(slope)],
        'intercept': [float(intercept)],
        'trend_direction': [direction],
    })

trends = df.groupBy('meter_id').applyInPandas(fit_trend, schema=trend_schema)
This fits a separate linear regression for each meter — millions of independent models, executed in parallel across the cluster, each using NumPy’s optimized algebra.
Grouped Map Performance Considerations

Factor Impact Mitigation Shuffle groupBy triggers full shuffle Pre-partition by key if possible Group size imbalance Large groups become stragglers Salt large keys or cap group size Memory per group Entire group must fit in one executor as a pandas DF Limit group size or increase executor memory Schema enforcement Output schema must be declared explicitly Validate output matches schema during testing Python overhead Still crosses JVM/Python boundary Use vectorized pandas ops inside the function

sections.title: Lesson 6: Grouped Aggregate UDFs — Custom Aggregations

blocks.content: Sometimes you need a custom aggregation — a function that takes a group of values and returns a single scalar result, like a custom median, weighted average, or domain-specific statistic.
Series to Scalar: Custom Aggregate Functions
Custom Percentiles and Robust Statistics
df.groupBy('customer_type').agg( trimmed_mean(col('daily_kw')).alias('robust_avg_kw') ).show()
Using Custom Aggregates in Window Functions
Grouped aggregate Pandas UDFs can also be used within window specifications:
df.withColumn('iqr_7d', rolling_iqr(col('daily_kw')).over(w))

When you need to process entire partitions without groupBy, mapInPandas applies a function to each partition as a pandas DataFrame. Unlike applyInPandas, it does not require a groupBy and does not trigger a shuffle.
Basic mapInPandas

def process_partition(iterator: Iterator[pd.DataFrame]) -> Iterator[pd.DataFrame]:
    """Process each partition. Useful for batch operations."""
    # Optional: one-time initialization
    import re
    pattern = re.compile(r'\d+')

    for pdf in iterator:
        # Apply operations on the pandas DataFrame
        pdf['meter_num'] = pdf['meter_id'].apply(
            lambda x: int(pattern.search(x).group()) if pattern.search(x) else 0
        )
        pdf['daily_mw'] = pdf['daily_kw'] / 1000.0
        yield pdf

result = df.mapInPandas(process_partition, schema=output_schema)
Key advantage: No shuffle. mapInPandas processes data where it already lives. Each partition is converted to a pandas DataFrame, processed, and converted back.
mapInPandas for External Service Calls
When you need to call an external API or service for enrichment, mapInPandas lets you batch requests efficiently:

def enrich_with_geocoding(iterator: Iterator[pd.DataFrame]) -> Iterator[pd.DataFrame]:
    """Call geocoding API in batches per partition."""
    import requests
    session = requests.Session()  # Reuse connection

    for pdf in iterator:
        # Batch API call instead of per-row
        addresses = pdf['address'].tolist()
        # ... batch geocoding logic ...
        pdf['lat'] = latitudes
        pdf['lon'] = longitudes
        yield pdf

enriched = df.mapInPandas(enrich_with_geocoding, schema=enriched_schema)
mapInPandas vs applyInPandas

Feature mapInPandas applyInPandas Input unit Partition Group (from groupBy) Requires groupBy? No Yes Triggers shuffle? No Yes (from the groupBy) Use case Per-partition transformations Per-group aggregations or models Data guarantee No ordering or grouping All rows for a key are together Typical use Enrichment, batch API calls, format conversion Per-entity ML, custom normalization

sections.title: Lesson 8: SQL UDFs — Lightweight Reusable Logic

blocks.content: For simple transformations that do not require Python libraries, Spark SQL offers built-in SQL UDFs. These execute entirely within the JVM, avoiding the Python overhead entirely.

CREATE FUNCTION Syntax

# Define a SQL UDF (runs in JVM, no Python overhead)
spark.sql("""
    CREATE OR REPLACE TEMPORARY FUNCTION classify_usage AS
    'SELECT CASE
        WHEN input < 200 THEN "LOW"
        WHEN input < 1000 THEN "MEDIUM"
        WHEN input < 5000 THEN "HIGH"
        ELSE "EXTREME"
    END'
""")

In practice, the most common approach is to use SQL expressions as inline computed columns rather than formal SQL UDFs. The power of SQL UDFs is in reusable, named functions that analysts can use without understanding the underlying expression.
Registering Python Functions for SQL
The more practical approach for most teams is registering Python UDFs or Pandas UDFs for SQL access:

# Register a Pandas UDF for SQL use
spark.udf.register('tariff_cost_v', tariff_cost_v)

# Now available in SQL queries
spark.sql("""
    SELECT
        meter_id,
        daily_kw,
        tariff_cost_v(daily_kw, customer_type, month) AS cost
    FROM readings
""").show()
Registering Pandas UDFs for SQL gives you the best of both worlds: SQL accessibility for analysts with vectorized performance for engineers.

sections.title: Lesson 9: The Decision Framework — What to Use When

blocks.content: This is the most important section of the module. Choosing between built-in functions, row-at-a-time UDFs, and Pandas UDFs is a decision you will make on every project.
The Decision Hierarchy
Decision Tree: How to Implement Custom Logic

Can you express it with built-in functions?
  |
  +-- YES --> Use built-in functions. STOP.
  |            (fastest, Catalyst-optimized, no Python overhead)
  |
  +-- NO  --> Can you express it with higher-order array functions?
              |
              +-- YES --> Use transform/filter/aggregate. STOP.
              |
              +-- NO  --> Is it a scalar (row-level) transformation?
                          |
                          +-- YES --> Use Pandas UDF (Series to Series).
                          |
                          +-- NO  --> Is it a group-level operation?
                                      |
                                      +-- YES, aggregation --> Pandas Aggregate UDF
                                      +-- YES, transformation --> applyInPandas
                                      +-- NO, partition-level --> mapInPandas
ONLY as a last resort: row-at-a-time UDF (when external libraries cannot accept pandas Series)
Performance Tier Comparison

Approach Relative Speed Catalyst Optimized? Python on Executors? When to Use Built-in functions 1x (baseline) Yes No Always prefer when possible Higher-order functions 1x Yes No Array transforms, filters, aggregates Pandas UDF (Series) 3-10x slower than built-in Partially (input/output optimized) Yes (vectorized) Custom scalar logic requiring Python Pandas UDF (Iterator) 3-10x slower Partially Yes (vectorized, init once) Scalar logic with expensive init (models) Pandas UDF (Grouped Agg) 5-20x slower Partially Yes Custom aggregations applyInPandas 10-50x slower No Yes (group-level) Per-group models, custom group transforms mapInPandas 5-20x slower No Yes (partition-level) Batch API calls, partition transforms Row-at-a-time UDF 10-100x slower No Yes (per row) Last resort only
Common Scenarios: What Would You Use?

Scenario Best Approach Why Multiply column by constant Built-in: col('kw') * 2 No UDF needed Conditional classification Built-in: when().otherwise() CASE logic is built-in Custom regex extraction Built-in: regexp_extract() Regex is built-in Custom string parsing not expressible in SQL Pandas UDF (Series to Series) Vectorized, batch processing Apply pre-trained sklearn model per row Pandas UDF (Iterator) Load model once, score in batches Fit a model per customer group applyInPandas Need all group data together Custom weighted percentile Pandas Aggregate UDF Custom aggregation logic Call external API for enrichment mapInPandas Batch API calls per partition Library that only accepts Python scalars Row-at-a-time UDF Cannot vectorize
Mistake 1: Using a UDF When Built-in Functions Exist
df.withColumn('upper_type', to_upper(col('customer_type')))

# GOOD: Use built-in function
from pyspark.sql.functions import upper
df.withColumn('upper_type', upper(col('customer_type')))
The built-in version is 10–100x faster. Every UDF usage should start with the question: "Can I do this without a UDF?"
Mistake 2: Python Loops Inside Pandas UDFs
A Python for-loop inside a Pandas UDF gives you the serialization overhead of a Pandas UDF with the per-element speed of a regular UDF. It is the worst of both worlds.
Mistake 3: Not Handling NULLs
Mistake 4: Broadcasting Large Objects Inside UDFs
Broadcast variables are serialized once by the Driver and sent to each executor. They are not resent for every batch or partition. For large lookup tables, this is the correct pattern.
Mistake 5: UDFs in Filter Conditions

# BAD: UDF in filter prevents predicate pushdown
df.filter(my_udf(col('kw')) > 100)  # Catalyst cannot push this down

# GOOD: Pre-compute, then filter with built-in comparison
df.withColumn('udf_result', my_udf(col('kw'))) \
  .filter(col('udf_result') > 100)

# BETTER: If possible, express the filter condition with built-ins
df.filter(col('kw') * 1.25 > 100)  # Pushable to data source

When a UDF appears in a filter, Catalyst cannot push the predicate down to the data source. Spark reads all data and then filters. For Parquet with hundreds of gigabytes, this eliminates a major optimization.

sections.title: Lesson 11: Testing UDFs and Production Patterns

blocks.content: Testing UDFs in Isolation
UDFs are Python functions. Test them as Python functions before using them in Spark:

# test_udfs.py
import pytest
import pandas as pd

# Test the underlying Python function
def test_classify_usage_low():
    assert classify_usage(100) == 'LOW'

def test_classify_usage_none():
    assert classify_usage(None) is None

def test_classify_usage_boundary():
    assert classify_usage(200) == 'MEDIUM'  # boundary case
    assert classify_usage(199) == 'LOW'

# Test vectorized version with pandas
def test_tariff_cost_vectorized():
    kw = pd.Series([100.0, 500.0, None])
    types = pd.Series(['RESIDENTIAL', 'COMMERCIAL', None])
    months = pd.Series([1, 7, 3])
    result = tariff_cost_v.func(kw, types, months)
    assert result[0] == pytest.approx(100 * 0.12 * 1.25)  # winter
    assert result[1] == pytest.approx(500 * 0.10)           # summer
    assert pd.isna(result[2])                                # null input
Test edge cases: NULLs, empty strings, boundary values, extreme numbers, and type mismatches. These are the errors that will crash executors at 3 AM on your production cluster.
Integration Testing with Spark

def test_udf_with_spark(spark):
    """Test UDF behavior within Spark execution."""
    data = [(1, 100.0), (2, None), (3, 5500.0)]
    df = spark.createDataFrame(data, ['id', 'kw'])

    result = df.withColumn('class', classify_udf(col('kw')))
    rows = result.collect()

assert rows[0]['class'] == 'LOW'
    assert rows[1]['class'] is None   # NULL handling
    assert rows[2]['class'] == 'EXTREME'
Production Pattern: Encapsulate UDFs in Modules
Encapsulating UDFs in importable modules enables code reuse, versioning, testing, and documentation — the same engineering practices you apply to any production code.
Dependency Management on Clusters
When your UDFs depend on external libraries (scipy, sklearn, custom packages), those libraries must be available on every executor. Approaches:

Approach How Best For Pre-installed on cluster image Include in Docker/AMI/init scripts Stable, shared libraries (numpy, scipy) spark.submit.pyFiles Ship .py files or .zip with spark-submit Custom modules without C extensions --archives flag Ship conda/venv environment as archive Complex environments with C extensions Databricks cluster libraries Install via UI or cluster policy Databricks environments pip install in init script Run pip on cluster startup Simple deps; slower startup
You now understand:

items: Why UDFs are expensive — the JVM-to-Python serialization round-trip, Catalyst blindness, and inter-process communication cost
How to write row-at-a-time UDFs with correct return types, NULL handling, and complex type returns (structs, arrays)
How to register UDFs for both DataFrame and SQL usage
Why Pandas UDFs are 3–100x faster than row-at-a-time UDFs, through Apache Arrow batching and vectorized NumPy operations
Series-to-Series Pandas UDFs for scalar transformations
Iterator Pandas UDFs for expensive one-time initialization (model loading)
applyInPandas for per-group custom logic (per-entity models, normalization, trend fitting)
Grouped Aggregate Pandas UDFs for custom aggregations (weighted averages, trimmed means, rolling IQR)
mapInPandas for shuffle-free partition-level processing (batch API calls, format conversion)
The decision framework: built-in → higher-order → Pandas UDF → applyInPandas → row-at-a-time (last resort)
Common anti-patterns: UDF for built-in logic, Python loops in Pandas UDFs, missing NULL handling, UDFs in filter conditions, recreating large objects per batch
Testing strategies: unit tests on raw Python functions, integration tests with Spark, edge case coverage
Production patterns: encapsulated modules, broadcast variables for lookups, dependency management on clusters

blocks.content: You are no longer limited by Spark’s built-in function library. But you know the cost of extending it — and you choose wisely.
In Module 10, we turn to Spark Structured Streaming — processing data not as static tables but as continuous, unbounded streams arriving in real time.

chapters.title: Module 10: Spark Structured Streaming
chapters.description: Processing Unbounded Data in Real Time Understand the mental model shift from batch to streaming, master Spark Structured Streaming’s architecture, triggers, output modes, windowed aggregations, watermarks, and stateful processing, and build production-grade streaming pipelines for near-real-time analytics.

blocks.content: Every module so far has treated data as a finite, bounded dataset. You read a file. You transform it. You write the result. The job ends.
Real systems do not work that way.

A smart meter sends a reading every 15 minutes — forever. A credit card network processes transactions 24 hours a day. A fleet of connected vehicles streams telemetry continuously. There is no "end of file." The data is unbounded.

Batch processing waits for data to accumulate, processes it, and writes results. Hours or minutes of latency are typical. Stream processing reacts to data as it arrives, processing it in near-real-time with latency measured in seconds or sub-seconds.

Spark Structured Streaming bridges these two worlds. It applies the same DataFrame and SQL APIs you already know to continuously arriving data. The mental model is a table that grows one row at a time — infinitely.
Batch vs Streaming Mental Model:
BATCH: STREAMING:

+--------+                      +-----------...
  | File   |  read -> process     | Unbounded |  continuous
  | (done) |  -> write -> STOP    | table     |  incremental
  +--------+                      +-----------...
                                  New rows arrive every second.
                                  Query runs continuously.
                                  Results update continuously.

The extraordinary insight of Structured Streaming is that you do not need to learn a new API. You write the same DataFrame transformations. Spark handles the incremental execution, state management, and fault tolerance.

sections.title: Lesson 2: The Programming Model — An Infinite Table

blocks.content: Structured Streaming’s core abstraction is beautifully simple: treat a live data stream as an unbounded table. New data arriving on the stream is like new rows being appended to the table.
Input, Query, Result, Output
Structured Streaming Model:

DATA SOURCE (Kafka, files, socket, ...)
       |
       | new rows arrive continuously
       v
  +---------------------+
  |   INPUT TABLE       |  <-- unbounded, grows forever
  |   (all data ever    |
  |    received)        |
  +---------------------+
       |
       | your query (filter, groupBy, join, ...)
       v
  +---------------------+
  |   RESULT TABLE      |  <-- updated incrementally
  |   (query result     |
  |    at any point     |
  |    in time)         |
  +---------------------+
       |
       | output mode (append, update, complete)
       v
  +---------------------+
  |   OUTPUT SINK       |  <-- Kafka, files, console, database
  +---------------------+
You define a query on the Input Table using the same DataFrame/SQL API. Spark incrementally processes only the new data, updates the Result Table, and writes changes to the Output Sink.
You do not write loops, manage offsets, or handle recovery. Spark does all of that.
Micro-Batch vs Continuous Processing
Under the hood, Structured Streaming processes data in one of two modes:

Mode How It Works Latency Maturity Micro-Batch (default) Accumulates data into small batches, processes each as a mini Spark job 100ms – seconds Production-ready, battle-tested Continuous (experimental) Processes each record as soon as it arrives, no batching ~1ms Experimental in Spark 3.x, limited operations

In practice, almost all production Structured Streaming deployments use micro-batch mode. The latency (100ms–seconds) is sufficient for the vast majority of use cases, and it inherits all of Spark’s batch optimizations: Catalyst, Tungsten, and partition-level parallelism.
This module focuses entirely on micro-batch mode.
Reading a Stream
Reading a stream looks almost identical to reading a batch DataFrame. The key difference: spark.readStream instead of spark.read.

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, from_json, current_timestamp
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, TimestampType

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('SmartGrid_Streaming') \
    .getOrCreate()

# Define schema (required for streaming — no schema inference)
meter_schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id', StringType()),
    StructField('timestamp', TimestampType()),
    StructField('kw', DoubleType()),
    StructField('voltage', DoubleType()),
])

# Read from a streaming source
stream_df = spark.readStream \
    .schema(meter_schema) \
    .json('/data/streaming/meter_readings/')
Critical difference from batch: You must provide a schema. Streaming sources cannot be scanned for schema inference because the data has not all arrived yet.
Transforming a Stream
Apply the same transformations you already know:

# Filter, add columns, select — identical to batch
clean_stream = stream_df \
    .filter(col('kw') >= 0) \
    .filter(col('kw') < 50000) \
    .withColumn('kw_mw', col('kw') / 1000) \
    .withColumn('processing_time', current_timestamp())
These transformations are lazy, exactly like batch. Nothing executes until you start the streaming query.
Writing a Stream

# Write to console (for development/debugging)
query = clean_stream.writeStream \
    .outputMode('append') \
    .format('console') \
    .option('truncate', False) \
    .start()

# Block until the stream is terminated
query.awaitTermination()
writeStream replaces write. Instead of writing once and stopping, the stream continuously processes new data and writes results.
The Streaming Lifecycle
Streaming Query Lifecycle:

readStream        Define source + schema
       |
  transformations    filter, select, withColumn, ...
       |
  writeStream        Define sink + output mode + trigger
       |
  .start()           Begin execution
       |
  [Micro-batch 0]    Process initial data
  [Micro-batch 1]    Process new arrivals
  [Micro-batch 2]    Process new arrivals
  [    ...     ]     Continues indefinitely
       |
  .stop()            Graceful shutdown
  or .awaitTermination()  Block until error/shutdown
A streaming query reads from a source and writes to a sink. Spark supports several built-in options for each.
Streaming Sources

Source Format String Description Production Use Kafka kafka Distributed message queue; most common production source Primary production source File Source json, csv, parquet, orc Monitors a directory for new files Common for file-based ingestion Delta Lake delta Change data feed from Delta tables Databricks pipelines Socket socket TCP socket text stream Development/testing ONLY Rate Source rate Generates rows at a fixed rate Testing and benchmarking
Kafka Source (Production Standard)

# Read from Kafka
kafka_stream = spark.readStream \
    .format('kafka') \
    .option('kafka.bootstrap.servers', 'broker1:9092,broker2:9092') \
    .option('subscribe', 'smart_meter_readings') \
    .option('startingOffsets', 'latest') \
    .load()

# Kafka produces key, value, topic, partition, offset, timestamp
# Value is a binary column containing the message payload
kafka_stream.printSchema()
# root
#  |-- key: binary
#  |-- value: binary     <-- your data is here
#  |-- topic: string
#  |-- partition: integer
#  |-- offset: long
#  |-- timestamp: timestamp
Kafka messages arrive as binary. You must deserialize the value column:

from pyspark.sql.functions import from_json, col

# Define the schema of the JSON payload inside the Kafka value
payload_schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id', StringType()),
    StructField('timestamp', TimestampType()),
    StructField('kw', DoubleType()),
    StructField('voltage', DoubleType()),
])

# Deserialize: binary -> string -> struct
parsed = kafka_stream \
    .selectExpr('CAST(value AS STRING) AS json_str') \
    .select(from_json(col('json_str'), payload_schema).alias('data')) \
    .select('data.*')

# Now you have a clean DataFrame with typed columns
# meter_id | timestamp | kw | voltage
File Source

# Monitor a directory for new JSON files
file_stream = spark.readStream \
    .schema(meter_schema) \
    .option('maxFilesPerTrigger', 10)  # Process 10 files per micro-batch \
    .json('/data/incoming/meter_readings/')

The file source watches the directory. When new files appear, they are processed in the next micro-batch. Processed files are tracked via a metadata log — they are never re-processed. maxFilesPerTrigger controls throughput and prevents overwhelming the cluster when a large backlog of files exists.
Streaming Sinks

Sink Format String Description Production Use Kafka kafka Write back to Kafka topics Event-driven architectures File Sink parquet, json, csv, delta Write files to storage Data lake ingestion Delta Lake delta Write to Delta tables with ACID Primary production sink Console console Print to stdout Development/debugging ONLY Memory memory Store in-memory table Testing ONLY foreach / foreachBatch foreach, foreachBatch Custom sink logic (database, API, etc.) Custom integrations
Writing to Parquet (File Sink)

query = clean_stream.writeStream \
    .outputMode('append') \
    .format('parquet') \
    .option('checkpointLocation', '/checkpoints/meter_stream/') \
    .option('path', '/data/silver/meter_readings_stream/') \
    .start()
Writing to Kafka

from pyspark.sql.functions import to_json, struct

# Kafka requires 'value' column (and optionally 'key')
output = clean_stream.select(
    col('meter_id').alias('key'),
    to_json(struct('meter_id', 'timestamp', 'kw', 'voltage')).alias('value')
)

query = output.writeStream \
    .format('kafka') \
    .option('kafka.bootstrap.servers', 'broker1:9092') \
    .option('topic', 'cleaned_readings') \
    .option('checkpointLocation', '/checkpoints/kafka_output/') \
    .start()
foreachBatch: Custom Sink Logic
foreachBatch gives you a regular batch DataFrame for each micro-batch, letting you apply arbitrary logic:

def write_to_multiple_sinks(batch_df, batch_id):
    """Write each micro-batch to both a data lake and a database."""
    # Write to Delta Lake
    batch_df.write \
        .mode('append') \
        .format('delta') \
        .save('/data/silver/meter_readings/')

    # Write aggregated summary to PostgreSQL
    summary = batch_df.groupBy('customer_type').agg({'kw': 'avg'})
    summary.write \
        .format('jdbc') \
        .option('url', 'jdbc:postgresql://host:5432/db') \
        .option('dbtable', 'streaming_summary') \
        .mode('append') \
        .save()

query = clean_stream.writeStream \
    .foreachBatch(write_to_multiple_sinks) \
    .option('checkpointLocation', '/checkpoints/multi_sink/') \
    .start()
foreachBatch is the most flexible sink. Inside the function, you have a full Spark batch DataFrame — you can use any batch operation including joins, broadcasts, writes to any format, and UDFs.

sections.title: Lesson 5: Output Modes — What Gets Written

blocks.content: The output mode determines which rows from the Result Table are written to the sink after each micro-batch.
The Three Output Modes
Output Modes Visualized:
Micro-batch 3 arrives. Result Table is now:

customer_type | count | avg_kw
    --------------|-------|-------
    RESIDENTIAL   | 5000  | 420.3    <-- existed, count updated
    COMMERCIAL    | 1200  | 890.1    <-- existed, unchanged
    INDUSTRIAL    |  300  | 3200.5   <-- NEW row

  APPEND mode:     writes [INDUSTRIAL, 300, 3200.5]    (new rows only)
  UPDATE mode:     writes [RESIDENTIAL] + [INDUSTRIAL] (changed + new)
  COMPLETE mode:   writes ALL 3 rows                   (full table)

Mode Writes Supported Queries Typical Sink Append Only new rows that will never change Filters, maps, stateless transforms; windowed aggregations AFTER watermark File sinks, Kafka, data lakes Update Only rows that changed in this micro-batch All aggregations; most stateful operations Databases, key-value stores, Kafka Complete The entire Result Table every time Aggregations only (must be bounded result) Dashboards, small summary tables
Choosing the Right Output Mode

Query Type Append Update Complete Stateless (filter, select, map) Yes (default) Yes No groupBy aggregation (no watermark) No Yes Yes groupBy aggregation (with watermark) Yes (after watermark expires) Yes Yes flatMap, explode Yes Yes No Stream-stream join Yes No No

Append is the most common mode for production pipelines writing to data lakes. Update is used for real-time dashboards. Complete is used only for small, bounded result tables (e.g., a count per customer type).
A trigger determines how often Spark processes a new micro-batch.
Trigger Types

from pyspark.sql.streaming import Trigger

# Default: process next micro-batch as soon as the previous one finishes
query = stream.writeStream \
    .trigger(processingTime='0 seconds') \
    .format('parquet') \
    .start()

# Fixed interval: process every 30 seconds
query = stream.writeStream \
    .trigger(processingTime='30 seconds') \
    .format('parquet') \
    .start()

# Once: process all available data, then stop
query = stream.writeStream \
    .trigger(once=True) \
    .format('parquet') \
    .start()

# Available-now: process all available data in multiple batches, then stop
query = stream.writeStream \
    .trigger(availableNow=True) \
    .format('parquet') \
    .start()
