Trigger Behavior Use Case processingTime='0 seconds' Process immediately after previous batch completes Lowest latency continuous processing processingTime='30 seconds' Wait 30 seconds between batches Rate-limited processing, cost control processingTime='5 minutes' Wait 5 minutes between batches Near-real-time with reduced resource usage once=True Process all available data as ONE batch, then stop Backfill, one-time catchup availableNow=True Process all available data in MULTIPLE batches, then stop Large backfill with rate limiting
Trigger Strategy for Production
The trigger choice affects latency, cost, and resource consumption:

items: Low latency required (fraud detection, real-time alerts): processingTime='0 seconds'
Near-real-time (dashboards updated every minute): processingTime='1 minute'
Cost-sensitive (cloud billing by compute time): processingTime='5 minutes' or longer
Backfill or migration: once=True or availableNow=True

blocks.content: The availableNow trigger (Spark 3.3+) is particularly useful: it processes a large backlog of data in multiple batches (respecting maxFilesPerTrigger or similar rate limits) and then cleanly terminates. This makes it ideal for scheduled batch-like jobs that use the streaming checkpoint for exactly-once semantics.

A production streaming pipeline must survive failures. The cluster may lose a node. The application may restart. The source may go temporarily offline. Despite all this, the pipeline must not lose data or process records twice.
The Checkpoint Directory
Every streaming query requires a checkpoint location — a directory (typically on HDFS, S3, or GCS) where Spark stores:

items: Offsets — Which data has been read from the source (Kafka offsets, file list)
State — Intermediate aggregation state for stateful operations (groupBy counts, window state)
Metadata — Query configuration and commit log

blocks.content:
query = stream.writeStream \
    .option('checkpointLocation', '/checkpoints/meter_stream_v1/') \
    .format('parquet') \
    .start()
Checkpoint Directory Structure:

/checkpoints/meter_stream_v1/
    |-- metadata          Query ID and configuration
    |-- offsets/           What data has been read
    |    |-- 0             Batch 0 offsets
    |    |-- 1             Batch 1 offsets
    |    |-- 2             ...
    |-- commits/           What has been successfully written
    |    |-- 0
    |    |-- 1
    |-- state/             Aggregation state (if stateful query)
         |-- 0/
         |-- 1/
The Recovery Guarantee
When a streaming query restarts (after a crash, deployment, or scaling event):

items: Spark reads the checkpoint to determine which data was last committed (successfully written to the sink).
It replays from the last committed offset, re-processing any data that was read but not committed.
The result is exactly-once semantics when the source is replayable (Kafka, files) and the sink is idempotent (overwrite by key, Delta Lake merge).

blocks.content: Guarantee Level Meaning Requires At-most-once Data may be lost on failure No checkpointing (never use in production) At-least-once Data may be processed twice Checkpointing + replayable source Exactly-once Each record processed exactly once Checkpointing + replayable source + idempotent sink
Checkpoint Rules

items: Every streaming query must have a unique checkpoint location. Two queries sharing a checkpoint will corrupt each other’s state.
Never delete a checkpoint unless you intentionally want to reprocess from scratch.
Store checkpoints on durable storage (S3, GCS, ADLS, HDFS). Local disk is not safe.
Query changes may be incompatible with existing checkpoints. Adding a new groupBy key, changing aggregation logic, or altering the schema may require a fresh checkpoint.

sections.title: Lesson 8: Windowed Aggregations — Grouping by Time

blocks.content: The most common streaming pattern is: "How much total consumption happened in the last 5 minutes?" or "What is the average voltage per meter per hour?"
These are windowed aggregations — grouping events by time windows.
Tumbling Windows
A tumbling window divides time into fixed, non-overlapping intervals.
Tumbling Windows (5-minute):

Window 1: [00:00 - 00:05)  --> aggregate events in this window
  Window 2: [00:05 - 00:10)  --> aggregate events in this window
  Window 3: [00:10 - 00:15)  --> aggregate events in this window
No overlap. Each event belongs to exactly one window.

from pyspark.sql.functions import window, sum as spark_sum, avg, count

# 5-minute tumbling window aggregation
windowed = parsed_stream \
    .groupBy(
        window(col('timestamp'), '5 minutes'),  # tumbling window
        col('meter_id')
    ) \
    .agg(
        spark_sum('kw').alias('total_kw'),
        avg('kw').alias('avg_kw'),
        count('*').alias('num_readings'),
    )

# The 'window' column is a struct with start and end timestamps
# window.start | window.end   | meter_id | total_kw | avg_kw
# 00:00:00     | 00:05:00     | M001     | 450.2    | 112.5
Sliding Windows
A sliding window has a fixed duration but overlaps with adjacent windows. Each event may belong to multiple windows.
Sliding Windows (10-min window, 5-min slide):
An event at 00:07 belongs to BOTH Window A and Window B. Overlapping windows enable moving averages.

# 10-minute window sliding every 5 minutes
sliding = parsed_stream \
    .groupBy(
        window(col('timestamp'), '10 minutes', '5 minutes'),
        col('meter_id')
    ) \
    .agg(
        avg('kw').alias('moving_avg_kw'),
    )
Session Windows (Spark 3.2+)
Session windows group events that occur close together in time, with gaps closing the session. Useful for detecting activity bursts.
Session Windows (gap = 10 min):

Events: *  *  * *         *  * *              * *
  Time:   0  2  4 5         20 22 25             50 52

  Session 1: [0 - 15)   (events close together, gap < 10 min)
  Session 2: [20 - 35)  (gap from 5 to 20 = 15 min, new session)
  Session 3: [50 - 62)  (gap from 25 to 50 = 25 min, new session)

from pyspark.sql.functions import session_window

# Group by dynamic sessions with 10-minute gap tolerance
sessions = parsed_stream \
    .groupBy(
        session_window(col('timestamp'), '10 minutes'),
        col('meter_id')
    ) \
    .agg(
        spark_sum('kw').alias('session_total_kw'),
        count('*').alias('session_event_count'),
    )
Window Type Comparison

Window Type Parameters Overlap? Use Case Tumbling window(ts, '5 min') No Fixed-interval reporting (hourly totals) Sliding window(ts, '10 min', '5 min') Yes Moving averages, smoothed trends Session session_window(ts, '10 min') No (dynamic) Activity bursts, user sessions
In a perfect world, events arrive in order. In reality, they don’t.

A smart meter reading timestamped 10:00:00 might arrive at 10:05:00 due to network buffering. A mobile app event might arrive minutes late because the user was in a subway tunnel. These are late events.
Without a watermark, Spark must keep all window state forever because any event could arrive arbitrarily late. This causes unbounded memory growth that will eventually crash the application.
What a Watermark Does
A watermark tells Spark: "I expect data to arrive at most X late. After X, drop the event and free the state."
Watermark = 10 minutes:

Current event time seen so far: 10:30:00
  Watermark position:             10:20:00
                                  (current - 10 min)
Event arrives with timestamp 10:22:00 --> 10:22 >= 10:20 (watermark) --> ACCEPTED, aggregated
Event arrives with timestamp 10:18:00 --> 10:18 < 10:20 (watermark) --> DROPPED as too late
Window [10:00 - 10:10) state: --> 10:10 < 10:20 (watermark) --> STATE CLEARED from memory
Setting a Watermark

# Accept data up to 10 minutes late
windowed = parsed_stream \
    .withWatermark('timestamp', '10 minutes') \
    .groupBy(
        window(col('timestamp'), '5 minutes'),
        col('meter_id')
    ) \
    .agg(
        spark_sum('kw').alias('total_kw'),
    )
The watermark must be defined BEFORE the aggregation. Spark uses it during query planning.
Watermarks Enable Append Mode for Aggregations

Without a watermark, aggregation results can change at any time (a late event updates a window). This means only Update or Complete mode works. With a watermark, once a window passes the watermark boundary, Spark guarantees the window will never receive new events. The final result is emitted in Append mode, and the state is freed.

Scenario Output Mode Options Aggregation without watermark Update, Complete only (state grows forever) Aggregation with watermark Append (emitted once window expires), Update, Complete Stateless transformations Append (default), Update
Choosing the Watermark Duration

Duration Trade-off Scenario Very short (30 sec) Low latency, may drop more late data Low-latency dashboards where minor data loss is acceptable Moderate (5–10 min) Balanced latency vs completeness Most production pipelines Long (1–24 hours) High completeness, large state in memory End-of-day reconciliation, mobile apps with long offline periods No watermark All data accepted, state grows forever Testing only (will crash in production)
The right watermark depends on your source characteristics and your tolerance for dropped late events. Monitor your actual late-arrival distribution and set the watermark to cover 95–99% of events.
Streaming data often needs to be enriched by joining with other data. Spark supports two join patterns.
Stream-Static Joins
Join a streaming DataFrame with a regular (static) DataFrame. The static table is re-read on each micro-batch (or cached).

# Static dimension table (batch DataFrame)
metadata = spark.read.parquet('/data/dim/meter_metadata/')

# Streaming fact table
readings_stream = spark.readStream \
    .schema(meter_schema) \
    .json('/data/streaming/readings/')

# Join streaming with static (inner join)
enriched = readings_stream.join(metadata, 'meter_id')

# All join types supported: inner, left, right
enriched_left = readings_stream.join(metadata, 'meter_id', 'left_outer')

Performance note: The static DataFrame is broadcast to executors by default when small enough. For large static tables, ensure adequate executor memory. The static table is re-read at the start of each micro-batch, so changes to the static data are picked up automatically.
Stream-Stream Joins
Join two streaming DataFrames. This is powerful but complex — Spark must buffer state from both streams to find matches.

# Two streams: meter readings and meter alerts
readings = spark.readStream.schema(readings_schema) \
    .json('/data/streaming/readings/')
alerts = spark.readStream.schema(alerts_schema) \
    .json('/data/streaming/alerts/')

# Join: find readings that occurred within 5 minutes of an alert
joined = readings \
    .withWatermark('reading_ts', '10 minutes') \
    .join(
        alerts.withWatermark('alert_ts', '10 minutes'),
        on=[readings.meter_id == alerts.meter_id],
        how='inner'
    ) \
    .where(
        (readings.reading_ts >= alerts.alert_ts) &
        (readings.reading_ts <= alerts.alert_ts + expr('INTERVAL 5 MINUTES'))
    )

Watermarks are essential for stream-stream joins. Without them, Spark buffers all state from both streams indefinitely. With watermarks, Spark knows when old events can no longer match and clears them.
Join Support Matrix

Join Pattern Inner Left Outer Right Outer Full Outer Stream-Static Yes Yes Yes No Static-Stream Yes Yes Yes No Stream-Stream (with watermark) Yes Yes (conditionally) Yes (conditionally) No Stream-Stream (no watermark) Yes (inner only) No No No
Windowed aggregations are the most common stateful operation, but Spark supports arbitrary stateful processing through mapGroupsWithState and flatMapGroupsWithState.
When You Need Custom State

items: Sessionization — Track user sessions that span multiple events
Pattern detection — Detect sequences of events (3 consecutive voltage drops → alert)
Running thresholds — Alert when cumulative consumption exceeds a daily budget
Deduplication within a time window — Suppress duplicate alerts within 10 minutes

blocks.content: Deduplicate Within a Stream
Spark provides built-in deduplication for streams using dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark:

# Deduplicate based on meter_id + timestamp within watermark
deduped = parsed_stream \
    .withWatermark('timestamp', '10 minutes') \
    .dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark(['meter_id', 'timestamp'])
This drops duplicate events (network retransmissions) within the watermark window. State is automatically cleared for events older than the watermark.
Arbitrary Stateful Processing with flatMapGroupsWithState
For custom stateful logic, use flatMapGroupsWithState (the most powerful but most complex streaming primitive):

from pyspark.sql.streaming import GroupState, GroupStateTimeout
from pyspark.sql.types import *

# State schema: track cumulative consumption per meter
state_schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id', StringType()),
    StructField('cumulative_kw', DoubleType()),
    StructField('alert_sent', BooleanType()),
])

# Output schema: alerts when budget exceeded
alert_schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id', StringType()),
    StructField('cumulative_kw', DoubleType()),
    StructField('alert_type', StringType()),
])

def budget_monitor(meter_id, events, state: GroupState):
    """Track cumulative kW and alert when daily budget exceeded."""
    DAILY_BUDGET = 500.0

    # Retrieve or initialize state
    if state.exists:
        cumulative = state.get[0]  # previous cumulative
        alert_sent = state.get[1]
    else:
        cumulative = 0.0
        alert_sent = False

    # Accumulate new events
    for event in events:
        cumulative += event.kw

    # Update state
    state.update((cumulative, alert_sent))

    # Emit alert if budget exceeded and not already alerted
    if cumulative > DAILY_BUDGET and not alert_sent:
        state.update((cumulative, True))
        yield (meter_id, cumulative, 'BUDGET_EXCEEDED')

flatMapGroupsWithState gives you complete control over per-key state: what to store, when to update it, and when to emit output. This is the most flexible streaming primitive Spark offers, but it requires careful state management to avoid memory growth.
Monitoring a Running Stream

# Check query status
query.status
# {'message': 'Processing new data',
#  'isDataAvailable': True,
#  'isTriggerActive': True}

# Get progress metrics for the last micro-batch
query.lastProgress
# {'id': '...',
#  'runId': '...',
#  'batchId': 42,
#  'numInputRows': 15000,
#  'inputRowsPerSecond': 5000.0,
#  'processedRowsPerSecond': 12000.0,
#  'durationMs': {'addBatch': 800, 'queryPlanning': 50, ...},
#  'stateOperators': [{'numRowsTotal': 500000, ...}]}

# List all active streaming queries
spark.streams.active
Key Metrics to Monitor

Metric What It Tells You Alert When inputRowsPerSecond Rate of data arriving Sudden spikes or drops (source issues) processedRowsPerSecond Rate of processing Below inputRowsPerSecond (falling behind) numInputRows Rows in this micro-batch Unexpectedly zero (source stalled) batchId Current batch number Not incrementing (query stuck) stateOperators.numRowsTotal Total state rows maintained Unbounded growth (missing watermark) durationMs.addBatch Time to process one batch Exceeding trigger interval (cannot keep up)
The most critical alert: processedRowsPerSecond consistently below inputRowsPerSecond. This means the stream is falling behind. If not addressed, the backlog grows until the system fails.
StreamingQueryListener for Custom Monitoring
Production Checklist

Category Item Why Checkpoint Durable storage (S3/GCS/ADLS) Survive node failures Checkpoint Unique path per query Prevent state corruption Schema Explicit schema, never infer Schema inference not supported in streaming Watermark Always set for stateful queries Prevent unbounded state growth Monitoring Alert on processedRows < inputRows Detect falling behind early Monitoring Alert on state size growth Detect missing or insufficient watermark Sink Idempotent writes (Delta, upsert) Enable exactly-once semantics Testing Test with rate source before real data Validate logic without Kafka dependency Recovery Test restart from checkpoint Verify recovery works before production Scaling Increase partitions for throughput Match parallelism to input rate

sections.title: Lesson 13: Complete Example — Smart Grid Streaming Pipeline

blocks.content: Here is a production-grade streaming pipeline that ingests smart meter readings from Kafka, cleans them, computes 5-minute windowed aggregations, and writes both the raw cleaned data and the aggregated summaries to Delta Lake.

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col, from_json, window, sum as spark_sum, avg, count,
    current_timestamp, expr
)
from pyspark.sql.types import *

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('SmartGrid_RealTime_Pipeline') \
    .getOrCreate()

# ---- SCHEMA --------------------------------------------------------
reading_schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id', StringType()),
    StructField('timestamp', TimestampType()),
    StructField('kw', DoubleType()),
    StructField('voltage', DoubleType()),
    StructField('customer_type', StringType()),
])

# ---- SOURCE: Kafka --------------------------------------------------
raw = spark.readStream \
    .format('kafka') \
    .option('kafka.bootstrap.servers', 'broker1:9092') \
    .option('subscribe', 'smart_meter_readings') \
    .option('startingOffsets', 'latest') \
    .load()

# ---- PARSE ----------------------------------------------------------
parsed = raw \
    .selectExpr('CAST(value AS STRING)') \
    .select(from_json(col('value'), reading_schema).alias('data')) \
    .select('data.*') \
    .withColumn('processing_time', current_timestamp())

# ---- CLEAN ----------------------------------------------------------
clean = parsed \
    .filter(col('kw').isNotNull()) \
    .filter(col('kw') >= 0) \
    .filter(col('kw') < 50000) \
    .filter(col('voltage').between(100, 260))

# ---- SINK 1: Cleaned readings to Delta (append) --------------------
readings_query = clean.writeStream \
    .outputMode('append') \
    .format('delta') \
    .option('checkpointLocation', '/checkpoints/clean_readings/') \
    .option('path', '/data/silver/streaming_readings/') \
    .trigger(processingTime='30 seconds') \
    .start()

# ---- SINK 2: 5-min windowed aggregation ----------------------------
windowed_agg = clean \
    .withWatermark('timestamp', '10 minutes') \
    .groupBy(
        window(col('timestamp'), '5 minutes'),
        col('customer_type')
    ) \
    .agg(
        spark_sum('kw').alias('total_kw'),
        avg('kw').alias('avg_kw'),
        count('*').alias('num_readings'),
        avg('voltage').alias('avg_voltage'),
    )

agg_query = windowed_agg.writeStream \
    .outputMode('append') \
    .format('delta') \
    .option('checkpointLocation', '/checkpoints/windowed_agg/') \
    .option('path', '/data/gold/streaming_5min_summary/') \
    .trigger(processingTime='1 minute') \
    .start()

# ---- WAIT -----------------------------------------------------------
spark.streams.awaitAnyTermination()

This pipeline demonstrates: Kafka source with JSON deserialization, data cleaning in the stream, two independent sinks from the same stream (fan-out), watermarked windowed aggregation with Append mode to Delta Lake, separate checkpoint locations per query, and different trigger intervals for different latency requirements.
You now understand:

items: The fundamental shift from bounded batch to unbounded streaming data processing
Structured Streaming’s programming model: an infinite table processed with the same DataFrame/SQL API
Micro-batch processing: how Spark incrementally processes small batches of arriving data
readStream and writeStream as the streaming counterparts to read and write
Streaming sources (Kafka, files, Delta) and sinks (Kafka, files, Delta, foreachBatch)
foreachBatch for custom multi-sink and complex sink logic
Output modes: Append (new rows only), Update (changed rows), Complete (full result table)
Triggers: processingTime for continuous, once and availableNow for batch-style processing
Checkpointing: how Spark tracks progress, recovers from failures, and provides exactly-once guarantees
Windowed aggregations: tumbling, sliding, and session windows for time-based grouping
Watermarks: how they bound late data, enable Append mode for aggregations, and prevent unbounded state growth
Stream-static joins for enrichment and stream-stream joins for correlating two live streams
Stateful processing: deduplication within watermarks and arbitrary state with flatMapGroupsWithState
Production monitoring: key metrics, StreamingQueryListener, the "falling behind" alert
A complete end-to-end streaming pipeline for the smart grid domain: Kafka → parse → clean → fan-out to raw sink + windowed aggregation sink

blocks.content: You are no longer limited to data at rest. You can now process data in motion — continuously, reliably, and at scale.
In Module 11, we turn to MLlib — Machine Learning at Scale — building, training, and deploying predictive models on the same distributed engine, using the same DataFrame abstractions you now command.

chapters.title: Module 11: Delta Lake & the Data Lakehouse
chapters.description: Reliable, Transactional Storage at Scale Understand why plain Parquet breaks in production, how Delta Lake adds ACID transactions, time travel, schema enforcement, and change data capture to the data lake, and how the Lakehouse architecture unifies data engineering and analytics on a single platform.

blocks.content: In Modules 1 through 10, you have read from Parquet, written to Parquet, and been told that Parquet is the gold standard for distributed analytics.
That was a simplification.

Parquet is an excellent file format. But a collection of Parquet files is not a reliable data system. It has no transactions, no rollback, no schema enforcement, no way to update or delete individual records, and no audit trail.

In a real production environment, these gaps become emergencies. A failed pipeline writes half the data and corrupts the table. A GDPR deletion request demands removing one customer’s records from a 500 TB dataset. A data engineer deploys a schema change that breaks every downstream dashboard.
Delta Lake solves these problems. It is an open-source storage layer that sits on top of Parquet and adds the reliability guarantees that production systems demand.
The Stack:
Delta Lake does not replace Parquet. It adds a transaction log ON TOP of Parquet files.
This module teaches you what breaks without Delta, how Delta fixes it, and how the Lakehouse architecture built on Delta is reshaping the modern data platform.

sections.title: Lesson 2: Why Plain Parquet Breaks in Production

blocks.content: Before understanding Delta Lake, you must understand what goes wrong without it. Every problem Delta solves is a problem that actually happens in production.
Problem 1: Failed Writes Corrupt Data
Your pipeline writes 200 Parquet files to a directory. Midway through, a node crashes. Now the directory has 120 files from the new write and an indeterminate mix of old files.
Downstream readers see a mix of old and new data. Some records appear twice. Some are missing. There is no way to distinguish complete writes from partial ones.
Failed Write Without Transactions:
BEFORE: [old_1.parquet] [old_2.parquet] [old_3.parquet]

WRITE:   overwrite with 200 new files...
           [new_001] [new_002] ... [new_120] <CRASH>

  AFTER:   [new_001] [new_002] ... [new_120] [old_2.parquet]
           ^                                  ^
           Partial new data                   Leftover old data
Readers see CORRUPTED table. No rollback possible.
Problem 2: No Updates or Deletes
Parquet files are immutable. Once written, they cannot be modified.

To update one record in a 500 TB table, you must read the entire dataset, modify the record, and rewrite everything. For a GDPR deletion request requiring removal of one customer’s data, this is operationally devastating.
Problem 3: Concurrent Readers and Writers Conflict
While one pipeline writes new data, another pipeline reads for analytics. The reader may see partially written files, inconsistent data, or errors from files being replaced mid-read.
Traditional databases solved this decades ago with transactions and isolation levels. Plain Parquet directories have none of this.
Problem 4: Schema Drift Goes Undetected
A pipeline starts writing a new column called "power_kw" instead of "daily_kw". Or a field that was DoubleType is accidentally written as StringType.
With plain Parquet, these schema changes are silently written. Downstream jobs fail with cryptic errors, or worse, silently produce incorrect results.
Problem 5: No Audit Trail or Rollback
"What data was in this table yesterday at 3 PM?" With plain Parquet, the answer is: no one knows.
If a bad pipeline corrupts a table, there is no way to undo the damage without restoring from backup — assuming a backup exists and is recent enough.
The Summary

Problem Impact Root Cause Failed writes Corrupted, inconsistent tables No atomic transactions No updates/deletes Cannot comply with GDPR, cannot fix records Parquet files are immutable Concurrent access Readers see partial writes No isolation or locking Schema drift Silent data quality degradation No schema enforcement No audit trail Cannot investigate, cannot rollback No history of changes
Delta Lake solves every one of these problems. Let’s see how.

sections.title: Lesson 3: The Transaction Log — Delta Lake’s Core Innovation

blocks.content: Delta Lake’s fundamental innovation is a transaction log (also called the Delta Log) that records every change ever made to a table.
How It Works
Delta Table on Disk:

/data/silver/meter_readings/
    _delta_log/                   <-- THE TRANSACTION LOG
      00000000000000000000.json   Commit 0: initial write
      00000000000000000001.json   Commit 1: append new data
      00000000000000000002.json   Commit 2: delete 50 records
      00000000000000000003.json   Commit 3: update schema
      00000000000000000010.checkpoint.parquet  (compacted snapshot)
    part-00000-abc123.parquet     Data file
    part-00001-def456.parquet     Data file
    part-00002-ghi789.parquet     Data file
    part-00003-jkl012.parquet     Data file (added in commit 1)
    part-00004-mno345.parquet     Data file (added in commit 1)
The transaction log is a sequence of JSON files, each recording one commit — an atomic set of changes. Each commit records:

items: Add actions — new Parquet files added to the table
Remove actions — Parquet files logically removed (not physically deleted yet)
Metadata changes — schema evolution, table properties
Transaction information — timestamp, operation type, user

blocks.content: Reconstructing Table State
To read a Delta table, Spark replays the transaction log:

items: Start with commit 0: the initial files
Apply commit 1: add new files
Apply commit 2: remove (logically delete) some files
Apply commit 3: update metadata
The final set of "active" files is the current table state

blocks.content: Replaying the Log:
Commit 0: ADD file_A, ADD file_B Commit 1: ADD file_C, ADD file_D Commit 2: REMOVE file_A (deleted records) Commit 3: ADD file_E (updated records)
Current table = {file_B, file_C, file_D, file_E} file_A is logically removed (still on disk, but not in the table).
Checkpoint files (every 10 commits by default) compact the log into a single Parquet file, so Spark does not need to replay from the very beginning on tables with thousands of commits.
ACID Transactions
The transaction log gives Delta Lake full ACID properties:

Property Meaning How Delta Provides It Atomicity A write either fully succeeds or fully fails Commit is a single JSON file; if the file is not written, the commit did not happen Consistency Table is always in a valid state Schema enforcement validates every write against the table schema Isolation Concurrent readers/writers do not interfere Optimistic concurrency control; readers see a consistent snapshot Durability Committed data is not lost Data and log stored on durable cloud storage (S3, GCS, ADLS)
Optimistic Concurrency Control
When two writers attempt to modify the same table simultaneously, Delta uses optimistic concurrency:

items: Both writers read the current table version.
Both prepare their changes.
The first to commit wins and writes the next log entry.
The second detects that the table has changed, checks for conflicts, and either commits if no conflict exists or retries.

blocks.content: For appends (the most common operation), two concurrent writers almost never conflict because they add different files. For updates touching the same rows, one writer must retry.

sections.title: Lesson 4: Reading and Writing Delta Tables

blocks.content: Writing a Delta Table

# Write a DataFrame as a Delta table
df.write \
    .format('delta') \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .save('/data/silver/meter_readings/')

# Write as a managed table (registered in the catalog)
df.write \
    .format('delta') \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .saveAsTable('silver.meter_readings')
The syntax is nearly identical to writing Parquet. Replace .parquet() with .format('delta').save(). Delta writes the same Parquet data files plus the _delta_log directory.
Reading a Delta Table

# Read from path
df = spark.read.format('delta').load('/data/silver/meter_readings/')

# Read from catalog
df = spark.table('silver.meter_readings')

# SQL
spark.sql('SELECT * FROM silver.meter_readings WHERE customer_type = "RESIDENTIAL"')
Readers always see a consistent snapshot. Even if a writer is actively adding files, the reader sees only files that were part of a committed transaction at the time the read began.
Appending Data

# Append new daily readings
new_readings.write \
    .format('delta') \
    .mode('append') \
    .save('/data/silver/meter_readings/')
Append is the most common write operation in data pipelines. New Parquet files are added, and a new commit entry is written to the log. Existing data is untouched.
Partitioned Delta Tables

# Partition by date for efficient time-range queries
df.write \
    .format('delta') \
    .partitionBy('date') \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .save('/data/silver/meter_readings_partitioned/')
Partitioning creates a directory structure (date=2026-02-25/) enabling partition pruning. Delta respects partitioning for both reads and writes.

Choosing partition columns: The same rules from Module 2 apply. Partition by columns used in WHERE clauses (date, region). Avoid high-cardinality columns (meter_id with millions of values creates millions of directories).

sections.title: Lesson 5: Updates, Deletes, and MERGE — Mutable Data at Scale

blocks.content: This is where Delta Lake transforms what is possible with data lakes. Parquet files are immutable. Delta makes them appear mutable through a copy-on-write strategy.
How Mutations Work Under the Hood
Update 'kw' for meter M001 on 2026-02-25:
BEFORE: file_A.parquet contains 10,000 rows including M001
Delta does NOT modify file_A. Instead:

items: Read file_A
Apply update to matching rows
Write NEW file_B.parquet (with the updated M001 row)
Commit: REMOVE file_A, ADD file_B

blocks.content: AFTER: file_B.parquet (file_A still on disk but logically removed)
The original file_A is kept for time travel. VACUUM (covered later) physically deletes old files.

DELETE

from delta.tables import DeltaTable

delta_table = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, '/data/silver/meter_readings/')

# Delete all readings for decommissioned meters
delta_table.delete("meter_id = 'M_DECOM_001'")

# Delete with complex conditions
delta_table.delete(
    (col('kw') < 0) | (col('kw') > 100000)
)

# SQL syntax
spark.sql("""
    DELETE FROM silver.meter_readings
    WHERE meter_id = 'M_DECOM_001'
""")

GDPR compliance: DELETE makes it possible to remove individual customer records from a petabyte-scale data lake without rewriting the entire dataset. Delta only rewrites the specific Parquet files that contained the deleted rows.

UPDATE

# Fix a known faulty meter's readings
delta_table.update(
    condition = "meter_id = 'M_FAULTY_007'",
    set = {'kw': col('kw') * 0.95}  # Apply correction factor
)

# SQL syntax
spark.sql("""
    UPDATE silver.meter_readings
    SET kw = kw * 0.95
    WHERE meter_id = 'M_FAULTY_007'
""")

MERGE (Upsert) — The Workhorse of Production Pipelines

MERGE is the most important Delta operation for production data engineering. It combines INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE in a single atomic operation.
The classic use case: a daily batch of readings arrives. Some records are new (insert). Some are corrections to previously loaded records (update). Some indicate decommissioned meters (delete).

# Load today's batch of incoming readings
incoming = spark.read.parquet('/data/bronze/daily_batch/2026-02-25/')

# Merge into the silver table
delta_table = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, '/data/silver/meter_readings/')

delta_table.alias('target').merge( incoming.alias('source'), 'target.meter_id = source.meter_id AND target.date = source.date' ) \ .whenMatchedUpdate(set={ 'kw': col('source.kw'), 'voltage': col('source.voltage'), 'updated_at': current_timestamp(), }) \ .whenNotMatchedInsert(values={ 'meter_id': col('source.meter_id'), 'date': col('source.date'), 'kw': col('source.kw'), 'voltage': col('source.voltage'), 'customer_type': col('source.customer_type'), 'updated_at': current_timestamp(), }) \ .execute()

# SQL syntax for the same MERGE
spark.sql("""
    MERGE INTO silver.meter_readings AS target
    USING bronze.daily_batch AS source
    ON target.meter_id = source.meter_id
       AND target.date = source.date
    WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET
        target.kw = source.kw,
        target.voltage = source.voltage,
        target.updated_at = current_timestamp()
    WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *
""")

MERGE with Delete

# Full CDC pattern: insert + update + delete in one operation
delta_table.alias('target').merge(
    incoming.alias('source'),
    'target.meter_id = source.meter_id AND target.date = source.date'
) \
.whenMatchedUpdate(
    condition = "source.operation != 'DELETE'",
    set={'kw': col('source.kw'), 'voltage': col('source.voltage')}
) \
.whenMatchedDelete(
    condition = "source.operation = 'DELETE'"
) \
.whenNotMatchedInsert(
    condition = "source.operation != 'DELETE'",
    values={'meter_id': col('source.meter_id'), 'date': col('source.date'),
            'kw': col('source.kw'), 'voltage': col('source.voltage'),
            'customer_type': col('source.customer_type')}
) \
.execute()

MERGE Performance Tips

Tip Why How Filter the target table Reduces files scanned during match Add date or partition filter to the merge condition Partition by merge key Only rewrites partitions with matches partitionBy('date') on initial write Keep source small MERGE broadcasts the source by default Filter source to only changed records before merge Use replaceWhere for full partition overwrites Avoids per-row matching entirely df.write.option('replaceWhere', "date = '2026-02-25'")

sections.title: Lesson 6: Time Travel — Querying the Past

blocks.content: Because the transaction log records every change and old data files are retained, you can query any previous version of a Delta table.
Query by Version

# Read version 3 of the table
df_v3 = spark.read \
    .format('delta') \
    .option('versionAsOf', 3) \
    .load('/data/silver/meter_readings/')

# SQL syntax
spark.sql('SELECT * FROM silver.meter_readings VERSION AS OF 3')
Query by Timestamp

# Read the table as it existed at a specific timestamp
df_yesterday = spark.read \
    .format('delta') \
    .option('timestampAsOf', '2026-02-24T15:00:00') \
    .load('/data/silver/meter_readings/')

# SQL syntax
spark.sql("""
    SELECT * FROM silver.meter_readings
    TIMESTAMP AS OF '2026-02-24T15:00:00'
""")
Viewing Table History

# Full history of all commits
delta_table = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, '/data/silver/meter_readings/')
delta_table.history().show(truncate=False)

# Returns: version, timestamp, operation, operationParameters,
#          userMetadata, operationMetrics

# SQL syntax
spark.sql('DESCRIBE HISTORY silver.meter_readings')
Use Cases for Time Travel

Use Case Example Command Audit What data did the regulator see on Feb 1? timestampAsOf '2026-02-01' Rollback Bad pipeline corrupted the table; restore to before RESTORE TABLE ... TO VERSION AS OF n Debugging Compare before/after a pipeline run Read version N-1 and N, diff them Reproducibility Reproduce an ML training run from exact data versionAsOf (logged with model) Impact analysis What changed between yesterday and today? Read two versions, compute diff
RESTORE — Undoing Mistakes

# Roll back to version 5 (undo all changes after version 5)
delta_table.restoreToVersion(5)

# Roll back to a timestamp
delta_table.restoreToTimestamp('2026-02-24T12:00:00')

# SQL syntax
spark.sql('RESTORE TABLE silver.meter_readings TO VERSION AS OF 5')
RESTORE does not delete data. It writes a new commit that sets the table’s file list back to a previous version. The operation itself can be rolled back.
Schema problems are one of the most common causes of data pipeline failures. Delta Lake provides two mechanisms: enforcement (reject bad writes) and evolution (safely accept new columns).
Schema Enforcement (Default)
By default, Delta rejects any write whose schema does not match the table’s existing schema.

# Table has columns: meter_id, date, kw, voltage, customer_type

# This write adds a column 'temperature' that doesn't exist in the table
new_data_with_extra_col.write \
    .format('delta') \
    .mode('append') \
    .save('/data/silver/meter_readings/')

# RESULT: AnalysisException!
# 'A schema mismatch detected when writing to the Delta table.'
# The write is REJECTED. Table is unchanged. Data is safe.
This is critical for production reliability. Without schema enforcement, a misconfigured pipeline can silently add wrong columns, change types, or drop fields — corrupting all downstream consumers.
Schema Evolution (Opt-in)
When you intentionally want to add new columns, enable schema evolution:

# Allow new columns to be added automatically
new_data_with_extra_col.write \
    .format('delta') \
    .mode('append') \
    .option('mergeSchema', True) \
    .save('/data/silver/meter_readings/')

# Now the table has: meter_id, date, kw, voltage, customer_type, temperature
# Existing rows will have NULL for 'temperature'
Schema evolution supports:

Change
Supported?
Notes
Add new column
Yes (with mergeSchema)
Existing rows get NULL for the new column
Widen type (int -> long)
Yes (with mergeSchema)
Safe widening is allowed
Rename column
Not automatic
Use ALTER TABLE ... RENAME COLUMN
Drop column
Not automatic
Use ALTER TABLE ... DROP COLUMN (Delta 2.0+)
Change type (string -> int)
No
Would corrupt existing data; requires rewrite
Change nullability
Yes (nullable -> not null)
Requires validation of existing data
Schema Evolution in MERGE

# Enable schema evolution during MERGE
spark.conf.set('spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled', 'true')

# Or per-operation
delta_table.alias('target').merge(
    source.alias('source'),
    'target.meter_id = source.meter_id AND target.date = source.date'
) \
.whenMatchedUpdateAll() \
.whenNotMatchedInsertAll() \
.execute()

# If source has new columns, they are added to the target table

sections.title: Lesson 8: Table Maintenance — OPTIMIZE and VACUUM

blocks.content: Over time, Delta tables accumulate small files (from streaming appends), obsolete files (from updates and deletes), and suboptimal data layouts. Table maintenance operations keep tables healthy and performant.
The Small File Problem in Delta
A streaming pipeline appending data every 30 seconds creates thousands of tiny Parquet files. Each micro-batch writes a few small files. After a day, the table has tens of thousands of files.
Small files hurt read performance: Spark must list all files (metadata overhead), open each file (IO overhead), and create one task per file (scheduling overhead).
OPTIMIZE — Compacting Small Files

# Compact small files into larger ones
delta_table.optimize().executeCompaction()

# SQL syntax
spark.sql('OPTIMIZE silver.meter_readings')

# Optimize a specific partition
spark.sql("OPTIMIZE silver.meter_readings WHERE date = '2026-02-25'")
OPTIMIZE reads all small files in a partition, combines them into fewer large files (target ~1 GB each), and writes a new commit. Old files are marked as removed.
Z-ORDER — Data Skipping Optimization
Z-Ordering colocates related data in the same files, dramatically improving data skipping (predicate pushdown via file-level min/max statistics).

# Optimize and Z-Order by meter_id for fast meter lookups
delta_table.optimize().executeZOrderBy('meter_id')

# SQL syntax
spark.sql('OPTIMIZE silver.meter_readings ZORDER BY (meter_id)')

# Z-Order by multiple columns (common for queries filtering on both)
spark.sql('OPTIMIZE silver.meter_readings ZORDER BY (customer_type, date)')
Without Z-Order: With Z-Order by meter_id:

file_1: M001, M500, M238...     file_1: M001, M002, M003...
  file_2: M003, M999, M001...     file_2: M100, M101, M102...
  file_3: M238, M001, M500...     file_3: M200, M201, M202...

  Query: WHERE meter_id = 'M001'  Query: WHERE meter_id = 'M001'
  Must scan ALL files             Scans ONLY file_1
  (M001 is scattered everywhere)  (M001 is colocated in one file)
Z-Order is one of the most impactful optimizations for analytical queries. It is especially effective on tables queried with different filter columns than the partitioning column.
VACUUM — Cleaning Up Old Files
Updates, deletes, and OPTIMIZE create new files but do not physically remove old ones (they’re needed for time travel). Over time, these obsolete files consume storage.

# Remove files no longer referenced by the table, older than 7 days
delta_table.vacuum(168)  # 168 hours = 7 days

# SQL syntax
spark.sql('VACUUM silver.meter_readings RETAIN 168 HOURS')
VACUUM deletes files permanently. After vacuuming, time travel to versions older than the retention period is no longer possible.

Operation What It Does When to Run OPTIMIZE Compacts small files into larger ones Daily or after heavy streaming/append workloads ZORDER Colocates data for faster queries After OPTIMIZE; on columns used in WHERE clauses VACUUM Physically deletes obsolete files Weekly; after confirming time travel needs are met

sections.title: Lesson 9: Change Data Feed — Tracking What Changed

blocks.content: Change Data Feed (CDF) records every row-level change to a Delta table: inserts, updates (both the before and after image), and deletes. This enables downstream consumers to process only the changes rather than re-reading the entire table.
Enabling CDF

# Enable on an existing table
spark.sql("""
    ALTER TABLE silver.meter_readings
    SET TBLPROPERTIES (delta.enableChangeDataFeed = true)
""")

# Or when creating a new table
df.write \
    .format('delta') \
    .option('delta.enableChangeDataFeed', 'true') \
    .save('/data/silver/meter_readings/')
Reading the Change Feed

# Read all changes since version 5
changes = spark.read \
    .format('delta') \
    .option('readChangeFeed', True) \
    .option('startingVersion', 5) \
    .load('/data/silver/meter_readings/')

changes.show()
# meter_id | date       | kw    | _change_type     | _commit_version
# M001     | 2026-02-25 | 450.5 | update_preimage  | 6
# M001     | 2026-02-25 | 465.2 | update_postimage | 6
# M999     | 2026-02-25 | 120.0 | insert           | 6
# M_OLD    | 2026-02-20 | 300.0 | delete           | 7

# Read changes by timestamp range
changes = spark.read \
    .format('delta') \
    .option('readChangeFeed', True) \
    .option('startingTimestamp', '2026-02-25T00:00:00') \
    .option('endingTimestamp', '2026-02-25T23:59:59') \
    .load('/data/silver/meter_readings/')
Streaming the Change Feed

# Use CDF as a streaming source
changes_stream = spark.readStream \
    .format('delta') \
    .option('readChangeFeed', True) \
    .option('startingVersion', 'latest') \
    .load('/data/silver/meter_readings/')

# Process only changes (not the full table) downstream
inserts = changes_stream.filter(col('_change_type') == 'insert')
updates = changes_stream.filter(col('_change_type') == 'update_postimage')
CDF Use Cases

Use Case Without CDF With CDF Silver-to-Gold propagation Re-read entire silver table every run Process only changed rows Downstream sync to warehouse Full table dump to Redshift/Snowflake Incremental sync of changes only Audit log Build custom logging infrastructure Built-in row-level change history Real-time materialized views Full recomputation every trigger Incremental update from change stream
Delta Lake is not just a file format enhancement. It is the foundation of a new architectural paradigm: the Data Lakehouse.
The Evolution of Data Architectures
Architecture Evolution:

ERA 1: Data Warehouse (1990s-2010s)
  +--------------------+
  | SQL Database        |  Structured data only.
  | (Oracle, Teradata,  |  Expensive. Limited scale.
  |  Redshift)          |  Strong governance.
  +--------------------+

  ERA 2: Data Lake (2010s)
  +--------------------+
  | Raw Files           |  Any data format.
  | (HDFS, S3, GCS)     |  Cheap, scalable.
  | + Spark/Hive        |  Weak governance.
  +--------------------+   Often became a 'data swamp.'

  ERA 3: Lambda Architecture (2010s-2020s)
  +--------+ +--------+
  | Batch  | | Stream |  Two systems, two codebases,
  | Layer  | | Layer  |  two sets of bugs.
  +--------+ +--------+  Expensive to maintain.

  ERA 4: Data Lakehouse (2020s)
  +--------------------+
  | Delta Lake          |  Warehouse reliability +
  | on Cloud Storage    |  Lake flexibility +
  | + Spark Unified     |  One engine for batch,
  |   Engine            |  streaming, SQL, ML.
  +--------------------+
Why the Lakehouse Won

Capability Data Warehouse Data Lake Lakehouse ACID transactions Yes No Yes (Delta) Schema enforcement Yes (strict) No Yes (enforcement + evolution) Structured data Yes Yes Yes Semi/unstructured data No (or limited) Yes Yes Time travel / audit Limited No Yes (built-in) Cost (at PB scale) Very high ($$$) Low ($) Low ($) + governance Streaming support Limited / separate Separate system Same engine (Structured Streaming) Open format No (proprietary) Yes (Parquet) Yes (Parquet + Delta) Updates / deletes Yes Very difficult Yes (MERGE, DELETE, UPDATE) ML workloads Limited Yes (but messy) Yes (governed, reliable)
The Lakehouse combines the governance and reliability of a data warehouse with the flexibility and cost of a data lake. Delta Lake is the technology that makes this possible.
The Medallion Architecture on Delta
In Module 7, we introduced the Medallion Architecture (Bronze → Silver → Gold). Delta Lake is the natural storage layer for each tier:
Medallion Architecture on Delta Lake:

BRONZE (raw)              SILVER (clean)           GOLD (business)
  +------------------+      +------------------+     +------------------+
  | Delta Table      |      | Delta Table      |     | Delta Table      |
  | Raw JSON ingested| ---> | Cleaned, typed,  | --> | Aggregated,      |
  | Schema on read   |      | deduplicated     |     | business metrics |
  | Append only      |      | Schema enforced  |     | MERGE updates    |
  +------------------+      +------------------+     +------------------+
Each layer is a Delta table with:

items: ACID transactions (no corrupt writes)
Time travel (audit and rollback at every layer)
Schema enforcement (catch drift at silver boundary)
CDF (propagate only changes from silver to gold)

sections.title: Lesson 11: Delta Lake with Structured Streaming

blocks.content: Delta Lake and Structured Streaming are deeply integrated. Delta tables can serve as both streaming sources and sinks, enabling end-to-end streaming pipelines with exactly-once guarantees.
Delta as a Streaming Source

# Read a Delta table as a stream
# Spark processes new commits as they arrive
stream = spark.readStream \
    .format('delta') \
    .load('/data/silver/meter_readings/')

# With rate limiting
stream = spark.readStream \
    .format('delta') \
    .option('maxFilesPerTrigger', 100) \
    .load('/data/silver/meter_readings/')

When a batch pipeline appends to the silver Delta table, the streaming consumer automatically picks up the new data. This is a powerful pattern for connecting batch ingestion to real-time downstream processing.
Delta as a Streaming Sink

# Write streaming data to Delta with exactly-once guarantees
query = clean_stream.writeStream \
    .format('delta') \
    .outputMode('append') \
    .option('checkpointLocation', '/checkpoints/silver_stream/') \
    .option('path', '/data/silver/meter_readings/') \
    .trigger(processingTime='30 seconds') \
    .start()
foreachBatch with MERGE for Streaming Upserts
The most powerful pattern: use foreachBatch to MERGE each micro-batch into a Delta table, enabling streaming upserts:

def upsert_to_delta(batch_df, batch_id):
    delta_table = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, '/data/silver/meter_readings/')
delta_table.alias('target').merge( batch_df.alias('source'), 'target.meter_id = source.meter_id AND target.date = source.date' ) \ .whenMatchedUpdateAll() \ .whenNotMatchedInsertAll() \ .execute()

query = clean_stream.writeStream \
    .foreachBatch(upsert_to_delta) \
    .option('checkpointLocation', '/checkpoints/upsert_stream/') \
    .trigger(processingTime='1 minute') \
    .start()
This pattern handles late-arriving corrections: if a reading arrives that should update an existing record, the MERGE handles it seamlessly.
Complete Streaming Medallion Pipeline
Streaming Medallion on Delta:

Kafka
    |
    v
  [Stream] --> Bronze Delta (append raw)
                 |
                 v
  [Stream] --> Silver Delta (clean + dedupe)
                 |
                 v  (CDF: read only changes)
  [Stream] --> Gold Delta (aggregate via MERGE)
                 |
                 v
              Dashboard / API
Each arrow is a Structured Streaming query. Each Delta table provides exactly-once guarantees. CDF ensures gold only processes changed records.

sections.title: Lesson 12: Delta Lake in the Ecosystem

blocks.content: Delta Lake is not the only table format that adds ACID transactions to data lakes. Understanding the alternatives helps you make informed platform decisions.
Open Table Formats Comparison

Feature Delta Lake Apache Iceberg Apache Hudi Creator Databricks Netflix / Apple Uber ACID transactions Yes Yes Yes Time travel Yes Yes Yes Schema evolution Yes Yes Yes Partition evolution Limited (rewrite needed) Yes (hidden partitions) Limited MERGE / upsert Yes Yes (Spark 3.x) Yes (core feature) Streaming source/sink Excellent (native Spark) Good (improving) Good (native feature) Spark integration Deepest (same creators) Excellent Good Multi-engine support Growing (Trino, Flink) Excellent (Trino, Flink, Presto) Good Databricks optimizations Yes (Photon, liquid clustering) No No Community size Very large Very large (growing fastest) Large Best fit Databricks-centric shops Multi-engine analytics Streaming-heavy ingestion

All three formats solve the same fundamental problems: ACID transactions, time travel, and schema management on top of cloud storage. The choice often depends on your platform: Databricks defaults to Delta, but all three are open-source and interoperable to varying degrees.

For this course: Delta Lake is the focus because of its deep integration with PySpark and its dominance in the Spark ecosystem. The concepts (transaction logs, ACID, time travel, MERGE) apply directly to Iceberg and Hudi as well.
Table Design Checklist

Decision Guidance Rationale Partition column Date or low-cardinality column used in WHERE Enables partition pruning; avoids too many partitions Z-Order columns Columns used in filters but NOT the partition column Improves data skipping within partitions File target size 1 GB per file (use OPTIMIZE) Balances read parallelism with file overhead Schema enforcement Always ON (default). mergeSchema only for planned changes Prevents silent data corruption Change Data Feed Enable on silver and gold tables Enables incremental downstream processing VACUUM retention 7 days minimum (default). 30 days for audit-critical tables Balances storage cost with time travel needs
Maintenance Schedule

Task Frequency Command OPTIMIZE heavily-written tables Daily (or after large writes) OPTIMIZE table_name Z-ORDER analytical tables After OPTIMIZE OPTIMIZE table_name ZORDER BY (col) VACUUM old files Weekly VACUUM table_name RETAIN 168 HOURS ANALYZE TABLE (compute stats) After large data changes ANALYZE TABLE table_name COMPUTE STATISTICS Monitor table size growth Ongoing DESCRIBE DETAIL table_name
Anti-Patterns to Avoid

Anti-Pattern Problem Solution Thousands of tiny appends without OPTIMIZE Small file problem degrades read performance Schedule daily OPTIMIZE; use OPTIMIZE WRITE in Databricks VACUUM with retention < 7 days Breaks concurrent readers who started before VACUUM Keep default 7-day retention Partitioning by high-cardinality column Millions of tiny partitions Use Z-ORDER instead of partitioning Using overwrite mode when append + MERGE works Rewrites entire table unnecessarily Use append for new data, MERGE for upserts Ignoring schema enforcement by always using mergeSchema Silent schema drift returns mergeSchema only for deliberate, reviewed changes No checkpoints on streaming Delta writes Data loss on failure Always specify checkpointLocation
You now understand:

items: Why plain Parquet breaks in production: no transactions, no updates, no schema enforcement, no audit trail
How the transaction log provides ACID guarantees through an append-only sequence of commits tracking file-level adds and removes
Optimistic concurrency control for safe concurrent reads and writes
How to read and write Delta tables with the same familiar PySpark API
DELETE, UPDATE, and MERGE for mutable data at scale, including the copy-on-write mechanism
MERGE as the production workhorse for upserts, CDC processing, and incremental pipelines
Time travel by version and timestamp for auditing, debugging, reproducibility, and rollback with RESTORE
Schema enforcement (reject bad writes by default) and schema evolution (safely add columns with mergeSchema)
OPTIMIZE for small file compaction, Z-ORDER for data skipping optimization, and VACUUM for storage cleanup
Change Data Feed for tracking row-level changes and enabling incremental downstream processing
The Lakehouse architecture and how it unifies warehouse reliability with data lake flexibility
Delta Lake as both streaming source and sink, including the foreachBatch + MERGE pattern for streaming upserts
How Delta, Iceberg, and Hudi compare as open table formats
Production best practices: table design, maintenance schedules, and anti-patterns to avoid

blocks.content: You are no longer writing to a collection of files. You are managing a reliable, transactional, auditable data system.
In Module 12, we turn to Testing, Data Quality, and Observability — how to ensure that the pipelines you build actually produce correct results, and how to know when they don’t.

chapters.title: Module 12: Testing, Data Quality & Observability
chapters.description: Proving Your Pipelines Actually Work Learn to write automated tests for PySpark pipelines, embed data quality checks directly into production workflows, build observability into every layer of the Medallion Architecture, and create the feedback loops that turn silent data failures into visible, actionable alerts.

blocks.content: You can now build distributed pipelines that read streaming data, clean it, join it, aggregate it, store it in Delta Lake, and serve it to dashboards. You have a formidable toolkit.
But here is the question no one asks until it is too late:
How do you know the pipeline is producing correct results?

A pipeline that runs without errors is not the same as a pipeline that produces correct data. Spark will happily filter your entire dataset to zero rows if your condition has a subtle bug. It will silently cast a string column to all NULLs if types drift. It will aggregate over a join that produced a Cartesian product and give you numbers that look plausible but are wrong by a factor of 100.

These are not crashes. These are silent failures. They are far more dangerous than errors, because they propagate through the data lake into dashboards, reports, and business decisions without anyone knowing.
The Danger Spectrum:

LOUD FAILURE                          SILENT FAILURE
  (crash, exception)                    (wrong data, no error)
  |                                     |
  | Easy to detect.                     | No one notices.
  | Stack trace tells you               | Downstream reports
  | exactly what went wrong.            | look 'reasonable.'
  |                                     | Discovered weeks later
  | Annoying but safe.                  | by a confused analyst.
  |                                     |
  +-------------------------------------+
            ^
            |
            This module exists to move failures LEFT.
            Make them loud. Make them early. Make them visible.

This module covers three disciplines that protect against silent failure: testing (prove correctness before deployment), data quality checks (validate data during pipeline execution), and observability (monitor pipeline health continuously in production).

sections.title: Lesson 2: The Testing Pyramid for Data Pipelines

blocks.content: Software engineering has a well-established testing hierarchy. Data engineering adapts this pyramid for pipelines that transform distributed data.
The Data Pipeline Testing Pyramid

/\
                 /  \
                / E2E \         End-to-end pipeline tests
               / Tests  \       (full pipeline, real-ish data)
              /----------\
             / Integration \     Spark-level tests
            /    Tests      \    (SparkSession, DataFrames)
           /----------------\
          /    Unit Tests     \   Pure Python function tests
         /     (no Spark)      \  (fastest, most numerous)
        /----------------------\

Bottom: Fast, cheap, many.
  Top:    Slow, expensive, few.

Level What It Tests Needs Spark? Speed Quantity Unit tests Pure Python functions, UDFs, business logic No Milliseconds Hundreds Integration tests DataFrame transformations, joins, aggregations Yes (local mode) Seconds Dozens End-to-end tests Full pipeline: read → transform → write → validate Yes (local mode) Seconds-Minutes A few per pipeline

sections.title: Lesson 3: Unit Testing — Pure Python, No Spark

blocks.content: Unit tests validate your business logic in isolation, without starting Spark. They are fast (milliseconds), easy to write, and should cover the most critical logic in your pipeline.
What to Unit Test

items: UDF functions (before wrapping them as UDFs)
Business logic functions (tariff calculations, classification rules)
Schema definitions (validate expected types and nullability)
Configuration parsing (environment settings, threshold values)
Helper functions (date parsing, string normalization)

blocks.content: Example: Testing a Tariff Calculation

if month in (12, 1, 2):
        rate *= 1.25  # winter surcharge
Run with: pytest test_business_logic.py -v

Notice: no Spark import, no SparkSession, no DataFrame. These tests run in under a second. They cover NULL handling, boundary conditions, default behavior, and every customer type. This is where the most bugs are caught, and where they are cheapest to fix.

sections.title: Lesson 4: Integration Testing — Spark in Local Mode

blocks.content: Integration tests validate that your DataFrame transformations produce correct results when Spark actually executes them. They require a SparkSession but run in local mode — no cluster needed.
The SparkSession Fixture

Create a shared SparkSession for all tests using a pytest fixture:

# conftest.py
import pytest
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

@pytest.fixture(scope='session')
def spark():
    """Create a SparkSession for the entire test session."""
    session = SparkSession.builder \
        .master('local[2]') \
        .appName('pipeline_tests') \
        .config('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '4') \
        .config('spark.ui.enabled', 'false') \
        .getOrCreate()
    yield session
    session.stop()
Key configurations for testing:

items: local[2] — Use 2 local cores (enough to test parallelism without overhead)
shuffle.partitions = 4 — Default is 200, which is wasteful for test-sized data
spark.ui.enabled = false — Disable the Spark UI for faster startup

blocks.content: Testing a Transformation Function

# transformations.py
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, when

def clean_readings(df):
    """Apply standard cleaning to meter readings."""
    return df \
        .filter(col('kw').isNotNull()) \
        .filter(col('kw') >= 0) \
        .filter(col('kw') < 50000) \
        .withColumn('usage_tier',
            when(col('kw') < 200, 'LOW')
            .when(col('kw') < 1000, 'MEDIUM')
            .when(col('kw') < 5000, 'HIGH')
            .otherwise('EXTREME')
        )

# test_transformations.py
from transformations import clean_readings

class TestCleanReadings:

    def test_filters_null_kw(self, spark):
        data = [('M001', None), ('M002', 100.0)]
        df = spark.createDataFrame(data, ['meter_id', 'kw'])
        result = clean_readings(df)
        assert result.count() == 1
        assert result.collect()[0]['meter_id'] == 'M002'

    def test_filters_negative_kw(self, spark):
        data = [('M001', -50.0), ('M002', 100.0)]
        df = spark.createDataFrame(data, ['meter_id', 'kw'])
        result = clean_readings(df)
        assert result.count() == 1

    def test_filters_extreme_kw(self, spark):
        data = [('M001', 60000.0), ('M002', 100.0)]
        df = spark.createDataFrame(data, ['meter_id', 'kw'])
        result = clean_readings(df)
        assert result.count() == 1

    def test_usage_tier_classification(self, spark):
        data = [
            ('M001', 50.0),    # LOW
            ('M002', 500.0),   # MEDIUM
            ('M003', 3000.0),  # HIGH
            ('M004', 8000.0),  # EXTREME
        ]
        df = spark.createDataFrame(data, ['meter_id', 'kw'])
        result = clean_readings(df).orderBy('meter_id').collect()
        assert result[0]['usage_tier'] == 'LOW'
        assert result[1]['usage_tier'] == 'MEDIUM'
        assert result[2]['usage_tier'] == 'HIGH'
        assert result[3]['usage_tier'] == 'EXTREME'

    def test_preserves_all_columns(self, spark):
        data = [('M001', 100.0)]
        df = spark.createDataFrame(data, ['meter_id', 'kw'])
        result = clean_readings(df)
        assert 'meter_id' in result.columns
        assert 'kw' in result.columns
        assert 'usage_tier' in result.columns

    def test_empty_input(self, spark):
        from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType
        schema = StructType([
            StructField('meter_id', StringType()),
            StructField('kw', DoubleType()),
        ])
        df = spark.createDataFrame([], schema)
        result = clean_readings(df)
        assert result.count() == 0
Testing Aggregations

# test_aggregations.py
from pyspark.sql.functions import col

def test_consumption_by_type(spark):
    data = [
        ('M001', 'RESIDENTIAL', 100.0),
        ('M002', 'RESIDENTIAL', 200.0),
        ('M003', 'COMMERCIAL', 500.0),
    ]
    df = spark.createDataFrame(data, ['meter_id', 'customer_type', 'kw'])

    result = df.groupBy('customer_type').sum('kw').collect()
    result_dict = {row['customer_type']: row['sum(kw)'] for row in result}

assert result_dict['RESIDENTIAL'] == 300.0
    assert result_dict['COMMERCIAL'] == 500.0
Testing Joins
assert result.count() == 3 # All readings preserved

# M999 has no metadata
    orphan = result.filter(col('meter_id') == 'M999').collect()[0]
    assert orphan['customer_type'] is None
    assert orphan['zone'] is None
Comparing DataFrames
For complex transformations, compare entire DataFrames against expected results:

def assert_dataframes_equal(actual, expected, order_by):
    """Compare two DataFrames for equality."""
    actual_sorted = actual.orderBy(order_by).collect()
    expected_sorted = expected.orderBy(order_by).collect()

    assert len(actual_sorted) == len(expected_sorted), \
        f'Row count mismatch: {len(actual_sorted)} vs {len(expected_sorted)}'

    for i, (a_row, e_row) in enumerate(zip(actual_sorted, expected_sorted)):
        assert a_row == e_row, \
            f'Row {i} mismatch:\n  Actual:   {a_row}\n  Expected: {e_row}'

def test_full_clean_pipeline(spark):
    input_data = spark.createDataFrame([
        ('M001', 100.0), ('M002', -50.0), ('M003', 200.0),
    ], ['meter_id', 'kw'])

    expected = spark.createDataFrame([
        ('M001', 100.0, 'LOW'), ('M003', 200.0, 'MEDIUM'),
    ], ['meter_id', 'kw', 'usage_tier'])

    result = clean_readings(input_data)
    assert_dataframes_equal(result, expected, 'meter_id')

sections.title: Lesson 5: End-to-End Testing — The Full Pipeline

blocks.content: End-to-end tests validate the entire pipeline: read from source, transform, write to sink, and verify the output. These tests are slower but catch integration issues that unit and integration tests miss.
Testing a Complete Pipeline with Delta

import os
import tempfile
import shutil

class TestSmartGridPipeline:

    def test_bronze_to_silver(self, spark):
        """Test the full bronze-to-silver cleaning pipeline."""
        # Setup: create temp directories
        tmp = tempfile.mkdtemp()
        bronze_path = os.path.join(tmp, 'bronze')
        silver_path = os.path.join(tmp, 'silver')

        try:
            # Write bronze test data
            bronze_data = spark.createDataFrame([
                ('M001', '2026-02-25', 100.0, 238.0, 'RESIDENTIAL'),
                ('M002', '2026-02-25', -50.0, 235.0, 'COMMERCIAL'),  # bad: negative
                ('M003', '2026-02-25', None, 240.0, 'INDUSTRIAL'),   # bad: null kw
                ('M004', '2026-02-25', 500.0, 237.0, 'RESIDENTIAL'),
                ('M001', '2026-02-25', 100.0, 238.0, 'RESIDENTIAL'), # duplicate
            ], ['meter_id', 'date', 'kw', 'voltage', 'customer_type'])
            bronze_data.write.format('delta').save(bronze_path)

            # Run the pipeline
            from pipeline import bronze_to_silver
            bronze_to_silver(spark, bronze_path, silver_path)

            # Validate silver output
            silver = spark.read.format('delta').load(silver_path)

            # Row count: 5 bronze - 1 negative - 1 null - 1 duplicate = 2
            assert silver.count() == 2

            # No nulls in kw
            assert silver.filter(col('kw').isNull()).count() == 0

            # No negatives
            assert silver.filter(col('kw') < 0).count() == 0

            # No duplicates
            dedup_count = silver.dropDuplicates(['meter_id', 'date']).count()
            assert dedup_count == silver.count()

        finally:
            shutil.rmtree(tmp)
End-to-end tests use temp directories, write real Delta tables, run the actual pipeline function, and validate the output. The try/finally block ensures cleanup even if assertions fail.
What End-to-End Tests Should Verify

Check What It Catches Example Row count Missing or duplicated data Expected 1000, got 0 (filter bug) Schema Wrong columns, wrong types Expected DoubleType, got StringType NULL counts Unexpected NULLs from bad joins or casts Join produced NULLs in 90% of rows Value ranges Out-of-bounds values surviving cleaning kw = -999 still in silver table Deduplication Duplicates not removed Same meter_id+date appears twice Referential integrity Orphan keys from failed joins meter_id in readings not in metadata Idempotency Running twice produces different results Row count doubles on second run

sections.title: Lesson 6: Data Quality Checks — Validation Inside the Pipeline

blocks.content: Tests run before deployment. Data quality checks run during pipeline execution, validating the actual production data flowing through the system.
The Difference: Tests vs Quality Checks

Aspect Tests Data Quality Checks When they run Before deployment (CI/CD) During every pipeline execution What they validate Code correctness with known data Real data correctness with production data Who maintains them Data engineers Data engineers + analysts + domain experts Failure mode Block deployment Alert, quarantine, or halt pipeline Data used Small, controlled test datasets Actual production data (millions/billions of rows)
Building a Quality Check Framework
Here is a reusable pattern for embedding quality checks directly into your pipeline:

# quality_checks.py
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List, Optional
from pyspark.sql import DataFrame
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, count, sum as spark_sum, when, isnan

@dataclass
class QualityCheckResult:
    check_name: str
    passed: bool
    severity: str        # 'WARN', 'ERROR', 'CRITICAL'
    metric_value: float
    threshold: float
    message: str

def check_not_empty(df: DataFrame, table_name: str) -> QualityCheckResult:
    """Table must have at least one row."""
    row_count = df.count()
    return QualityCheckResult(
        check_name=f'{table_name}_not_empty',
        passed=row_count > 0,
        severity='CRITICAL',
        metric_value=row_count,
        threshold=1,
        message=f'{table_name} has {row_count} rows'
    )

def check_null_rate(df: DataFrame, column: str,
                    max_rate: float = 0.01) -> QualityCheckResult:
    """Column NULL rate must be below threshold."""
    total = df.count()
    if total == 0:
        return QualityCheckResult(f'null_rate_{column}', True, 'WARN', 0, max_rate, 'Empty DF')
    null_count = df.filter(col(column).isNull() | isnan(col(column))).count()
    rate = null_count / total
    return QualityCheckResult(
        check_name=f'null_rate_{column}',
        passed=rate <= max_rate,
        severity='ERROR',
        metric_value=round(rate, 4),
        threshold=max_rate,
        message=f'{column} null rate: {rate:.2%} (threshold: {max_rate:.2%})'
    )

def check_value_range(df: DataFrame, column: str,
                      min_val: float, max_val: float) -> QualityCheckResult:
    """All values in column must be within [min_val, max_val]."""
    total = df.count()
    if total == 0:
        return QualityCheckResult(f'range_{column}', True, 'WARN', 0, 0, 'Empty DF')
    violators = df.filter(
        (col(column) < min_val) | (col(column) > max_val)
    ).count()
    rate = violators / total
    return QualityCheckResult(
        check_name=f'range_{column}',
        passed=violators == 0,
        severity='ERROR',
        metric_value=violators,
        threshold=0,
        message=f'{column}: {violators} values outside [{min_val}, {max_val}]'
    )

def check_uniqueness(df: DataFrame, columns: List[str]) -> QualityCheckResult:
    """Combination of columns must be unique."""
    total = df.count()
    distinct = df.dropDuplicates(columns).count()
    duplicates = total - distinct
    return QualityCheckResult(
        check_name=f'unique_{"_".join(columns)}',
        passed=duplicates == 0,
        severity='ERROR',
        metric_value=duplicates,
        threshold=0,
        message=f'{duplicates} duplicate rows on {columns}'
    )

def check_referential_integrity(df: DataFrame, ref_df: DataFrame,
                                 key: str) -> QualityCheckResult:
    """All keys in df must exist in ref_df."""
    orphans = df.join(ref_df.select(key).distinct(), key, 'left_anti').count()
    return QualityCheckResult(
        check_name=f'ref_integrity_{key}',
        passed=orphans == 0,
        severity='WARN',
        metric_value=orphans,
        threshold=0,
        message=f'{orphans} orphan keys in {key}'
    )
Running Checks in the Pipeline

# pipeline.py
from quality_checks import *

def bronze_to_silver(spark, bronze_path, silver_path):
    # Read bronze
    bronze = spark.read.format('delta').load(bronze_path)

    # Transform
    silver = clean_readings(bronze)

    # ----- QUALITY GATE -----
    results = [
        check_not_empty(silver, 'silver_readings'),
        check_null_rate(silver, 'kw', max_rate=0.0),
        check_null_rate(silver, 'meter_id', max_rate=0.0),
        check_value_range(silver, 'kw', 0, 50000),
        check_uniqueness(silver, ['meter_id', 'date']),
    ]

    # Log all results
    for r in results:
        status = 'PASS' if r.passed else f'FAIL [{r.severity}]'
        print(f'  {status}: {r.message}')

    # Block on CRITICAL failures
    critical_failures = [r for r in results if not r.passed and r.severity == 'CRITICAL']
    if critical_failures:
        raise RuntimeError(f'CRITICAL quality checks failed: {critical_failures}')

    # Warn on ERROR failures but continue (or halt, depending on policy)
    error_failures = [r for r in results if not r.passed and r.severity == 'ERROR']
    if error_failures:
        print(f'  WARNING: {len(error_failures)} quality checks failed')
        # Policy decision: raise or continue with alerting

    # Write silver
    silver.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').save(silver_path)

    return results
While hand-written checks work well, dedicated frameworks provide richer functionality, better reporting, and ecosystem integration.
Great Expectations

Great Expectations (GX) is the most widely adopted open-source data quality framework. It provides a declarative API for defining expectations (quality checks) with built-in profiling, documentation, and alerting.

# pip install great-expectations
import great_expectations as gx

# Create a context
context = gx.get_context()

# Connect to a Spark DataFrame
datasource = context.sources.add_or_update_spark(name='spark_source')
data_asset = datasource.add_dataframe_asset(name='silver_readings')

# Build a batch request from your DataFrame
batch_request = data_asset.build_batch_request(dataframe=silver_df)

# Create expectations
validator = context.get_validator(batch_request=batch_request)

# Expect no NULLs in meter_id
validator.expect_column_values_to_not_be_null('meter_id')

# Expect kw to be between 0 and 50000
validator.expect_column_values_to_be_between('kw', min_value=0, max_value=50000)

# Expect customer_type to be in a known set
validator.expect_column_values_to_be_in_set(
    'customer_type', ['RESIDENTIAL', 'COMMERCIAL', 'INDUSTRIAL']
)

# Expect uniqueness
validator.expect_compound_columns_to_be_unique(['meter_id', 'date'])

# Expect row count within range
validator.expect_table_row_count_to_be_between(min_value=1000, max_value=10000000)

# Run all expectations
results = validator.validate()
print(f'Success: {results.success}')

Great Expectations generates human-readable HTML reports ("Data Docs") showing which expectations passed and failed, with detailed statistics. This is invaluable for sharing data quality status with non-engineering stakeholders.
Deequ (Amazon)
Deequ is a data quality library built on top of Spark by Amazon. It is designed for large-scale quality profiling and constraint verification directly on Spark DataFrames.

# Available as a Spark package (Scala-based with Python wrapper)
# pip install pydeequ

from pydeequ.checks import Check, CheckLevel
from pydeequ.verification import VerificationSuite, VerificationResult

check = Check(spark, CheckLevel.Error, 'Silver readings quality') \
    .isComplete('meter_id') \
    .isComplete('kw') \
    .isNonNegative('kw') \
    .isContainedIn('customer_type', ['RESIDENTIAL', 'COMMERCIAL', 'INDUSTRIAL']) \
    .isUnique('meter_id', 'date') \
    .hasSize(lambda x: x > 0)

result = VerificationSuite(spark) \
    .onData(silver_df) \
    .addCheck(check) \
    .run()

result_df = VerificationResult.checkResultsAsDataFrame(spark, result)
result_df.show(truncate=False)
Delta Live Tables Expectations (Databricks)
On Databricks, Delta Live Tables (DLT) provides declarative expectations that are built into the pipeline definition:

DLT Expectation Behavior on Violation Use When @dlt.expect Log violation, keep the row Soft check: track quality metrics @dlt.expect_or_drop Drop violating rows silently Filter invalid data (like our Module 7 quarantine) @dlt.expect_or_fail Halt the pipeline Critical: meter_id must never be null
Framework Comparison

Framework Platform Strengths Best For Hand-written checks Any Full control, no dependencies Simple pipelines, custom logic Great Expectations Any Rich UI, docs, profiling, large community Multi-platform orgs, compliance Deequ (pydeequ) Any Spark Deep Spark integration, anomaly detection Spark-centric, Amazon ecosystem Delta Live Tables Databricks only Declarative, built into pipeline Databricks shops

sections.title: Lesson 8: Quality Metrics Tables — The Audit Trail

blocks.content: Quality checks that only print to logs are checks that get ignored. Production-grade quality systems persist check results into a dedicated metrics table, creating a historical audit trail.
Building the Quality Metrics Table

from pyspark.sql.functions import current_timestamp, lit
from pyspark.sql.types import *

quality_schema = StructType([
    StructField('pipeline_name', StringType()),
    StructField('table_name', StringType()),
    StructField('check_name', StringType()),
    StructField('check_time', TimestampType()),
    StructField('passed', BooleanType()),
    StructField('severity', StringType()),
    StructField('metric_value', DoubleType()),
    StructField('threshold', DoubleType()),
    StructField('message', StringType()),
])

def persist_quality_results(spark, results, pipeline_name, table_name, metrics_path):
    """Write quality check results to a Delta metrics table."""
    rows = [(
        pipeline_name,
        table_name,
        r.check_name,
        None,  # will be overwritten by current_timestamp
        r.passed,
        r.severity,
        float(r.metric_value),
        float(r.threshold),
        r.message,
    ) for r in results]

    metrics_df = spark.createDataFrame(rows, quality_schema) \
        .withColumn('check_time', current_timestamp())

    metrics_df.write \
        .format('delta') \
        .mode('append') \
        .save(metrics_path)
Querying Quality History

# Read the quality metrics table
metrics = spark.read.format('delta').load('/data/quality_metrics/')

# Show all failures in the last 7 days
metrics.filter(
    (col('passed') == False) &
    (col('check_time') >= current_timestamp() - expr('INTERVAL 7 DAYS'))
).orderBy('check_time', ascending=False).show()

# Trend: NULL rate for 'kw' column over time
metrics.filter(
    (col('check_name') == 'null_rate_kw')
).orderBy('check_time').show()

# Failure rate by pipeline over last 30 days
metrics.filter(
    col('check_time') >= current_timestamp() - expr('INTERVAL 30 DAYS')
).groupBy('pipeline_name').agg(
    count('*').alias('total_checks'),
    spark_sum(when(col('passed') == False, 1).otherwise(0)).alias('failed_checks'),
).withColumn('failure_rate',
    col('failed_checks') / col('total_checks')
).orderBy('failure_rate', ascending=False).show()
This metrics table becomes the foundation for quality dashboards, trend analysis, and SLA reporting. When a stakeholder asks "How reliable is the meter readings pipeline?", you have data to answer.

sections.title: Lesson 9: Anomaly Detection — Catching What Rules Miss
