blocks.content: Static rules (kw > 0, NULL rate < 1%) catch known problems. But what about unknown problems? A pipeline that usually produces 2 million rows suddenly produces 200,000. No rule was violated — every individual row is valid. But something is clearly wrong.
Row Count Anomaly Detection

from pyspark.sql.functions import avg, stddev, abs as spark_abs

def check_row_count_anomaly(spark, current_count, metrics_path,
                            pipeline_name, table_name,
                            sigma_threshold=3.0):
    """Alert if current row count deviates significantly from historical norm."""
    # Read historical row counts
    history = spark.read.format('delta').load(metrics_path) \
        .filter(col('pipeline_name') == pipeline_name) \
        .filter(col('table_name') == table_name) \
        .filter(col('check_name') == 'row_count') \
        .filter(col('passed') == True) \
        .orderBy(col('check_time').desc()) \
        .limit(30)  # Last 30 runs

    stats = history.agg(
        avg('metric_value').alias('mean'),
        stddev('metric_value').alias('std'),
    ).collect()[0]
if stats['std'] is None or stats['std'] == 0: return QualityCheckResult( 'row_count_anomaly', True, 'WARN', current_count, 0, 'Insufficient history for anomaly detection' )

z_score = abs(current_count - stats['mean']) / stats['std']
    passed = z_score < sigma_threshold

    return QualityCheckResult(
        check_name='row_count_anomaly',
        passed=passed,
        severity='ERROR',
        metric_value=current_count,
        threshold=stats['mean'],
        message=f'Row count {current_count:,.0f} (z-score: {z_score:.1f}, '
                f'mean: {stats["mean"]:,.0f}, std: {stats["std"]:,.0f})'
    )
Freshness Checks
Data freshness answers: "Is this table up to date?" A table with perfect quality is useless if the pipeline stopped running two days ago.

from pyspark.sql.functions import max as spark_max, datediff, current_date

def check_freshness(df, timestamp_column, max_staleness_hours=24):
    """Verify that the most recent record is within acceptable staleness."""
    latest = df.agg(spark_max(col(timestamp_column))).collect()[0][0]
if latest is None: return QualityCheckResult( 'freshness', False, 'CRITICAL', 0, max_staleness_hours, f'No data found in {timestamp_column}' )

from datetime import datetime
    hours_stale = (datetime.now() - latest).total_seconds() / 3600
    passed = hours_stale <= max_staleness_hours

    return QualityCheckResult(
        check_name='freshness',
        passed=passed,
        severity='CRITICAL',
        metric_value=round(hours_stale, 1),
        threshold=max_staleness_hours,
        message=f'Latest record: {hours_stale:.1f}h ago (max: {max_staleness_hours}h)'
    )
Volume Consistency Across Layers

def check_layer_consistency(bronze_df, silver_df, gold_df, date_filter):
    """Verify row counts are consistent across Medallion layers."""
    bronze_count = bronze_df.filter(col('date') == date_filter).count()
    silver_count = silver_df.filter(col('date') == date_filter).count()

    # Silver should be <= bronze (cleaning removes rows)
    # Silver should be >= 80% of bronze (if less, something is wrong)
    ratio = silver_count / bronze_count if bronze_count > 0 else 0
    passed = 0.80 <= ratio <= 1.0

    return QualityCheckResult(
        check_name='layer_consistency',
        passed=passed,
        severity='ERROR',
        metric_value=round(ratio, 3),
        threshold=0.80,
        message=f'Silver/Bronze ratio: {ratio:.1%} '
                f'(bronze: {bronze_count:,}, silver: {silver_count:,})'
    )

sections.title: Lesson 10: Observability — Seeing Your Pipelines in Production

blocks.content: Testing proves correctness before deployment. Quality checks validate data during execution. Observability provides continuous visibility into pipeline health after deployment.
The Three Pillars of Pipeline Observability

Pillar What It Provides Example Metrics Numeric measurements over time Row count per run, NULL rate trend, latency Logs Timestamped events and details Pipeline started, 5 checks passed, 1 check failed Alerts Proactive notification on anomalies Slack message: 'Silver NULL rate exceeded 5%'
Structured Logging
log_pipeline_event('PIPELINE_COMPLETE', 'bronze_to_silver', { 'rows_written': silver_count, 'duration_seconds': elapsed, })
Structured JSON logs can be ingested by any log aggregation system (Datadog, Splunk, CloudWatch, ELK) for searching, dashboarding, and alerting.
Alerting Patterns

def send_quality_alert(results, pipeline_name, channel='#data-alerts'):
    """Send alerts for failed quality checks."""
    failures = [r for r in results if not r.passed]
    if not failures:
        return

    message = f':warning: *Quality Alert: {pipeline_name}*\n'
    for f in failures:
        emoji = ':red_circle:' if f.severity == 'CRITICAL' else ':orange_circle:'
        message += f'{emoji} [{f.severity}] {f.message}\n'

    # Send via webhook (Slack, Teams, PagerDuty, etc.)
    # requests.post(webhook_url, json={'text': message})
    print(message)  # Placeholder for actual webhook call
The Observability Dashboard
A quality metrics Delta table (Part 7) can power a dashboard that shows:

items: Pipeline execution history — When did each pipeline last run? How long did it take?
Quality trend lines — NULL rate, duplicate rate, and orphan count over time
Freshness indicators — How stale is each table? Is any table falling behind?
Anomaly alerts — Row count deviations, sudden quality drops
Layer consistency — Bronze-to-silver and silver-to-gold volume ratios

blocks.content: Quality Dashboard (conceptual):
Streaming pipelines present unique testing challenges: they run continuously, process data incrementally, and maintain state. Here are patterns for testing them effectively.
Using Memory Sink for Testing

def test_streaming_filter(spark):
    """Test a streaming pipeline using memory sink."""
    from pyspark.sql.types import *

    # Create test data as a static DataFrame
    test_data = spark.createDataFrame([
        ('M001', 100.0), ('M002', -50.0), ('M003', 200.0),
    ], ['meter_id', 'kw'])

    # Write to a temp directory as source
    import tempfile, os
    tmp = tempfile.mkdtemp()
    test_data.write.json(os.path.join(tmp, 'batch1'))

    # Read as stream
    schema = test_data.schema
    stream = spark.readStream.schema(schema).json(tmp)

    # Apply transformations
    filtered = stream.filter(col('kw') >= 0)

    # Write to memory sink
    query = filtered.writeStream \
        .outputMode('append') \
        .format('memory') \
        .queryName('test_output') \
        .trigger(once=True) \
        .start()
    query.awaitTermination()

    # Validate results
    result = spark.sql('SELECT * FROM test_output')
    assert result.count() == 2  # M001 and M003 (M002 filtered out)

    import shutil
    shutil.rmtree(tmp)
The trigger(once=True) pattern is essential for testing: it processes all available data in one batch and then stops, making the stream behave like a batch job for test purposes.
Testing foreachBatch Logic

def test_foreach_batch_upsert(spark):
    """Test the foreachBatch MERGE function with batch DataFrames."""
    import tempfile, shutil
    tmp = tempfile.mkdtemp()
    target_path = os.path.join(tmp, 'target')

    try:
        # Setup: create initial target table
        initial = spark.createDataFrame([
            ('M001', '2026-02-25', 100.0),
        ], ['meter_id', 'date', 'kw'])
        initial.write.format('delta').save(target_path)

        # Simulate a micro-batch with an update and an insert
        batch = spark.createDataFrame([
            ('M001', '2026-02-25', 110.0),  # update
            ('M002', '2026-02-25', 200.0),  # insert
        ], ['meter_id', 'date', 'kw'])

        # Call the foreachBatch function directly (no streaming needed)
        from pipeline import upsert_to_delta
        upsert_to_delta(batch, 0)  # batch_id = 0

        # Validate
        result = spark.read.format('delta').load(target_path)
        assert result.count() == 2

        m001 = result.filter(col('meter_id') == 'M001').collect()[0]
        assert m001['kw'] == 110.0  # Updated
    finally:
        shutil.rmtree(tmp)
Key insight: The foreachBatch function takes a regular batch DataFrame. You can test it without streaming at all — just call it directly with test data.
Tests are only valuable if they run automatically. A CI/CD pipeline ensures that every code change is tested before it reaches production.
The Pipeline Testing Workflow
CI/CD Workflow for Data Pipelines:

[Developer]
       |
       | git push (feature branch)
       v
  [CI Server] (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins)
       |
       | 1. Install dependencies (pyspark, pytest, delta)
       | 2. Run unit tests (no Spark, < 10 seconds)
       | 3. Run integration tests (local Spark, < 5 minutes)
       | 4. Run linting (flake8, mypy)
       |
       +-- All pass? --> Merge to main
       |                      |
       +-- Any fail? --> Block merge, notify developer
                              |
                         [CD Pipeline]
                              |
                    Deploy to staging
                              |
                    Run E2E tests on staging data
                              |
                    Deploy to production
Example: GitHub Actions Configuration

# .github/workflows/test.yml
name: PySpark Pipeline Tests
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs: test: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-python@v5 with: python-version: '3.11' - uses: actions/setup-java@v4 with: distribution: 'temurin' java-version: '17' - name: Install dependencies run: | pip install pyspark==3.5.* pytest delta-spark - name: Run unit tests run: pytest tests/unit/ -v - name: Run integration tests run: pytest tests/integration/ -v
Test Organization
Recommended Project Structure:

smart_grid_pipeline/
    src/
      pipeline.py           Pipeline entry point
      transformations.py    DataFrame transformation functions
      business_logic.py     Pure Python business logic
      quality_checks.py     Quality check functions
      udfs/
        energy.py           UDF definitions
    tests/
      conftest.py           Shared SparkSession fixture
      unit/
        test_business_logic.py
        test_udfs.py
      integration/
        test_transformations.py
        test_aggregations.py
        test_joins.py
      e2e/
        test_bronze_to_silver.py
        test_silver_to_gold.py
    .github/workflows/test.yml
Here is how all the pieces fit together into a production quality system:
The Quality Lifecycle
Quality at Every Stage:

DEVELOP          BUILD              RUN              MONITOR
  --------         --------           --------         --------
  Unit tests       CI pipeline        Quality checks   Quality metrics
  Integration      Automated          Anomaly          Freshness alerts
  tests            test gate          detection        Trend dashboards
  Code review      Lint + type        Quarantine       SLA tracking
                   check              bad data         Incident response
                                      Structured logs  Postmortems

  BEFORE deployment                   DURING execution
  (prove correctness)                 (validate data & alert on failure)
Quality Check Placement in Medallion Architecture

Layer
Checks to Apply
Failure Action
Bronze (raw ingest)
Schema validation, not empty, freshness
CRITICAL: halt ingest if source is broken
Bronze → Silver
NULL rates, value ranges, deduplication, type casting validation
ERROR: quarantine bad records, continue with clean data
Silver
Referential integrity, uniqueness, row count anomaly
ERROR: alert team, investigate before gold
Silver → Gold
Aggregation totals match, no unmatched keys
ERROR: halt gold write until investigated
Gold
Freshness, business metric ranges, trend anomaly
CRITICAL: alert stakeholders, flag dashboard
The Maturity Model

Level Name Practices Outcome 0 No testing Hope nothing breaks Frequent silent failures 1 Manual checks Eyeball row counts in notebooks Catches some issues, misses most 2 Unit + integration tests Automated tests in CI, basic quality checks Catches code bugs before deployment 3 Embedded quality gates Quality checks in every pipeline, quarantine, alerts Silent failures become loud alerts 4 Full observability Metrics tables, anomaly detection, freshness monitoring, dashboards Proactive: issues detected before stakeholders notice 5 Data contracts Producer-consumer SLAs, automated governance, self-healing pipelines Data quality is an organizational discipline
Most organizations operate at Level 1 or 2. This module equips you to build Level 3–4 systems. Level 5 requires organizational alignment beyond engineering.
You now understand:

items: Why silent failures are more dangerous than crashes, and the goal of making failures loud and early
The testing pyramid for data pipelines: unit tests (fast, no Spark) → integration tests (SparkSession, local mode) → end-to-end tests (full pipeline with Delta)
How to write unit tests for business logic and UDFs with pytest, covering NULLs, boundaries, and edge cases
How to write integration tests with a shared SparkSession fixture, testing transformations, aggregations, and joins
How to write end-to-end tests using temp directories and Delta tables to validate complete pipelines
The assert_dataframes_equal pattern for comparing actual vs expected results
How to build a reusable quality check framework with check functions for emptiness, NULL rates, value ranges, uniqueness, and referential integrity
How to embed quality gates in pipelines with severity levels (WARN, ERROR, CRITICAL) and configurable failure actions
Quality frameworks: Great Expectations, Deequ, and Delta Live Tables expectations
How to build a quality metrics Delta table for historical tracking and trend analysis
Anomaly detection for row counts, freshness monitoring, and cross-layer volume consistency
The three pillars of observability: metrics, structured logs, and alerts
How to integrate tests into CI/CD with GitHub Actions, recommended project structure
The quality check placement across the Medallion Architecture and the quality maturity model from Level 0 to Level 5

blocks.content: You are no longer building pipelines that you hope produce correct results. You are building pipelines that prove they produce correct results — and scream when they don’t.
In Module 13, we turn to Production Deployment and Orchestration — how to schedule, coordinate, and operate multi-pipeline data platforms at scale.

chapters.title: Module 13: Production Deployment & Orchestration
chapters.description: Running Data Platforms at Scale Learn how to package PySpark applications for deployment, schedule and orchestrate multi-pipeline workflows with Apache Airflow, manage environments and dependencies, implement deployment strategies for zero-downtime releases, and operate production Spark workloads on managed platforms.

blocks.content: You can now build, test, and validate distributed data pipelines. You can process bounded and unbounded data, store it reliably in Delta Lake, and prove correctness through automated tests and quality gates.
But none of that matters if the pipeline only runs when you press Enter in a notebook.

A production data pipeline runs without human intervention. It starts on schedule or on trigger. It handles failures gracefully. It recovers from crashes. It scales under load. It is deployed through a repeatable process, not by someone copying files to a server.
This is the gap between a prototype and a production system. This module closes that gap.
The Production Gap:

NOTEBOOK (prototype)          PRODUCTION (real system)
  ----------------------        ----------------------
  Run cells manually            Scheduled automatically
  One pipeline, one file        Dozens of interdependent pipelines
  Developer watches it          No one watches it (alerts do)
  Fails? Re-run the cell        Fails? Auto-retry, alert, recover
  "Works on my laptop"          Works on any cluster, any time
  Dependencies? pip install     Dependencies? Versioned, locked, tested
  Deploy? Copy the notebook     Deploy? CI/CD, staged rollout, rollback
This module covers the full production lifecycle: packaging your code, scheduling with orchestrators, managing dependencies, deploying safely, and operating at scale.
The first step toward production is separating your code from the notebook. Production PySpark runs as packaged applications — structured Python projects submitted to Spark clusters.
From Notebook to Project
Notebook (prototype): Production Project:

notebook.ipynb                smart_grid_pipeline/
    Cell 1: imports               src/
    Cell 2: read data               __init__.py
    Cell 3: clean                    main.py          (entry point)
    Cell 4: aggregate                transformations.py
    Cell 5: write                    quality_checks.py
    Cell 6: show results             config.py
                                   tests/
  One file, linear,               setup.py or pyproject.toml
  hard to test,                   requirements.txt
  hard to version.                Makefile or Dockerfile
The Entry Point: main.py

# src/main.py
"""Smart Grid Pipeline — Bronze to Silver."""
import argparse
import sys
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from transformations import clean_readings
from quality_checks import run_quality_gate
from config import get_config

def main():
    # Parse CLI arguments
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Smart Grid Pipeline')
    parser.add_argument('--env', required=True, choices=['dev', 'staging', 'prod'])
    parser.add_argument('--date', required=True, help='Processing date YYYY-MM-DD')
    args = parser.parse_args()

    # Load environment-specific config
    config = get_config(args.env)

    # Initialize Spark
    spark = SparkSession.builder \
        .appName(f'smart_grid_bronze_to_silver_{args.date}') \
        .getOrCreate()

    try:
        # Read bronze
        bronze = spark.read.format('delta').load(config.bronze_path)
        bronze = bronze.filter(f"date = '{args.date}'")

        # Transform
        silver = clean_readings(bronze)

        # Quality gate
        run_quality_gate(silver, config.quality_metrics_path,
                         pipeline_name='bronze_to_silver')

        # Write silver
        silver.write \
            .format('delta') \
            .mode('overwrite') \
            .option('replaceWhere', f"date = '{args.date}'") \
            .save(config.silver_path)
print(f'SUCCESS: Wrote {silver.count()} rows for {args.date}')

except Exception as e:
        print(f'FAILURE: {e}', file=sys.stderr)
        raise

    finally:
        spark.stop()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
Environment Configuration

# src/config.py
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class PipelineConfig:
    bronze_path: str
    silver_path: str
    gold_path: str
    quality_metrics_path: str
    checkpoint_path: str

CONFIGS = {
    'dev': PipelineConfig(
        bronze_path='/data/dev/bronze/meter_readings/',
        silver_path='/data/dev/silver/meter_readings/',
        gold_path='/data/dev/gold/consumption_summary/',
        quality_metrics_path='/data/dev/quality_metrics/',
        checkpoint_path='/checkpoints/dev/',
    ),
    'staging': PipelineConfig(
        bronze_path='/data/staging/bronze/meter_readings/',
        silver_path='/data/staging/silver/meter_readings/',
        gold_path='/data/staging/gold/consumption_summary/',
        quality_metrics_path='/data/staging/quality_metrics/',
        checkpoint_path='/checkpoints/staging/',
    ),
    'prod': PipelineConfig(
        bronze_path='/data/prod/bronze/meter_readings/',
        silver_path='/data/prod/silver/meter_readings/',
        gold_path='/data/prod/gold/consumption_summary/',
        quality_metrics_path='/data/prod/quality_metrics/',
        checkpoint_path='/checkpoints/prod/',
    ),
}

def get_config(env: str) -> PipelineConfig:
    if env not in CONFIGS:
        raise ValueError(f'Unknown environment: {env}')
    return CONFIGS[env]
Submitting to a Cluster

# spark-submit: the standard way to submit Spark applications

# Local mode (testing)
spark-submit \
    --master local[4] \
    src/main.py --env dev --date 2026-02-25

# YARN cluster (production)
spark-submit \
    --master yarn \
    --deploy-mode cluster \
    --num-executors 20 \
    --executor-memory 8g \
    --executor-cores 4 \
    --driver-memory 4g \
    --py-files dist/smart_grid_pipeline-1.0.0.zip \
    src/main.py --env prod --date 2026-02-25

spark-submit Key Parameters

Parameter
Purpose
Example
--master
Cluster manager URL
yarn, k8s://host, local[4]
--deploy-mode
Where the Driver runs
client (local Driver), cluster (remote Driver)
--num-executors
Number of executor processes
20
--executor-memory
RAM per executor
8g
--executor-cores
CPU cores per executor
4
--driver-memory
RAM for the Driver process
4g
--py-files
Python dependencies (zip, egg, .py)
dist/pipeline.zip
--packages
Maven packages to include
io.delta:delta-spark_2.12:3.1.0
--conf
Spark configuration overrides
spark.sql.shuffle.partitions=400
Packaging Python Code for --py-files

# Option 1: ZIP the source directory
cd smart_grid_pipeline/
zip -r dist/smart_grid_pipeline.zip src/

# Option 2: Build a wheel (standard Python packaging)
pip install build
python -m build
# Produces: dist/smart_grid_pipeline-1.0.0-py3-none-any.whl

# Submit with the wheel
spark-submit \
    --py-files dist/smart_grid_pipeline-1.0.0-py3-none-any.whl \
    src/main.py --env prod --date 2026-02-25

sections.title: Lesson 3: Client Mode vs Cluster Mode

blocks.content: When you submit a Spark application, the deploy mode determines where the Driver process runs. This affects failure behavior, logging, and resource management.
Client Mode
Client Mode (--deploy-mode client):

+------------------+
  | Your Machine     |
  |   [DRIVER]       |  <-- Driver runs HERE (on the submitting machine)
  +--------+---------+
           |
     +-----+------+------+
     |     |      |      |
  [Exec] [Exec] [Exec] [Exec]   (on cluster nodes)

  Pros: stdout/stderr visible locally, easy debugging
  Cons: if your machine disconnects, the job dies
  Use:  development, interactive debugging
Cluster Mode
Cluster Mode (--deploy-mode cluster):

+------------------+
  | Your Machine     |
  |  (submit & exit) |  <-- Your machine is FREE after submission
  +--------+---------+
           |
           v
  +------------------+
  | Cluster Node     |
  |   [DRIVER]       |  <-- Driver runs on a cluster node
  +--------+---------+
           |
     +-----+------+------+
     |     |      |      |
  [Exec] [Exec] [Exec] [Exec]
Pros: job survives if your machine disconnects Cons: logs only available through cluster manager (YARN UI, K8s logs) Use: production, scheduled jobs

Aspect Client Mode Cluster Mode Driver location Submitting machine Cluster node Submit machine dependency Must stay connected Can disconnect after submit Log access Direct stdout/stderr Via cluster manager (YARN, K8s) Network to executors Submitter → cluster (potential bottleneck) All intra-cluster (optimal) Use case Development, debugging Production, scheduled jobs
Rule: Always use cluster mode for production jobs. Client mode is for development only.
One of the most frustrating production problems is: "It works on my laptop but fails on the cluster because library X is missing." Dependency management solves this.
The Dependency Challenge
The Problem:

Developer's Laptop:        Cluster Executors:
  Python 3.11                Python 3.9 (different!)
  pandas 2.1.0               pandas 1.5.3 (different!)
  numpy 1.26                 numpy 1.24 (different!)
  great-expectations 0.18    (not installed!)

  Your UDF imports pandas 2.x API --> crashes on executor.
  Your quality check imports GX   --> ModuleNotFoundError.
Strategy 1: --py-files (Simple Dependencies)
For pure Python modules with no C extensions, zip them and ship with the job:

# Install dependencies locally, then zip
pip install -t deps/ great-expectations pydeequ
cd deps/ && zip -r ../deps.zip . && cd ..

spark-submit \
    --py-files deps.zip,dist/smart_grid_pipeline.zip \
    src/main.py --env prod --date 2026-02-25
Strategy 2: Conda/Virtualenv Archive (Complex Dependencies)
For libraries with C extensions (numpy, pandas, scikit-learn), ship a full environment:

# Create a conda environment and pack it
conda create -n spark_env python=3.11 numpy pandas scikit-learn -y
conda activate spark_env
pip install great-expectations
conda pack -n spark_env -o spark_env.tar.gz

# Submit with the packed environment
spark-submit \
    --master yarn \
    --deploy-mode cluster \
    --archives spark_env.tar.gz#environment \
    --conf spark.yarn.appMasterEnv.PYSPARK_PYTHON=./environment/bin/python \
    src/main.py --env prod --date 2026-02-25
Strategy 3: Docker/Container Images (Kubernetes)

# Dockerfile for Spark on Kubernetes
FROM spark:3.5.0-python3

# Install Python dependencies
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy application code
COPY src/ /opt/spark/work-dir/src/
ENTRYPOINT ["/opt/entrypoint.sh"]

# Submit to Kubernetes
spark-submit \
    --master k8s://https://k8s-api:6443 \
    --deploy-mode cluster \
    --conf spark.kubernetes.container.image=registry/spark-pipeline:1.0.0 \
    --conf spark.kubernetes.namespace=data-pipelines \
    local:///opt/spark/work-dir/src/main.py --env prod --date 2026-02-25
Strategy 4: Platform-Managed (Databricks)

# Databricks: dependencies defined in cluster configuration
# Option A: Cluster libraries (installed on all nodes)
#   Cluster > Libraries > Install New > PyPI: great-expectations

# Option B: Notebook-scoped (ad hoc, not recommended for production)
%pip install great-expectations

# Option C: Init script (runs on cluster startup)
# dbfs:/init-scripts/install_deps.sh
#!/bin/bash
pip install great-expectations==0.18.0 pydeequ==1.1.0
Dependency Strategy Comparison

Strategy Complexity C Extensions? Best For --py-files (zip) Low No Simple, pure-Python deps conda pack (archive) Medium Yes YARN clusters with native libs Docker image Medium-High Yes Kubernetes deployments Platform-managed Low Yes Databricks, EMR, Dataproc requirements.txt in image Medium Yes Custom cluster images (AMI, GCE)
Critical rule: Pin exact versions in requirements.txt (numpy==1.26.4, not numpy>=1.26). Unpinned dependencies cause "worked yesterday, broken today" failures when a new version is released.

sections.title: Lesson 5: Orchestration — Why Schedulers Are Not Enough

blocks.content: A single pipeline running on a cron schedule is simple. But real data platforms have dozens of interdependent pipelines:
Real-World Pipeline Dependencies:

[Ingest Meters]     [Ingest Weather]     [Ingest Metadata]
       |                    |                     |
       v                    v                     v
  [Clean Meters]      [Clean Weather]       [Load Metadata]
       |                    |                     |
       +--------+-----------+                     |
                |                                 |
                v                                 |
        [Join Readings + Weather]                 |
                |                                 |
                +---------------------------------+
                |
                v
        [Enrich with Metadata]
                |
                v
        [Compute Gold Aggregations]
                |
          +-----+-----+
          |           |
          v           v
   [Dashboard]   [Report Export]

A cron schedule cannot express: "Run Clean Meters only after Ingest Meters succeeds." It cannot handle: "If Ingest Weather fails, skip Join but still run Dashboard with yesterday’s data." It cannot manage: "Retry Clean Meters 3 times with exponential backoff before alerting."
This is why you need an orchestrator — a system that manages the scheduling, dependencies, retries, and monitoring of multi-pipeline workflows.
Orchestration vs Scheduling

Capability Cron Orchestrator Time-based scheduling Yes Yes Dependency management No Yes (DAGs) Retry with backoff No Yes (configurable) Conditional branching No Yes (if task A fails, skip B) Monitoring UI No Yes (task status, logs, history) Backfill historical runs No Yes (reprocess past dates) Alerting on failure Manual Built-in (email, Slack, PagerDuty) Parameterization Limited Yes (date, env, config per run) Cross-pipeline dependencies No Yes (sensors, triggers) Audit trail No Yes (run history, logs, duration)

sections.title: Lesson 6: Apache Airflow — The Industry Standard Orchestrator

blocks.content: Apache Airflow is the most widely adopted open-source orchestration platform for data pipelines. It was created at Airbnb in 2014 and is now an Apache top-level project used by thousands of companies.
Core Concepts

Concept Definition Analogy DAG Directed Acyclic Graph of tasks A pipeline blueprint Task A single unit of work One spark-submit, one quality check Operator Template for a type of task SparkSubmitOperator, PythonOperator Execution Date The logical date the pipeline processes "Process data for 2026-02-25" DAG Run One execution of a DAG for a specific date "The Feb 25 run of bronze_to_silver" Sensor Task that waits for a condition Wait for a file to appear XCom Cross-task communication Pass row count from task A to task B Connection Stored credentials for external systems Spark cluster, S3, Slack webhook
Your First Airflow DAG

# dags/smart_grid_daily.py
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.providers.apache.spark.operators.spark_submit import SparkSubmitOperator
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from airflow.operators.dummy import DummyOperator

default_args = {
    'owner': 'data-engineering',
    'depends_on_past': False,
    'email_on_failure': True,
    'email': ['data-team@company.com'],
    'retries': 2,
    'retry_delay': timedelta(minutes=5),
    'retry_exponential_backoff': True,
}

with DAG(
    dag_id='smart_grid_daily',
    default_args=default_args,
    description='Daily Smart Grid Medallion Pipeline',
    schedule_interval='0 6 * * *',  # 6 AM daily
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['smart-grid', 'production'],
) as dag:

    start = DummyOperator(task_id='start')

    ingest_meters = SparkSubmitOperator(
        task_id='ingest_meters',
        application='/pipelines/ingest/meters.py',
        conn_id='spark_cluster',
        application_args=['--env', 'prod', '--date', '{{ ds }}'],
        num_executors=10,
        executor_memory='8g',
        executor_cores=4,
    )

    clean_meters = SparkSubmitOperator(
        task_id='clean_meters',
        application='/pipelines/clean/meters.py',
        conn_id='spark_cluster',
        application_args=['--env', 'prod', '--date', '{{ ds }}'],
        num_executors=20,
        executor_memory='8g',
        executor_cores=4,
    )

    quality_check = PythonOperator(
        task_id='silver_quality_check',
        python_callable=run_silver_quality_check,
        op_kwargs={'date': '{{ ds }}'},
    )

    build_gold = SparkSubmitOperator(
        task_id='build_gold',
        application='/pipelines/gold/consumption_summary.py',
        conn_id='spark_cluster',
        application_args=['--env', 'prod', '--date', '{{ ds }}'],
        num_executors=30,
        executor_memory='16g',
        executor_cores=4,
    )

    end = DummyOperator(task_id='end')

    # Define dependencies
    start >> ingest_meters >> clean_meters >> quality_check >> build_gold >> end
The {{ ds }} Template Variable
{{ ds }} is Airflow’s most important template variable. It contains the execution date in YYYY-MM-DD format.
When the DAG runs on February 26 for the schedule of February 25, {{ ds }} = '2026-02-25'. This is the logical date — the date the data pertains to, not the date the DAG actually runs.

Variable Value (for Feb 25 run) Use Case {{ ds }} 2026-02-25 Partition filter: date = '{{ ds }}' {{ ds_nodash }} 20260225 File names: readings_{{ ds_nodash }}.parquet {{ data_interval_start }} 2026-02-25T00:00:00 Timestamp-based filters {{ data_interval_end }} 2026-02-26T00:00:00 Upper bound for time ranges {{ prev_ds }} 2026-02-24 Previous day’s partition

Idempotency: By using {{ ds }} to filter data, each DAG run processes exactly one day. Running the same DAG again for the same date produces the same result. This makes pipelines safe to re-run and backfill.
Parallel Fan-Out and Fan-In

# Multiple independent ingestion tasks run in parallel
    ingest_meters = SparkSubmitOperator(task_id='ingest_meters', ...)
    ingest_weather = SparkSubmitOperator(task_id='ingest_weather', ...)
    ingest_metadata = SparkSubmitOperator(task_id='ingest_metadata', ...)

    # Join task waits for ALL ingestions to complete
    join_data = SparkSubmitOperator(task_id='join_readings_weather', ...)

    # Fan-out then fan-in
    start >> [ingest_meters, ingest_weather, ingest_metadata] >> join_data >> end
Fan-Out / Fan-In:

start
              / | \
             /  |  \
     meters  weather  metadata    (parallel)
             \  |  /
              \ | /
            join_data              (waits for all three)
               |
              end
Sensors: Waiting for External Conditions

from airflow.sensors.filesystem import FileSensor
from airflow.providers.amazon.aws.sensors.s3 import S3KeySensor

# Wait for the meter data file to arrive before processing
wait_for_data = S3KeySensor(
    task_id='wait_for_meter_data',
    bucket_name='smart-grid-raw',
    bucket_key='meter_readings/{{ ds }}/data.parquet',
    timeout=3600,          # Wait up to 1 hour
    poke_interval=300,     # Check every 5 minutes
    mode='reschedule',     # Free up worker slot while waiting
)
wait_for_data >> ingest_meters >> clean_meters
Sensors prevent pipelines from failing when upstream data arrives late. Instead of crashing, the pipeline waits until the data is available.
Conditional Branching
clean_meters >> quality_check >> branch branch >> build_gold >> end branch >> alert_team >> end
Backfilling Historical Data

# Backfill the last 30 days
airflow dags backfill \
    --start-date 2026-01-25 \
    --end-date 2026-02-25 \
    smart_grid_daily

Airflow creates one DAG Run per execution date, each processing its own date’s data via {{ ds }}. This is why idempotency matters — backfilling reruns the pipeline for past dates, and each run must produce correct, repeatable results.
Dynamic Task Generation

# Generate one task per region dynamically
regions = ['northeast', 'southeast', 'midwest', 'west']

regional_tasks = []
for region in regions:
    task = SparkSubmitOperator(
        task_id=f'process_{region}',
        application='/pipelines/regional_aggregation.py',
        application_args=['--env', 'prod', '--date', '{{ ds }}',
                          '--region', region],
        conn_id='spark_cluster',
    )
    regional_tasks.append(task)
clean_meters >> regional_tasks >> build_gold
Airflow is the industry standard, but alternatives exist for specific use cases:

Orchestrator Creator Strengths Best For Apache Airflow Airbnb / Apache Largest ecosystem, most operators, huge community General-purpose orchestration Databricks Workflows Databricks Native Spark integration, no separate infrastructure Databricks-centric organizations Prefect Prefect Python-native, simpler than Airflow, modern API Python-first teams wanting simplicity Dagster Dagster Labs Asset-based (data-aware), type safety, dev experience Data platforms with complex lineage AWS Step Functions Amazon Serverless, deep AWS integration AWS-centric, event-driven workflows Google Cloud Composer Google Managed Airflow on GCP GCP shops wanting managed Airflow MWAA Amazon Managed Airflow on AWS AWS shops wanting managed Airflow
Databricks Workflows
If your entire platform runs on Databricks, Workflows provides native orchestration without external infrastructure:

Databricks Workflows is simpler than Airflow for Databricks-native shops: no separate Airflow deployment, native cluster management, and integrated monitoring. However, it is limited to Databricks and less flexible for non-Spark tasks.

sections.title: Lesson 9: Deployment Strategies — Getting Code to Production Safely

blocks.content: Deploying new pipeline code to production is one of the riskiest operations in data engineering. A bad deployment can corrupt tables, produce wrong results, or crash downstream systems. Deployment strategies minimize this risk.
The Deployment Pipeline
Code to Production:

[Developer] --> [Git Push] --> [CI: Tests Pass]
                                      |
                                      v
                                [Build Artifact]
                                (wheel, zip, Docker image)
                                      |
                       +--------------+--------------+
                       |              |              |
                       v              v              v
                    [Dev]         [Staging]       [Prod]
                  Run on test    Run on staging   Run on
                  data           data (copy of    production
                                 prod)            data
                                      |
                                 Validate output
                                 Compare with prod
                                      |
                                 Promote to prod
Strategy 1: Blue-Green Deployment
Blue-Green Deployment:

[Blue (current v1.2)] ----> [Reads/Writes active tables]
                               ^
                               | switch after validation
  [Green (new v1.3)]   ----> [Writes to staging tables]
                               |
                          Validate green output
                          If good: swap blue <-> green
                          If bad:  discard green, blue stays
Two identical environments run simultaneously. The new version writes to staging tables. After validation, traffic is switched. If the new version has problems, switch back instantly.
Strategy 2: Shadow/Parallel Execution

# Run both old and new pipeline; compare outputs

# Old pipeline writes to production tables (unchanged)
spark-submit old_pipeline.py --env prod --date {{ ds }}

# New pipeline writes to shadow tables
spark-submit new_pipeline.py --env prod-shadow --date {{ ds }}

# Compare outputs
prod_output = spark.read.format('delta').load('/data/prod/silver/')
shadow_output = spark.read.format('delta').load('/data/prod-shadow/silver/')

# Row count comparison
assert abs(prod_output.count() - shadow_output.count()) / prod_output.count() < 0.01

# Schema match
assert prod_output.schema == shadow_output.schema
Run the new pipeline in parallel with the old one, writing to separate tables. Compare outputs. Only promote the new version when results match.
Strategy 3: Canary Deployment
Deploy the new version to process a small subset of data (one region, one customer type) while the old version handles the rest. Gradually increase the new version’s scope.
Strategy 4: Feature Flags
Rollback
Every deployment strategy must have a rollback plan:

What to Rollback How Recovery Time Pipeline code Redeploy previous artifact version Minutes (CI/CD) Delta table data RESTORE TABLE TO VERSION AS OF n Minutes (Delta time travel) Airflow DAG Revert DAG file to previous git commit Minutes Cluster config Revert config to previous version Minutes Schema change ALTER TABLE or RESTORE Minutes to hours
Delta Lake time travel (Module 11) is your safety net. Even if bad code writes bad data, you can RESTORE the table to its pre-deployment state.

sections.title: Lesson 10: Cluster Sizing and Cost Management

blocks.content: Production Spark runs on cloud infrastructure, and cloud infrastructure costs money. Cluster sizing directly impacts both performance and cost.
The Sizing Decision
Cluster Sizing Variables:

DATA SIZE      PROCESSING       CLUSTER SIZE       COST
  (100 GB)   x   COMPLEXITY   =   (what you need)  = ($$$)
              (joins, MLlib,
               aggregations)
Too small: jobs fail (OOM) or take hours Too large: money wasted on idle resources Right-sized: fast, reliable, cost-effective
Sizing Rules of Thumb

Parameter Starting Point Adjust Based On Number of executors Total data size / 1 GB per executor CPU utilization, task duration Executor memory 4–8 GB for simple ETL, 16–32 GB for joins/ML Spill metrics, OOM errors Executor cores 4 cores per executor (standard) Task parallelism needs Driver memory 2–4 GB for batch, 8–16 GB for complex plans Driver OOM, collect() usage Shuffle partitions 2–3x total executor cores Shuffle metrics, task duration
Cost Optimization Strategies

Strategy Savings Trade-off Spot/Preemptible instances for executors 60–90% per executor Executors can be reclaimed (Spark handles with retry) Auto-scaling clusters Pay only for what you use Cold start latency when scaling up Ephemeral clusters (spin up, process, tear down) No idle cost between jobs Startup time (1–5 minutes per cluster) Right-size executors (avoid over-provisioning) 20–50% reduction Requires monitoring and tuning Optimize queries first (filter early, broadcast) 2–10x faster = 2–10x cheaper Engineering effort Use Delta OPTIMIZE + ZORDER Faster reads = shorter cluster time Maintenance overhead Schedule off-peak (nights/weekends) 10–30% discount on some platforms Results arrive later
The Spot Instance Pattern

# EMR: Use Spot Instances for executors, On-Demand for Driver
spark-submit \
    --master yarn \
    --deploy-mode cluster \
    --conf spark.yarn.am.resource.type=ON_DEMAND \   # Driver: stable
    --conf spark.executor.resource.type=SPOT \        # Executors: cheap
    --conf spark.speculation=true \                   # Re-launch slow tasks
    --num-executors 50 \
    src/main.py --env prod --date 2026-02-25

Driver on On-Demand: The Driver is a single point of failure. Losing it kills the job. Use stable, on-demand instances. Executors on Spot: Executors are expendable. If a Spot instance is reclaimed, Spark re-runs the lost tasks on remaining executors. Enable speculation to pre-emptively duplicate slow tasks.
Databricks
Amazon EMR

# AWS CLI: submit a step to an EMR cluster
aws emr add-steps \
    --cluster-id j-1234567890ABC \
    --steps Type=Spark,Name='bronze_to_silver',\
ActionOnFailure=CONTINUE,\
Args=[\
  '--deploy-mode','cluster',\
  '--py-files','s3://pipelines/smart_grid.zip',\
  's3://pipelines/main.py',\
  '--env','prod','--date','2026-02-25'\
]

# EMR Serverless: no cluster management
aws emr-serverless start-job-run \
    --application-id app-123456 \
    --execution-role-arn arn:aws:iam::123:role/emr-role \
    --job-driver '{
      "sparkSubmit": {
        "entryPoint": "s3://pipelines/main.py",
        "entryPointArguments": ["--env", "prod", "--date", "2026-02-25"],
        "sparkSubmitParameters": "--py-files s3://pipelines/smart_grid.zip"
      }
    }'
Google Cloud Dataproc

# gcloud CLI: submit to Dataproc
gcloud dataproc jobs submit pyspark \
    --cluster=smart-grid-cluster \
    --region=us-central1 \
    --py-files=gs://pipelines/smart_grid.zip \
    gs://pipelines/main.py \
    -- --env prod --date 2026-02-25

# Dataproc Serverless
gcloud dataproc batches submit pyspark \
    --region=us-central1 \
    --py-files=gs://pipelines/smart_grid.zip \
    gs://pipelines/main.py \
    -- --env prod --date 2026-02-25
Platform Comparison

Platform Cluster Management Serverless Option Best For Databricks Managed, auto-scaling Serverless Compute Unified analytics platform, notebook-first teams Amazon EMR Managed, manual/auto-scaling EMR Serverless AWS-native shops, cost-sensitive (Spot instances) Google Dataproc Managed, auto-scaling Dataproc Serverless GCP-native shops, BigQuery integration Azure Synapse Managed Spark pools Serverless pools Azure-native shops, SQL + Spark hybrid Kubernetes (Spark-on-K8s) Self-managed N/A Multi-cloud, K8s-standardized organizations
Running pipelines in production requires operational patterns that handle the messy reality of distributed systems: failures, slow runs, data arrivals, and resource contention.
Retry with Exponential Backoff

# Airflow task with retry configuration
clean_meters = SparkSubmitOperator(
    task_id='clean_meters',
    application='/pipelines/clean/meters.py',
    retries=3,                    # Retry up to 3 times
    retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=5),   # First retry after 5 min
    retry_exponential_backoff=True,     # 5 min, 10 min, 20 min
    max_retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=60),  # Cap at 60 min
    ...
)
Transient failures (network timeouts, Spot instance reclamation, temporary storage unavailability) are common in distributed systems. Retries with backoff handle them automatically.
SLA Monitoring

# Airflow: SLA miss callback
def sla_miss_callback(dag, task_list, blocking_task_list,
                      slas, blocking_tis):
    """Called when a task exceeds its SLA."""
    message = f':rotating_light: SLA MISS: {dag.dag_id}\n'
    for task in task_list:
        message += f'  Task: {task.task_id}\n'
    # Send alert to Slack/PagerDuty
    send_alert(message)
with DAG( dag_id='smart_grid_daily', sla_miss_callback=sla_miss_callback, ... ) as dag:

build_gold = SparkSubmitOperator(
        task_id='build_gold',
        sla=timedelta(hours=2),  # Must complete within 2 hours
        ...
    )
Idempotency Patterns

Pattern Implementation Guarantees replaceWhere df.write.option('replaceWhere', "date = '2026-02-25'").save() Overwrites only the target partition MERGE (upsert) DeltaTable.merge(...).whenMatchedUpdate().whenNotMatchedInsert() Inserts new, updates existing, idempotent Tombstone + append Delete where date = X, then append new data Clean slate for the processing date Write with dedup dropDuplicates before write, or MERGE on natural key No duplicates regardless of re-runs

Every production pipeline must be idempotent: running it twice for the same date must produce the same result. This is non-negotiable because retries, backfills, and manual re-runs all execute the pipeline multiple times.
Graceful Failure Handling

# Pattern: partial success with quarantine
def bronze_to_silver(spark, date, config):
    bronze = spark.read.format('delta').load(config.bronze_path)
    bronze = bronze.filter(f"date = '{date}'")

    # Separate good from bad
    good = bronze.filter(col('kw').isNotNull() & (col('kw') >= 0))
    bad = bronze.filter(col('kw').isNull() | (col('kw') < 0))

    # Write good data to silver
    good.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite') \
        .option('replaceWhere', f"date = '{date}'") \
        .save(config.silver_path)

    # Quarantine bad data for investigation
    if bad.count() > 0:
        bad.write.format('delta').mode('append') \
            .save(config.quarantine_path)

        # Alert but do NOT fail the pipeline
        send_alert(f'{bad.count()} records quarantined for {date}')

    return good.count()
Here is the full picture of a production PySpark platform, integrating every concept from this module:
Production Smart Grid Data Platform:

+-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  SOURCE SYSTEMS                                       |
  |  [Smart Meters] [Weather API] [Metadata DB]           |
  +----------+---------------+-----------------+----------+
             |               |                 |
             v               v                 v
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  INGESTION LAYER (Kafka / S3 / API)                   |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
             |
             v
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  ORCHESTRATOR (Airflow / Databricks Workflows)        |
  |                                                       |
  |  DAG: smart_grid_daily                                |
  |  Schedule: 6 AM UTC daily                             |
  |  Retries: 3 with exponential backoff                  |
  |  SLA: complete by 8 AM UTC                            |
  |                                                       |
  |  [Sensor]  --> [Ingest] --> [Clean]   --> [Quality]   |
  |  (wait for     (Bronze)     (Silver)      Gate        |
  |   data)                                               |
  |                              [Quarantine]             |
  |                                                       |
  |  [Quality] --> [Build Gold] --> [Export] --> [Notify]  |
  |   Gate          (MERGE)         (Reports)   (Slack)   |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
             |                         |
             v                         v
  +---------------------+   +-------------------+
  |  DELTA LAKE TABLES  |   |  QUALITY METRICS  |
  |  Bronze / Silver /  |   |  Delta Table      |
  |  Gold               |   |  (historical      |
  |  (ACID, time travel |   |   check results)  |
  |   schema enforced)  |   +-------------------+
  +---------------------+            |
             |                        v
             v                 +-------------------+
  +---------------------+     |  OBSERVABILITY    |
  |  CONSUMERS          |     |  Dashboard        |
  |  [BI Dashboard]     |     |  Trend monitoring |
  |  [ML Pipeline]      |     |  Alerts (Slack,   |
  |  [API]              |     |   PagerDuty)      |
  |  [Analyst SQL]      |     +-------------------+
  +---------------------+
How the Modules Connect

Module Role in Production Architecture Module 1–3: Foundations Spark internals inform cluster sizing and query optimization Module 4: Performance Tuning Execution plans, shuffle management, partition strategy Module 5: Advanced Joins Efficient distributed joins in gold aggregation layer Module 6: Spark SQL SQL interface for analyst consumers Module 7: Data Cleaning Bronze → Silver transformation and medallion architecture Module 8: Complex Types Parsing nested JSON from IoT sources Module 9: UDFs Custom business logic (tariff calculations, anomaly scoring) Module 10: Streaming Real-time ingestion with Structured Streaming Module 11: Delta Lake ACID storage, MERGE, time travel, schema enforcement Module 12: Testing & Quality Quality gates, metrics tables, observability Module 13: This Module Orchestration, deployment, operations, cost management
You now understand:

items: How to package PySpark applications as structured projects with entry points, config, and CLI arguments
How to submit applications with spark-submit, including executor/driver sizing and py-files
The difference between client mode (development) and cluster mode (production) deployment
Dependency management strategies: py-files, conda-pack, Docker images, and platform-managed libraries
Why orchestration is necessary beyond simple scheduling: dependencies, retries, branching, backfill, monitoring
How to build Airflow DAGs with SparkSubmitOperator, template variables ({{ ds }}), sensors, branching, and dynamic task generation
Alternative orchestrators: Databricks Workflows, Prefect, Dagster, and managed Airflow (MWAA, Cloud Composer)
Deployment strategies: blue-green, shadow execution, canary, and feature flags for zero-downtime releases
Rollback using previous artifact versions and Delta Lake time travel
Cluster sizing rules of thumb and cost optimization: Spot instances, auto-scaling, ephemeral clusters, right-sizing
Deploying on managed platforms: Databricks Jobs, Amazon EMR / EMR Serverless, Google Dataproc / Dataproc Serverless
Operational patterns: retry with backoff, SLA monitoring, idempotency, and graceful failure with quarantine
The complete production architecture connecting all 13 modules into a unified smart grid data platform

blocks.content: You are no longer building pipelines that run in notebooks. You are building production data platforms that run autonomously, recover from failures, deploy safely, and operate at scale.
In Module 14, we turn to Security, Governance & Access Control — ensuring that the data platform you have built is not just reliable, but also secure, compliant, and properly governed.

chapters.title: Module 14: Security, Governance & Access Control
chapters.description: Protecting Data at Every Layer Understand the security and governance landscape for production data platforms, implement authentication, authorization, encryption, data masking, row-level and column-level access control, audit logging, and regulatory compliance patterns using PySpark and the modern Lakehouse stack.

blocks.content: You have built a production data platform. It ingests data from smart meters. It cleans, transforms, and aggregates it across Medallion layers. It is tested, orchestrated, and deployed through CI/CD.

Now imagine: a junior analyst accidentally queries the silver table and exports 10 million customer records — including addresses, consumption patterns, and billing information — to a shared CSV on their laptop. Or a contractor’s credentials are compromised, giving an attacker read access to every table in the data lake. Or a regulatory audit reveals that your pipeline has been processing EU customer data on US-based servers for six months without consent.
None of your tests caught these. None of your quality checks flagged them. Your pipeline is technically correct. But your data platform is a liability.
Security and governance are not optional features bolted on after the fact. They are architectural requirements that must be designed into the platform from the beginning.
The Security Landscape:

WHO can access?          Authentication   (identity)
  WHAT can they do?        Authorization    (permissions)
  WHICH data can they see? Access Control   (row/column level)
  IS the data protected?   Encryption       (at rest + in transit)
  CAN we prove it?         Audit Logging    (who did what, when)
  ARE we compliant?        Governance       (GDPR, HIPAA, SOX)
This module covers all six.

sections.title: Lesson 2: The Threat Model — What Can Go Wrong

blocks.content: Before implementing security controls, you must understand what you are protecting against. A data platform faces threats from multiple directions.
Threat Categories

Threat Example Impact Control Unauthorized access Compromised credentials, misconfigured IAM Data breach, regulatory fines Authentication + Authorization Privilege escalation Analyst gains admin access to production tables Data corruption, mass export Least privilege, RBAC Data exfiltration Employee downloads PII to personal device Reputation damage, lawsuits Access control, audit logging Insider misuse Engineer queries salary data out of curiosity Privacy violation, HR/legal issue Row/column masking, audit Data residency violation EU data processed in US region GDPR fine (up to 4% of revenue) Governance, data classification Schema/pipeline tampering Unauthorized schema change breaks downstream Data corruption, outage Change control, permissions Accidental exposure PII written to unencrypted public bucket Data breach Encryption, access policies
Notice: most threats are not sophisticated cyberattacks. They are misconfigurations, accidents, and insider misuse. The controls in this module address the most common real-world risks.
Authentication answers: Who are you? Before a user or service can access the data platform, they must prove their identity.
Authentication in Spark Environments

Environment Authentication Method How It Works Databricks SSO (SAML/OIDC) + Personal Access Tokens Users log in via company identity provider (Okta, Azure AD) EMR on AWS IAM Roles + Kerberos Cluster nodes assume IAM roles; users authenticate via Kerberos Dataproc on GCP Google IAM + Service Accounts Users use Google identity; jobs run as service accounts Kubernetes (Spark-on-K8s) K8s RBAC + Service Accounts Pods run as K8s service accounts with defined permissions On-premise Hadoop Kerberos KDC issues tickets; every service authenticates via keytab
Service Accounts vs User Accounts
Two Types of Identity:

USER ACCOUNT                    SERVICE ACCOUNT
  (human)                         (pipeline/application)

  alice@company.com               svc-smart-grid-pipeline
  Logs in via SSO                 Uses API key or IAM role
  Interactive queries             Automated jobs
  Subject to MFA                  No MFA (machine identity)
  Credentials expire              Credentials auto-rotated
Rule: Pipelines NEVER run as a human user account. Always use dedicated service accounts with scoped permissions.
Why this matters: If a pipeline runs under alice@company.com and Alice leaves the company, the pipeline breaks when her account is deactivated. Service accounts are independent of employee lifecycle.
Secrets Management
Credentials — database passwords, API keys, service account tokens — must never appear in code, notebooks, or configuration files checked into version control.

Bad Practice Why It Is Dangerous Correct Approach Password in code: .option('password', 'abc123') Visible in git history forever Secrets manager: dbutils.secrets.get('scope', 'db_password') API key in environment variable on shared cluster Any user on the cluster can read it Platform-managed secrets (Databricks Secrets, AWS Secrets Manager) Credentials in .env file committed to git Exposed in repository .gitignore + vault-based retrieval Shared service account password in wiki No rotation, no access control IAM roles with automatic rotation

# Databricks: retrieve secrets at runtime
db_password = dbutils.secrets.get(scope='smart-grid', key='postgres_password')

df = spark.read \
    .format('jdbc') \
    .option('url', 'jdbc:postgresql://host:5432/meters') \
    .option('user', 'svc_reader') \
    .option('password', db_password) \
    .load()

# AWS: use IAM roles (no credentials in code at all)
# The EMR cluster assumes an IAM role that has S3 read access
df = spark.read.parquet('s3://smart-grid-data/silver/meter_readings/')
# No credentials needed — IAM role handles it

sections.title: Lesson 4: Authorization — What Can You Do?

blocks.content: Authentication proves identity. Authorization determines what that identity is allowed to do. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is the standard model.
The Principle of Least Privilege
Every user and service account should have the minimum permissions necessary to perform their job. Nothing more.
Least Privilege in Practice:
ROLE: analyst_readonly CAN: SELECT on gold tables CANNOT: SELECT on bronze/silver, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, DROP

ROLE: data_engineer
  CAN:    SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE on silver/gold
  CANNOT: DROP TABLE, ALTER permissions, access raw PII

  ROLE: pipeline_service
  CAN:    READ bronze, WRITE silver/gold, MERGE, OPTIMIZE
  CANNOT: DROP TABLE, GRANT permissions

  ROLE: admin
  CAN:    Everything
  WHO:    2-3 senior engineers only
SQL-Based Authorization
-- Grant read access on gold tables to analysts GRANT SELECT ON TABLE gold.consumption_summary TO `analyst_readonly`; GRANT SELECT ON TABLE gold.daily_report TO `analyst_readonly`;
-- Grant read/write on silver to the pipeline service account GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON TABLE silver.meter_readings TO `svc_smart_grid_pipeline`;
-- Revoke direct access to bronze (raw, potentially sensitive) REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES ON TABLE bronze.raw_readings FROM `analyst_readonly`;
-- Show current grants SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE gold.consumption_summary;
Common Role Design

Role Bronze Silver Gold Typical Users admin Full control Full control Full control Platform team (2–3 people) data_engineer Read Read + Write Read + Write Engineering team pipeline_service Read Read + Write + MERGE Read + Write + MERGE Automated pipelines only analyst_readonly No access No access Read only Business analysts, BI tools analyst_sensitive No access Read (masked PII) Read Analysts who need customer-level data external_partner No access No access Read (subset only) Vendors, contractors, partners
Object Hierarchy
Permissions are granted at different levels of the object hierarchy. Higher-level grants cascade down:
Object Hierarchy (Databricks Unity Catalog):

METASTORE          (top-level container)
    |
    +-- CATALOG       (e.g., 'smart_grid')
          |
          +-- SCHEMA   (e.g., 'silver', 'gold')
                |
                +-- TABLE    (e.g., 'meter_readings')
                |
                +-- VIEW     (e.g., 'meter_readings_masked')
                |
                +-- FUNCTION (e.g., 'calculate_tariff')

  GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA gold TO `analyst_readonly`;
  --> Analyst can read ALL tables in the gold schema.
  --> New tables added to gold are automatically accessible.

-- Grant at schema level (covers all current and future tables) GRANT USAGE ON CATALOG smart_grid TO `analyst_readonly`; GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA smart_grid.gold TO `analyst_readonly`; GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA smart_grid.gold TO `analyst_readonly`;
-- Grant at table level (more restrictive) GRANT SELECT ON TABLE smart_grid.gold.consumption_summary TO `external_partner`;

sections.title: Lesson 5: Column-Level Security — Hiding Sensitive Fields

blocks.content: Not every user should see every column. A table might contain both analytical fields (kw, voltage, date) and sensitive fields (customer_name, address, billing_account). Column-level security restricts which columns specific roles can access.
Strategy 1: Secure Views
The simplest and most portable approach: create views that exclude or mask sensitive columns, and grant access to the view instead of the underlying table.

-- The base table has all columns including PII
-- silver.meter_readings: meter_id, customer_name, address,
--                        customer_type, date, kw, voltage

-- Create a view that masks PII for analysts
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW gold.meter_readings_analyst AS
SELECT
    meter_id,
    customer_type,
    date,
    kw,
    voltage
    -- customer_name and address are NOT included
FROM silver.meter_readings;
-- Grant analysts access to the view only GRANT SELECT ON VIEW gold.meter_readings_analyst TO `analyst_readonly`;
-- Analysts can query the view but cannot see the underlying table
Strategy 2: Dynamic Column Masking
Some platforms support dynamic data masking where the same query returns different results depending on who runs it:
-- Databricks Unity Catalog: Column Masks

-- Create a masking function CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION mask_name(original STRING) RETURNS STRING RETURN CASE WHEN is_member('pii_authorized') THEN original ELSE CONCAT(LEFT(original, 1), '****') END;
-- Apply the mask to a column ALTER TABLE silver.meter_readings ALTER COLUMN customer_name SET MASK mask_name;

-- Now:
-- Admin sees:    'John Smith'
-- Analyst sees:  'J****'
-- Same table, same query, different results based on role.
Dynamic Masking in Action:
Same query: SELECT customer_name, kw FROM meter_readings
The mask is transparent. No separate views needed.
Common Masking Patterns

Data Type Original Value Masked Value Technique Name John Smith J**** Keep first letter, mask rest Email john@company.com j***@***.com Mask username and domain Phone +1-555-123-4567 ***-***-4567 Keep last 4 digits SSN / National ID 123-45-6789 ***-**-6789 Keep last 4 digits Address 123 Main St, Apt 4 REDACTED Full replacement Credit card 4111-1111-1111-1234 ****-****-****-1234 Keep last 4 digits Date of birth 1985-03-15 1985-**-** Keep year only IP address 192.168.1.45 192.168.x.x Mask last two octets

sections.title: Lesson 6: Row-Level Security — Filtering by Identity

blocks.content: Column-level security hides fields. Row-level security hides entire records. A regional manager should only see data for their region. A partner should only see data for meters they manage.
Strategy 1: View-Based Row Filtering

-- Create a view that filters rows based on the current user's region CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW gold.regional_readings AS SELECT * FROM silver.meter_readings WHERE region IN ( SELECT region FROM access_control.user_regions WHERE username = current_user() );

-- User 'alice' is assigned region 'NORTHEAST' in user_regions table
-- When alice queries this view, she only sees NORTHEAST data

-- User 'bob' is assigned regions 'SOUTHEAST', 'MIDWEST'
-- Bob sees data for both regions
Strategy 2: Row Filters (Databricks Unity Catalog)

-- Create a row filter function CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION region_filter(region_val STRING) RETURNS BOOLEAN RETURN is_member('admin') OR region_val IN ( SELECT region FROM access_control.user_regions WHERE username = current_user() );
-- Apply the row filter to the table ALTER TABLE silver.meter_readings SET ROW FILTER region_filter ON (region);

-- Now the same table automatically filters rows by identity
-- Admin sees all rows
-- Regional manager sees only their region(s)
-- No separate views needed
PySpark Implementation (Platform-Agnostic)
If your platform does not support native row-level security, implement it in your pipeline:

# access_control.py
from pyspark.sql import DataFrame
from pyspark.sql.functions import col

def apply_row_level_security(df: DataFrame, user: str,
                              acl_df: DataFrame) -> DataFrame:
    """Filter rows based on user's authorized regions."""
    # Get user's authorized regions
    user_regions = acl_df.filter(col('username') == user) \
        .select('region').distinct()

    # Inner join: only return rows in authorized regions
    return df.join(user_regions, 'region', 'inner')

# Usage
acl = spark.read.format('delta').load('/data/access_control/user_regions/')
readings = spark.read.format('delta').load('/data/silver/meter_readings/')

# Alice only sees her authorized regions
alice_data = apply_row_level_security(readings, 'alice', acl)
Combining Row and Column Security
Layered Access Control:
Base Table: meter_id | customer_name | address | region | kw | voltage
Admin: meter_id | customer_name | address | region | kw | voltage (all rows, all columns)
Engineer: meter_id | customer_name | address | region | kw | voltage (all rows, all columns)

Analyst:    meter_id | C****         | [hidden]| region | kw | voltage
              (all rows, masked PII, no address)

  Regional:   meter_id | C****         | [hidden]| NE     | kw | voltage
              (NE rows only, masked PII, no address)

  Partner:    meter_id |               |         | NE     | kw |
              (assigned rows, no PII, no voltage)

sections.title: Lesson 7: Encryption — Protecting Data at Rest and in Transit

blocks.content: Even with perfect access control, data can be intercepted during network transfer or extracted from stolen storage media. Encryption is the defense against physical and network-level threats.
Encryption at Rest
Encryption at rest protects data stored on disk. If a hard drive is stolen or a storage bucket is misconfigured, the data is unreadable without the encryption key.

Platform Default Encryption Key Management AWS S3 SSE-S3 (AWS-managed keys) or SSE-KMS (customer-managed) AWS KMS Google Cloud Storage AES-256 by default Google KMS or customer-managed (CMEK) Azure Data Lake Storage AES-256 by default Azure Key Vault Databricks Platform encryption + optional CMEK Platform or cloud KMS Delta Lake on any cloud Inherits storage-level encryption Cloud KMS

# AWS: Enable S3 server-side encryption with KMS
# (configured at bucket level, transparent to Spark)

# Spark reads/writes to S3 normally — encryption is transparent
df.write.parquet('s3://smart-grid-encrypted/silver/readings/')
# S3 encrypts with KMS automatically on write
# S3 decrypts automatically on read (if IAM role has kms:Decrypt)

# No code changes needed. Encryption is infrastructure-level.
Encryption in Transit
Encryption in transit protects data moving between components: Driver to executors, executors to storage, and cluster to external services.

# Enable Spark internal encryption (shuffle data between executors)
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .config('spark.network.crypto.enabled', 'true') \
    .config('spark.network.crypto.keyLength', '256') \
    .config('spark.authenticate', 'true') \
    .config('spark.io.encryption.enabled', 'true') \
    .getOrCreate()

# For JDBC connections: use SSL
df = spark.read \
    .format('jdbc') \
    .option('url', 'jdbc:postgresql://host:5432/db?ssl=true&sslmode=verify-full') \
    .option('user', 'svc_reader') \
    .option('password', db_password) \
    .load()
Column-Level Encryption
For extremely sensitive fields, encrypt individual columns so that even database administrators cannot read them without the application-level key:

from pyspark.sql.functions import sha2, concat, lit, col

# Hash-based pseudonymization (one-way, irreversible)
anonymized = df.withColumn(
    'customer_id_hash',
    sha2(concat(col('customer_name'), lit('salt_value')), 256)
).drop('customer_name')

# The original name cannot be recovered from the hash.
# But the same name always produces the same hash,
# allowing joins and aggregations on the pseudonym.

Technique Reversible? Use Case Storage-level encryption (S3, ADLS) Transparent (auto) Baseline: always on Network encryption (TLS/SSL) Transparent Data in transit: always on Spark internal encryption Transparent Shuffle and IO between components Hashing / pseudonymization (SHA-256) No (one-way) Anonymize PII while preserving joins Tokenization Yes (via token vault) Replace sensitive values with tokens; reverse with key Application-level encryption (AES) Yes (with key) Maximum protection: only app can decrypt

sections.title: Lesson 8: Data Classification — Knowing What You Have

blocks.content: You cannot protect data if you do not know what it is. Data classification assigns sensitivity levels to every column and table, enabling appropriate security controls.
Classification Levels

Level Definition Examples Controls PUBLIC No sensitivity; can be shared externally Weather data, public tariff rates No special controls INTERNAL Business-sensitive; not for external sharing Aggregated consumption reports, grid metrics Authentication required CONFIDENTIAL Contains business-critical or customer data Customer type, meter location, billing amount RBAC, audit logging RESTRICTED Contains PII or regulated data Customer name, address, SSN, health data Encryption, masking, row-level security, audit
Tagging Tables and Columns
-- Databricks Unity Catalog: apply tags for classification ALTER TABLE silver.meter_readings SET TAGS ('data_classification' = 'CONFIDENTIAL');
ALTER TABLE silver.meter_readings ALTER COLUMN customer_name SET TAGS ('pii' = 'true', 'data_classification' = 'RESTRICTED');
ALTER TABLE silver.meter_readings ALTER COLUMN address SET TAGS ('pii' = 'true', 'data_classification' = 'RESTRICTED');
ALTER TABLE silver.meter_readings ALTER COLUMN kw SET TAGS ('data_classification' = 'INTERNAL');
-- Query tags programmatically SELECT * FROM system.information_schema.column_tags WHERE table_name = 'meter_readings' AND tag_name = 'pii';
Automated PII Detection
Manual classification is error-prone. Automated tools can scan tables and suggest classifications:

sections.title: Lesson 9: Audit Logging — Who Did What, When

blocks.content: Access control prevents unauthorized actions. Audit logging records all actions — authorized or not — creating an immutable trail for compliance, investigation, and accountability.
What to Audit

Event Category Examples Why It Matters Data access SELECT queries, DataFrame reads, exports Track who accessed sensitive data Data modification INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, DROP Track all changes to tables Schema changes ALTER TABLE, ADD COLUMN, change type Detect unauthorized schema modifications Permission changes GRANT, REVOKE, role assignments Detect privilege escalation Authentication events Login success/failure, token usage Detect unauthorized access attempts Pipeline execution Job start/stop, duration, success/failure Operational accountability
Delta Lake Audit Trail (Built-in)
Delta Lake’s transaction log (Module 11) provides a built-in audit trail for data modifications:
-- View complete history of changes to a table DESCRIBE HISTORY silver.meter_readings;
Platform Audit Logs

Platform Audit Log System What It Captures Databricks Unity Catalog Audit Logs (delivered to cloud storage) Every query, table access, permission change, cluster event AWS EMR CloudTrail + S3 access logs API calls, S3 object access, IAM events GCP Dataproc Cloud Audit Logs Admin activity, data access, system events Azure Synapse Azure Monitor + Diagnostic Logs SQL queries, Spark operations, access events
Custom Audit Logging in Pipelines

# Append to a dedicated audit Delta table
from pyspark.sql.functions import current_timestamp, lit

def log_data_access(spark, user, table_name, operation,
                     row_count, audit_path):
    """Log every data access event."""
    audit_record = spark.createDataFrame([(
        user, table_name, operation, row_count,
    )], ['user', 'table_name', 'operation', 'row_count'])

    audit_record \
        .withColumn('event_time', current_timestamp()) \
        .write.format('delta') \
        .mode('append') \
        .save(audit_path)

# Usage in pipeline
result = spark.read.format('delta').load('/data/silver/meter_readings/')
result = result.filter(col('region') == 'NORTHEAST')
log_data_access( spark, 'alice@company.com', 'silver.meter_readings', 'SELECT (filtered by region)', result.count(), '/data/audit/access_log/' )

sections.title: Lesson 10: Regulatory Compliance — GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA

blocks.content: Data regulations impose legal requirements on how personal data is collected, stored, processed, and deleted. Non-compliance can result in catastrophic fines and reputational damage.
Key Regulations

Regulation Region Key Requirements for Data Platforms GDPR EU / EEA Right to erasure, data portability, consent, data minimization, breach notification within 72 hours CCPA / CPRA California, USA Right to delete, right to know, opt-out of sale, data inventory HIPAA USA (healthcare) Protected health information (PHI): encryption, access controls, audit trails SOX USA (public companies) Financial data integrity: access controls, change management, audit trails PCI DSS Global (payments) Cardholder data: encryption, network segmentation, access logging
Right to Erasure (GDPR Article 17)
A customer requests deletion of all their data. In a traditional data lake of Parquet files, this was an operational nightmare. With Delta Lake, it is a single command:

from delta.tables import DeltaTable

def gdpr_delete_customer(spark, customer_id, table_paths):
    """Delete all records for a customer across all tables."""
    for path in table_paths:
        delta_table = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, path)
        delta_table.delete(f"customer_id = '{customer_id}'")

    # Log the deletion for compliance audit
    log_data_access(spark, 'gdpr_service', 'all_tables',
                     f'GDPR DELETE customer_id={customer_id}', -1,
                     '/data/audit/gdpr_deletions/')

# Execute deletion across all Medallion layers
gdpr_delete_customer(spark, 'CUST-12345', [
    '/data/bronze/meter_readings/',
    '/data/silver/meter_readings/',
    '/data/gold/consumption_summary/',
])

# After deletion, VACUUM to physically remove the data
# (otherwise it remains accessible via time travel)
for path in table_paths:
    DeltaTable.forPath(spark, path).vacuum(0)  # 0 hours = immediate
    # WARNING: vacuum(0) requires disabling the safety check:
    # spark.conf.set('spark.databricks.delta.retentionDurationCheck.enabled', 'false')
Data Minimization
GDPR requires that you collect and retain only the data you actually need. In PySpark pipelines:

items: Collect only necessary columns when ingesting from source systems (select early, drop PII if not needed)
Pseudonymize at the bronze layer: replace customer_name with a hash before it enters silver
Aggregate in gold so that individual-level PII does not propagate to analytics tables
Retention policies: DELETE or VACUUM data older than the retention period (e.g., 7 years for financial, 3 years for analytics)

blocks.content:
# Pseudonymize at ingestion (bronze -> silver boundary)
from pyspark.sql.functions import sha2, concat, col, lit

silver = bronze \
    .withColumn('customer_id_pseudo',
        sha2(concat(col('customer_id'), lit('hmac_secret')), 256)) \
    .drop('customer_name', 'address', 'phone', 'email') \
    .withColumnRenamed('customer_id_pseudo', 'customer_id')

# Silver table has no PII. Only the pseudonymized ID.
# Gold table aggregates by customer_type, region — no individual data at all.
Unity Catalog is Databricks’ centralized governance solution. It provides a single place to manage permissions, audit access, discover data, and enforce policies across all data assets.
Even if you do not use Databricks, understanding Unity Catalog illustrates the capabilities a mature governance layer should provide. The concepts apply to any platform.
Unity Catalog Capabilities

Capability What It Provides Without It Centralized permissions GRANT/REVOKE at catalog, schema, table, column level Permissions managed per-cluster or per-workspace (fragmented) Column masking Dynamic masks based on user identity Manual view creation for each access pattern Row filters Automatic row filtering by identity Application-level filtering (error-prone) Data lineage Automatic tracking: which tables feed which tables Manual documentation (always out of date) Audit logs Every access event logged to cloud storage Custom logging (inconsistent coverage) Data discovery Search, browse, and tag tables across the organization Ask around, check wiki (usually stale) Tags and classification Label columns as PII, classify tables by sensitivity Spreadsheets and tribal knowledge External tables Govern tables stored in S3/GCS/ADLS, not just Databricks No governance for external data
Lineage Tracking
Unity Catalog automatically tracks data lineage: which tables are read by which pipelines, and which tables are written. This answers critical governance questions:

items: Impact analysis: "If I change the schema of silver.meter_readings, which gold tables and dashboards break?"
Root cause: "Gold numbers look wrong. Which upstream tables and pipelines produced this data?"
Compliance: "Which tables contain data derived from EU customer records?"

blocks.content: Automatic Lineage (Unity Catalog):

bronze.raw_readings
       |
       | [pipeline: bronze_to_silver]
       v
  silver.meter_readings
       |                \
       | [pipeline:      \ [pipeline: export_to_warehouse]
       |  silver_to_gold]  \
       v                    v
  gold.consumption_summary  external.snowflake.readings
       |
       v
  [Dashboard: Executive KPI]
All connections tracked automatically. No manual documentation required.
Platform-Agnostic Governance Alternatives

Tool Platform Key Capability Unity Catalog Databricks Full governance: permissions, masking, lineage, audit, classification AWS Lake Formation AWS Permissions, column-level security, data sharing for S3/Glue Google Dataplex GCP Data discovery, classification, quality, governance for BigQuery + GCS Apache Ranger Hadoop / on-premise Fine-grained authorization for Hive, HDFS, Kafka Apache Atlas Hadoop / on-premise Metadata management, lineage tracking, classification Collibra / Alation Any (SaaS) Data catalog, governance workflows, business glossary OpenMetadata Any (open-source) Data discovery, lineage, quality, collaboration
