Data platforms often need to share data with external parties: partners, vendors, regulators, or other business units. Sharing must be governed: controlled access, limited scope, and audited.
Delta Sharing (Open Protocol)
Delta Sharing is an open protocol for secure, cross-organization data sharing. It allows a data provider to share specific tables (or subsets) with external recipients without copying data.
Delta Sharing Model:

PROVIDER (your organization)            RECIPIENT (partner)
  +----------------------------+          +----------------------+
  | Delta Lake Tables          |          | PySpark / pandas /   |
  | silver.meter_readings      |  share   | Power BI / Tableau   |
  | gold.consumption_summary   | -------> | Any tool supporting  |
  +----------------------------+          | Delta Sharing client |
                                          +----------------------+

items: Data stays in provider's storage (no copying).
Provider controls which tables, columns, and rows are shared.
All access is authenticated and audited.
Recipient reads via standard APIs (no Databricks required).

blocks.content: -- Provider: create a share and add tables CREATE SHARE smart_grid_partner_share;
ALTER SHARE smart_grid_partner_share ADD TABLE gold.consumption_summary;
-- Share only specific partitions ALTER SHARE smart_grid_partner_share ADD TABLE gold.regional_summary PARTITION (region = 'NORTHEAST');
-- Grant share access to a recipient CREATE RECIPIENT partner_acme; GRANT SELECT ON SHARE smart_grid_partner_share TO RECIPIENT partner_acme;
Sharing Anti-Patterns

Anti-Pattern Risk Correct Approach Email CSV exports of PII No access control, no audit trail, persists forever Delta Sharing or governed export pipeline Shared S3 bucket with broad IAM No table-level control, no audit Delta Sharing or Lake Formation grants Copy data to partner's account No revocation, stale data, uncontrolled copies Delta Sharing (live, revocable access) Direct database credentials to partner Full access to everything, credential leakage risk Service account with scoped permissions
Here is the layered security model for a production smart grid data platform, integrating every control from this module:
Layered Security Architecture:

+-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  NETWORK LAYER                                        |
  |  VPC / Private Subnets / Firewall Rules / VPN         |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  AUTHENTICATION LAYER                                 |
  |  SSO (SAML/OIDC) | Service Accounts | MFA | IAM Roles |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  AUTHORIZATION LAYER                                  |
  |  RBAC | GRANT/REVOKE | Least Privilege | Roles        |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  DATA ACCESS CONTROL                                  |
  |  Column Masking | Row Filters | Secure Views          |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  ENCRYPTION LAYER                                     |
  |  At-Rest (KMS) | In-Transit (TLS) | Column-Level      |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  GOVERNANCE LAYER                                     |
  |  Classification | Tags | Lineage | Data Catalog       |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  AUDIT LAYER                                          |
  |  Platform Logs | Delta History | Custom Access Logs    |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
  |  COMPLIANCE LAYER                                     |
  |  GDPR Deletion | Retention Policies | Data Residency  |
  +-------------------------------------------------------+
Security Checklist for Production

Category Requirement Implementation Authentication All access requires identity verification SSO + service accounts, no shared credentials Authorization Least privilege on all tables RBAC with role hierarchy; review quarterly Column security PII masked for non-authorized users Dynamic masking or secure views Row security Users see only their authorized scope Row filters or view-based filtering Encryption at rest All storage encrypted Cloud KMS (enabled by default on most platforms) Encryption in transit All network traffic encrypted TLS for external, Spark internal encryption for shuffle Secrets management No credentials in code or config files Vault / Secrets Manager / platform secrets Data classification Every table and PII column tagged Tags in Unity Catalog / Lake Formation / manual registry Audit logging All access and modifications logged Platform audit logs + Delta history + custom logging GDPR readiness Can delete individual customer data within 30 days Delta DELETE + VACUUM across all layers Data retention Old data deleted per policy Scheduled VACUUM + DELETE WHERE date < retention_date Data sharing External sharing governed and auditable Delta Sharing or governed export pipeline
The Human Element
The most sophisticated security architecture fails if people do not follow it. Production security requires:

items: Onboarding: Every new team member gets role-appropriate access on day one — not admin by default
Offboarding: Credentials revoked within 24 hours of departure
Quarterly access reviews: Do all current permissions still make sense?
Incident response plan: What happens when a breach is detected? Who is called? What is the containment process?
Training: Engineers and analysts understand why these controls exist, not just how to work around them

blocks.content: You now understand:

items: The threat model for data platforms: unauthorized access, privilege escalation, exfiltration, insider misuse, residency violations, and accidental exposure
Authentication: SSO, service accounts, IAM roles, and why pipelines must never run under human accounts
Secrets management: keeping credentials out of code using platform secrets and vaults
Authorization with RBAC: the principle of least privilege, role design, SQL GRANT/REVOKE, and the object hierarchy (catalog → schema → table → column)
Column-level security: secure views, dynamic column masking, and common masking patterns for PII
Row-level security: view-based filtering, platform row filters, and PySpark implementation for ACL-driven row restriction
Encryption: at rest (cloud KMS), in transit (TLS, Spark internal encryption), and column-level (hashing, pseudonymization, tokenization)
Data classification: sensitivity levels (PUBLIC through RESTRICTED), tagging columns and tables, and automated PII detection
Audit logging: what to audit, Delta Lake’s built-in history, platform audit logs, and custom access logging to Delta tables
Regulatory compliance: GDPR right to erasure with Delta DELETE + VACUUM, data minimization, pseudonymization at ingestion, and retention policies
Unity Catalog and centralized governance: permissions, masking, row filters, lineage tracking, data discovery, and platform-agnostic alternatives
Secure data sharing with Delta Sharing and the anti-patterns of CSV exports and shared credentials
The layered security architecture and the production security checklist

blocks.content: You are no longer building a platform that just works. You are building a platform that is secure, governed, auditable, and compliant.

In Module 15, we take a deep dive into Window Functions — the most powerful analytical tool in PySpark SQL, covering ranking, running totals, lag/lead, rolling aggregations, cumulative distribution, and time-series patterns on the smart grid dataset.

chapters.title: Module 15: Window Functions Deep Dive
chapters.description: The Most Powerful Analytical Tool in PySpark Master every category of window function in PySpark — ranking, analytical (lag/lead), aggregate, cumulative, and frame-based — understand their distributed execution model, and apply them to solve real-world time-series, comparison, and anomaly detection problems on the smart grid dataset.

blocks.content: Window functions appeared briefly in Module 5, where we used lag() to avoid a self-join. In Module 6, we touched on ranking in Spark SQL. But we never gave window functions the dedicated attention they deserve.
That changes now.

Window functions are arguably the single most powerful analytical tool in PySpark SQL. They allow you to compute values across a set of rows related to the current row — without collapsing the data into groups. Unlike groupBy(), which reduces millions of rows into a few aggregate rows, window functions keep every row intact and enrich it with contextual calculations.
groupBy() vs Window Functions:
groupBy('customer_type').sum('kw')

INPUT (6 rows):                    OUTPUT (3 rows):
  meter | type       | kw            type       | total_kw
  M001  | RESIDENTIAL| 100           RESIDENTIAL| 550
  M002  | COMMERCIAL | 500           COMMERCIAL | 700
  M003  | RESIDENTIAL| 200           INDUSTRIAL | 5000
  M004  | INDUSTRIAL | 5000
  M005  | COMMERCIAL | 200
  M006  | RESIDENTIAL| 250
Rows are COLLAPSED. Individual meter data is LOST.
Window Function: sum('kw').over(partitionBy('customer_type'))

INPUT (6 rows):                    OUTPUT (6 rows):
  meter | type       | kw            meter | type       | kw  | total_kw
  M001  | RESIDENTIAL| 100           M001  | RESIDENTIAL| 100 | 550
  M002  | COMMERCIAL | 500           M002  | COMMERCIAL | 500 | 700
  M003  | RESIDENTIAL| 200           M003  | RESIDENTIAL| 200 | 550
  M004  | INDUSTRIAL | 5000          M004  | INDUSTRIAL |5000 | 5000
  M005  | COMMERCIAL | 200           M005  | COMMERCIAL | 200 | 700
  M006  | RESIDENTIAL| 250           M006  | RESIDENTIAL| 250 | 550
Every row PRESERVED. Each row gains context from its group.

This is the fundamental insight: window functions add information to rows without destroying them. That capability unlocks an enormous range of analyses that are impossible or extremely awkward with groupBy alone.

sections.title: Lesson 2: The Anatomy of a Window Specification

blocks.content: Every window function has three components. Understanding all three is essential.
The Three Components of a Window:
1. partitionBy — Defining the Window Groups
partitionBy divides the data into independent groups. The window function operates within each partition separately. It is conceptually similar to groupBy, but rows are not collapsed.

from pyspark.sql.window import Window

# Partition by customer type: each type is an independent window
window_by_type = Window.partitionBy('customer_type')

# Partition by meter: each meter is an independent window
window_by_meter = Window.partitionBy('meter_id')

# Partition by multiple columns
window_by_region_type = Window.partitionBy('region', 'customer_type')

Distributed insight: partitionBy controls how Spark shuffles data. All rows with the same partition key must land on the same executor. This is identical to the shuffle in groupBy. Choosing a good partition key matters for both correctness and performance.
2. orderBy — Sequencing Within the Window
orderBy defines the sequence of rows inside each partition. This is required for ranking functions, lag/lead, and running totals.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, desc

# Order by timestamp ascending (time series)
window_ts = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('timestamp')

# Order by kw descending (ranking highest first)
window_rank = Window.partitionBy('customer_type').orderBy(desc('kw'))

# Order by multiple columns
window_multi = Window.partitionBy('region').orderBy('date', 'meter_id')
3. Frame Specification — The Invisible Third Dimension
The frame defines which rows relative to the current row are included in the calculation. This is the most misunderstood component, and getting it wrong produces silently incorrect results.
Frame Boundaries (for current row marked with *):

Row -3:  |  UNBOUNDED PRECEDING
  Row -2:  |  ...
  Row -1:  |  1 PRECEDING
  Row  0:  * <-- CURRENT ROW
  Row +1:  |  1 FOLLOWING
  Row +2:  |  ...
  Row +3:  |  UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING

  rowsBetween(-2, 0)         = 3-row trailing window
  rowsBetween(0, 2)          = 3-row leading window
  rowsBetween(-1, 1)         = 3-row centered window
  rowsBetween(UNBOUNDED, 0)  = all rows from start to current
  rowsBetween(UNBOUNDED, UNBOUNDED) = entire partition

from pyspark.sql.window import Window
import sys

# Trailing 3-row window (current + 2 preceding)
trailing_3 = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date') \
    .rowsBetween(-2, 0)

# Running total (all rows from partition start to current)
running = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date') \
    .rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)

# Entire partition (no frame restriction)
full_partition = Window.partitionBy('customer_type') \
    .rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.unboundedFollowing)
rowsBetween vs rangeBetween

Method Boundary Based On When To Use Gotcha rowsBetween Physical row position (count of rows) Fixed-size sliding windows, rolling averages Gaps in data are ignored rangeBetween Logical value range of the ORDER BY column Time-based windows (last 7 days), value-based ranges ORDER BY must be numeric or date; works on value, not position

Critical distinction: rowsBetween(-2, 0) always includes exactly 3 rows. rangeBetween(-2, 0) includes all rows whose ORDER BY value is within 2 units of the current row — which could be 0 rows, 3 rows, or 100 rows depending on the data.
Default Frame Behavior
If you do not specify a frame, Spark applies different defaults depending on whether you have an ORDER BY:
Scenario Default Frame Effect No orderBy UNBOUNDED PRECEDING to UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING Entire partition (expected) With orderBy UNBOUNDED PRECEDING to CURRENT ROW Running calculation (often surprising!)

This default catches many engineers off guard. If you write sum('kw').over(Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date')), you get a running sum, not a partition total. To get the partition total with an ORDER BY, you must explicitly set the frame to unboundedPreceding through unboundedFollowing.
Ranking functions assign a position number to each row within its partition based on the ordering.
The Four Ranking Functions
Given: Partition by customer_type, Order by kw DESC

customer_type | meter | kw   | row_number | rank | dense_rank | ntile(3)
  RESIDENTIAL   | M006  | 450  |     1      |  1   |     1      |    1
  RESIDENTIAL   | M003  | 380  |     2      |  2   |     2      |    1
  RESIDENTIAL   | M001  | 380  |     3      |  2   |     2      |    2
  RESIDENTIAL   | M007  | 200  |     4      |  4   |     3      |    2
  RESIDENTIAL   | M009  | 100  |     5      |  5   |     4      |    3
  RESIDENTIAL   | M010  | 50   |     6      |  6   |     5      |    3

  row_number: always unique (1,2,3,4,5,6) - ties broken arbitrarily
  rank:       ties get same rank, gaps follow (1,2,2,4,5,6)
  dense_rank: ties get same rank, no gaps    (1,2,2,3,4,5)
  ntile(3):   divides into 3 roughly equal buckets (1,1,2,2,3,3)

from pyspark.sql.functions import row_number, rank, dense_rank, ntile, desc
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

rank_window = Window.partitionBy('customer_type').orderBy(desc('kw'))

ranked = df \
    .withColumn('row_num', row_number().over(rank_window)) \
    .withColumn('rank', rank().over(rank_window)) \
    .withColumn('dense_rank', dense_rank().over(rank_window)) \
    .withColumn('quartile', ntile(4).over(rank_window))
Which Ranking Function to Use

Function Ties Gaps Best For row_number() Broken arbitrarily Never Deduplication, top-N per group, unique ordering rank() Same rank Yes (gaps after ties) Competition ranking, leaderboards dense_rank() Same rank No gaps Ranking when gaps are confusing (tier assignment) ntile(n) N/A (bucket assignment) N/A Percentile buckets, quartile analysis
Pattern: Top-N Per Group
One of the most common analytical patterns: find the top 3 highest-consuming meters per region.

from pyspark.sql.functions import row_number, desc, col
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

# Define window: partition by region, order by total consumption descending
top_window = Window.partitionBy('region').orderBy(desc('total_kw'))

# Rank within each region
ranked = daily_summary \
    .withColumn('rank', row_number().over(top_window))

# Filter to top 3
top_3_per_region = ranked.filter(col('rank') <= 3)
top_3_per_region.show()
Why row_number() for top-N? Because it guarantees exactly N results per group. rank() could return more than N if there are ties. Use rank() only when you intentionally want all tied rows included.
Pattern: Deduplication with row_number()
When duplicate records arrive (common in streaming and batch ingestion), use row_number() to keep only the latest:

# Keep the most recent reading per meter per date
dedup_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id', 'date') \
    .orderBy(desc('ingestion_timestamp'))

deduped = df \
    .withColumn('rn', row_number().over(dedup_window)) \
    .filter(col('rn') == 1) \
    .drop('rn')

# rn == 1 keeps the latest record for each meter_id + date
# This is a standard deduplication pattern in production pipelines.
Pattern: Percentile Analysis with ntile()

# Divide meters into consumption quartiles
quartile_window = Window.partitionBy('region').orderBy('total_kw')

quartile_df = monthly_summary \
    .withColumn('quartile', ntile(4).over(quartile_window)) \
    .withColumn('quartile_label',
        when(col('quartile') == 1, 'Bottom 25%')
        .when(col('quartile') == 2, '25-50%')
        .when(col('quartile') == 3, '50-75%')
        .otherwise('Top 25%')
    )

# Now you can compare behaviors across consumption tiers
quartile_df.groupBy('region', 'quartile_label').avg('total_kw').show()

sections.title: Lesson 4: Analytical Functions — lag(), lead(), and Neighbors

blocks.content: Analytical functions let you access values from other rows within the same partition. This is essential for time-series analysis: comparing today’s reading to yesterday’s, detecting changes, and calculating row-to-row differences.
lag() and lead()
lag() and lead() within a partition (ordered by date):

date       | kw   | lag(kw,1) | lead(kw,1)
  2026-02-20 | 100  |   NULL    |    200
  2026-02-21 | 200  |   100     |    180
  2026-02-22 | 180  |   200     |    450
  2026-02-23 | 450  |   180     |    210
  2026-02-24 | 210  |   450     |    NULL

  lag(kw, 1)  = previous row's kw  (look backward)
  lead(kw, 1) = next row's kw      (look forward)
  First row has no previous -> NULL
  Last row has no next     -> NULL

from pyspark.sql.functions import lag, lead, col
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

ts_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date')

with_neighbors = readings \
    .withColumn('prev_kw', lag('kw', 1).over(ts_window)) \
    .withColumn('next_kw', lead('kw', 1).over(ts_window)) \
    .withColumn('prev_2_kw', lag('kw', 2).over(ts_window))  # 2 rows back
Default Values for NULL Edges

# Provide a default for the first/last rows (no NULL)
with_defaults = readings \
    .withColumn('prev_kw', lag('kw', 1, 0.0).over(ts_window)) \
    .withColumn('next_kw', lead('kw', 1, 0.0).over(ts_window))

# lag(column, offset, default)
# The third argument replaces NULL at partition boundaries.
Pattern: Day-Over-Day Change

# Calculate absolute and percentage change from previous reading
change_df = readings \
    .withColumn('prev_kw', lag('kw', 1).over(ts_window)) \
    .withColumn('kw_change', col('kw') - col('prev_kw')) \
    .withColumn('kw_change_pct',
        when(col('prev_kw') != 0,
            ((col('kw') - col('prev_kw')) / col('prev_kw') * 100))
        .otherwise(None)
    )

# Show meters with >100% increase (potential anomaly or meter error)
change_df.filter(col('kw_change_pct') > 100).show()
Pattern: Spike Detection

# Detect sudden spikes: current reading > 3x the previous reading
spikes = readings \
    .withColumn('prev_kw', lag('kw', 1).over(ts_window)) \
    .withColumn('is_spike',
        (col('kw') > col('prev_kw') * 3) & col('prev_kw').isNotNull()
    ) \
    .filter(col('is_spike') == True)

# These records may indicate:
#   - Faulty meter
#   - Unauthorized consumption
#   - Legitimate event (HVAC startup in winter)
# Flag for investigation, don't auto-delete.
first_value() and last_value()

from pyspark.sql.functions import first, last

# Get the first and last reading for each meter in the month
month_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id') \
    .orderBy('date') \
    .rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.unboundedFollowing)

with_bounds = readings \
    .withColumn('first_reading', first('kw').over(month_window)) \
    .withColumn('last_reading', last('kw').over(month_window)) \
    .withColumn('month_delta', col('last_reading') - col('first_reading'))

# IMPORTANT: last() requires explicit frame to UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING
# Without it, the default frame (UNBOUNDED PRECEDING to CURRENT ROW)
# makes last() return the current row's value — NOT the partition's last.
Standard aggregate functions (sum, avg, count, min, max) can all be used as window functions. Instead of collapsing rows, they compute the aggregate across the window and attach it to every row.
Partition-Level Aggregates (No Frame)

from pyspark.sql.functions import sum, avg, count, min, max, col
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

# Aggregate across entire partition (no order, no frame)
type_window = Window.partitionBy('customer_type')

enriched = readings \
    .withColumn('type_avg_kw', avg('kw').over(type_window)) \
    .withColumn('type_total_kw', sum('kw').over(type_window)) \
    .withColumn('type_count', count('kw').over(type_window)) \
    .withColumn('type_min_kw', min('kw').over(type_window)) \
    .withColumn('type_max_kw', max('kw').over(type_window))

# Every row now has its group average, total, count, min, and max
# WITHOUT collapsing the data.
Pattern: Deviation from Group Average

# How does each meter compare to its customer type average?
deviation_df = enriched \
    .withColumn('deviation_from_avg',
        col('kw') - col('type_avg_kw')) \
    .withColumn('pct_of_avg',
        (col('kw') / col('type_avg_kw') * 100).cast('int'))

# Meters at 200%+ of average may be investigation targets
deviation_df.filter(col('pct_of_avg') > 200).show()
Pattern: Percentage of Total

# What percentage of regional consumption does each meter represent?
region_window = Window.partitionBy('region')

pct_df = readings \
    .withColumn('region_total', sum('kw').over(region_window)) \
    .withColumn('pct_of_region',
        (col('kw') / col('region_total') * 100))

# If one meter accounts for 15% of a region's consumption,
# that's an industrial facility or a data error.

Running calculations accumulate values as you move through the ordered partition. They answer questions like: "What is the total consumption so far this month?" or "At what point did we cross the 10,000 kW threshold?"
Running Sum (Cumulative Total)
Running sum over ordered partition:

# Running sum: total consumption from month start to current date
running_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id', 'month') \
    .orderBy('date') \
    .rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)

cumulative = readings \
    .withColumn('running_total_kw', sum('kw').over(running_window)) \
    .withColumn('running_avg_kw', avg('kw').over(running_window)) \
    .withColumn('running_max_kw', max('kw').over(running_window)) \
    .withColumn('reading_count', count('kw').over(running_window))
Pattern: Budget Threshold Detection

# Find the date when each meter crossed its monthly budget
MONTHLY_BUDGET = 5000  # kW

budget_df = readings \
    .withColumn('running_total', sum('kw').over(running_window)) \
    .withColumn('over_budget', col('running_total') > MONTHLY_BUDGET)

# First date of budget exceedance per meter per month
first_over = budget_df.filter(col('over_budget') == True)

first_breach_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id', 'month') \
    .orderBy('date')

first_breach = first_over \
    .withColumn('rn', row_number().over(first_breach_window)) \
    .filter(col('rn') == 1) \
    .select('meter_id', 'month', 'date', 'running_total')
first_breach.show() # Shows: Meter M001 crossed 5000 kW on Feb 15 (cumulative: 5120 kW)
Running Count and Cumulative Distribution

from pyspark.sql.functions import percent_rank, cume_dist

dist_window = Window.partitionBy('customer_type').orderBy('kw')

distribution = readings \
    .withColumn('percent_rank', percent_rank().over(dist_window)) \
    .withColumn('cume_dist', cume_dist().over(dist_window))

# percent_rank: proportion of rows with value < current row
#   Value 0.0 = lowest in partition
#   Value 1.0 = highest in partition

# cume_dist: proportion of rows with value <= current row
#   Always > 0, always reaches 1.0 at the last row

# Find meters at the 95th percentile of consumption
distribution.filter(col('percent_rank') >= 0.95).show()
Rolling windows compute aggregates over a fixed-size sliding window of rows. They are essential for smoothing noisy time-series data and detecting trends.
Row-Based Rolling Average
7-day rolling average (rowsBetween(-6, 0)):

date       | kw   | rolling_avg_7d
  Feb 01     | 100  | 100.0          (only 1 row available)
  Feb 02     | 200  | 150.0          (2 rows)
  Feb 03     | 150  | 150.0          (3 rows)
  Feb 04     | 300  | 187.5          (4 rows)
  Feb 05     | 250  | 200.0          (5 rows)
  Feb 06     | 180  | 196.7          (6 rows)
  Feb 07     | 220  | 200.0          (7 rows: full window)
  Feb 08     | 190  | 212.9          (Feb 02-08: 7 rows)
  Feb 09     | 300  | 227.1          (Feb 03-09: 7 rows)
Window slides: each row uses itself + 6 preceding rows.

# 7-day rolling average per meter
rolling_7d = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date') \
    .rowsBetween(-6, 0)

smoothed = readings \
    .withColumn('rolling_avg_7d', avg('kw').over(rolling_7d)) \
    .withColumn('rolling_min_7d', min('kw').over(rolling_7d)) \
    .withColumn('rolling_max_7d', max('kw').over(rolling_7d)) \
    .withColumn('rolling_sum_7d', sum('kw').over(rolling_7d))
Time-Based Rolling Window with rangeBetween
rowsBetween counts physical rows. If data has gaps (missing days), a 7-row window might span more than 7 days. Use rangeBetween for true time-based windows:

# Convert date to seconds-since-epoch for rangeBetween
from pyspark.sql.functions import unix_timestamp, col

readings_ts = readings \
    .withColumn('date_seconds', unix_timestamp('date'))

# 7-day range window (7 * 86400 seconds)
SEVEN_DAYS = 7 * 86400

range_7d = Window.partitionBy('meter_id') \
    .orderBy('date_seconds') \
    .rangeBetween(-SEVEN_DAYS, 0)

# True 7-day rolling average (handles missing days correctly)
time_smoothed = readings_ts \
    .withColumn('rolling_avg_7d', avg('kw').over(range_7d)) \
    .withColumn('readings_in_window', count('kw').over(range_7d))

# readings_in_window tells you how many data points are in each window.
# If a meter has gaps, some windows will have fewer readings.
Pattern: Anomaly Detection with Rolling Statistics

from pyspark.sql.functions import stddev, abs as spark_abs

# 30-day rolling mean and standard deviation
rolling_30d = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date') \
    .rowsBetween(-29, 0)

anomaly_df = readings \
    .withColumn('rolling_mean', avg('kw').over(rolling_30d)) \
    .withColumn('rolling_std', stddev('kw').over(rolling_30d)) \
    .withColumn('z_score',
        when(col('rolling_std') > 0,
            spark_abs(col('kw') - col('rolling_mean')) / col('rolling_std'))
        .otherwise(0)
    ) \
    .withColumn('is_anomaly', col('z_score') > 3.0)

# Readings with z-score > 3 are more than 3 standard deviations
# from the rolling mean — statistical outliers.
anomaly_df.filter(col('is_anomaly')).show()

sections.title: Lesson 8: Gap Filling and Missing Data Patterns

blocks.content: Time-series data from smart meters often has gaps: missing readings due to network outages, maintenance, or transmission failures. Window functions provide elegant solutions for detecting and filling these gaps.
Detecting Gaps

from pyspark.sql.functions import lag, datediff, col

ts_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date')

gap_analysis = readings \
    .withColumn('prev_date', lag('date', 1).over(ts_window)) \
    .withColumn('days_since_prev', datediff(col('date'), col('prev_date')))

# Rows with days_since_prev > 1 indicate missing days
gaps = gap_analysis.filter(col('days_since_prev') > 1)
gaps.select('meter_id', 'prev_date', 'date', 'days_since_prev').show()

# Example output:
# meter_id | prev_date  | date       | days_since_prev
# M001     | 2026-02-10 | 2026-02-14 | 4   <-- 3 missing days
# M003     | 2026-02-20 | 2026-02-23 | 3   <-- 2 missing days
Forward Fill (Last Observation Carried Forward)
A common imputation strategy: use the last known value to fill missing readings.

from pyspark.sql.functions import last, col

# After exploding date ranges to include missing dates,
# fill NULLs with the last known value.

fill_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date') \
    .rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)

filled = readings_with_gaps \
    .withColumn('kw_filled',
        last('kw', ignorenulls=True).over(fill_window))

# last(..., ignorenulls=True) skips NULLs and carries forward
# the most recent non-NULL value.

# Before fill:  100, NULL, NULL, NULL, 200, NULL, 180
# After fill:   100, 100,  100,  100,  200, 200,  180
Linear Interpolation

# For numeric time series, interpolate between known values
from pyspark.sql.functions import first as first_val, last as last_val
from pyspark.sql.functions import row_number, count, col, when

# Step 1: Identify boundaries (non-null values)
interp_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date')

# Step 2: Forward-fill and backward-fill to get boundary values
fwd_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date') \
    .rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)
bwd_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date') \
    .rowsBetween(Window.currentRow, Window.unboundedFollowing)

interpolated = readings_with_gaps \
    .withColumn('prev_known', last_val('kw', ignorenulls=True).over(fwd_window)) \
    .withColumn('next_known', first_val('kw', ignorenulls=True).over(bwd_window)) \
    .withColumn('kw_interpolated',
        when(col('kw').isNotNull(), col('kw'))
        .otherwise((col('prev_known') + col('next_known')) / 2)
    )

# Simplified linear interpolation using average of boundaries.
# For production, use distance-weighted interpolation based on
# position between the two known values.

sections.title: Lesson 9: Session Detection and State Change Analysis

blocks.content: Window functions can detect transitions and group consecutive rows into sessions. This is powerful for analyzing equipment states, outage periods, and behavioral patterns.
Detecting State Changes

# Detect when a meter transitions between usage tiers
from pyspark.sql.functions import lag, col, when, sum as spark_sum

ts_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date')

# Step 1: Identify transitions
state_changes = readings \
    .withColumn('prev_tier', lag('usage_tier', 1).over(ts_window)) \
    .withColumn('tier_changed',
        when(col('usage_tier') != col('prev_tier'), 1).otherwise(0)
    )

# Step 2: Create session IDs using running sum of changes
session_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date') \
    .rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)

sessions = state_changes \
    .withColumn('session_id', spark_sum('tier_changed').over(session_window))

# Now consecutive rows with the same tier share a session_id.
# You can aggregate per session to find duration, avg consumption, etc.
Session Detection via Running Sum:

date  | tier   | prev_tier | changed | session_id
  Feb 1 | LOW    | NULL      |   0     |    0
  Feb 2 | LOW    | LOW       |   0     |    0
  Feb 3 | MEDIUM | LOW       |   1     |    1       <-- state change
  Feb 4 | MEDIUM | MEDIUM    |   0     |    1
  Feb 5 | MEDIUM | MEDIUM    |   0     |    1
  Feb 6 | HIGH   | MEDIUM    |   1     |    2       <-- state change
  Feb 7 | HIGH   | HIGH      |   0     |    2
  Feb 8 | LOW    | HIGH      |   1     |    3       <-- state change
Each session = a continuous period in the same tier.
Pattern: Outage Duration Analysis

# Find continuous periods where voltage was below threshold (outage)
from pyspark.sql.functions import min as spark_min, max as spark_max, count

voltage_states = readings \
    .withColumn('is_low_voltage', (col('voltage') < 220).cast('int')) \
    .withColumn('prev_state', lag('is_low_voltage', 1).over(ts_window)) \
    .withColumn('state_changed',
        when(col('is_low_voltage') != col('prev_state'), 1).otherwise(0)) \
    .withColumn('session_id',
        spark_sum('state_changed').over(session_window))

# Aggregate per session: find outage start, end, and duration
outages = voltage_states \
    .filter(col('is_low_voltage') == 1) \
    .groupBy('meter_id', 'session_id') \
    .agg(
        spark_min('date').alias('outage_start'),
        spark_max('date').alias('outage_end'),
        count('*').alias('outage_days'),
    ) \
    .orderBy('meter_id', 'outage_start')
outages.show()
Pattern: Year-Over-Year Comparison

# Compare each month's consumption to the same month last year
from pyspark.sql.functions import lag, col, year, month as spark_month

monthly = readings \
    .groupBy('meter_id', year('date').alias('year'),
             spark_month('date').alias('month')) \
    .agg(sum('kw').alias('monthly_kw'))

yoy_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id', 'month').orderBy('year')

yoy = monthly \
    .withColumn('prev_year_kw', lag('monthly_kw', 1).over(yoy_window)) \
    .withColumn('yoy_change',
        col('monthly_kw') - col('prev_year_kw')) \
    .withColumn('yoy_change_pct',
        when(col('prev_year_kw') > 0,
            (col('yoy_change') / col('prev_year_kw') * 100))
        .otherwise(None)
    )

# Shows: Meter M001, Feb 2026: 4500 kW, Feb 2025: 4200 kW, +7.1%
Pattern: Consecutive Days Above Threshold

# Find the longest consecutive streak of days where kw > 500

streaks = readings \
    .withColumn('above_threshold', (col('kw') > 500).cast('int')) \
    .withColumn('prev_above', lag('above_threshold', 1, 0).over(ts_window)) \
    .withColumn('streak_start',
        when((col('above_threshold') == 1) & (col('prev_above') == 0), 1)
        .otherwise(0)) \
    .withColumn('streak_id',
        spark_sum('streak_start').over(session_window))

# Count streak length
streak_lengths = streaks \
    .filter(col('above_threshold') == 1) \
    .groupBy('meter_id', 'streak_id') \
    .agg(count('*').alias('streak_days'),
         spark_min('date').alias('streak_start_date'),
         spark_max('date').alias('streak_end_date'))

# Longest streak per meter
longest_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy(desc('streak_days'))
longest = streak_lengths \
    .withColumn('rn', row_number().over(longest_window)) \
    .filter(col('rn') == 1)
Pattern: Moving Ratio (Efficiency Tracking)

# 7-day rolling ratio of peak kw to average kw (load factor)
rolling_7d = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date') \
    .rowsBetween(-6, 0)

load_factor = readings \
    .withColumn('rolling_avg', avg('kw').over(rolling_7d)) \
    .withColumn('rolling_max', max('kw').over(rolling_7d)) \
    .withColumn('load_factor',
        when(col('rolling_max') > 0,
            col('rolling_avg') / col('rolling_max'))
        .otherwise(None)
    )

# Load factor close to 1.0 = steady consumption
# Load factor close to 0.0 = spiky consumption (inefficient)
# Useful for identifying meters that could benefit from load balancing.

sections.title: Lesson 11: Window Functions in Spark SQL

blocks.content: Every window function available in the DataFrame API is also available in SQL syntax. For analysts who prefer SQL, the syntax maps directly:
SQL Window Syntax

-- Ranking: top 3 meters per region SELECT * FROM ( SELECT meter_id, region, total_kw, ROW_NUMBER() OVER ( PARTITION BY region ORDER BY total_kw DESC ) AS rank FROM gold.consumption_summary ) WHERE rank <= 3;

-- Day-over-day change SELECT meter_id, date, kw, LAG(kw, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY meter_id ORDER BY date) AS prev_kw, kw - LAG(kw, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY meter_id ORDER BY date) AS daily_change FROM silver.meter_readings;

-- Named window (reusable within a query) SELECT meter_id, date, kw, AVG(kw) OVER meter_ts AS rolling_avg_7d, MIN(kw) OVER meter_ts AS rolling_min_7d, MAX(kw) OVER meter_ts AS rolling_max_7d, SUM(kw) OVER meter_ts AS rolling_sum_7d FROM silver.meter_readings WINDOW meter_ts AS ( PARTITION BY meter_id ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW );
The WINDOW clause defines a named window that can be referenced by multiple functions, avoiding repetition.

-- Running total with explicit frame SELECT meter_id, date, kw, SUM(kw) OVER ( PARTITION BY meter_id ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW ) AS running_total FROM silver.meter_readings;

-- Cumulative distribution SELECT meter_id, kw, customer_type, PERCENT_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_type ORDER BY kw) AS pct_rank, NTILE(10) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_type ORDER BY kw) AS decile FROM silver.meter_readings;

sections.title: Lesson 12: Window Function Performance in Distributed Systems

blocks.content: Window functions have specific performance characteristics in Spark. Understanding them prevents surprises on large datasets.
How Spark Executes Window Functions
Execution of: sum('kw').over(Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date'))
Step 1: SHUFFLE (Exchange hashpartitioning on meter_id) All rows for the same meter_id -> same executor
Step 2: SORT within each partition by date
Step 3: COMPUTE the window function locally on each executor
Cost: shuffle (network) + sort (CPU + memory)
Same cost as groupBy() on the same key. Window functions are NOT free — they require shuffle.
Performance Guidelines

Guideline Why Example Filter before windowing Fewer rows = less shuffle and sort Filter to one month before computing rolling avg Select only needed columns before windowing Less data shuffled across network drop unused columns first Avoid multiple different partitionBy keys Each distinct key causes a separate shuffle Combine into one window spec if possible Use row-based frames over range-based when possible Row frames are cheaper to compute rowsBetween(-6,0) vs rangeBetween(...) Beware data skew in partition keys One meter with 10M readings creates a straggler Salt heavy keys or pre-aggregate Cache before multiple window passes Avoid re-reading and re-shuffling the same data df.cache() before multiple withColumn(window)
Combining Multiple Windows Efficiently
If multiple window functions share the same partitionBy and orderBy, Spark can compute them in a single shuffle pass:

# EFFICIENT: Same window spec = one shuffle
ts_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date')

result = readings \
    .withColumn('prev_kw', lag('kw', 1).over(ts_window)) \
    .withColumn('rank', row_number().over(ts_window)) \
    .withColumn('running_sum', sum('kw').over(
        ts_window.rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)))

# All three use the same partition + order -> ONE shuffle.

# EXPENSIVE: Different partition keys = separate shuffles
by_meter = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date')
by_region = Window.partitionBy('region').orderBy('date')

result = readings \
    .withColumn('meter_rank', row_number().over(by_meter)) \
    .withColumn('region_rank', row_number().over(by_region))

# Two different partition keys -> TWO separate shuffles.
# If possible, compute one at a time and cache between.
Execution Plan Inspection

# Always check the plan for window functions
result.explain('formatted')

# Look for:
#   Exchange hashpartitioning(meter_id)  <-- shuffle
#   Sort [meter_id ASC, date ASC]        <-- sort within partition
#   Window [sum(kw), lag(kw)]            <-- window computation

# Multiple Exchange nodes = multiple shuffles.
# Try to minimize by using consistent partition keys.
All Window Functions at a Glance

Category Function Requires orderBy? Description Ranking row_number() Yes Unique sequential number (1,2,3...), ties broken arbitrarily Ranking rank() Yes Same rank for ties, gaps after (1,2,2,4) Ranking dense_rank() Yes Same rank for ties, no gaps (1,2,2,3) Ranking ntile(n) Yes Divide into n roughly equal buckets Ranking percent_rank() Yes Relative rank as fraction (0.0 to 1.0) Ranking cume_dist() Yes Cumulative distribution (fraction of rows <= current) Analytical lag(col, n, default) Yes Value from n rows before current Analytical lead(col, n, default) Yes Value from n rows after current Analytical first(col, ignorenulls) Recommended First value in frame (needs explicit frame for last) Analytical last(col, ignorenulls) Recommended Last value in frame (MUST set frame to unbounded following) Analytical nth_value(col, n) Yes Value from nth row in window frame Aggregate sum(col) Optional Sum across window frame Aggregate avg(col) Optional Average across window frame Aggregate count(col) Optional Count across window frame Aggregate min(col) Optional Minimum across window frame Aggregate max(col) Optional Maximum across window frame Aggregate stddev(col) Optional Standard deviation across window frame Aggregate collect_list(col) Optional Collect values in frame as array
Common Window Specifications Quick Reference

Pattern Window Specification Use Case Partition aggregate partitionBy('key') (no order, no frame) Group total/avg attached to every row Ranking partitionBy('key').orderBy(desc('value')) Top-N, deduplication, percentile Time series navigation partitionBy('id').orderBy('date') lag/lead, day-over-day change Running total partitionBy('id').orderBy('date').rowsBetween(unbounded, current) Cumulative sum, budget tracking Rolling average partitionBy('id').orderBy('date').rowsBetween(-6, 0) 7-day moving average, smoothing Full partition with order partitionBy('id').orderBy('date').rowsBetween(unbounded, unbounded) first/last value in partition
You now understand:

items: The fundamental difference between groupBy (collapses rows) and window functions (preserves rows, adds context)
The three components of a window specification: partitionBy (groups), orderBy (sequence), and frame (which surrounding rows)
rowsBetween (physical row count) vs rangeBetween (logical value range) and when to use each
The default frame trap: with orderBy, Spark defaults to UNBOUNDED PRECEDING to CURRENT ROW (running calculation, not full partition)
Ranking functions: row_number (unique, for top-N and dedup), rank (ties with gaps), dense_rank (ties without gaps), ntile (bucket assignment), percent_rank, cume_dist
Analytical functions: lag/lead for row-to-row comparison, day-over-day change, spike detection; first/last with explicit frames
Aggregate window functions: sum, avg, count, min, max over partitions for deviation analysis, percentage of total, and contextual enrichment
Running and cumulative calculations: running sums for budget tracking, cumulative distribution for percentile analysis
Rolling (sliding) windows: 7-day moving averages, rolling statistics for anomaly detection with z-scores
Gap detection and filling: using lag to find missing dates, forward fill with last(ignorenulls=True), linear interpolation with boundary values
Session detection: state change identification via lag + running sum pattern, outage duration analysis, consecutive streak counting
Advanced patterns: year-over-year comparison, load factor calculation, longest consecutive streak
SQL window syntax including the WINDOW clause for named, reusable window specifications
Performance: window functions trigger shuffle; share partition keys to minimize shuffles; filter and select before windowing; inspect execution plans for Exchange nodes

blocks.content: You are no longer limited to aggregate summaries. You can now enrich every row with the full context of its group, its history, and its neighbors — the foundation of time-series analytics, anomaly detection, and operational intelligence.

In Module 16, we go deeper into Advanced Performance Engineering — Adaptive Query Execution, Dynamic Partition Pruning, bucketing, Spark UI mastery, and the diagnostic workflow of a senior Spark engineer.

chapters.title: Module 16: Advanced Performance Engineering
chapters.description: The Senior Engineer’s Diagnostic Toolkit Master the advanced performance features of modern Spark — Adaptive Query Execution, Dynamic Partition Pruning, bucketing, bloom filters, join hints, and the Spark UI — and learn the systematic diagnostic workflow that separates senior Spark engineers from everyone else.

blocks.content: Module 4 taught you the fundamentals of performance tuning: what shuffle is, why spilling hurts, how to read execution plans, and when to use broadcast joins or salting.
That was the foundation. This is the advanced floor.

Modern Spark (3.x and beyond) includes a suite of automatic optimizations that did not exist when Spark was first designed. Adaptive Query Execution rewrites your query plan mid-flight based on actual data statistics. Dynamic Partition Pruning eliminates irrelevant partitions at runtime. Bucketing pre-organizes data to skip shuffles entirely. These features can turn a 2-hour job into a 10-minute job without changing a single line of your transformation code.

But these features are not magic. They have prerequisites, limitations, and failure modes. This module teaches you exactly when they fire, why they sometimes do not, and how to diagnose performance issues systematically using the Spark UI.
The Performance Engineering Mindset:

Junior:  "It's slow. Let me add more executors."
  Mid:     "It's slow. Let me check for skew and broadcast small tables."
  Senior:  "It's slow. Let me read the execution plan, check the Spark UI
            for spill and skew, verify AQE is firing, inspect partition
            pruning, and measure before and after each change."
This module teaches the senior workflow.

Before Spark 3.0, the Catalyst optimizer made all decisions before execution, based on estimated statistics. If those estimates were wrong — and they often were — the chosen plan could be catastrophically suboptimal.

Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) changes this. It collects real statistics during execution and re-optimizes the remaining plan based on actual data. It is the single most impactful performance feature in modern Spark.
How AQE Works
Traditional Execution (pre-AQE):

Logical Plan --> Catalyst --> Physical Plan --> Execute ALL stages
                  (estimates)   (fixed)           (hope for the best)
AQE Execution (Spark 3.x):

Logical Plan --> Catalyst --> Physical Plan --> Execute Stage 1
                  (estimates)   (initial)              |
                                                 Collect REAL stats
                                                       |
                                                 Re-optimize Plan
                                                       |
                                                 Execute Stage 2
                                                       |
                                                 Collect REAL stats
                                                       |
                                                 Re-optimize Plan
                                                       |
                                                 Execute Stage 3
The plan ADAPTS between stages based on actual data.
Enabling AQE

# AQE is enabled by default in Spark 3.2+
# To verify or enable explicitly:
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.enabled', 'true')

# Check current setting
print(spark.conf.get('spark.sql.adaptive.enabled'))
AQE Feature 1: Coalescing Shuffle Partitions

The default shuffle partition count is 200 (spark.sql.shuffle.partitions). For a 10 GB dataset, that is reasonable. For a 100 MB dataset after filtering, 200 partitions means 200 tiny tasks of 500 KB each — massive scheduling overhead.
AQE automatically coalesces small partitions after a shuffle:
Without AQE: With AQE:

200 partitions after shuffle         200 partitions after shuffle
  Many are tiny (< 1 MB)                    |
  200 tasks, most finish instantly     AQE measures partition sizes
  Scheduler overhead dominates               |
                                       Coalesces tiny partitions
                                             |
                                       40 balanced partitions
                                       40 tasks, each ~2.5 MB
                                       Much less scheduler overhead

# AQE coalescing configuration
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled', 'true')  # default: true
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.minPartitionSize', '1m')  # minimum
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes', '128m')  # target size
Practical impact: With AQE coalescing enabled, you can leave spark.sql.shuffle.partitions at its default (200) and let AQE adjust. This eliminates one of the most common tuning headaches.
AQE Feature 2: Converting Sort-Merge Join to Broadcast Join
Catalyst chooses a join strategy based on estimated table sizes. If it overestimates a table’s size, it picks a sort-merge join (expensive shuffle on both sides). AQE fixes this at runtime:

Catalyst estimate: Table A = 500 MB  (too large for broadcast)
Planned strategy:  Sort-Merge Join   (shuffle both tables)
After Stage 1 runs, AQE discovers: Actual Table A after filtering = 8 MB (easily broadcastable!)
AQE re-plans: Switch to Broadcast Hash Join (no shuffle on large table)
Result: Massive reduction in network I/O and execution time.

# AQE broadcast threshold (uses the standard threshold)
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold', '10m')  # default

# AQE decides AFTER filtering, so it uses the post-filter size,
# not the pre-filter estimate. This is much more accurate.
AQE Feature 3: Optimizing Skew Joins
In Module 4, you learned to handle skew manually with salting. AQE can detect and fix skew automatically:
Without AQE (skewed join):

Partition A: 900M rows (RESIDENTIAL)   <-- straggler
  Partition B:  80M rows (COMMERCIAL)
  Partition C:  20M rows (INDUSTRIAL)
Total time = time of slowest partition = Partition A
With AQE skew optimization:
AQE detects: Partition A is 10x larger than median. AQE splits Partition A into smaller sub-partitions:

Partition A1: 300M rows   \
  Partition A2: 300M rows    > processed in parallel
  Partition A3: 300M rows   /
  Partition B:   80M rows
  Partition C:   20M rows
Total time reduced by ~3x with no code changes.

# AQE skew join configuration
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled', 'true')  # default: true

# A partition is "skewed" if it is N times larger than the median
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor', '5')  # default: 5

# AND larger than this absolute threshold
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes', '256m')

When AQE skew optimization does NOT help: It only works for joins, not for groupBy aggregations. If your groupBy has a skewed key, you still need manual salting (Module 4). AQE cannot split a single aggregation partition.
AQE Summary

Feature What It Does Replaces Manual Work Coalesce partitions Merges small post-shuffle partitions Manual coalesce(), tuning shuffle.partitions Broadcast conversion Switches to broadcast join if runtime size is small Manual broadcast() hints Skew join optimization Splits skewed partitions automatically Manual salting for join skew

Dynamic Partition Pruning eliminates reading irrelevant data partitions at runtime, based on the results of another query. It is most impactful for star-schema joins between large fact tables and small dimension tables.
The Problem DPP Solves
Star-Schema Query Without DPP:

SELECT sum(kw) FROM meter_readings r
  JOIN meter_metadata m ON r.meter_id = m.meter_id
  WHERE m.region = 'NORTHEAST'

Step 1: Scan ALL of meter_readings (1 TB)       <-- wasteful
  Step 2: Scan meter_metadata, filter to NORTHEAST
  Step 3: Join (shuffle 1 TB + tiny table)
Problem: Only 10% of readings belong to NORTHEAST meters, but Spark reads ALL readings because the filter is on the dimension table, not the fact table.
With DPP:

Step 1: Scan meter_metadata, filter to NORTHEAST Step 2: Collect NORTHEAST meter_ids Step 3: Push those IDs into the fact table scan as a filter Step 4: Scan only NORTHEAST partitions of meter_readings Step 5: Join (much less data)
Result: Read 100 GB instead of 1 TB. 10x less I/O.
When DPP Fires
DPP activates when all of these conditions are met:

items: The fact table is partitioned by the join key (or a column related to the join key)
The dimension table filter significantly reduces the set of matching partition values
The dimension table is small enough to collect its filtered keys on the Driver
spark.sql.optimizer.dynamicPartitionPruning.enabled is true (default in Spark 3.x)

blocks.content:
# DPP configuration
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.optimizer.dynamicPartitionPruning.enabled', 'true')  # default

# For DPP to work, the fact table must be file-partitioned:
# /data/meter_readings/region=NORTHEAST/
# /data/meter_readings/region=SOUTHEAST/
# /data/meter_readings/region=MIDWEST/
# /data/meter_readings/region=WEST/

# Then this query benefits from DPP:
spark.sql("""
    SELECT sum(r.kw)
    FROM meter_readings r
    JOIN meter_metadata m ON r.region = m.region
    WHERE m.state = 'New York'
""")

# Spark first filters metadata to New York,
# finds that only region='NORTHEAST' qualifies,
# and reads ONLY the NORTHEAST partition of the fact table.
Verifying DPP in the Execution Plan

# Check the physical plan for DynamicPruningExpression
result.explain('formatted')

# Look for this in the plan:
#   +- FileScan parquet [kw, region]
#      PartitionFilters: [isnotnull(region),
#        dynamicpruningexpression(region IN subquery)]

# 'dynamicpruningexpression' confirms DPP is active.
# If you don't see it, check:
#   - Is the fact table partitioned by the join key?
#   - Is the dimension filter selective enough?
#   - Is DPP enabled in configuration?
DPP vs Static Partition Pruning

Type When Filter Is Known Example Mechanism Static At compile time (literal value) WHERE region = 'NORTHEAST' Catalyst skips partitions before execution Dynamic At runtime (from subquery/join) WHERE region IN (subquery from dimension) DPP pushes runtime values into scan
Static pruning works when the filter value is a constant in your query. DPP extends this to filters that come from joins — which is the common case in star-schema analytics.

sections.title: Lesson 4: Bucketing — Pre-Organizing Data to Eliminate Shuffle

blocks.content: Shuffle is expensive. Broadcast joins avoid it for small tables. But what about joining two large tables? Normally, both must be shuffled by the join key.
Bucketing pre-sorts and pre-distributes data at write time so that future joins and aggregations on the bucketed column can skip shuffle entirely.
How Bucketing Works
Without Bucketing (join two large tables):

meter_readings (1 TB)    weather_data (500 GB)
       |                        |
   [SHUFFLE by meter_id]    [SHUFFLE by meter_id]
       |                        |
       +--------JOIN-----------+
                 |
           Network cost: ~1.5 TB transferred
With Bucketing (both tables bucketed by meter_id, 256 buckets):

meter_readings             weather_data
  bucket_0  [M001, M257...]  bucket_0  [M001, M257...]
  bucket_1  [M002, M258...]  bucket_1  [M002, M258...]
  ...                        ...
  bucket_255 [M256, ...]     bucket_255 [M256, ...]
Bucket 0 of readings joins with bucket 0 of weather. No shuffle needed. Data is already co-located.
Network cost: 0 bytes transferred
Creating Bucketed Tables

# Write a bucketed table (must use saveAsTable, not save)
readings.write \
    .bucketBy(256, 'meter_id') \
    .sortBy('meter_id') \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .saveAsTable('bucketed_readings')

weather.write \
    .bucketBy(256, 'meter_id') \
    .sortBy('meter_id') \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .saveAsTable('bucketed_weather')

# CRITICAL: Both tables must have the SAME number of buckets
# and be bucketed on the SAME column for shuffle-free joins.
Joining Bucketed Tables

# Disable auto-broadcast to see the bucketing benefit
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold', '-1')

bucketed_join = spark.table('bucketed_readings') \
    .join(spark.table('bucketed_weather'), 'meter_id')

bucketed_join.explain('formatted')
# Look for: NO Exchange (shuffle) nodes in the plan
# Instead: SortMergeJoin directly on bucketed data
Bucketing Requirements and Limitations

Requirement Detail Must use saveAsTable Bucketing writes to the Hive metastore; .save() to paths does not support it Same bucket count Both sides of a join must have identical bucket count Same bucket column Both tables must be bucketed on the join key Sorted within buckets sortBy() ensures efficient sort-merge join within each bucket Read via spark.table() Reading by path may lose bucketing metadata Not supported in Delta Lake As of Spark 3.x, Delta does not support Hive-style bucketing (use Z-ORDER instead)
When to Use Bucketing

Scenario Bucketing Benefit Alternative Two large tables joined frequently on the same key Eliminates shuffle on every join Pre-join and materialize Large table joined with many small tables on the same key Eliminates shuffle for each join Broadcast the small tables Frequent aggregations on the same column Eliminates shuffle for groupBy Repartition and cache Ad-hoc joins on unpredictable keys No benefit (wrong bucket column) AQE + broadcast

Bucketing is a write-time investment for read-time savings. It costs extra at write (sorting and distributing) but pays back on every subsequent join or aggregation. Use it for tables that are read and joined far more often than they are written.

sections.title: Lesson 5: Data Skipping — Bloom Filters and Z-ORDER

blocks.content: Predicate pushdown (Module 2) skips Parquet row groups when column statistics show no matching values. Data skipping extends this concept with more advanced structures.
Z-ORDER (Delta Lake)
We introduced Z-ORDER in Module 11 for table maintenance. Here we examine its performance impact in depth.
Z-ORDER is a multi-dimensional clustering technique. It co-locates rows with similar values across multiple columns within the same files, dramatically improving file-level pruning.
Without Z-ORDER (random file distribution):

File 1: meter_ids [M001, M500, M999, M042, M777]  regions [NE, SW, W, SE, MW]
  File 2: meter_ids [M003, M888, M050, M444, M100]  regions [MW, NE, SE, W, NE]
  File 3: meter_ids [M200, M001, M600, M333, M888]  regions [SE, NE, MW, W, NE]
Query: WHERE meter_id = 'M001' Must read: File 1 + File 3 (M001 is scattered) Query: WHERE region = 'NORTHEAST' Must read: All 3 files (NE appears in every file)
With Z-ORDER BY (meter_id, region):

File 1: meter_ids [M001-M100]  regions [MW, NE]
  File 2: meter_ids [M100-M500]  regions [NE, SE]
  File 3: meter_ids [M500-M999]  regions [SE, SW, W]
Query: WHERE meter_id = 'M001' Reads: File 1 only (M001 is in the M001-M100 range) Query: WHERE region = 'NORTHEAST' Reads: File 1 + File 2 only (NE only in those files)
Data skipping eliminates 33-66% of I/O.
-- Apply Z-ORDER on frequently filtered columns OPTIMIZE silver.meter_readings ZORDER BY (meter_id, date);

-- Choose Z-ORDER columns based on common query patterns:
-- 1. Columns frequently in WHERE clauses
-- 2. High-cardinality columns benefit more than low-cardinality
-- 3. Limit to 2-4 columns (effectiveness decreases with more)
Bloom Filter Indexes

A bloom filter is a probabilistic data structure that can answer: "Is value X definitely NOT in this file?" It can have false positives (says maybe when the answer is no) but never false negatives (if it says no, the value is definitely absent).
-- Delta Lake: Create a bloom filter index (Databricks) CREATE BLOOMFILTER INDEX ON TABLE silver.meter_readings FOR COLUMNS (meter_id) OPTIONS (fpp = 0.01, numItems = 1000000);

-- fpp: false positive probability (0.01 = 1%)
-- numItems: expected distinct values

-- Now when querying:
-- SELECT * FROM silver.meter_readings WHERE meter_id = 'M12345'
-- Spark checks the bloom filter for each file.
-- If bloom filter says 'definitely not here', the file is skipped.
-- Typically skips 90%+ of files for point lookups.
Data Skipping Strategy

Technique How It Works Best For Platform Parquet min/max stats Skip row groups where column range excludes filter value Range queries on sorted data All (built into Parquet) File-level partition pruning Skip entire directories based on partition column Low-cardinality filters (date, region) All (Hive-style partitioning) Z-ORDER Cluster related values in same files for multi-column pruning Point lookups and range filters on 2-4 columns Delta Lake Bloom filter Probabilistic check: value definitely not in file Exact-match lookups on high-cardinality columns Delta Lake (Databricks) Dynamic Partition Pruning Runtime filter from join pushes into fact table scan Star-schema joins Spark 3.x
Catalyst chooses join strategies based on statistics. When it chooses wrong — because statistics are stale, estimated incorrectly, or unavailable — you can override the decision with join hints.
Available Join Hints

Hint Forces Strategy When To Use BROADCAST / BROADCASTJOIN / MAPJOIN Broadcast hash join Small table not auto-detected as broadcastable MERGE / SHUFFLE_MERGE / MERGEJOIN Sort-merge join Both tables large, sorted, or bucketed SHUFFLE_HASH Shuffle hash join One table significantly smaller but too large for broadcast SHUFFLE_REPLICATE_NL Shuffle-and-replicate nested loop Non-equi joins (rare, expensive)
DataFrame API Hints

from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

# Force broadcast join (DataFrame API)
result = readings.join(broadcast(metadata), 'meter_id')

# Hint syntax (alternative)
result = readings.hint('broadcast').join(metadata, 'meter_id')

# Force sort-merge join (even if one table is small)
result = readings.hint('merge').join(weather, 'date')

# Force shuffle hash join
result = readings.hint('shuffle_hash').join(tariffs, 'tariff_code')
SQL Hints
-- SQL broadcast hint SELECT /*+ BROADCAST(m) */ r.*, m.region FROM meter_readings r JOIN meter_metadata m ON r.meter_id = m.meter_id;
-- SQL merge join hint SELECT /*+ MERGE(r, w) */ r.*, w.temperature FROM meter_readings r JOIN weather_data w ON r.date = w.date AND r.region = w.region;
-- SQL shuffle hash hint SELECT /*+ SHUFFLE_HASH(t) */ r.*, t.rate FROM meter_readings r JOIN tariff_codes t ON r.tariff_code = t.tariff_code;
When to Use Hints (and When Not To)

Situation Recommendation AQE is enabled and working Usually no hints needed — AQE adapts at runtime Table statistics are stale or missing ANALYZE TABLE first; use hints only as a last resort You KNOW a table is small but Spark doesn't Use broadcast() hint Bucketed tables not recognized Use merge hint to force sort-merge join Performance regression after Spark upgrade Hints can lock in the previous strategy while investigating General coding style Avoid hints unless you have measured a specific problem

Rule: Hints are overrides, not defaults. Use them when you have measured a problem, understood the root cause, and verified the hint improves performance. Do not scatter broadcast hints everywhere "just in case."
Catalyst’s cost-based optimizer (CBO) uses table and column statistics to choose join strategies and operation ordering. Without statistics, it falls back to heuristics that are often wrong.
Computing Statistics
-- Compute table-level statistics (row count, size in bytes) ANALYZE TABLE silver.meter_readings COMPUTE STATISTICS;
-- Compute column-level statistics (min, max, nulls, distinct count, histogram) ANALYZE TABLE silver.meter_readings COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS meter_id, customer_type, kw, date;
-- View statistics DESCRIBE EXTENDED silver.meter_readings;
-- For a specific column DESCRIBE EXTENDED silver.meter_readings meter_id;
What Statistics Enable

Statistic Used By Catalyst For Without It Table size (bytes) Deciding broadcast vs shuffle join Assumes large; never auto-broadcasts Row count Estimating join output size, partition count Heuristic guess Column distinct count Join selectivity estimation, aggregation planning Assumes uniform distribution Column min/max Range filter estimation, partition pruning Conservative estimates (reads too much) Column null count NULL-aware optimization, join planning Assumes no NULLs Histogram Non-uniform distribution awareness, skew detection Assumes uniform (misses skew)
When to Run ANALYZE TABLE

items: After large data loads — statistics from the previous load are now stale
After OPTIMIZE or Z-ORDER — file sizes and distributions have changed
Before performance-critical queries — ensure Catalyst has accurate information
As part of maintenance schedules — alongside OPTIMIZE and VACUUM (Module 11)

blocks.content: On Databricks: Delta Lake automatically maintains file-level statistics. Column-level statistics and histograms still require explicit ANALYZE TABLE for the full CBO benefit.

sections.title: Lesson 8: The Spark UI — Your Diagnostic Dashboard

blocks.content: The Spark UI is the most important diagnostic tool for performance engineering. It shows exactly what happened during execution: how long each stage took, how much data was shuffled, whether tasks were skewed, and where memory spilled.
Spark UI Tabs

Tab What It Shows Key Metrics Jobs All jobs triggered by actions Job duration, stages per job, status Stages All stages with task-level detail Task duration, shuffle read/write, spill, skew Storage Cached DataFrames Memory used, disk spill, fraction cached Environment Spark configuration All spark.conf values, JVM settings Executors Executor health and utilization Memory usage, task count, GC time, shuffle SQL SQL/DataFrame query plans DAG visualization, per-operator metrics
The Stages Tab: Where Problems Live
The Stages tab is where you spend most of your diagnostic time. For each stage, it shows per-task metrics that reveal bottlenecks:

Metric Healthy Range Warning Sign Root Cause Duration (median vs max) Max < 2x median Max > 10x median Data skew: one partition is huge Shuffle Read Even across tasks One task reads 10x others Join key skew Shuffle Write Proportional to input Very large relative to input Unnecessary shuffle, wide data Spill (Memory) 0 bytes Any amount > 0 Insufficient memory, large partitions Spill (Disk) 0 bytes Any amount > 0 Serious memory pressure GC Time < 10% of task time > 20% of task time Too many objects, executor needs more memory Input Size Even across tasks Highly uneven Unbalanced partitions, small file problem
Reading the Task Distribution
Healthy Task Distribution (Stages tab -> Summary Metrics):

Duration:    Min: 2s    25th: 3s    Median: 4s    75th: 5s    Max: 7s
  Shuffle Read: Min: 50MB  25th: 55MB  Median: 60MB  75th: 65MB  Max: 80MB
  Spill: 0 bytes
Interpretation: Tasks are balanced. No skew. No spill. Healthy.
Unhealthy Task Distribution (DATA SKEW):

Duration:    Min: 1s    25th: 2s    Median: 3s    75th: 4s    Max: 45 min
  Shuffle Read: Min: 10MB  25th: 15MB  Median: 20MB  75th: 25MB  Max: 90 GB
  Spill (Disk): Max: 50 GB

Interpretation: ONE task took 45 minutes (others: seconds). ONE task read 90 GB (others: ~20 MB). MASSIVE SKEW. The 50 GB disk spill means that task also ran out of memory. Action: Identify the skewed key, salt or repartition.
The SQL Tab: Visualizing the Query Plan
The SQL tab shows a visual DAG of the physical plan with actual runtime metrics attached to each operator:

items: Scan nodes show: rows read, bytes read, time spent scanning
Filter nodes show: rows before and after filter (shows selectivity)
Exchange nodes show: shuffle bytes written, partitions created
HashAggregate / SortMergeJoin nodes show: rows processed, time
BroadcastExchange nodes show: broadcast size, time to broadcast

blocks.content: Click on any operator to see detailed metrics. The most useful diagnostic is comparing rows in vs rows out at each stage to understand where data expands (joins, explosions) or contracts (filters, aggregations).

sections.title: Lesson 9: The Senior Engineer’s Diagnostic Workflow

blocks.content: When a Spark job is slow, follow this systematic workflow. Do not guess. Do not randomly change configurations. Diagnose, measure, fix, verify.
Diagnostic Workflow:

items: IDENTIFY which stage is slow       (Jobs tab -> Stages tab)
CHECK task distribution in that stage (Summary Metrics)
LOOK for skew                      (Max >> Median duration)
LOOK for spill                     (Spill Memory / Disk > 0)
LOOK for shuffle volume            (Exchange nodes in SQL tab)
READ the execution plan            (SQL tab -> query details)
CHECK if AQE fired                 (AdaptiveSparkPlan in plan)
CHECK if DPP fired                 (dynamicpruningexpression)
FORM a hypothesis                  (skew? bad join? no pruning?)
APPLY one fix                     (broadcast, salt, repartition)
MEASURE the result                (compare before/after)
REPEAT if necessary

blocks.content: Step-by-Step Example: Diagnosing a Slow Join
Symptom: Pipeline takes 90 minutes instead of the usual 15 minutes.

Step 1: Open Spark UI → Jobs tab. Job 3 took 85 minutes. Click into it. Step 2: Stage 5 (the join stage) took 82 minutes. All other stages under 2 minutes. Step 3: Stage 5 Summary Metrics: Median task duration = 8 seconds. Max task duration = 81 minutes. Massive skew. Step 4: Spill (Disk) on the max task = 120 GB. Memory pressure. Step 5: SQL tab shows Exchange hashpartitioning(meter_id) before the join. Shuffle write = 800 GB total. Step 6: The plan shows SortMergeJoin on meter_id. One side is meter_readings (1 TB), the other is weather_data (50 GB).
Hypothesis: One meter_id has a disproportionate number of readings (e.g., a large industrial site with sub-minute readings).
Verification:

# Check distribution of join key
readings.groupBy('meter_id').count() \
    .orderBy(desc('count')) \
    .show(10)

# Output:
# meter_id | count
# M_PLANT_1| 450,000,000   <-- one meter has 450M rows!
# M002     | 12,000
# M003     | 11,800
# ...
Fix options:

items: If weather_data is actually small enough (50 GB might be too large, but check post-filter size): try broadcast(weather_data)
If the skewed key is on both sides: apply salting on meter_id (Module 4)
If AQE skew optimization should have fired: verify configuration and check if the partition exceeded the threshold

blocks.content: After fix: Re-run, open Spark UI, verify Stage 5 now shows balanced task distribution and no spill. Pipeline completes in 12 minutes.

Anti-Pattern Why It Hurts Fix df.count() for debugging in production code Full scan every time, adds minutes to pipeline Remove debug counts; use Spark UI metrics instead collect() or toPandas() on large DataFrames Pulls all data to Driver; OOM crash Write to storage, use show(20), sample first Chaining many withColumn() calls Complex plan; Catalyst spends time optimizing Use single select() with all computed columns orderBy() before write Global sort triggers full shuffle + sort Remove unless downstream truly needs sorted output Using Python UDFs for simple transformations Serialization overhead; breaks Tungsten pipeline Use built-in functions (Module 9 decision framework) Not filtering before joins Joins process rows that will be filtered later Push filters before join (Catalyst helps, but help it) Caching everything Consumes executor memory; forces eviction Cache only DataFrames used 3+ times; measure benefit Ignoring shuffle partition count 200 default may be too few or too many Enable AQE coalescing; or set explicitly based on data size Reading CSV in production pipelines Row-based, no pushdown, no compression, slow Convert to Parquet/Delta at ingestion; read Parquet downstream Multiple shuffles with different partition keys Each key causes a separate shuffle Redesign query to share partition keys where possible
Essential Performance Configurations

Configuration Default Recommended Effect spark.sql.adaptive.enabled true (3.2+) true Enables AQE (coalesce, broadcast, skew) spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled true true Auto-coalesce small shuffle partitions spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled true true Auto-split skewed join partitions spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes 64m 128m-256m Target partition size after AQE coalesce spark.sql.shuffle.partitions 200 AQE adjusts; or set based on data Partitions after shuffle (AQE overrides this) spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold 10m 10m-50m Max table size for auto-broadcast spark.sql.optimizer.dynamicPartitionPruning.enabled true true Enables DPP for star-schema joins spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes 128m 128m-256m Max bytes per partition when reading files spark.sql.parquet.filterPushdown true true Push filters into Parquet reader spark.sql.inMemoryColumnarStorage.compressed true true Compress cached data spark.serializer JavaSerializer org.apache.spark.serializer.KryoSerializer Faster serialization (especially for RDD ops) spark.speculation false true (for Spot/preemptible) Re-launch slow tasks on other executors
Performance optimizations must be measured, not assumed. A change that helps one query may hurt another. Establish baselines and test systematically.
Benchmarking Framework

import time
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class BenchmarkResult:
    name: str
    duration_seconds: float
    row_count: int

def benchmark(name: str, fn: Callable) -> BenchmarkResult:
    """Time a function and return results."""
    start = time.time()
    result = fn()
    duration = time.time() - start
    row_count = result.count() if hasattr(result, 'count') else -1
    return BenchmarkResult(name, round(duration, 2), row_count)

# Compare two approaches
baseline = benchmark('sort_merge_join', lambda:
    readings.join(weather, 'meter_id'))

optimized = benchmark('broadcast_join', lambda:
    readings.join(broadcast(weather), 'meter_id'))

print(f'Baseline:  {baseline.duration_seconds}s')
print(f'Optimized: {optimized.duration_seconds}s')
print(f'Speedup:   {baseline.duration_seconds / optimized.duration_seconds:.1f}x')

# CRITICAL: verify row counts match!
assert baseline.row_count == optimized.row_count, \
    f'Row count mismatch: {baseline.row_count} vs {optimized.row_count}'
Performance Regression Detection

# Store benchmark results in Delta for trend tracking
from pyspark.sql.functions import current_timestamp, lit

def log_benchmark(spark, result, pipeline_version, metrics_path):
    row = spark.createDataFrame([(
        result.name,
        result.duration_seconds,
        result.row_count,
        pipeline_version,
    )], ['benchmark_name', 'duration_seconds', 'row_count', 'version'])

    row.withColumn('timestamp', current_timestamp()) \
        .write.format('delta').mode('append').save(metrics_path)

# Detect regressions: alert if new version is 2x slower
benchmarks = spark.read.format('delta').load(metrics_path)
latest = benchmarks.filter(col('version') == 'v2.1')
previous = benchmarks.filter(col('version') == 'v2.0')

# Compare average duration per benchmark
comparison = latest.alias('new').join(previous.alias('old'), 'benchmark_name')
regressions = comparison.filter(
    col('new.duration_seconds') > col('old.duration_seconds') * 2
)
if regressions.count() > 0:
    print('PERFORMANCE REGRESSION DETECTED!')
    regressions.show()
When facing a performance problem, use this decision tree to select the right optimization technique:
Performance Decision Tree:

Job is slow.
  |
  +-- Is ONE stage disproportionately slow?
  |   |
  |   +-- YES: Check task distribution in that stage.
  |   |   |
  |   |   +-- Max >> Median? --> DATA SKEW
  |   |   |   |
  |   |   |   +-- Is it a join? --> AQE skew join, or manual salting
  |   |   |   +-- Is it a groupBy? --> Manual salting (AQE doesn't help)
  |   |   |
  |   |   +-- Spill > 0? --> MEMORY PRESSURE
  |   |   |   |
  |   |   |   +-- Increase executor memory
  |   |   |   +-- Reduce partition size (more partitions)
  |   |   |   +-- Filter earlier to reduce data volume
  |   |   |
  |   |   +-- Large shuffle volume? --> SHUFFLE COST
  |   |       |
  |   |       +-- Can small table be broadcast? --> broadcast()
  |   |       +-- Both tables large? --> Bucketing
  |   |       +-- Filter before join to reduce volume
  |   |
  |   +-- NO: All stages slow proportionally
  |       |
  |       +-- Not enough parallelism? --> repartition, add executors
  |       +-- Reading too much data? --> Partition pruning, DPP, Z-ORDER
  |       +-- Redundant computation? --> Cache intermediate results
  |
  +-- Is the Driver crashing (OOM)?
      |
      +-- collect() or toPandas()? --> Remove them
      +-- Large broadcast table? --> Reduce threshold or use shuffle join
      +-- Complex query plan? --> Simplify; break into stages
Optimization Technique Summary

Problem First Try Second Try Advanced Slow scan (reading too much) Filter early; select fewer columns Partition pruning; DPP Z-ORDER + bloom filters Slow shuffle (data movement) broadcast() small tables AQE coalesce partitions Bucketing for repeated joins Data skew (one task slow) AQE skew join (automatic) Manual salting Custom partitioning strategy Memory spill Increase executor memory More partitions (smaller tasks) Filter earlier; drop columns Too many small tasks AQE coalesce (automatic) Manual coalesce() before write Fewer input files (OPTIMIZE) Driver OOM Remove collect()/toPandas() Increase driver memory Write results to storage instead Slow aggregation Filter before groupBy AQE (coalesce + skew) Pre-aggregate at ingestion Slow window function Filter before windowing Cache if multiple window passes Consistent partition keys to share shuffles
You now understand:

items: Adaptive Query Execution (AQE): how it coalesces small partitions, converts to broadcast joins at runtime, and automatically splits skewed join partitions — and its limitation (does not help groupBy skew)
Dynamic Partition Pruning (DPP): how it pushes runtime filter values from dimension tables into fact table scans for star-schema joins, and how to verify it in the execution plan
Bucketing: pre-organizing data at write time to eliminate shuffle on future joins and aggregations, its requirements (saveAsTable, same bucket count), and limitations (not supported in Delta Lake)
Data skipping techniques: Z-ORDER for multi-column file-level pruning, bloom filters for point lookups, and how they layer on top of Parquet min/max statistics and partition pruning
Join strategy hints: broadcast, merge, shuffle_hash in DataFrame and SQL syntax, and when to use them (measured problem with stale statistics) vs when to avoid them (premature optimization)
ANALYZE TABLE: computing table and column statistics for the cost-based optimizer, what each statistic enables, and when to refresh
Spark UI mastery: navigating Jobs, Stages, SQL, and Executors tabs; reading task distribution for skew detection; identifying spill, shuffle cost, and GC pressure
The senior engineer’s diagnostic workflow: a 12-step systematic process from symptom to root cause to verified fix
Performance anti-patterns: the 10 most common mistakes (count() in production, collect(), chained withColumn, orderBy before write, Python UDFs, caching everything)
Essential performance configurations: 12 key settings for AQE, DPP, broadcast, partitioning, pushdown, serialization, and speculation
