Benchmarking and regression testing: timing functions, comparing approaches, verifying row count correctness, and tracking performance trends in Delta
The performance decision tree: a systematic framework for choosing the right optimization technique based on the diagnosed root cause

blocks.content: You are no longer guessing at performance. You are diagnosing, measuring, fixing, and verifying — the workflow that defines senior Spark engineering.

In Module 17, we turn to Data Modeling for the Lakehouse — star schemas on Delta, Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD Types 1/2/3), fact/dimension design, and bridging traditional data warehousing with the modern Lakehouse architecture.

chapters.title: Module 17: Data Modeling for the Lakehouse
chapters.description: From Dimensional Modeling to Modern Lakehouse Architecture Learn the principles of dimensional data modeling — star schemas, fact and dimension tables, surrogate keys, Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD Types 1, 2, and 3), and grain selection — and implement them on Delta Lake using PySpark and MERGE, bridging classical data warehousing with modern Lakehouse architecture.

blocks.content: You have spent 16 modules mastering the engine. You know how to ingest data, clean it, transform it, stream it, test it, deploy it, secure it, and tune it for performance.
But here is a question none of those modules answered:
How should you structure the gold layer?

The Medallion architecture (Module 7) tells you that gold is the business-ready layer. Module 11 gave you Delta Lake for ACID storage. But neither prescribes how to organize the tables, how to handle changes over time, or how to design for analysts who will query the data with SQL.
That is the domain of data modeling.

Data modeling is older than Spark. It is older than Hadoop. It was formalized in the 1990s by Ralph Kimball and Bill Inmon for traditional data warehouses running on Oracle and Teradata. But its principles — facts, dimensions, slowly changing dimensions, grain — are more relevant than ever in the Lakehouse era.
The Modeling Gap:
Without data modeling: With data modeling:

gold/                           gold/
    big_table_everything.delta      fact_meter_readings.delta
    report_v2_final_FINAL.delta     dim_customers.delta
    monthly_kw_fixed.delta          dim_meters.delta
    alice_temp_join.delta           dim_regions.delta
                                    dim_tariffs.delta
  Analysts: "Which table do I      dim_dates.delta
  use? What does this column
  mean? Is this current?"        Analysts: "Star schema. I know
                                  exactly how these connect."

sections.title: Lesson 2: Why Dimensional Modeling Still Matters

blocks.content: The data lake revolution promised freedom from rigid schemas. You could dump everything in and figure it out later. This was liberating for data engineers but created a new problem: data swamps. Lakes full of data that nobody can navigate, trust, or use effectively.
Dimensional modeling brings order to the gold layer. It is not about restricting flexibility. It is about creating a contract between data producers and data consumers.
Benefits of Dimensional Modeling in the Lakehouse

Benefit How It Helps Without It Queryability Analysts write simple joins on star schema Complex multi-hop joins across denormalized blobs Discoverability Fact and dimension naming convention is self-documenting Table names are arbitrary; must ask the creator Performance Star schema enables broadcast joins, DPP, partition pruning Wide tables with redundant columns; no optimization path Historical tracking SCD Type 2 preserves full history of changes Only current state; historical analysis impossible Consistency Single source of truth for each business entity Multiple conflicting copies of customer/product data Reusability Dimension tables shared across multiple fact tables Each pipeline builds its own lookup logic
Kimball vs Inmon: A Brief Orientation

Approach Core Idea Lakehouse Relevance Kimball (dimensional) Build star schemas organized by business process; bottom-up Gold layer: one star schema per business process (readings, billing, maintenance) Inmon (enterprise) Build normalized enterprise data model first; top-down Silver layer: cleaned, normalized entities; gold derived from silver Data Vault Hub-satellite model for raw history; timeline agnostic Bronze-to-silver: append-only, auditable, link-based

In practice, modern Lakehouse teams often use a hybrid: the silver layer follows Inmon-style normalization (clean entity tables), while the gold layer follows Kimball-style star schemas (optimized for analytics). This module focuses on the Kimball approach for the gold layer, as it is the most widely adopted pattern for analyst-facing data.

sections.title: Lesson 3: The Star Schema — Facts and Dimensions

blocks.content: A star schema organizes data around a central fact table surrounded by dimension tables. The fact table records measurable events. Dimension tables provide context for those events.
Star Schema for Smart Grid:

+------------------+
                     |   dim_dates      |
                     |  date_key (PK)   |
                     |  full_date       |
                     |  day_of_week     |
                     |  month           |
                     |  quarter         |
                     |  year            |
                     |  is_holiday      |
                     |  season          |
                     +--------+---------+
                              |
  +------------------+   +----+------------------+   +------------------+
  |  dim_customers   |   | fact_meter_readings   |   |  dim_meters      |
  |  customer_key(PK)|   |  reading_id (PK)      |   |  meter_key (PK)  |
  |  customer_id (NK)|---|  customer_key (FK)     |---|  meter_id (NK)   |
  |  customer_name   |   |  meter_key (FK)        |   |  meter_type      |
  |  customer_type   |   |  date_key (FK)         |   |  install_date    |
  |  address         |   |  region_key (FK)       |   |  manufacturer    |
  |  signup_date     |   |  kw                    |   |  firmware_ver    |
  |  segment         |   |  voltage               |   +------------------+
  +------------------+   |  power_factor          |
                         |  reading_timestamp     |   +------------------+
                         +----+------------------+   |  dim_regions     |
                              |                      |  region_key (PK) |
                              +----------------------|  region_name     |
                                                     |  state           |
                                                     |  timezone        |
                                                     |  grid_operator   |
                                                     +------------------+
Fact Tables
A fact table records measurable business events at a specific grain (level of detail). Each row represents one event or measurement.

Property Description Smart Grid Example Grain What does one row represent? One meter reading at one timestamp Foreign keys Links to dimension tables customer_key, meter_key, date_key, region_key Measures (metrics) Numeric values to aggregate kw, voltage, power_factor Degenerate dimensions Identifiers without a separate dimension table reading_id (unique per event) Volume Typically the largest table by far Billions of rows (one per reading per meter)
Dimension Tables
Dimension tables provide the context for facts. They contain descriptive attributes used for filtering, grouping, and labeling in queries.

Property Description Smart Grid Example Surrogate key System-generated unique identifier (not from source) customer_key (integer, auto-increment) Natural key Business identifier from source system customer_id ('CUST-12345') Attributes Descriptive columns for filtering and grouping customer_name, customer_type, segment, address Volume Small relative to fact table Thousands to millions of rows Change tracking Managed via SCD patterns (Types 1, 2, 3) Customer type changes from RESIDENTIAL to COMMERCIAL
Why Surrogate Keys?

Natural keys (customer_id, meter_id) come from source systems and can change, be recycled, or collide across systems. Surrogate keys are system-generated integers that are stable, compact, and independent of source systems.

Aspect Natural Key Surrogate Key Source Business system Generated by data warehouse Stability May change (mergers, system migration) Never changes once assigned Type Often string (CUST-12345) Integer (fast joins, small storage) Uniqueness Unique within source; may collide across sources Globally unique within warehouse SCD Type 2 Same natural key, multiple versions Unique key per version (enables joins to specific version)

# Generating surrogate keys in PySpark
from pyspark.sql.functions import monotonically_increasing_id

dim_customers = silver_customers \
    .withColumn('customer_key', monotonically_increasing_id())

# WARNING: monotonically_increasing_id() is not sequential.
# It generates unique 64-bit integers but with gaps.
# For sequential keys, use row_number():

from pyspark.sql.functions import row_number, lit
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

key_window = Window.orderBy(lit(1))  # deterministic but expensive
dim_customers = silver_customers \
    .withColumn('customer_key', row_number().over(key_window))

# For production at scale, consider:
# - Hash-based keys: sha2(concat(natural_key, effective_date), 256)
# - Identity columns (Databricks Delta): GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY

sections.title: Lesson 4: Grain — The Most Important Decision

blocks.content: The grain of a fact table defines what one row represents. It is the single most important decision in dimensional modeling. Get the grain wrong and everything downstream is wrong.
Grain Examples

Fact Table Grain One Row = fact_meter_readings One reading per meter per timestamp Meter M001 at 2026-02-25 14:15:00 = 450 kW fact_daily_consumption One day per meter Meter M001 on 2026-02-25 = total 4,200 kW fact_monthly_billing One month per customer Customer C-123 for February 2026 = $342.50 fact_outage_events One outage event per meter Meter M001 outage starting 2026-02-20 lasting 3 hours
Choosing the Right Grain
The fundamental tension: finer grain preserves more detail but creates larger tables.
Grain Trade-offs:
FINE GRAIN (per reading, every 15 minutes) + Maximum analytical flexibility + Can always aggregate up (daily, monthly, yearly) + Supports time-of-day analysis, spike detection

items: Billions of rows (storage + compute cost)
Queries slower without proper partitioning

blocks.content: COARSE GRAIN (per day per meter) + Manageable size (millions of rows) + Fast queries for daily/monthly reporting

items: Cannot drill into sub-daily patterns
Lost ability to detect 15-minute spikes

blocks.content: RULE: Start with the finest grain your business questions require. You can always aggregate fine data into coarse summaries. You can NEVER disaggregate coarse data into fine detail.
Multiple Fact Tables at Different Grains
In practice, build multiple fact tables at different grains for different use cases:

Table Grain Audience Refresh fact_meter_readings Per reading (15 min) Engineers, anomaly detection Streaming or hourly fact_daily_consumption Per meter per day Analysts, operational reports Daily fact_monthly_billing Per customer per month Finance, executive dashboards Monthly
Each table shares the same dimension tables. The star schema is the same; only the grain changes.
1. Transaction Fact Table
Records individual events at the moment they occur. One row per event. This is the most common type.

# Transaction fact: one row per meter reading
fact_readings_schema = '''
    reading_id      BIGINT,
    meter_key       INT,
    customer_key    INT,
    date_key        INT,
    region_key      INT,
    reading_timestamp TIMESTAMP,
    kw              DOUBLE,
    voltage         DOUBLE,
    power_factor    DOUBLE
'''

# Grain: one reading per meter per timestamp.
# Insert-only: readings are never updated.
2. Periodic Snapshot Fact Table
Captures the state of a process at regular intervals. One row per entity per period. Useful for tracking cumulative or balance-oriented metrics.

# Periodic snapshot: one row per meter per day
fact_daily_schema = '''
    meter_key       INT,
    customer_key    INT,
    date_key        INT,
    region_key      INT,
    total_kw        DOUBLE,
    avg_kw          DOUBLE,
    max_kw          DOUBLE,
    min_kw          DOUBLE,
    reading_count   INT,
    peak_hour       INT
'''

# Grain: one day per meter.
# Built by aggregating fact_meter_readings.
# Every day adds exactly one row per active meter.
3. Accumulating Snapshot Fact Table
Tracks the lifecycle of a process that has a defined beginning and end. The row is updated as the process progresses through milestones.

# Accumulating snapshot: one row per outage event
fact_outages_schema = '''
    outage_key          BIGINT,
    meter_key           INT,
    region_key          INT,
    reported_date_key   INT,
    dispatched_date_key INT,
    resolved_date_key   INT,
    reported_timestamp  TIMESTAMP,
    dispatched_timestamp TIMESTAMP,
    resolved_timestamp  TIMESTAMP,
    outage_duration_min INT,
    cause_code          STRING,
    status              STRING
'''

# Grain: one row per outage event.
# Row is UPDATED as milestones are reached:
#   Created -> dispatched_timestamp filled
#   Dispatched -> resolved_timestamp filled, duration calculated
# Use Delta MERGE to update in place.
Fact Table Type Comparison

Type Row Behavior Grain Best For Transaction Insert only; never updated One event Individual readings, transactions, clicks Periodic Snapshot Insert only; one row per period One period per entity Daily balances, monthly summaries, inventory Accumulating Snapshot Updated as process progresses One lifecycle per entity Order fulfillment, outage tracking, support tickets

sections.title: Lesson 6: The Date Dimension — The Universal Dimension

blocks.content: Almost every fact table has a date key. The date dimension is the most widely used and most valuable dimension in any star schema. It enables time-based analysis that is impossible with raw timestamps.

# Build a comprehensive date dimension in PySpark
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col, lit, date_format, dayofweek, dayofmonth, dayofyear,
    month, quarter, year, weekofyear, when, concat, lpad,
    expr, sequence, explode
)
from pyspark.sql.types import DateType

# Generate date range
start_date = '2020-01-01'
end_date = '2030-12-31'

dates = spark.sql(f"""
    SELECT explode(
        sequence(to_date('{start_date}'), to_date('{end_date}'), interval 1 day)
    ) AS full_date
""")

dim_dates = dates \
    .withColumn('date_key',
        date_format('full_date', 'yyyyMMdd').cast('int')) \
    .withColumn('day_of_week', dayofweek('full_date')) \
    .withColumn('day_name', date_format('full_date', 'EEEE')) \
    .withColumn('day_of_month', dayofmonth('full_date')) \
    .withColumn('day_of_year', dayofyear('full_date')) \
    .withColumn('week_of_year', weekofyear('full_date')) \
    .withColumn('month_num', month('full_date')) \
    .withColumn('month_name', date_format('full_date', 'MMMM')) \
    .withColumn('quarter', quarter('full_date')) \
    .withColumn('year', year('full_date')) \
    .withColumn('is_weekend',
        when(dayofweek('full_date').isin(1, 7), True).otherwise(False)) \
    .withColumn('season',
        when(month('full_date').isin(12, 1, 2), 'Winter')
        .when(month('full_date').isin(3, 4, 5), 'Spring')
        .when(month('full_date').isin(6, 7, 8), 'Summer')
        .otherwise('Fall'))

dim_dates.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite') \
    .save('/data/gold/dim_dates/')

Why not just use raw dates? Because analysts should be able to filter by season, fiscal quarter, holiday, or weekend without computing those values in every query. The date dimension pre-computes all time-based attributes once and makes them available to every fact table via a simple join.
Using the Date Key in Queries

-- Analyst query: total consumption by season by year SELECT d.year, d.season, SUM(f.kw) AS total_kw FROM fact_meter_readings f JOIN dim_dates d ON f.date_key = d.date_key GROUP BY d.year, d.season ORDER BY d.year, d.season;

-- Without dim_dates, the analyst would need:
-- CASE WHEN MONTH(timestamp) IN (12,1,2) THEN 'Winter' ...
-- in EVERY query. The dimension eliminates this repetition.

sections.title: Lesson 7: Slowly Changing Dimensions — SCD Type 1 (Overwrite)

blocks.content: Dimensions change. A customer moves to a new address. A meter is reclassified. A region is reorganized. How you handle these changes defines your SCD strategy.
SCD Type 1 simply overwrites the old value with the new value. No history is preserved.
SCD Type 1: Overwrite

BEFORE the change:                AFTER the change:
  customer_key | name    | type     customer_key | name    | type
  1001         | Chen    | RESIDENTIAL  1001     | Chen    | COMMERCIAL
The RESIDENTIAL history is gone. Permanently. All past fact rows linking to customer_key 1001 now appear as COMMERCIAL, even for readings that occurred when the customer was RESIDENTIAL.
Implementation with Delta MERGE

from delta.tables import DeltaTable

# Source: new/updated customer records from silver
updates = spark.read.format('delta').load('/data/silver/customers/')

# Target: existing dimension table
dim_table = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, '/data/gold/dim_customers/')

# SCD Type 1: Overwrite matching records, insert new ones
dim_table.alias('target').merge(
    updates.alias('source'),
    'target.customer_id = source.customer_id'
).whenMatchedUpdate(set={
    'customer_name': 'source.customer_name',
    'customer_type': 'source.customer_type',
    'address': 'source.address',
    'segment': 'source.segment',
    'last_updated': 'current_timestamp()',
}).whenNotMatchedInsert(values={
    'customer_key': 'monotonically_increasing_id()',
    'customer_id': 'source.customer_id',
    'customer_name': 'source.customer_name',
    'customer_type': 'source.customer_type',
    'address': 'source.address',
    'segment': 'source.segment',
    'last_updated': 'current_timestamp()',
}).execute()
When to Use SCD Type 1

Use When Avoid When History does not matter for this attribute You need to analyze historical state Corrections (fixing typos, wrong data) Actual business changes (customer reclassification) The change is trivial (formatting, standardization) Regulatory requirements demand audit trail Storage and complexity must be minimized Analysts need to compare 'before' and 'after'

sections.title: Lesson 8: Slowly Changing Dimensions — SCD Type 2 (Versioning)

blocks.content: SCD Type 2 preserves the complete history of changes. When a dimension value changes, the old row is closed (end-dated) and a new row is created with a new surrogate key.
This is the most important and most commonly required SCD pattern in data warehousing.
SCD Type 2: Versioning
One customer, two versions:

customer_key | customer_id | type       | effective | end_date   | is_current
  1001         | CUST-500    | RESIDENTIAL| 2020-01-15| 2026-02-01 | false
  1002         | CUST-500    | COMMERCIAL | 2026-02-01| 9999-12-31 | true

items: Same natural key (CUST-500) but TWO surrogate keys (1001, 1002)
Old version end-dated to 2026-02-01, is_current = false
New version starts 2026-02-01, end_date = 9999-12-31 (open-ended)

blocks.content: Fact rows from 2020-2026 link to customer_key 1001 (RESIDENTIAL) Fact rows from 2026+ link to customer_key 1002 (COMMERCIAL) History is PRESERVED. Queries are CORRECT for each time period.
SCD Type 2 Implementation with Delta MERGE
SCD Type 2 with MERGE requires two operations in one transaction: close old rows and insert new versions.

from delta.tables import DeltaTable
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col, lit, current_date, when, coalesce
)

# --- Step 1: Identify which records have actually changed ---
current_dim = spark.read.format('delta') \
    .load('/data/gold/dim_customers/') \
    .filter(col('is_current') == True)

incoming = spark.read.format('delta').load('/data/silver/customers/')

# Find records where attributes have changed
changed = incoming.alias('new').join(
    current_dim.alias('old'),
    col('new.customer_id') == col('old.customer_id')
).filter(
    (col('new.customer_type') != col('old.customer_type')) |
    (col('new.address') != col('old.address')) |
    (col('new.segment') != col('old.segment'))
).select('new.*')

# Find genuinely new records (not in dimension at all)
new_records = incoming.join(
    current_dim, incoming.customer_id == current_dim.customer_id, 'left_anti'
)

# --- Step 2: Build the updates and inserts ---

# Rows to close (expire the old version)
rows_to_close = changed.select('customer_id') \
    .withColumn('merge_key', col('customer_id'))

# New versions of changed records
new_versions = changed \
    .withColumn('effective_date', current_date()) \
    .withColumn('end_date', lit('9999-12-31').cast('date')) \
    .withColumn('is_current', lit(True)) \
    .withColumn('merge_key', lit(None).cast('string'))  # force NOT MATCHED

# Brand new customers
brand_new = new_records \
    .withColumn('effective_date', current_date()) \
    .withColumn('end_date', lit('9999-12-31').cast('date')) \
    .withColumn('is_current', lit(True)) \
    .withColumn('merge_key', lit(None).cast('string'))  # force NOT MATCHED

# Combine: rows that need to close + new versions + brand new
staged = rows_to_close.unionByName(new_versions, allowMissingColumns=True) \
    .unionByName(brand_new, allowMissingColumns=True)

# --- Step 3: MERGE into dimension table ---

dim_table = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, '/data/gold/dim_customers/')

dim_table.alias('target').merge( staged.alias('source'), 'target.customer_id = source.merge_key AND target.is_current = true' ).whenMatchedUpdate( # Close the old version set={ 'end_date': 'current_date()', 'is_current': 'false', } ).whenNotMatchedInsert( # Insert new version or brand new record values={ 'customer_key': 'monotonically_increasing_id()', 'customer_id': 'source.customer_id', 'customer_name': 'source.customer_name', 'customer_type': 'source.customer_type', 'address': 'source.address', 'segment': 'source.segment', 'effective_date': 'source.effective_date', 'end_date': 'source.end_date', 'is_current': 'source.is_current', } ).execute()
Querying SCD Type 2
-- Current state: get the latest version of each customer SELECT * FROM dim_customers WHERE is_current = true;
-- Historical state: find the customer version active on a specific date SELECT * FROM dim_customers WHERE customer_id = 'CUST-500' AND effective_date <= '2024-06-15' AND end_date > '2024-06-15';
-- Join fact to dimension at the correct historical point SELECT f.*, d.customer_type, d.segment FROM fact_meter_readings f JOIN dim_customers d ON f.customer_key = d.customer_key;

-- Because fact rows store the surrogate key at the time of reading,
-- the join automatically links to the correct historical version.
The Surrogate Key Advantage in SCD Type 2

This is precisely why surrogate keys exist. When a customer changes from RESIDENTIAL to COMMERCIAL, a new customer_key (1002) is created. New fact rows use 1002. Old fact rows keep 1001. The join is always correct without complex date-range logic in every query.

sections.title: Lesson 9: Slowly Changing Dimensions — SCD Type 3 (Previous Value)

blocks.content: SCD Type 3 keeps limited history by adding a column for the previous value. It tracks exactly one change — not a full audit trail.
SCD Type 3: Previous Value Column

customer_key | customer_id | type       | prev_type   | type_change_date
  1001         | CUST-500    | COMMERCIAL | RESIDENTIAL | 2026-02-01
Only ONE previous value is stored. If the customer changes type again, the previous RESIDENTIAL is lost.
Use case: "Compare current segment to the segment they were in before."
SCD Type Comparison

Aspect Type 1 (Overwrite) Type 2 (Versioning) Type 3 (Previous Value) History preserved None Complete One previous value only Row count growth No growth Grows with each change No growth Schema complexity Simple Adds effective_date, end_date, is_current Adds prev_ columns Query complexity Simple Filter by is_current or date range Simple (one row per entity) Storage cost Lowest Highest Low Use case Corrections, trivial changes Full audit, regulatory compliance Simple before/after comparison Smart grid example Fix typo in meter name Customer changes from RES to COM Compare current vs previous tariff

sections.title: Lesson 10: Denormalization Trade-offs in the Lakehouse

blocks.content: Traditional data warehousing strict stars are sometimes traded for denormalized (wide) tables in the Lakehouse. Understanding when to denormalize and when to keep the star schema is a modeling judgment call.
Star Schema vs Wide Table

Aspect Star Schema (Normalized Dimensions) Wide Table (Denormalized) Structure Fact + dimension tables joined at query time Single table with all attributes flattened Storage Efficient (dimension attrs stored once) Redundant (customer_type repeated on every reading row) Query simplicity Requires joins (but they are simple) No joins needed (SELECT directly from one table) Update complexity Update dimension table only Must update every row containing the changed attribute Consistency Single source of truth per entity Risk of inconsistency across duplicated values BI tool compatibility Star schemas are native to BI tools Some tools prefer single-table; others need star Lakehouse fit Gold layer for analysts and BI Gold layer for ML feature stores, simple dashboards
When to Denormalize

items: ML feature stores: Machine learning models need a single flat table with all features; star schemas add unnecessary join complexity in training pipelines
Self-service BI for non-technical users: If users cannot write JOINs, a pre-joined wide table reduces errors
Very small dimensions that never change: Embedding a 3-value customer_type column directly into the fact table avoids a join for negligible storage cost
Performance-critical dashboards: Pre-joined tables eliminate join cost at query time (trade write-time cost for read-time speed)

blocks.content: When to Keep Star Schema

items: Dimensions that change over time (SCD Type 2): Denormalized tables must update billions of fact rows on every dimension change; star schemas update only the dimension
Multiple fact tables sharing dimensions: dim_customers is used by fact_readings, fact_billing, and fact_outages; denormalizing requires maintaining customer attributes in three places
Regulatory and audit requirements: Star schemas with SCD Type 2 provide a clean historical record; denormalized tables lose change tracking
Skilled SQL users: Analysts comfortable with JOINs benefit from the clarity and flexibility of star schemas

blocks.content: The Hybrid Approach
Gold Layer: Hybrid Modeling
Star Schema (for analysts): Wide Tables (for ML/dashboards):

fact_meter_readings                 ml_features_daily
    |-- dim_customers                   (pre-joined, flat, one row
    |-- dim_meters                       per meter per day with all
    |-- dim_dates                        attributes embedded)
    |-- dim_regions
                                      executive_dashboard
  Maintained by: Data Engineering       (pre-aggregated, wide,
  Updated: Daily pipeline               refreshed daily)
  Consumers: SQL analysts, BI tools
                                      Maintained by: Same pipeline
                                      Consumers: ML training, Tableau
Both are derived from the SAME silver layer. Star schema is the primary model; wide tables are secondary views.

Dimensional modeling defines the logical structure. Partitioning defines the physical storage layout. In Module 2, you learned about Spark partitions (in-memory parallelism). Here we discuss disk-level partitioning of Delta tables.
Choosing Partition Columns

Table Recommended Partition Column(s) Why fact_meter_readings date (daily) Queries almost always filter by date; enables partition pruning + DPP fact_daily_consumption date (monthly or daily) Same as above; monthly reduces directory count fact_monthly_billing year + month Queries by billing period; small volume per partition dim_customers None (or region if very large) Dimensions are small; partitioning adds overhead dim_dates None Tiny table (<5000 rows); never partition dim_meters None Small table; scan is faster than partition overhead
Partitioning Rules of Thumb

items: Partition by columns that frequently appear in WHERE clauses — this enables partition pruning (skip entire directories)
Each partition should contain at least 1 GB of data — smaller partitions cause the small-file problem (Module 2)
Avoid high-cardinality partition columns — meter_id with 1 million values creates 1 million directories
Fact tables: partition by date — the universal filter; enables incremental processing (process only today’s partition)
Dimension tables: usually do not partition — they are small enough to scan entirely

blocks.content:
# Write fact table partitioned by date
fact_readings.write \
    .format('delta') \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .partitionBy('date') \
    .save('/data/gold/fact_meter_readings/')

# Directory structure:
# /data/gold/fact_meter_readings/
#   date=2026-02-23/
#     part-00000.snappy.parquet
#     part-00001.snappy.parquet
#   date=2026-02-24/
#     part-00000.snappy.parquet
#   date=2026-02-25/
#     part-00000.snappy.parquet

# Query that benefits from partition pruning:
# SELECT * FROM fact_meter_readings WHERE date = '2026-02-25'
# Only reads one directory; skips all other dates.

sections.title: Lesson 12: Building the Complete Gold Layer Pipeline

blocks.content: Let us combine everything into a complete gold layer build pipeline for the smart grid.
Pipeline Architecture
Gold Layer Build Pipeline:

SILVER LAYER (cleaned, normalized entities)
    |
    +-- silver.meter_readings  (one reading per meter per timestamp)
    +-- silver.customers       (current state of customers)
    +-- silver.meters          (current state of meters)
    +-- silver.regions         (region reference data)
    |
    v
  GOLD LAYER BUILD
    |
    +-- [Step 1] Build/Update dim_dates (one-time, then incremental)
    +-- [Step 2] Build/Update dim_customers (SCD Type 2)
    +-- [Step 3] Build/Update dim_meters (SCD Type 1)
    +-- [Step 4] Build/Update dim_regions (SCD Type 1)
    +-- [Step 5] Build fact_daily_consumption (aggregate readings)
    +-- [Step 6] Build ml_features_daily (denormalized wide table)
    |
    v
  GOLD LAYER (star schema + wide tables)
Step 5: Building the Fact Table

# Build daily consumption fact from silver readings
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    sum, avg, max as spark_max, min as spark_min,
    count, col, date_format, hour
)

silver_readings = spark.read.format('delta') \
    .load('/data/silver/meter_readings/') \
    .filter(col('date') == processing_date)  # incremental: today only

# Look up dimension keys
dim_customers = spark.read.format('delta') \
    .load('/data/gold/dim_customers/') \
    .filter(col('is_current') == True) \
    .select('customer_key', 'customer_id')

dim_meters = spark.read.format('delta') \
    .load('/data/gold/dim_meters/') \
    .select('meter_key', 'meter_id')

dim_regions = spark.read.format('delta') \
    .load('/data/gold/dim_regions/') \
    .select('region_key', 'region_name')

# Aggregate to daily grain
daily = silver_readings \
    .groupBy('meter_id', 'customer_id', 'region', 'date') \
    .agg(
        sum('kw').alias('total_kw'),
        avg('kw').alias('avg_kw'),
        spark_max('kw').alias('max_kw'),
        spark_min('kw').alias('min_kw'),
        count('*').alias('reading_count'),
    )

# Join to get surrogate keys
fact_daily = daily \
    .join(dim_customers, 'customer_id', 'left') \
    .join(dim_meters, 'meter_id', 'left') \
    .join(dim_regions, daily.region == dim_regions.region_name, 'left') \
    .withColumn('date_key', date_format('date', 'yyyyMMdd').cast('int')) \
    .select(
        'meter_key', 'customer_key', 'date_key', 'region_key',
        'total_kw', 'avg_kw', 'max_kw', 'min_kw', 'reading_count'
    )

# Write fact table (append for incremental, or replaceWhere for idempotent)
fact_daily.write.format('delta') \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .option('replaceWhere', f"date_key = {processing_date_key}") \
    .partitionBy('date_key') \
    .save('/data/gold/fact_daily_consumption/')
Step 6: Building the Wide Table for ML

# Pre-joined wide table: one row per meter per day with all attributes
ml_features = fact_daily \
    .join(spark.read.format('delta').load('/data/gold/dim_customers/') \
          .filter(col('is_current') == True),
          'customer_key', 'left') \
    .join(spark.read.format('delta').load('/data/gold/dim_meters/'),
          'meter_key', 'left') \
    .join(spark.read.format('delta').load('/data/gold/dim_dates/'),
          'date_key', 'left') \
    .join(spark.read.format('delta').load('/data/gold/dim_regions/'),
          'region_key', 'left') \
    .select(
        # Fact measures
        'total_kw', 'avg_kw', 'max_kw', 'min_kw', 'reading_count',
        # Customer attributes
        'customer_type', 'segment',
        # Meter attributes
        'meter_type', 'manufacturer',
        # Date attributes
        'day_of_week', 'month_num', 'is_weekend', 'season',
        # Region attributes
        'region_name', 'state', 'timezone',
    )

ml_features.write.format('delta') \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .option('replaceWhere', f"date_key = {processing_date_key}") \
    .save('/data/gold/ml_features_daily/')

Pitfall Consequence Fix Wrong grain (too coarse) Cannot answer detailed questions; data lost forever Start at finest grain needed; aggregate into secondary tables Mixing grains in one fact table Aggregations double-count or produce wrong results One grain per fact table; separate tables for different grains Using natural keys instead of surrogates SCD Type 2 breaks; joins become ambiguous Always use surrogate keys in fact-to-dimension joins No date dimension Analysts re-derive day_of_week, season, etc. in every query Build dim_dates once; use date_key FK in all facts Partitioning by high-cardinality column Millions of tiny directories; scan overhead Partition facts by date; dimensions usually not partitioned SCD Type 2 without is_current flag Every query needs date-range logic to find current version Add is_current boolean; index for fast current-state queries Not handling late-arriving facts Facts arrive after dimension change; link to wrong version Match on effective_date range, not just current dimension Over-denormalization Customer name stored in 10 billion fact rows; updates are nightmares Keep star schema as primary; build wide tables as derived views No quality checks on surrogate key assignment Duplicate keys, broken joins, orphan facts Validate uniqueness of surrogate keys; check referential integrity Ignoring NULL handling in joins Fact rows with NULL keys silently dropped or produce cartesian Use left joins; log unmatched fact rows as data quality issues

sections.title: Lesson 14: The Complete Smart Grid Gold Layer Schema

blocks.content: Complete Gold Layer:

+------------------------------------------------------------------+
  |                       STAR SCHEMA                                |
  |                                                                  |
  |  dim_dates (date_key PK)                                        |
  |  dim_customers (customer_key PK) -- SCD Type 2                  |
  |  dim_meters (meter_key PK) -- SCD Type 1                        |
  |  dim_regions (region_key PK) -- SCD Type 1                      |
  |  dim_tariffs (tariff_key PK) -- SCD Type 2                      |
  |                                                                  |
  |  fact_meter_readings (reading_id PK) -- transaction grain       |
  |  fact_daily_consumption (meter_key+date_key) -- snapshot grain   |
  |  fact_monthly_billing (customer_key+month) -- snapshot grain     |
  |  fact_outage_events (outage_key PK) -- accumulating grain       |
  |                                                                  |
  +------------------------------------------------------------------+
  |                    DERIVED WIDE TABLES                           |
  |                                                                  |
  |  ml_features_daily -- pre-joined for ML pipelines               |
  |  executive_dashboard -- pre-aggregated for BI                    |
  |  regional_kpi -- pre-filtered by region for partners            |
  |                                                                  |
  +------------------------------------------------------------------+

This schema serves every consumer: SQL analysts use the star schema with familiar JOINs, ML engineers use the flat wide tables, executives see pre-aggregated dashboards, and partners get filtered views. All derived from the same trusted silver layer.
You now understand:

items: Why dimensional modeling matters in the Lakehouse era — turning data swamps into navigable star schemas with clear contracts between producers and consumers
The Kimball, Inmon, and Data Vault approaches and how they map to Medallion layers (Inmon for silver, Kimball for gold)
Star schema structure: fact tables (measurable events) surrounded by dimension tables (descriptive context), connected by foreign key relationships
Surrogate keys vs natural keys: why system-generated integers are essential for SCD Type 2, cross-system integration, and stable joins
Grain: the most important modeling decision; one row = one specific thing; start fine, aggregate coarse; never mix grains in one table
Three types of fact tables: transaction (insert-only events), periodic snapshot (regular interval summaries), and accumulating snapshot (lifecycle tracking with milestone updates)
The date dimension: pre-computed time attributes (season, day_of_week, is_holiday, quarter) eliminating repetitive date logic in every query
SCD Type 1 (overwrite): simple, no history; implemented with Delta MERGE whenMatchedUpdate

SCD Type 2 (versioning): complete history with effective_date, end_date, is_current; implemented with Delta MERGE closing old rows and inserting new versions; surrogate keys link facts to correct historical version
SCD Type 3 (previous value): one-change history with prev_ columns; simple schema, limited tracking
The SCD type comparison: Type 1 for corrections, Type 2 for auditable history, Type 3 for simple before/after
Denormalization trade-offs: star schema vs wide tables; when to denormalize (ML, self-service BI) and when to keep the star (SCD tracking, shared dimensions, regulatory needs)
Physical partitioning strategy: fact tables partitioned by date; dimensions usually not partitioned; target 1 GB+ per partition; avoid high-cardinality partition columns
A complete gold layer pipeline: building dim tables (SCD Type 1 and 2 via MERGE), aggregating silver into facts with surrogate key lookups, and deriving wide tables for ML and dashboards

Ten common modeling pitfalls: wrong grain, mixed grains, natural keys in facts, no date dimension, high-cardinality partitioning, missing is_current flags, late-arriving facts, over-denormalization, broken surrogate keys, and NULL key handling

blocks.content: You are no longer just writing transformations. You are architecting a gold layer that serves analysts, ML engineers, executives, and partners — with history, consistency, and performance built in from the foundation.

In Module 18, we explore the pandas API on Spark (pyspark.pandas) — enabling analysts to use familiar pandas syntax on distributed datasets, understanding what works, what does not, and when to choose pandas API over native DataFrames.

chapters.title: Module 18: The pandas API on Spark
chapters.description: Familiar Syntax, Distributed Power Understand pyspark.pandas (formerly Koalas) — what it is, how it translates pandas operations into Spark execution plans, which operations map cleanly and which do not, when to use it versus native PySpark DataFrames, and how to migrate existing pandas workflows to distributed scale without rewriting everything.

blocks.content: For 17 modules, you have written PySpark using the native DataFrame API. You have learned to think in transformations and actions, partitions and shuffles, lazy evaluation and execution plans.

But here is a reality of the data world: millions of analysts and data scientists write pandas every day. They do not think in partitions. They think in DataFrames with an index, .loc and .iloc accessors, in-place modifications, and immediate execution. They are not wrong. pandas is an extraordinary library for data analysis on a single machine.
The problem is scale. When a dataset grows beyond the memory of a single machine, pandas breaks. The analyst faces a choice: learn an entirely new API (PySpark DataFrames), or find a bridge.
That bridge is pyspark.pandas — a pandas-compatible API that runs on Spark.
The Bridge:

pandas (single machine)      pyspark.pandas (distributed)     PySpark DataFrame
  +-----------------------+    +-----------------------+        +-----------------------+
  | import pandas as pd   |    | import pyspark.pandas |        | from pyspark.sql      |
  | df = pd.read_csv(...) |    |   as ps               |        |   import SparkSession |
  | df.groupby('x').sum() |    | df = ps.read_csv(...) |        | df = spark.read.csv() |
  | df.head()             |    | df.groupby('x').sum() |        | df.groupBy('x').sum() |
  +-----------------------+    | df.head()             |        | df.show()             |
                               +-----------------------+        +-----------------------+
  Limit: ~10 GB (RAM)         Limit: petabytes (cluster)       Limit: petabytes (cluster)
  Execution: immediate        Execution: lazy (Spark engine)   Execution: lazy (Spark)
  Syntax: pandas               Syntax: ~95% pandas compatible   Syntax: PySpark-native
This module teaches you when this bridge is the right choice, how it works under the hood, what translates cleanly, and where the illusion of pandas compatibility breaks down.
From Koalas to pyspark.pandas

The pandas API on Spark began as an independent open-source project called Koalas, created by Databricks engineers in 2019. Its goal was simple: let pandas users work at Spark scale with minimal code changes.
In Spark 3.2 (released 2021), Koalas was officially merged into PySpark as pyspark.pandas. It is now a built-in part of PySpark — no separate installation needed.
Version Name Status Pre-Spark 3.2 Koalas (databricks.koalas) Separate pip install; third-party project Spark 3.2+ pyspark.pandas Built into PySpark; import pyspark.pandas as ps
If you encounter code with import databricks.koalas, that is the legacy version. In modern PySpark, always use import pyspark.pandas.
Architecture: How It Works Under the Hood
pyspark.pandas Execution Model:
Your Code (pandas syntax) | pyspark.pandas Translation Layer | v PySpark DataFrame Operations | v Catalyst Optimizer | v DAG Scheduler + Executors | v Distributed Execution
You write: df.groupby('customer_type')['kw'].mean() Spark runs: df.groupBy('customer_type').agg(avg('kw'))
The syntax is pandas. The execution is Spark.

Every pyspark.pandas operation is translated into one or more PySpark DataFrame operations. There is no separate execution engine. The pandas API is a facade over the exact same Spark engine you have been using for 17 modules.

This means: every performance concept you have learned — lazy evaluation, shuffle cost, partitioning, broadcast joins, AQE — applies equally to pyspark.pandas code. The syntax changes. The engine does not.
Importing

import pyspark.pandas as ps

# Convention: 'ps' is the standard alias for pyspark.pandas,
# just as 'pd' is the standard for pandas.
# Some codebases use 'pp' or 'ppd'. We use 'ps' throughout.
Creating DataFrames

# From a Python dictionary (identical to pandas)
df = ps.DataFrame({
    'meter_id': ['M001', 'M002', 'M003', 'M004'],
    'customer_type': ['Residential', 'Commercial', 'Residential', 'Industrial'],
    'kw': [450.5, 1200.0, 380.2, 5500.8],
    'date': ['2026-02-21', '2026-02-21', '2026-02-21', '2026-02-21'],
})

# From a list of lists
df = ps.DataFrame(
    [['M001', 450.5], ['M002', 1200.0]],
    columns=['meter_id', 'kw']
)
print(type(df)) # <class 'pyspark.pandas.frame.DataFrame'>
Reading Files

# Read CSV (pandas syntax)
df = ps.read_csv('/data/smart_meters.csv')

# Read Parquet
df = ps.read_parquet('/data/smart_meters/')

# Read Delta
df = ps.read_delta('/data/silver/meter_readings/')

# Read from a Spark table
df = ps.read_table('silver.meter_readings')

# All of these return a pyspark.pandas DataFrame,
# backed by a Spark DataFrame underneath.
Inspecting Data

# Familiar pandas inspection methods
df.head()         # First 5 rows (returns pandas DataFrame)
df.head(10)       # First 10 rows
df.tail()         # Last 5 rows (expensive: requires full scan + sort)
df.shape          # (row_count, col_count) — triggers count() action
df.dtypes         # Column types
df.columns        # Column names
df.describe()     # Summary statistics
df.info()         # Schema, non-null counts, memory estimate

# CAUTION: shape and describe() trigger Spark actions.
# On terabyte datasets, these are NOT instant.

sections.title: Lesson 4: Core Operations — What Maps Cleanly

blocks.content: Most standard pandas operations translate directly to pyspark.pandas. The syntax is identical. The execution is distributed.
Column Selection

# Select by name (identical to pandas)
df['kw']
df[['meter_id', 'kw']]

# Attribute access
df.kw

# All return pyspark.pandas Series or DataFrame objects.
Filtering

# Boolean indexing (identical to pandas)
high_usage = df[df['kw'] > 1000]

# Multiple conditions
residential_high = df[(df['customer_type'] == 'Residential') & (df['kw'] > 500)]

# isin()
types = df[df['customer_type'].isin(['Residential', 'Commercial'])]

# Negation
non_industrial = df[~(df['customer_type'] == 'Industrial')]

# query() method
result = df.query('kw > 1000 and customer_type == "Residential"')
Adding and Modifying Columns

# New column from arithmetic
df['daily_mw'] = df['kw'] / 1000

# Conditional column
import numpy as np
df['usage_tier'] = np.where(df['kw'] < 500, 'Low',
                   np.where(df['kw'] < 2000, 'Medium', 'High'))

# apply() with a lambda (works but has overhead — see Part 7)
df['kw_rounded'] = df['kw'].apply(lambda x: round(x, 0))

# String operations
df['meter_prefix'] = df['meter_id'].str[:3]
df['type_upper'] = df['customer_type'].str.upper()
Aggregations

# groupby (note: groupby, not groupBy)
df.groupby('customer_type')['kw'].sum()
df.groupby('customer_type')['kw'].mean()

# Multiple aggregations
df.groupby('customer_type').agg({
    'kw': ['sum', 'mean', 'max', 'min', 'count'],
    'meter_id': 'nunique',
})

# Named aggregation (pandas 0.25+ syntax)
df.groupby('customer_type').agg(
    total_kw=('kw', 'sum'),
    avg_kw=('kw', 'mean'),
    meter_count=('meter_id', 'count'),
)
Sorting

# Sort (identical to pandas)
df.sort_values('kw', ascending=False)
df.sort_values(['customer_type', 'kw'], ascending=[True, False])

# WARNING: sort_values() triggers a full shuffle + global sort.
# On large datasets, this is expensive (same as PySpark orderBy).
Merging (Joins)

# merge() (pandas-style joins)
merged = readings.merge(metadata, on='meter_id', how='left')

# Equivalent to:
# readings.join(metadata, 'meter_id', 'left')  in PySpark

# Multiple join keys
merged = readings.merge(weather, on=['region', 'date'], how='inner')

# Different column names
merged = readings.merge(regions,
    left_on='region_code', right_on='code', how='left')
Value Counts and Unique Values

# Value counts (familiar pandas method)
df['customer_type'].value_counts()

# Unique values
df['customer_type'].unique()

# Number of unique values
df['customer_type'].nunique()

# Drop duplicates
df.drop_duplicates()
df.drop_duplicates(subset=['meter_id', 'date'])
NULL Handling

# NULL detection (identical to pandas)
df.isnull().sum()       # Count NULLs per column
df['kw'].isna()         # Boolean Series

# Drop NULLs
df.dropna()             # Drop rows with any NULL
df.dropna(subset=['kw'])  # Drop only if kw is NULL

# Fill NULLs
df.fillna(0)            # Fill all with 0
df['kw'].fillna(df['kw'].mean())  # Fill with mean

sections.title: Lesson 5: The Index — The Biggest Conceptual Difference

blocks.content: The most fundamental difference between pandas and PySpark DataFrames is the index. pandas DataFrames have an index — a special column that identifies each row and enables .loc, .iloc, and alignment-based operations.
PySpark DataFrames have no index. Rows in Spark are unordered bags of data distributed across partitions. There is no concept of "row 5" or "the row with index value X."
pyspark.pandas must bridge this gap. It does so by maintaining a virtual index backed by an internal Spark column. This has significant implications.
Default Index Behavior

df = ps.DataFrame({'kw': [100, 200, 300, 400, 500]})
print(df)

#    kw
# 0  100
# 1  200
# 2  300
# 3  400
# 4  500

# The index (0, 1, 2, 3, 4) is generated by Spark.
# It is NOT a physical column in the underlying Spark DataFrame.
# It is computed on demand.
Index Types

Index Type How It Works Cost When Used sequence (default) Generates sequential integers using a distributed computation Moderate (requires scan) Default for most operations distributed-sequence Globally sequential across all partitions High (requires full scan + coordination) When strict ordering matters distributed Monotonically increasing but with gaps between partitions Low (no coordination) When approximate ordering is sufficient

# Configure default index type
ps.set_option('compute.default_index_type', 'distributed-sequence')

# Or set a meaningful column as index (preferred)
df = df.set_index('meter_id')

# This is the best practice: use a business key as the index
# to avoid the cost of generating artificial indices.
.loc and .iloc

df.loc['M001']           # Row with index 'M001'
df.loc['M001':'M002']    # Rows from M001 to M002
df.loc[:, 'kw']          # All rows, 'kw' column

# .iloc: position-based indexing
df.iloc[0]               # First row
df.iloc[0:3]             # First 3 rows
df.iloc[:, 0]            # All rows, first column

# WARNING: .iloc on distributed data is expensive.
# Spark has no concept of row position.
# .iloc[1000] may require scanning the entire dataset
# to determine which partition contains row 1000.
The Index Performance Trap

In pandas, .loc and .iloc are O(1) operations — essentially free. In pyspark.pandas, they can trigger full dataset scans. This is the most common performance surprise for pandas users migrating to Spark.

Operation
pandas Cost
pyspark.pandas Cost
Why
df.iloc[0]
O(1) instant
O(n) full scan possible
No row ordering in Spark
df.loc['M001']
O(1) with index
O(n) unless column is partitioned
Spark scans all partitions for key
df.tail(5)
O(1) instant
O(n) full scan + sort
Spark must find global last 5 rows
df.shape
O(1) cached
O(n) triggers count() action
Spark counts by scanning all partitions
len(df)
O(1) cached
O(n) triggers count() action
Same as df.shape
Rule: If your workflow relies heavily on .iloc, .loc, .tail(), or frequent len() calls, pyspark.pandas will be slower than you expect. Redesign those patterns to use filter-based approaches instead.

sections.title: Lesson 6: What Does Not Map Cleanly

blocks.content: pyspark.pandas covers approximately 80-90% of the pandas API. But the remaining 10-20% includes operations that are fundamentally incompatible with distributed computation.
Operations That Do Not Work (or Work Poorly)

Category
pandas Operation
pyspark.pandas Behavior
Alternative
Row iteration
for index, row in df.iterrows()
Not supported (anti-pattern in Spark)
Use vectorized operations or apply()
In-place mutation
df.iloc[5, 3] = 999
Not supported (Spark is immutable)
df = df.withColumn or create new column
Multi-index
df.set_index(['a', 'b'])
Supported but with performance caveats
Use groupby instead when possible
Custom objects in cells
df['col'] = [MyObj(), MyObj()]
Not supported (Spark needs serializable types)
Flatten objects into primitive columns
Memory-dependent plots
df.plot()
Works but collects data to Driver first
Sample before plotting: df.sample(0.01).to_pandas().plot()
Arbitrary Python in apply
df.apply(complex_function)
Works via Pandas UDF but with overhead
Prefer built-in Spark functions
Pivot tables (large)
df.pivot_table()
Supported but expensive (wide shuffle)
Pre-aggregate before pivoting
eval() / query() (complex)
df.eval('a + b * c')
Supported for simple expressions
Use direct column arithmetic
The iterrows() Anti-Pattern
This is the most common pattern that pandas users bring to Spark, and it is the single worst thing you can do for performance:

# NEVER DO THIS (even in pandas, it's slow)
for index, row in df.iterrows():
    if row['kw'] > 1000:
        results.append(row['meter_id'])

# In pyspark.pandas, iterrows() is either not available
# or falls back to collecting all data to the Driver.

# CORRECT: vectorized operation
high_usage = df[df['kw'] > 1000]['meter_id']

# This translates to a Spark filter + select,
# distributed across all executors. No row-by-row loop.
The In-Place Mutation Problem
pandas DataFrames are mutable. You can change a value at a specific position:

# pandas: works fine (mutable DataFrame)
import pandas as pd
pdf = pd.DataFrame({'kw': [100, 200, 300]})
pdf.loc[1, 'kw'] = 999  # Change second row's kw to 999

# pyspark.pandas: NOT supported
# Spark DataFrames are IMMUTABLE.
# You cannot modify a specific cell in a distributed dataset.

# Instead: create a new column with the corrected value
import pyspark.pandas as ps
df = ps.DataFrame({'kw': [100, 200, 300]})
# df.loc[1, 'kw'] = 999  # This will raise an error or not persist

# Correct approach: use conditional logic
import numpy as np
df['kw'] = np.where(df.index == 1, 999, df['kw'])
You will often need to move between pandas, pyspark.pandas, and native PySpark DataFrames. Understanding the conversion costs is essential.
Conversion Methods
Conversion Map:

pandas DataFrame
       |  .to_pandas()  ^  ps.from_pandas()
       v                |
  pyspark.pandas DataFrame
       |  .to_spark()   ^  .pandas_api()
       v                |
  PySpark DataFrame

import pandas as pd
import pyspark.pandas as ps

# ---- pandas -> pyspark.pandas ----
pdf = pd.DataFrame({'kw': [100, 200, 300]})
psdf = ps.from_pandas(pdf)
# Data moves from Driver to Spark executors. Safe for small data.

# ---- pyspark.pandas -> pandas ----
pdf = psdf.to_pandas()
# WARNING: ALL data comes to Driver. Same danger as collect().
# Only safe after aggressive filtering/aggregation.

# ---- PySpark DataFrame -> pyspark.pandas ----
spark_df = spark.read.parquet('/data/readings/')
psdf = spark_df.pandas_api()
# Zero-copy: wraps the Spark DataFrame with pandas API. No data moves.

# ---- pyspark.pandas -> PySpark DataFrame ----
spark_df = psdf.to_spark()
# Zero-copy: unwraps to the underlying Spark DataFrame. No data moves.
Conversion Cost Summary

Conversion
Data Movement
Cost
Safe for Large Data?
ps.from_pandas(pdf)
Driver -> executors
Moderate
Only for small data (< 1 GB)
psdf.to_pandas()
Executors -> Driver
HIGH (collect)
NO — aggregate/filter first
spark_df.pandas_api()
None (wrapper only)
Free
Yes — no data moves
psdf.to_spark()
None (unwrapper only)
Free
Yes — no data moves
ps.read_parquet()
Disk -> executors
Normal Spark read
Yes (distributed read)
psdf.to_parquet()
Executors -> disk
Normal Spark write
Yes (distributed write)

The key insight: conversions between pyspark.pandas and PySpark DataFrames are free (just wrapping/unwrapping). Conversions between pyspark.pandas and regular pandas involve data movement and carry the same dangers as collect() and toPandas().
pandas users rely heavily on apply() for custom transformations. In pyspark.pandas, apply() works but with important performance implications.
How apply() Works in pyspark.pandas
apply() Execution Path:
df['result'] = df['kw'].apply(my_function)
Under the hood:

items: pyspark.pandas creates a Pandas UDF (vectorized)
Each partition's data is sent to a Python process
The function runs on each partition as a pandas operation
Results are sent back to the JVM

blocks.content: Cost: serialization + deserialization + Python execution vs. built-in functions: pure JVM execution (no Python overhead)

# apply() with a simple function (works, but suboptimal)
df['kw_tier'] = df['kw'].apply(lambda x: 'Low' if x < 500 else 'High')

# Better: use numpy.where (vectorized, runs in JVM)
import numpy as np
df['kw_tier'] = np.where(df['kw'] < 500, 'Low', 'High')

# Best: convert to Spark and use built-in functions
from pyspark.sql.functions import when, col
spark_df = df.to_spark()
spark_df = spark_df.withColumn('kw_tier',
    when(col('kw') < 500, 'Low').otherwise('High'))
df = spark_df.pandas_api()
apply() on DataFrame (Row-wise)

# Row-wise apply (EXPENSIVE: triggers Python row-by-row processing)
def classify_reading(row):
    if row['kw'] > 5000:
        return 'Critical'
    elif row['kw'] > 1000:
        return 'High'
    else:
        return 'Normal'

# This works but is slow on large datasets
df['classification'] = df.apply(classify_reading, axis=1)

# Better: vectorized with numpy
conditions = [df['kw'] > 5000, df['kw'] > 1000]
choices = ['Critical', 'High']
df['classification'] = np.select(conditions, choices, default='Normal')
The Decision Framework

Approach Performance When To Use Built-in Spark functions (via .to_spark()) Best (pure JVM) Complex logic, production pipelines numpy vectorized operations Good (compiled C) Simple arithmetic, conditionals, string ops Series.apply(function) Moderate (Pandas UDF) Simple per-element transforms; prototyping DataFrame.apply(function, axis=1) Poor (row-by-row Python) Last resort; consider rewriting as vectorized iterrows() / itertuples() Terrible (sequential Python) Never use on distributed data

sections.title: Lesson 9: Window Operations and Time Series in pyspark.pandas

blocks.content: Rolling Windows

# Sort by date first (required for meaningful rolling)
df = df.sort_values('date')

# 7-day rolling average (pandas syntax)
df['rolling_avg_7d'] = df.groupby('meter_id')['kw'].rolling(7).mean().reset_index(drop=True)

# Rolling sum
df['rolling_sum_7d'] = df.groupby('meter_id')['kw'].rolling(7).sum().reset_index(drop=True)

# NOTE: rolling() in pyspark.pandas translates to Spark window functions.
# The underlying execution is identical to Module 15.
# But the pandas syntax is more concise for simple cases.
Shift (lag/lead Equivalent)

# shift() = lag in pandas terminology
df['prev_kw'] = df.groupby('meter_id')['kw'].shift(1)
df['next_kw'] = df.groupby('meter_id')['kw'].shift(-1)

# Day-over-day change
df['kw_change'] = df['kw'] - df['prev_kw']

# Percentage change
df['kw_pct_change'] = df.groupby('meter_id')['kw'].pct_change()

# These translate to Spark lag() and lead() window functions.
Expanding Windows (Cumulative)

# Cumulative sum (expanding window)
df['cumulative_kw'] = df.groupby('meter_id')['kw'].expanding().sum().reset_index(drop=True)

# Cumulative max
df['running_max'] = df.groupby('meter_id')['kw'].expanding().max().reset_index(drop=True)

# These translate to Spark window functions with
# UNBOUNDED PRECEDING to CURRENT ROW frame.
Resampling (Time-Based Aggregation)

# Set date as index for time-series operations
df['date'] = ps.to_datetime(df['date'])
df = df.set_index('date')

# Resample to monthly frequency
monthly = df.groupby('meter_id').resample('M')['kw'].sum()

# Resample to weekly
weekly = df.groupby('meter_id').resample('W')['kw'].mean()

# NOTE: resample support in pyspark.pandas is limited.
# For complex time-series operations, use Spark SQL
# window functions directly (Module 15).
pyspark.pandas supports .plot() using matplotlib, just like pandas. But there is an important caveat: plotting requires collecting data to the Driver.
Safe Plotting Patterns

# SAFE: Plot aggregated data (small result set)
summary = df.groupby('customer_type')['kw'].sum()
summary.plot.bar(title='Total Consumption by Type')

# SAFE: Plot after sampling
sample = df.sample(frac=0.01)  # 1% random sample
sample['kw'].plot.hist(bins=50, title='kW Distribution (1% sample)')

# SAFE: Plot a pre-aggregated time series
daily = df.groupby('date')['kw'].mean()
daily.plot.line(title='Average Daily Consumption')

# DANGEROUS: Plot raw data (tries to collect everything)
# df['kw'].plot.hist()  # On 10 GB dataset = Driver OOM

# RULE: Always aggregate or sample BEFORE plotting.
# The plot function runs on the Driver using matplotlib.
# All data must fit in Driver memory.
Converting to pandas for Advanced Visualization

# For complex visualizations, aggregate in Spark,
# then convert to pandas for the visualization library.

# Step 1: Aggregate at scale (runs on Spark cluster)
hourly_avg = df.groupby(['region', 'hour']).agg(
    avg_kw=('kw', 'mean'),
    count=('kw', 'count'),
)

# Step 2: Convert to pandas (small result: ~24 rows per region)
pdf = hourly_avg.to_pandas()

# Step 3: Use any Python visualization library
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

pivot = pdf.pivot(index='hour', columns='region', values='avg_kw')
sns.heatmap(pivot, cmap='YlOrRd', annot=True)
plt.title('Average kW by Hour and Region')
plt.show()

sections.title: Lesson 11: Migrating pandas Code to pyspark.pandas

blocks.content: If you have an existing pandas codebase that needs to scale, here is a systematic migration approach.
Step-by-Step Migration
Migration Workflow:
Step 1: Change import import pandas as pd --> import pyspark.pandas as ps

Step 2: Change readers
          pd.read_csv(...)     -->  ps.read_csv(...)
          pd.read_parquet(...) -->  ps.read_parquet(...)
Step 3: Run and identify failures Most operations will work unchanged. Note which operations fail or are slow.
Step 4: Fix incompatibilities Replace iterrows() with vectorized ops. Replace .iloc mutations with conditional logic. Replace .tail() with sorting + filtering.
Step 5: Optimize hot paths Convert performance-critical sections to native PySpark DataFrame API.
Step 6: Validate results Compare output of pandas version vs pyspark.pandas on a small test dataset to verify correctness.
Common Migration Patterns

pandas Pattern
pyspark.pandas Equivalent
Notes
pd.read_csv('file.csv')
ps.read_csv('file.csv')
Direct replacement; supports distributed paths
df.to_csv('out.csv')
df.to_csv('out.csv')
Writes multiple files (one per partition)
df.to_csv('out.csv', index=False)
df.to_pandas().to_csv('out.csv', index=False)
For single file: collect to pandas first (small data only)
pd.concat([df1, df2])
ps.concat([df1, df2])
Direct replacement
df.pivot_table(...)
df.pivot_table(...)
Supported but can be expensive with many values
df.apply(fn, axis=1)
df.apply(fn, axis=1)
Works but slow; prefer vectorized operations
df.iterrows()
NOT AVAILABLE
Must rewrite as vectorized operation
df.iloc[5] = value
NOT AVAILABLE
Must rewrite with conditional column assignment
df.plot()
df.plot() or df.sample().to_pandas().plot()
Aggregate or sample first for large data
df.memory_usage()
Not meaningful (data is on cluster)
Use Spark UI for memory monitoring
Migration Example: Smart Grid Analysis

# ======== ORIGINAL pandas CODE ========
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('meter_readings.csv')
df['date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['date'])
df = df[df['kw'] > 0]  # Remove invalid readings
df = df.drop_duplicates(subset=['meter_id', 'date'])

# Compute daily summary per customer type
summary = df.groupby(['customer_type', 'date']).agg({
    'kw': ['sum', 'mean', 'max'],
    'meter_id': 'nunique'
})
summary.columns = ['total_kw', 'avg_kw', 'max_kw', 'meter_count']
summary = summary.reset_index()
summary.to_parquet('daily_summary.parquet')

# ======== MIGRATED pyspark.pandas CODE ========
import pyspark.pandas as ps

df = ps.read_csv('/data/meter_readings/')      # distributed path
df['date'] = ps.to_datetime(df['date'])
df = df[df['kw'] > 0]
df = df.drop_duplicates(subset=['meter_id', 'date'])

summary = df.groupby(['customer_type', 'date']).agg(
    total_kw=('kw', 'sum'),
    avg_kw=('kw', 'mean'),
    max_kw=('kw', 'max'),
    meter_count=('meter_id', 'nunique'),
)
summary = summary.reset_index()
summary.to_parquet('/output/daily_summary/')    # distributed output

# Changes: 3 lines (import, input path, output path)
# Everything else is IDENTICAL.
# But this version runs on a 500-node cluster processing terabytes.

sections.title: Lesson 12: When to Use Which API

blocks.content: The existence of three APIs (pandas, pyspark.pandas, native PySpark DataFrame) raises the question: which should you use when?
Decision Framework
API Decision Tree:

Does data fit in memory of a single machine?
  |
  +-- YES (< 10 GB): Use pandas.
  |   Fastest for small data. Full API. No overhead.
  |
  +-- NO (> 10 GB): Is this exploration or production?
      |
      +-- EXPLORATION (notebook, prototyping):
      |   Use pyspark.pandas.
      |   Familiar syntax. Quick iteration. Easy plotting.
      |
      +-- PRODUCTION (pipeline, orchestrated job):
          Use native PySpark DataFrames.
          Full control. Best performance. Explicit plans.
Detailed Comparison

Aspect pandas pyspark.pandas PySpark DataFrame Scale limit ~10 GB (single machine RAM) Petabytes (Spark cluster) Petabytes (Spark cluster) Execution Immediate (eager) Lazy (Spark engine) Lazy (Spark engine) Syntax familiarity Standard (millions know it) ~90% pandas compatible Different API (learning curve) Performance control Limited (single machine) Limited (hides Spark details) Full (partitions, hints, plans) Index support Full (.loc, .iloc, MultiIndex) Partial (with performance caveats) None (no row ordering concept) Plotting Direct (matplotlib, seaborn) Direct (collects to Driver) Via toPandas() after aggregation Apply/UDF performance Fast (CPython/NumPy) Moderate (Pandas UDF overhead) Best via built-in functions Production suitability Small pipelines Prototyping, migration bridge Production pipelines Debugging Line-by-line (eager) Harder (lazy; errors delayed) Harder (lazy; use explain()) Testing pytest (fast) Needs SparkSession (slower) Needs SparkSession (slower)
The Hybrid Workflow
In practice, the best teams use all three APIs at different stages:
Hybrid Workflow:
1. EXPLORE with pyspark.pandas in a notebook - Quick iteration, familiar syntax, easy .plot() - Understand the data, identify patterns
2. PROTOTYPE transformations with pyspark.pandas - Build the logic, validate on sample data - Use .to_spark() for any steps that need native API

3. PRODUCTIONIZE with native PySpark DataFrames - Rewrite performance-critical paths - Add explicit partitioning, broadcast hints, caching - Use explain() to verify execution plans - Wrap in functions, add quality checks, deploy via CI/CD
4. VISUALIZE with pandas - Aggregate at scale with Spark - Convert small results to pandas for matplotlib/seaborn/plotly

sections.title: Lesson 13: Tips, Configuration, and Common Gotchas

blocks.content: Key Configuration Options

import pyspark.pandas as ps

# Show all rows in display (default: 1000)
ps.set_option('display.max_rows', 200)

# Default index type (affects performance of index-heavy operations)
ps.set_option('compute.default_index_type', 'distributed-sequence')

# Operations resulting in small DataFrames: compute eagerly
ps.set_option('compute.shortcut_limit', 1000)

# Convert to Spark operations mode for debugging
ps.set_option('compute.ops_on_diff_frames', True)
# By default, pyspark.pandas raises an error if you combine
# two DataFrames not derived from the same source.
# This option allows it (with implicit join on index).
The ops_on_diff_frames Gotcha
One of the most confusing behaviors for pandas users:

# pandas: this works (aligns by index)
import pandas as pd
a = pd.Series([1, 2, 3])
b = pd.Series([10, 20, 30])
result = a + b  # [11, 22, 33]

# pyspark.pandas: ERROR by default
import pyspark.pandas as ps
a = ps.Series([1, 2, 3])
b = ps.Series([10, 20, 30])
# result = a + b  # PandasNotImplementedError!

# Why? In Spark, there is no guaranteed row alignment between
# two independent DataFrames. pandas aligns by index.
# Spark has no index, so alignment is undefined.

# Fix 1: Enable ops_on_diff_frames (Spark joins by index)
ps.set_option('compute.ops_on_diff_frames', True)
result = a + b  # Works, but triggers a join (potential shuffle)

# Fix 2: Derive both from the same DataFrame
df = ps.DataFrame({'a': [1, 2, 3], 'b': [10, 20, 30]})
df['result'] = df['a'] + df['b']  # No error, no extra join
Performance Gotchas Summary

Gotcha Why It Happens Mitigation df.shape is slow Triggers count() action across cluster Avoid in loops; use Spark UI for row counts df.tail() is slow Requires full scan and global sort Use df.head() (reads limited partitions) .iloc[n] is slow No row ordering in Spark; requires full scan Use boolean indexing or filter instead apply() is slower than expected Triggers Python serialization for each partition Use numpy or built-in Spark functions .plot() crashes on large data Collects all data to Driver for matplotlib Aggregate or sample first Operations on different DataFrames fail Default ops_on_diff_frames is False Derive from same DataFrame or enable option sort_values() is expensive Triggers full shuffle + global sort Only sort when necessary; use nsmallest()/nlargest() for top-N Column assignment creates copies Spark DataFrames are immutable Chain operations; minimize intermediate DataFrames String operations are slow Each .str method may trigger separate passes Batch string operations; prefer regexp_replace via Spark to_csv() creates multiple files Distributed write: one file per partition Use .to_pandas().to_csv() for single file (small data only)
You now understand:

items: What pyspark.pandas is: a pandas-compatible API layer over PySpark DataFrames, merged from the Koalas project in Spark 3.2; your code is pandas syntax, execution is Spark engine
Architecture: every pyspark.pandas operation translates to PySpark DataFrame operations → Catalyst → DAG → distributed execution; all performance concepts from previous modules apply

Core operations that map cleanly: column selection, filtering, boolean indexing, arithmetic, groupby().agg(), merge(), sort_values(), value_counts(), drop_duplicates(), NULL handling — approximately 80-90% of the pandas API
The index difference: pandas has O(1) index-based access; pyspark.pandas maintains a virtual index backed by Spark; .loc, .iloc, .tail(), len() can be expensive (O(n) scans)

What does not map cleanly: iterrows() (not available), in-place cell mutation (Spark is immutable), complex MultiIndex operations, memory-dependent plotting on raw data, and arbitrary apply() functions
Conversion paths: pandas ↔ pyspark.pandas (data moves, collection risk) vs PySpark DataFrame ↔ pyspark.pandas (free wrapper, zero data movement)
apply() performance hierarchy: built-in Spark functions (best) > numpy vectorized (good) > Series.apply (moderate) > DataFrame.apply(axis=1) (poor) > iterrows (never)
Window operations: rolling(), shift(), expanding(), pct_change() all translate to Spark window functions; resample() has limited support
Plotting: always aggregate or sample before .plot(); plotting collects data to Driver; use pandas for advanced visualization after aggregation
Migration workflow: change import, change readers, run and fix failures, optimize hot paths to native PySpark, validate results
When to use which API: pandas for small data, pyspark.pandas for exploration and migration bridge, native PySpark DataFrame for production pipelines
Configuration: ops_on_diff_frames, default_index_type, display settings; the ops_on_diff_frames gotcha (operations on independent DataFrames fail by default)
Performance gotchas: shape/tail/iloc are expensive, apply() has serialization overhead, plot() collects to Driver, sort_values() triggers shuffle, string operations are slow

blocks.content: You now have three levels of the Python data stack at your disposal: pandas for single-machine agility, pyspark.pandas for the bridge between familiarity and scale, and native PySpark DataFrames for full distributed control.
Knowing when to use each — and how to move between them fluidly — is the mark of a versatile data professional.

In Module 19, we build the End-to-End Capstone: Smart Grid Platform — a complete, deployable data platform integrating everything from Modules 1–18: ingestion, cleaning, quality gates, star schema gold layer, streaming alerts, orchestration, security, and operational monitoring.

chapters.title: Module 19: End-to-End Capstone
chapters.description: The Smart Grid Analytics Platform Design and build a complete, production-grade data platform from raw ingestion through business-ready analytics — integrating every concept from Modules 1–18 into a single cohesive system: Medallion architecture on Delta Lake, SCD Type 2 dimensions, star schema gold layer, streaming anomaly detection, data quality gates, orchestration, security, performance optimization, and operational monitoring.

blocks.content: For 18 modules, you have learned skills in isolation. You learned DataFrames in one module, joins in another, streaming in another, Delta Lake in another. Each module taught a focused capability.
But production platforms are not built from isolated skills.

They are systems. A system where the streaming layer depends on the schema defined in the gold layer. Where the quality checks in the silver layer protect the accuracy of dashboards in the gold layer. Where the orchestration layer must understand the dependency chain from bronze through silver through gold. Where security policies span every table. Where a single poorly partitioned join can cascade into a 4-hour pipeline delay.
This module is where everything connects.

You will build the Smart Grid Analytics Platform — a complete, deployable data platform for a utility company monitoring 1 million smart meters. This is not a toy exercise. The architecture patterns, code organization, and operational practices in this module are what you would encounter on Day 1 of a senior data engineering role.
What You Will Build:

RAW DATA (streaming + batch)
       |
  [ BRONZE ] -- raw ingestion, append-only, audit trail
       |
  [ SILVER ] -- cleaned, validated, quality-gated
       |
  [ GOLD ]   -- star schema + wide tables + streaming alerts
       |
  [ CONSUMERS ] -- BI dashboards, ML models, operational alerts
Orchestrated by Airflow. Secured by RBAC. Monitored by metrics tables. Tested by quality checks.
Module Map: Where Every Concept Lives

Capstone Component Modules Applied Bronze ingestion (batch + streaming) M2 (DataFrames), M7 (cleaning patterns), M10 (Structured Streaming), M11 (Delta Lake) Silver cleaning + validation M7 (data cleaning), M8 (complex types), M12 (data quality), M15 (window functions) Gold star schema + SCD M5 (joins), M6 (SQL), M17 (data modeling), M11 (MERGE) Streaming anomaly detection M10 (streaming), M15 (window functions), M9 (UDFs) Performance optimization M3 (internals), M4 (tuning), M16 (AQE, DPP, bucketing) Quality gates M12 (testing/quality), M7 (medallion architecture) Orchestration M13 (deployment/Airflow) Security and governance M14 (RBAC, masking, audit) Operational monitoring M12 (observability), M16 (benchmarking) Exploration and visualization M18 (pandas API on Spark)
