settings.py

# config/settings.py
"""Central configuration for all paths and constants."""

# ---- Storage Paths ----
BRONZE_PATH = '/data/bronze'
SILVER_PATH = '/data/silver'
GOLD_PATH   = '/data/gold'
METRICS_PATH = '/data/ops/metrics'
CHECKPOINTS  = '/data/checkpoints'

# ---- Table Names ----
BRONZE_READINGS  = f'{BRONZE_PATH}/meter_readings'
BRONZE_CUSTOMERS = f'{BRONZE_PATH}/customers'
BRONZE_WEATHER   = f'{BRONZE_PATH}/weather_daily'
BRONZE_METERS    = f'{BRONZE_PATH}/meters'

SILVER_READINGS  = f'{SILVER_PATH}/meter_readings'
SILVER_CUSTOMERS = f'{SILVER_PATH}/customers'
SILVER_WEATHER   = f'{SILVER_PATH}/weather_daily'
SILVER_METERS    = f'{SILVER_PATH}/meters'

GOLD_DIM_DATES     = f'{GOLD_PATH}/dim_dates'
GOLD_DIM_CUSTOMERS = f'{GOLD_PATH}/dim_customers'
GOLD_DIM_METERS    = f'{GOLD_PATH}/dim_meters'
GOLD_DIM_REGIONS   = f'{GOLD_PATH}/dim_regions'
GOLD_FACT_DAILY    = f'{GOLD_PATH}/fact_daily_consumption'
GOLD_ML_FEATURES   = f'{GOLD_PATH}/ml_features_daily'

# ---- Quality Thresholds ----
MAX_VALID_KW = 15000       # Maximum physically possible reading
MIN_VALID_KW = 0           # Minimum valid reading
MAX_NULL_RATE = 0.05       # Fail if > 5% nulls in critical columns
MIN_ROW_COUNT_READINGS = 80_000_000  # Expect at least 80M readings/day

# ---- Retention ----
BRONZE_RETENTION_DAYS = 90
SILVER_RETENTION_DAYS = 730   # 2 years
VACUUM_HOURS = 168            # 7 days
schemas.py

# config/schemas.py
"""Explicit schemas for all ingested data."""

from pyspark.sql.types import (
    StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType,
    TimestampType, DateType, IntegerType
)

METER_READING_SCHEMA = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id', StringType(), nullable=False),
    StructField('timestamp', TimestampType(), nullable=False),
    StructField('kw', DoubleType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('voltage', DoubleType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('power_factor', DoubleType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('region', StringType(), nullable=True),
])

CUSTOMER_SCHEMA = StructType([
    StructField('customer_id', StringType(), nullable=False),
    StructField('customer_name', StringType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('customer_type', StringType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('address', StringType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('region', StringType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('signup_date', DateType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('segment', StringType(), nullable=True),
])

WEATHER_SCHEMA = StructType([
    StructField('region', StringType(), nullable=False),
    StructField('date', DateType(), nullable=False),
    StructField('temp_high_f', DoubleType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('temp_low_f', DoubleType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('precipitation_in', DoubleType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('wind_speed_mph', DoubleType(), nullable=True),
])
The bronze layer captures raw data exactly as received. No transformations. No cleaning. Append-only. This is your audit trail and recovery point.
Batch Ingestion

# ingestion/bronze_batch.py
"""Batch ingestion for customers, meters, weather."""

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import current_timestamp, lit, current_date
from config.settings import *
from config.schemas import CUSTOMER_SCHEMA, WEATHER_SCHEMA

def ingest_bronze_batch(spark, source_path, target_path, schema, source_name):
    """Generic batch ingestion to bronze layer."""
    raw = spark.read \
        .schema(schema) \
        .option('header', True) \
        .csv(source_path)

    enriched = raw \
        .withColumn('_ingestion_timestamp', current_timestamp()) \
        .withColumn('_source_system', lit(source_name)) \
        .withColumn('_ingestion_date', current_date())

    enriched.write \
        .format('delta') \
        .mode('append') \
        .partitionBy('_ingestion_date') \
        .save(target_path)

    row_count = enriched.count()
    return row_count

def run_daily_batch(spark, processing_date):
    """Orchestrated daily batch ingestion."""
    counts = {}
counts['customers'] = ingest_bronze_batch( spark, f'/landing/customers/{processing_date}/', BRONZE_CUSTOMERS, CUSTOMER_SCHEMA, 'CRM_EXPORT' )
counts['weather'] = ingest_bronze_batch( spark, f'/landing/weather/{processing_date}/', BRONZE_WEATHER, WEATHER_SCHEMA, 'WEATHER_API' )
return counts
Streaming Ingestion

# ingestion/bronze_streaming.py
"""Streaming ingestion for meter readings (Structured Streaming)."""

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import current_timestamp, lit, to_date, col
from config.settings import BRONZE_READINGS, CHECKPOINTS
from config.schemas import METER_READING_SCHEMA

def start_meter_reading_stream(spark):
    """Ingest meter readings from Kafka to bronze Delta table."""

    raw_stream = spark.readStream \
        .format('kafka') \
        .option('kafka.bootstrap.servers', 'kafka:9092') \
        .option('subscribe', 'smart_meter_readings') \
        .option('startingOffsets', 'latest') \
        .load()

    # Parse JSON payload from Kafka value
    from pyspark.sql.functions import from_json

    parsed = raw_stream \
        .select(from_json(
            col('value').cast('string'),
            METER_READING_SCHEMA
        ).alias('data')) \
        .select('data.*') \
        .withColumn('_ingestion_timestamp', current_timestamp()) \
        .withColumn('_source_system', lit('KAFKA_METERS')) \
        .withColumn('_ingestion_date', to_date(col('timestamp')))

    # Write to bronze Delta with checkpointing
    query = parsed.writeStream \
        .format('delta') \
        .outputMode('append') \
        .option('checkpointLocation', f'{CHECKPOINTS}/bronze_readings') \
        .partitionBy('_ingestion_date') \
        .trigger(processingTime='1 minute') \
        .start(BRONZE_READINGS)

    return query

Key design decisions (M2, M10, M11): Explicit schemas prevent silent type drift. Kafka ingestion uses Structured Streaming with exactly-once guarantees via checkpointing. Partitioning by _ingestion_date enables efficient incremental processing. Append-only bronze preserves the raw audit trail.

sections.title: Lesson 6: Silver Layer — Cleaning, Validation, and Quality Gates

blocks.content: Silver Readings Pipeline

# processing/silver_readings.py
"""Bronze -> Silver meter readings transformation."""

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col, when, lit, row_number, lag, avg, abs as spark_abs,
    to_date, current_timestamp, datediff
)
from pyspark.sql.window import Window
from config.settings import *

def process_silver_readings(spark, processing_date):
    """
    Transform bronze readings to silver:
    1. Deduplicate (M15: row_number window function)
    2. Validate ranges (M7: cleaning patterns)
    3. Flag anomalies (M15: rolling window + z-score)
    4. Gap-fill missing readings (M15: forward fill)
    5. Write to silver Delta (M11: replaceWhere for idempotency)
    """

    # --- Read today's bronze data ---
    bronze = spark.read.format('delta') \
        .load(BRONZE_READINGS) \
        .filter(col('_ingestion_date') == processing_date)

    # --- Step 1: Deduplicate (Module 15) ---
    dedup_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id', 'timestamp') \
        .orderBy(col('_ingestion_timestamp').desc())

    deduped = bronze \
        .withColumn('_rn', row_number().over(dedup_window)) \
        .filter(col('_rn') == 1) \
        .drop('_rn')

    # --- Step 2: Validate ranges (Module 7) ---
    validated = deduped \
        .withColumn('kw_valid',
            when(
                (col('kw') >= MIN_VALID_KW) & (col('kw') <= MAX_VALID_KW),
                col('kw')
            ).otherwise(lit(None))
        ) \
        .withColumn('is_invalid_kw',
            when(col('kw_valid').isNull() & col('kw').isNotNull(), True)
            .otherwise(False)
        )

    # --- Step 3: Anomaly detection (Module 15) ---
    stats_window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id') \
        .orderBy('timestamp') \
        .rowsBetween(-96, -1)  # Previous 24 hours (96 x 15-min)

    with_stats = validated \
        .withColumn('rolling_mean', avg('kw_valid').over(stats_window)) \
        .withColumn('kw_deviation',
            spark_abs(col('kw_valid') - col('rolling_mean'))) \
        .withColumn('is_anomaly',
            when(col('kw_deviation') > col('rolling_mean') * 3, True)
            .otherwise(False))

    # --- Step 4: Select silver columns ---
    silver = with_stats.select(
        'meter_id',
        'timestamp',
        col('kw_valid').alias('kw'),
        'voltage',
        'power_factor',
        'region',
        'is_invalid_kw',
        'is_anomaly',
        to_date('timestamp').alias('date'),
        current_timestamp().alias('_processed_timestamp'),
    )

    # --- Step 5: Write to silver (Module 11 — idempotent) ---
    silver.write.format('delta') \
        .mode('overwrite') \
        .option('replaceWhere', f"date = '{processing_date}'") \
        .partitionBy('date') \
        .save(SILVER_READINGS)

    return silver.count()
Silver Customers Pipeline

# processing/silver_customers.py
"""Bronze -> Silver customers with PII pseudonymization."""

from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col, trim, upper, sha2, concat, lit, current_timestamp
)
from config.settings import *

def process_silver_customers(spark, processing_date):
    """
    Clean and pseudonymize customer data:
    1. Trim and standardize strings (M7)
    2. Pseudonymize PII (M14: sha2 hashing)
    3. Write to silver Delta
    """
    bronze = spark.read.format('delta') \
        .load(BRONZE_CUSTOMERS) \
        .filter(col('_ingestion_date') == processing_date)

    silver = bronze \
        .withColumn('customer_type', trim(upper(col('customer_type')))) \
        .withColumn('region', trim(upper(col('region')))) \
        .withColumn('segment', trim(upper(col('segment')))) \
        .withColumn('customer_name_hash',
            sha2(concat(col('customer_name'), lit('|SALT|grid2026')), 256)) \
        .withColumn('address_hash',
            sha2(concat(col('address'), lit('|SALT|grid2026')), 256)) \
        .drop('customer_name', 'address') \
        .withColumn('_processed_timestamp', current_timestamp())

    silver.write.format('delta') \
        .mode('overwrite') \
        .save(SILVER_CUSTOMERS)

    return silver.count()

sections.title: Lesson 7: Quality Gates — Guarding Layer Transitions

blocks.content: Quality Gate Orchestration

# quality/gates.py
"""Quality gate logic: pass/fail decisions for layer transitions."""

from quality.checks import *
from monitoring.metrics import log_quality_result
from config.settings import MAX_NULL_RATE, MIN_ROW_COUNT_READINGS

def run_silver_readings_gate(spark, processing_date):
    """Quality gate between silver readings and gold layer."""
    silver = spark.read.format('delta') \
        .load(SILVER_READINGS) \
        .filter(col('date') == processing_date)

    results = [
        check_null_rate(silver, 'meter_id', 0.0),
        check_null_rate(silver, 'kw', MAX_NULL_RATE),
        check_min_row_count(silver, MIN_ROW_COUNT_READINGS),
        check_no_duplicates(silver, ['meter_id', 'timestamp']),
    ]

    # Log all results to metrics table
    for result in results:
        log_quality_result(spark, result, 'silver_readings', processing_date)

    # Gate decision: ALL checks must pass
    all_passed = all(r.passed for r in results)
    failed = [r for r in results if not r.passed]
if not all_passed: raise ValueError( f'Quality gate FAILED for silver_readings on {processing_date}. ' f'Failed checks: {[f.check_name for f in failed]}' )
return results

Design rationale (M12): Quality gates are fail-fast barriers. If silver data fails validation, the gold layer is NOT updated. This prevents corrupted data from reaching analysts and dashboards. Every quality result is logged to a metrics table for trend analysis.

sections.title: Lesson 8: Gold Layer — Star Schema and SCD

blocks.content: SCD Type 2: dim_customers

# modeling/dim_customers.py
"""SCD Type 2 customer dimension (Module 17)."""

from delta.tables import DeltaTable
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col, lit, current_date, sha2, concat, row_number
)
from pyspark.sql.window import Window
from config.settings import SILVER_CUSTOMERS, GOLD_DIM_CUSTOMERS

def update_dim_customers(spark):
    """Apply SCD Type 2 logic to customer dimension."""

    incoming = spark.read.format('delta').load(SILVER_CUSTOMERS)

    # Check if dimension table exists
    try:
        dim_table = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, GOLD_DIM_CUSTOMERS)
    except Exception:
        # First run: create dimension from scratch
        initial = incoming \
            .withColumn('customer_key',
                row_number().over(Window.orderBy('customer_id'))) \
            .withColumn('effective_date', current_date()) \
            .withColumn('end_date', lit('9999-12-31').cast('date')) \
            .withColumn('is_current', lit(True))
        initial.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite') \
            .save(GOLD_DIM_CUSTOMERS)
        return initial.count()

    # --- Identify changes (SCD Type 2 columns) ---
    current_dim = spark.read.format('delta') \
        .load(GOLD_DIM_CUSTOMERS) \
        .filter(col('is_current') == True)

    scd_columns = ['customer_type', 'region', 'segment']

    changed = incoming.alias('new').join(
        current_dim.alias('old'),
        col('new.customer_id') == col('old.customer_id')
    ).filter(
        (col('new.customer_type') != col('old.customer_type')) |
        (col('new.region') != col('old.region')) |
        (col('new.segment') != col('old.segment'))
    ).select('new.*')

    new_records = incoming.join(
        current_dim, 'customer_id', 'left_anti'
    )

    # --- Build staged merge data ---
    rows_to_close = changed.select(
        col('customer_id').alias('merge_key')
    )

    new_versions = changed \
        .withColumn('effective_date', current_date()) \
        .withColumn('end_date', lit('9999-12-31').cast('date')) \
        .withColumn('is_current', lit(True)) \
        .withColumn('merge_key', lit(None).cast('string'))

    brand_new = new_records \
        .withColumn('effective_date', current_date()) \
        .withColumn('end_date', lit('9999-12-31').cast('date')) \
        .withColumn('is_current', lit(True)) \
        .withColumn('merge_key', lit(None).cast('string'))

    staged = rows_to_close \
        .unionByName(new_versions, allowMissingColumns=True) \
        .unionByName(brand_new, allowMissingColumns=True)

    # --- Execute MERGE ---
    dim_table.alias('target').merge(
        staged.alias('source'),
        'target.customer_id = source.merge_key AND target.is_current = true'
    ).whenMatchedUpdate(set={
        'end_date': 'current_date()',
        'is_current': 'false',
    }).whenNotMatchedInsertAll().execute()

    return changed.count() + new_records.count()
Fact Table: fact_daily_consumption

# modeling/fact_daily.py
"""Build daily consumption fact table (Module 17)."""

from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col, sum, avg, max as spark_max, min as spark_min, count,
    date_format, broadcast
)
from config.settings import *

def build_fact_daily(spark, processing_date):
    """
    Aggregate silver readings to daily grain,
    join to dimension surrogate keys,
    write with replaceWhere for idempotency.
    """
    # Read silver readings for today
    readings = spark.read.format('delta') \
        .load(SILVER_READINGS) \
        .filter((col('date') == processing_date) &
               (col('is_invalid_kw') == False))

    # Aggregate to daily grain
    daily = readings.groupBy('meter_id', 'region', 'date').agg(
        sum('kw').alias('total_kw'),
        avg('kw').alias('avg_kw'),
        spark_max('kw').alias('max_kw'),
        spark_min('kw').alias('min_kw'),
        count('*').alias('reading_count'),
        sum(col('is_anomaly').cast('int')).alias('anomaly_count'),
    )

    # --- Join to dimension surrogate keys (M4: broadcast small dims) ---
    dim_customers = spark.read.format('delta') \
        .load(GOLD_DIM_CUSTOMERS) \
        .filter(col('is_current') == True) \
        .select('customer_key', 'customer_id')

    # Lookup customer_id for each meter from silver.meters
    meter_customer = spark.read.format('delta') \
        .load(SILVER_METERS) \
        .select('meter_id', 'customer_id')

    dim_meters = spark.read.format('delta') \
        .load(GOLD_DIM_METERS) \
        .select('meter_key', 'meter_id')

    dim_regions = spark.read.format('delta') \
        .load(GOLD_DIM_REGIONS) \
        .select('region_key', 'region_name')

    # Build fact with surrogate keys
    fact = daily \
        .join(broadcast(meter_customer), 'meter_id', 'left') \
        .join(broadcast(dim_customers), 'customer_id', 'left') \
        .join(broadcast(dim_meters), 'meter_id', 'left') \
        .join(broadcast(dim_regions),
              daily.region == dim_regions.region_name, 'left') \
        .withColumn('date_key',
            date_format('date', 'yyyyMMdd').cast('int')) \
        .select(
            'meter_key', 'customer_key', 'date_key', 'region_key',
            'total_kw', 'avg_kw', 'max_kw', 'min_kw',
            'reading_count', 'anomaly_count',
        )

    # Write idempotently
    date_key_val = processing_date.replace('-', '')
    fact.write.format('delta') \
        .mode('overwrite') \
        .option('replaceWhere', f"date_key = {date_key_val}") \
        .save(GOLD_FACT_DAILY)

    return fact.count()

Performance decisions (M4, M16): All dimension lookups use broadcast joins because dimension tables are small (< 50 MB each). The fact table is partitioned by date_key for DPP and efficient incremental writes. replaceWhere ensures idempotent re-runs.

# streaming/anomaly_detector.py
"""Near real-time anomaly detection (Module 10)."""

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import (
    col, window, avg, stddev, abs as spark_abs,
    current_timestamp, lit, from_json
)
from config.schemas import METER_READING_SCHEMA
from config.settings import CHECKPOINTS

def start_anomaly_stream(spark):
    """
    Detect anomalous readings in near real-time:
    - Ingest from Kafka
    - Compute 1-hour tumbling window statistics per meter
    - Flag readings > 3 standard deviations from window mean
    - Write alerts to Delta table for operational dashboards
    """
    raw = spark.readStream \
        .format('kafka') \
        .option('kafka.bootstrap.servers', 'kafka:9092') \
        .option('subscribe', 'smart_meter_readings') \
        .load()

    parsed = raw.select(
        from_json(col('value').cast('string'), METER_READING_SCHEMA).alias('d')
    ).select('d.*')

    # 1-hour tumbling window aggregation with watermark
    windowed = parsed \
        .withWatermark('timestamp', '15 minutes') \
        .groupBy(
            col('meter_id'),
            col('region'),
            window('timestamp', '1 hour')
        ).agg(
            avg('kw').alias('avg_kw'),
            stddev('kw').alias('std_kw'),
        )

    # Flag anomalous windows (std_kw spike = unstable readings)
    alerts = windowed \
        .filter(
            (col('avg_kw') > 10000) |  # Absolute threshold
            (col('std_kw') > col('avg_kw') * 0.5)  # High variability
        ) \
        .withColumn('alert_type',
            lit('ANOMALOUS_CONSUMPTION')) \
        .withColumn('alert_timestamp', current_timestamp()) \
        .select(
            'meter_id', 'region',
            col('window.start').alias('window_start'),
            col('window.end').alias('window_end'),
            'avg_kw', 'std_kw', 'alert_type', 'alert_timestamp',
        )

    # Write alerts to Delta for dashboard consumption
    query = alerts.writeStream \
        .format('delta') \
        .outputMode('append') \
        .option('checkpointLocation',
            f'{CHECKPOINTS}/anomaly_alerts') \
        .start('/data/gold/alerts/anomaly_detections')

    return query

Design rationale (M10): Watermarking at 15 minutes handles late-arriving data. Tumbling windows aggregate per meter per hour for meaningful statistics. Alerts write to Delta for ACID guarantees and easy dashboard integration. The streaming pipeline runs alongside the batch pipeline — bronze feeds both.

# maintenance/optimize_tables.py
"""Delta table maintenance (Module 11)."""

from config.settings import *

TABLES_TO_OPTIMIZE = [
    {'path': SILVER_READINGS,  'zorder': ['meter_id', 'date']},
    {'path': GOLD_FACT_DAILY,  'zorder': ['meter_key', 'date_key']},
    {'path': GOLD_DIM_CUSTOMERS, 'zorder': ['customer_id']},
]

def run_optimize(spark):
    """OPTIMIZE + Z-ORDER all configured tables."""
    for table in TABLES_TO_OPTIMIZE:
        zorder_cols = ', '.join(table['zorder'])
        spark.sql(f"""
            OPTIMIZE delta.`{table['path']}`
            ZORDER BY ({zorder_cols})
        """)

def run_vacuum(spark):
    """VACUUM old versions from all Delta tables."""
    all_paths = [
        BRONZE_READINGS, BRONZE_CUSTOMERS, BRONZE_WEATHER, BRONZE_METERS,
        SILVER_READINGS, SILVER_CUSTOMERS, SILVER_WEATHER, SILVER_METERS,
        GOLD_DIM_CUSTOMERS, GOLD_DIM_METERS, GOLD_DIM_REGIONS,
        GOLD_FACT_DAILY, GOLD_ML_FEATURES,
    ]
    for path in all_paths:
        try:
            spark.sql(f"VACUUM delta.`{path}` RETAIN {VACUUM_HOURS} HOURS")
        except Exception as e:
            print(f'VACUUM failed for {path}: {e}')
ANALYZE TABLE for Statistics

def run_analyze(spark):
    """Compute table statistics for Catalyst CBO (Module 16)."""
    spark.sql(f"""
        ANALYZE TABLE delta.`{GOLD_FACT_DAILY}`
        COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS
            meter_key, customer_key, date_key, region_key, total_kw
    """)
    spark.sql(f"""
        ANALYZE TABLE delta.`{GOLD_DIM_CUSTOMERS}`
        COMPUTE STATISTICS
    """)

# monitoring/metrics.py
"""Pipeline metrics logging to Delta (Module 12)."""

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import current_timestamp, lit
from config.settings import METRICS_PATH

def log_pipeline_metric(spark, pipeline_name, metric_name, value,
                        processing_date):
    """Log a single metric to the metrics Delta table."""
    row = spark.createDataFrame([(
        pipeline_name, metric_name, float(value), processing_date
    )], ['pipeline', 'metric', 'value', 'date'])

    row.withColumn('logged_at', current_timestamp()) \
        .write.format('delta') \
        .mode('append') \
        .save(f'{METRICS_PATH}/pipeline_metrics')

def log_quality_result(spark, result, table_name, processing_date):
    """Log a quality check result."""
    row = spark.createDataFrame([(
        table_name,
        result.check_name,
        result.passed,
        float(result.metric),
        float(result.threshold),
        result.detail,
        processing_date,
    )], ['table', 'check', 'passed', 'metric', 'threshold',
         'detail', 'date'])

    row.withColumn('logged_at', current_timestamp()) \
        .write.format('delta') \
        .mode('append') \
        .save(f'{METRICS_PATH}/quality_results')
Anomaly Detection on Pipeline Metrics

# monitoring/alerts.py
"""Detect pipeline anomalies from historical metrics."""

from pyspark.sql.functions import avg, stddev, col, abs as spark_abs

def check_pipeline_anomalies(spark, pipeline_name, metric_name,
                              lookback_days=30):
    """
    Compare today's metric to historical average.
    Alert if > 3 standard deviations from mean.
    """
    metrics = spark.read.format('delta') \
        .load(f'{METRICS_PATH}/pipeline_metrics') \
        .filter(
            (col('pipeline') == pipeline_name) &
            (col('metric') == metric_name)
        )

    stats = metrics.filter(
        col('date') >= f'today - {lookback_days} days'
    ).agg(
        avg('value').alias('hist_mean'),
        stddev('value').alias('hist_std'),
    ).collect()[0]

    latest = metrics.orderBy(col('date').desc()).first()

if latest and stats.hist_std and stats.hist_std > 0: z_score = abs(latest.value - stats.hist_mean) / stats.hist_std if z_score > 3.0: return { 'alert': True, 'pipeline': pipeline_name, 'metric': metric_name, 'value': latest.value, 'mean': stats.hist_mean, 'z_score': z_score, } return {'alert': False}

# orchestration/dags/daily_pipeline.py
"""Airflow DAG: Daily Smart Grid Pipeline (Module 13)."""

from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.providers.apache.spark.operators.spark_submit import (
    SparkSubmitOperator
)
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator, BranchPythonOperator
from airflow.operators.email import EmailOperator

default_args = {
    'owner': 'data-engineering',
    'retries': 2,
    'retry_delay': timedelta(minutes=5),
    'email_on_failure': True,
    'email': ['data-team@smartgrid.com'],
}

with DAG(
    dag_id='smart_grid_daily_pipeline',
    default_args=default_args,
    schedule_interval='0 6 * * *',  # 6 AM daily
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['smart-grid', 'production'],
) as dag:

    # ---- BRONZE ----
    bronze_batch = SparkSubmitOperator(
        task_id='bronze_batch_ingestion',
        application='ingestion/bronze_batch.py',
        conn_id='spark_cluster',
        conf={'spark.sql.adaptive.enabled': 'true'},
    )

    # ---- SILVER ----
    silver_readings = SparkSubmitOperator(
        task_id='silver_readings',
        application='processing/silver_readings.py',
        conn_id='spark_cluster',
    )
    silver_customers = SparkSubmitOperator(
        task_id='silver_customers',
        application='processing/silver_customers.py',
        conn_id='spark_cluster',
    )

    # ---- QUALITY GATES ----
    quality_gate = SparkSubmitOperator(
        task_id='quality_gate_silver',
        application='quality/gates.py',
        conn_id='spark_cluster',
    )

    # ---- GOLD ----
    dim_customers = SparkSubmitOperator(
        task_id='gold_dim_customers_scd2',
        application='modeling/dim_customers.py',
        conn_id='spark_cluster',
    )
    fact_daily = SparkSubmitOperator(
        task_id='gold_fact_daily',
        application='modeling/fact_daily.py',
        conn_id='spark_cluster',
    )
    ml_features = SparkSubmitOperator(
        task_id='gold_ml_features',
        application='modeling/wide_ml_features.py',
        conn_id='spark_cluster',
    )

    # ---- MONITORING ----
    check_metrics = SparkSubmitOperator(
        task_id='check_pipeline_metrics',
        application='monitoring/alerts.py',
        conn_id='spark_cluster',
    )

    # ---- DEPENDENCY CHAIN ----
    bronze_batch >> [silver_readings, silver_customers]
    [silver_readings, silver_customers] >> quality_gate
    quality_gate >> dim_customers
    dim_customers >> fact_daily
    fact_daily >> ml_features
    ml_features >> check_metrics
DAG Dependency Graph:

bronze_batch
       |
       +----> silver_readings ---+
       |                         |
       +----> silver_customers --+
                                 |
                          quality_gate
                                 |
                          dim_customers (SCD2)
                                 |
                           fact_daily
                                 |
                           ml_features
                                 |
                          check_metrics
# Security configuration applied at deployment time (Module 14).

-- RBAC: Role-based access to gold layer tables GRANT SELECT ON gold.fact_daily_consumption TO analyst_role; GRANT SELECT ON gold.dim_customers TO analyst_role; GRANT SELECT ON gold.dim_dates TO analyst_role;

-- Column masking: Analysts see hashed PII only
-- (PII was already hashed at silver layer)
-- Gold dimensions contain customer_name_hash, not customer_name.
-- No additional masking needed in gold — PII never leaves silver.

-- Row-level security for regional partners CREATE FUNCTION gold.region_filter(region STRING) RETURNS BOOLEAN RETURN (region IN ( SELECT allowed_region FROM security.partner_acl WHERE partner_id = current_user() ));
ALTER TABLE gold.fact_daily_consumption SET ROW FILTER gold.region_filter ON (region_key);

Role Bronze Access Silver Access Gold Access data_engineer READ/WRITE READ/WRITE READ/WRITE analyst NONE NONE SELECT (star schema) ml_engineer NONE SELECT (readings) SELECT (ml_features) partner_api NONE NONE SELECT (filtered by region) pipeline_service READ/WRITE READ/WRITE READ/WRITE admin ALL ALL ALL + GRANT

Decision Technique Module Impact Dimension joins broadcast() all dim tables M4, M5 Eliminates shuffle on 1 TB fact build Fact partitioning partitionBy('date_key') M2, M17 Enables DPP and partition pruning Incremental writes replaceWhere for idempotency M11, M13 Re-runnable; no duplicates Data skipping Z-ORDER(meter_id, date) M16 Reduces scan I/O by 60-80% Streaming latency 1-minute trigger, 15-min watermark M10 Near real-time alerts with late data handling Silver deduplication Window row_number() M15 Exact dedup without shuffle (partition by key) Anomaly detection Rolling window + z-score M15 Distributed anomaly flagging per meter AQE enabled spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true M16 Auto-coalesce, auto-broadcast, skew handling CBO statistics ANALYZE TABLE after OPTIMIZE M16 Catalyst makes better join/plan decisions Parquet format Delta Lake (Parquet + transaction log) M2, M11 Columnar, pushdown, compression

You have built a complete, production-grade data platform.
Every module in this course contributed a piece:

items: Module 1: The reason this platform exists — 1 million meters generating 96 million readings per day cannot fit on a single machine
Module 2: SparkSession, DataFrames, lazy evaluation, partitions — the foundation of every transformation in the platform
Module 3: Catalyst optimizes every query plan in bronze, silver, and gold processing
Module 4: Broadcast joins on dimensions, shuffle awareness in fact builds, spill prevention
Module 5: Left joins for surrogate key lookups, anti-joins for quality checks, broadcast joins for dimension enrichment
Module 6: SQL interface for OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, ANALYZE, GRANT, and analyst queries on the star schema
Module 7: Medallion architecture (bronze → silver → gold) structuring the entire platform
Module 8: JSON parsing of Kafka messages in streaming ingestion
Module 9: UDF awareness — preferring built-in functions throughout for Tungsten optimization
Module 10: Structured Streaming for Kafka ingestion and real-time anomaly detection with watermarks and windowed aggregation
Module 11: Delta Lake for ACID writes, MERGE for SCD, replaceWhere for idempotency, OPTIMIZE + Z-ORDER + VACUUM for maintenance
Module 12: Quality check framework (null rate, row count, referential integrity, dedup), metrics logging, pipeline observability
Module 13: Airflow DAG with SparkSubmitOperator, dependency chains, retry policies, failure alerting
Module 14: RBAC, PII pseudonymization at silver boundary, row-level security for partners, column masking, audit trail via Delta history
Module 15: Window functions for deduplication (row_number), anomaly detection (rolling avg + z-score), and gap filling
Module 16: AQE for adaptive optimization, DPP for star-schema scans, Z-ORDER for data skipping, ANALYZE TABLE for CBO statistics
Module 17: Star schema design (fact_daily + dim_customers/meters/regions/dates), SCD Type 2, grain selection, surrogate keys
Module 18: pyspark.pandas available for analyst exploration of the gold layer

blocks.content: This is not a collection of scripts. It is a system — with dependencies, contracts, quality gates, security boundaries, and operational monitoring.
You are no longer learning PySpark. You are building production platforms.

In Module 20, the final module, we shift focus to your career: interview preparation, portfolio strategy, the skills matrix that hiring managers evaluate, and how to demonstrate the depth you have built across these 19 modules.

chapters.title: Module 20: Career, Interviews, and What Comes Next
chapters.description: Turning Technical Depth into Professional Opportunity Translate the technical depth you have built across 19 modules into career strategy — understand the roles that use PySpark, the skills matrix hiring managers evaluate, how to build a portfolio that demonstrates distributed systems thinking, how to answer the most common and most difficult interview questions, and how to position yourself for senior data engineering roles.

blocks.content: You have completed 19 modules. You understand distributed systems from first principles. You can build a production data platform end to end. You can diagnose performance bottlenecks by reading execution plans. You know when to broadcast, when to salt, when to bucket, and when to let AQE handle it.
But technical skill alone does not get you hired.

The gap between knowing how to build something and convincing someone you can build it is real. This module bridges that gap. It teaches you how to present your knowledge in interviews, how to structure a portfolio that demonstrates depth rather than breadth, and how to navigate the landscape of roles that use PySpark in production.

This is not a generic career module. Every piece of advice here is specific to PySpark and distributed data engineering. The interview questions come from real hiring loops. The portfolio strategy is designed around the exact skills this course taught you.

sections.title: Lesson 2: The Role Landscape — Where PySpark Lives

blocks.content: PySpark is not a niche tool. It is the backbone of data infrastructure at companies ranging from startups to the Fortune 10. But the roles that use PySpark differ significantly in scope, responsibility, and required depth.

sections.title: Lesson 3: The Skills Matrix — What Hiring Managers Evaluate

blocks.content: Hiring managers for data engineering roles evaluate candidates across multiple dimensions. Understanding these dimensions lets you prepare strategically.
Technical Skills Assessment

Skill Area Junior (0-2 yr) Mid (2-5 yr) Senior (5+ yr) PySpark API Read/write DataFrames, basic transforms, groupBy Joins, window functions, UDFs, caching strategy Performance tuning, custom partitioning, execution plan analysis Spark Internals Knows lazy evaluation exists Understands shuffle, DAG, stages, partitions Reads execution plans fluently, diagnoses skew/spill/OOM from Spark UI Data Modeling Understands tables and joins Implements star schema, basic SCD Designs enterprise models, SCD Type 2, grain decisions, denorm trade-offs Streaming Knows Spark has streaming Can build basic streaming pipeline Watermarks, state management, exactly-once, streaming + batch integration Delta Lake Reads/writes Delta tables MERGE, time travel, OPTIMIZE Delta architecture decisions, VACUUM strategy, CDF, multi-hop pipelines SQL SELECT, JOIN, GROUP BY Window functions, CTEs, subqueries Complex analytical SQL, query optimization, catalog management Cloud Platforms Basic S3/GCS/ADLS usage EMR/Dataproc/Databricks setup and usage Architecture decisions, cost optimization, autoscaling configuration Orchestration Knows Airflow exists Can write basic DAGs Complex dependency chains, error handling, backfill strategy, CI/CD integration Data Quality Manual validation Basic quality checks in pipeline Quality frameworks, anomaly detection on metrics, observability strategy Security Understands access control concept Basic RBAC implementation Column masking, row-level security, PII handling, compliance strategy
Where This Course Positions You

If you have genuinely engaged with all 19 prior modules — not just read them but understood the reasoning and could implement the patterns — you are positioned at the mid-to-senior boundary across most skill areas. The gap between you and a senior engineer is primarily production experience: having debugged real failures at 3 AM, having navigated ambiguous requirements, and having mentored others.
The technical knowledge is here. What remains is applying it under pressure, in production, with real stakes.
A portfolio is not a collection of Jupyter notebooks with df.show() at the end. A portfolio that impresses hiring managers demonstrates systems thinking, not just API knowledge.
What Hiring Managers Want to See

What Impresses What Does Not Impress End-to-end pipeline (ingestion through analytics) Isolated notebook with 10 cells of basic transforms Explicit schema definitions inferSchema=True everywhere Quality checks that halt the pipeline No validation at all Delta Lake with MERGE for incremental loads overwrite mode on every run SCD Type 2 dimension management No history tracking Execution plan analysis in documentation No mention of performance Airflow DAG with dependency management No orchestration Clear README with architecture diagram No documentation Tests (even basic ones) No tests Discussion of trade-offs and alternatives Only one approach with no justification
The Capstone as Your Portfolio Piece
Module 19 — the Smart Grid Capstone — is designed to be your portfolio centerpiece. Here is how to present it:

items: GitHub Repository: Clean project structure (config/, ingestion/, processing/, modeling/, quality/, tests/). Hiring managers skim structure before reading code.

README.md: Architecture diagram (the ASCII from Part 1 of Module 19 converted to a proper diagram). Brief description of each layer. Tech stack (PySpark, Delta Lake, Airflow). Not a wall of text — concise and visual.

Architecture Decision Records: Short documents explaining WHY you chose SCD Type 2 for customers, WHY you partition by date, WHY you broadcast dimension joins. These demonstrate engineering judgment, not just coding ability.
Performance Analysis: Include a section showing an execution plan, identifying a bottleneck, and demonstrating the fix. Before/after metrics. This is rare in portfolios and immediately signals depth.
Quality Framework: The quality check functions from Module 12/19 are a differentiator. Most portfolios have zero quality checks. Yours has a gate that blocks bad data from reaching the gold layer.

Beyond the capstone, consider adding one or two smaller projects that demonstrate specific depth:

Your resume is not a list of technologies. It is a collection of impact statements that happen to involve technology.
The Impact Statement Formula
Formula: [Action verb] + [What you built/improved] + [Scale] + [Business impact]
WEAK: "Used PySpark to process data."

STRONG: "Designed and built a Medallion-architecture data platform processing 96M daily meter readings on Delta Lake, reducing pipeline runtime from 4 hours to 25 minutes through broadcast join optimization and AQE tuning, enabling same-day consumption analytics for 200 field operators."
Components:

items: Action:   Designed and built
What:     Medallion-architecture data platform
Scale:    96M daily records
Tech:     Delta Lake, broadcast joins, AQE
Impact:   4h -> 25min runtime; 200 operators enabled

blocks.content: Example Resume Bullets

Skill Area Example Impact Statement Pipeline Development Built end-to-end PySpark pipelines ingesting 500M+ daily events from Kafka through bronze/silver/gold layers on Delta Lake, with automated quality gates blocking invalid data from analyst-facing tables Performance Tuning Reduced a 3-hour daily pipeline to 18 minutes by diagnosing data skew in Spark UI, implementing salted joins for a 900M-row skewed key, and enabling AQE for automatic partition coalescing Data Modeling Designed star schema gold layer with SCD Type 2 customer dimensions, enabling historical analysis of customer segment migrations across 5 years of billing data Streaming Implemented near real-time anomaly detection on smart meter readings using Structured Streaming with 15-minute watermarks and tumbling window statistics, reducing outage response time from hours to minutes Data Quality Developed a quality gate framework with null rate, referential integrity, and row count checks, preventing 47 potential data incidents in the first quarter of deployment Orchestration Managed production Airflow DAGs with 30+ tasks, implementing idempotent writes via Delta replaceWhere, enabling safe backfills and automatic retry on transient failures
Technologies Section
List technologies in categories, ordered by relevance:
TECHNOLOGIES

Distributed Processing: PySpark, Spark SQL, Structured Streaming
  Storage & Lakehouse:    Delta Lake, Parquet, Hive Metastore
  Cloud Platforms:        Databricks, AWS EMR, S3 (or your actual experience)
  Orchestration:          Apache Airflow
  Data Modeling:          Star Schema, SCD Type 2, Medallion Architecture
  Languages:              Python, SQL
  Quality & Testing:      pytest, Great Expectations (if used)
  Other:                  Kafka, Docker, Git, CI/CD

These questions test whether you understand distributed systems, not just API syntax. For each question, we provide the key points your answer should cover and the module where you learned the concept.
Q1: What happens when you call df.show() in PySpark?

Key points: Spark takes the entire DAG of lazy transformations, passes it through the Catalyst optimizer (predicate pushdown, projection pruning, join reordering), generates a physical plan, splits it into stages at shuffle boundaries, divides stages into tasks (one per partition), distributes tasks to executors, executors process data in parallel, results return to the Driver for display. show() is an action that triggers this entire pipeline. (M2, M3)
Q2: Explain the difference between transformations and actions.

Key points: Transformations (filter, select, groupBy, join) are lazy — they build a DAG but execute nothing. Actions (show, count, collect, write) trigger execution of the entire plan. This laziness enables Catalyst to optimize the full query before any data is read. Without laziness, each operation would execute independently, missing optimization opportunities like predicate pushdown. (M2)
Q3: What is shuffle and why is it expensive?

Key points: Shuffle occurs when data must be redistributed across partitions (groupBy, join, distinct, orderBy). It involves writing intermediate data to disk, transferring over the network, and sorting on the receiving side. It is expensive because it touches every slow component: disk I/O, network I/O, serialization, and sorting. Minimizing shuffle is a primary performance goal. (M3, M4)
Q4: Your job takes 40 minutes. Yesterday it took 4 hours. How do you diagnose it?

Key points: Open Spark UI. Check Jobs tab for which job is slow. Check Stages tab for the slow stage. Look at task distribution: if Max >> Median, it is data skew. Check for spill (memory/disk). Check shuffle volume. Read the SQL tab for Exchange nodes. Verify AQE fired (AdaptiveSparkPlan). Form a hypothesis (skew? bad join strategy? no pruning?). Apply one fix. Measure. This is the systematic diagnostic workflow. (M4, M16)
Q5: Explain broadcast joins. When would you use one?

Key points: A broadcast join copies the small table to every executor, eliminating shuffle of the large table. Use when one table is small (typically < 10-50 MB). The threshold is based on bytes, not row count. A wide table with 50K rows can be larger than a narrow table with 5M rows. AQE can automatically switch to broadcast at runtime if post-filter size is small enough. Classic use case: fact table joined with dimension/lookup table. (M4, M5, M16)
Q6: What is data skew and how do you handle it?

Key points: Data skew occurs when one key has disproportionately more data than others, causing one partition to be much larger. One task becomes a straggler while others finish quickly. For join skew: AQE skew optimization (automatic in Spark 3.x) or manual salting. For aggregation skew: manual salting (AQE does not help groupBy skew). Salting adds a random suffix to the heavy key, splitting it into N pseudo-keys for parallel processing, then re-aggregating. (M4)
Q7: Explain the Medallion architecture.

Key points: Three layers. Bronze: raw data, append-only, no transformations, audit trail. Silver: cleaned, deduplicated, validated, standardized, quality-gated. Gold: business-ready, star schema or denormalized, optimized for consumption. Each layer transition has quality checks. Bronze is for recovery, silver is for reusability, gold is for analytics. The pattern ensures data quality improves at each stage while maintaining lineage. (M7, M19)
Q8: What is SCD Type 2 and how do you implement it?

Key points: SCD Type 2 preserves full history of dimension changes. When an attribute changes, the old row is closed (end_date set, is_current = false) and a new row is inserted with a new surrogate key, effective_date = today, end_date = 9999-12-31, is_current = true. Fact rows link via surrogate key, so historical queries automatically join to the correct version. Implemented with Delta MERGE: close matching rows in whenMatchedUpdate, insert new versions in whenNotMatchedInsert. (M17)
Coding challenges test your ability to write correct, efficient PySpark code under pressure. Here are common patterns with model answers.
Challenge 1: Deduplicate a DataFrame
Prompt: Given a DataFrame of meter readings with potential duplicates (same meter_id and timestamp), keep only the most recent record by ingestion time.

from pyspark.sql.functions import row_number, col
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id', 'timestamp') \
    .orderBy(col('ingestion_timestamp').desc())

deduped = df \
    .withColumn('rn', row_number().over(window)) \
    .filter(col('rn') == 1) \
    .drop('rn')

# Why this is better than dropDuplicates():
# - Deterministic: you control WHICH duplicate survives
# - Flexible: any tiebreaker column can be used
# - Transparent: explicit in the execution plan
Challenge 2: Top-N per Group
Prompt: Find the top 3 highest consumption meters per region per day.

from pyspark.sql.functions import row_number, col, desc
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

window = Window.partitionBy('region', 'date') \
    .orderBy(desc('kw'))

top3 = df \
    .withColumn('rank', row_number().over(window)) \
    .filter(col('rank') <= 3) \
    .drop('rank')
Challenge 3: Day-over-Day Change
Prompt: Calculate the percentage change in daily consumption for each meter compared to the previous day.

from pyspark.sql.functions import lag, col
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id').orderBy('date')

result = df \
    .withColumn('prev_kw', lag('daily_kw', 1).over(window)) \
    .withColumn('pct_change',
        (col('daily_kw') - col('prev_kw')) / col('prev_kw') * 100
    )
Challenge 4: Pivoting with Aggregation
Prompt: Create a summary showing total consumption per customer_type per month as columns.

from pyspark.sql.functions import sum, month

result = df \
    .withColumn('month', month('date')) \
    .groupBy('customer_type') \
    .pivot('month') \
    .agg(sum('kw'))

# Note: pivot() with many distinct values is expensive.
# Limit the pivot column cardinality or pre-filter.
Challenge 5: Cumulative Running Total
Prompt: Calculate a running total of consumption per meter, ordered by date.

from pyspark.sql.functions import sum, col
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

window = Window.partitionBy('meter_id') \
    .orderBy('date') \
    .rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, Window.currentRow)

result = df.withColumn('running_total', sum('daily_kw').over(window))

# Key point: the explicit rowsBetween is technically the default
# when orderBy is present, but making it explicit shows awareness
# of the default frame trap (Module 15).
Challenge 6: Joining with Null Safety
Prompt: Join readings to customer metadata, but ensure readings with NULL customer_id are preserved (not silently dropped).

# WRONG: inner join silently drops NULL keys
# result = readings.join(customers, 'customer_id')

# CORRECT: left join preserves all readings
result = readings.join(customers, 'customer_id', 'left')

# Then flag orphans for data quality monitoring
from pyspark.sql.functions import when, col
result = result.withColumn('has_customer_match',
    when(col('customer_name').isNotNull(), True).otherwise(False))

# Log orphan rate as a quality metric
orphan_rate = result.filter(~col('has_customer_match')).count() / result.count()
System design questions test your ability to architect solutions, not just write code. These are common in senior-level interviews.
Q1: Design a real-time fraud detection pipeline
Expected answer structure:
Sources: Transaction stream (Kafka), customer profiles (batch)
Bronze: Append raw transactions from Kafka via Structured Streaming. Store in Delta, partitioned by date.
Silver: Enrich with customer profile (broadcast join). Validate transaction amounts. Compute velocity features (window functions: txn count in last 1hr, 24hr, 7d per card).
Gold - Batch: Daily aggregations for model training. Feature store with historical patterns.

Gold - Streaming: Real-time scoring pipeline. Watermark for late data (30 seconds). Tumbling window for velocity features. Score each transaction against ML model. Write alerts to Delta + push to notification service.
Trade-offs discussed:

items: Latency vs accuracy (wider windows = more accurate but slower)
Model complexity vs serving speed
False positive rate vs fraud catch rate
Stream-stream join if customer profile changes frequently

blocks.content: Q2: Your daily pipeline processes 500M rows. It currently takes 2 hours. How would you get it under 30 minutes?

Expected answer structure: Start with diagnosis, not solutions. Open Spark UI. Identify the slowest stage. Check for skew (task max >> median). Check for spill. Check shuffle volume. Then apply targeted fixes: broadcast small tables, salt skewed keys, increase parallelism (repartition), enable AQE, convert to Parquet if still reading CSV, push filters before joins, drop unnecessary columns early. Verify with Z-ORDER and ANALYZE TABLE for data skipping and CBO. Measure after each change. Do not guess.
Q3: Design the data model for an e-commerce analytics platform

Expected answer structure: Star schema. Fact tables: fact_orders (transaction grain, one row per order line item), fact_daily_sessions (periodic snapshot, one row per user per day). Dimensions: dim_products (SCD Type 2 for price changes), dim_customers (SCD Type 2 for segment changes), dim_dates, dim_sellers, dim_geography. Partition fact tables by date. Broadcast all dimensions. Build denormalized ML features table for recommendation engine. Quality gates between layers. Discuss grain choice: order-line vs order-header vs session-level.
Q4: How would you migrate a legacy Hive pipeline to Delta Lake?

Expected answer structure: Phased approach. Phase 1: Convert storage from Hive tables to Delta (CONVERT TO DELTA or rewrite as Delta). Phase 2: Replace INSERT OVERWRITE with MERGE for incremental loads. Phase 3: Implement Medallion architecture (bronze from raw, silver cleaned, gold modeled). Phase 4: Add OPTIMIZE + Z-ORDER + VACUUM to maintenance. Phase 5: Enable schema evolution for forward compatibility. Phase 6: Implement quality gates and monitoring. Run old and new pipelines in parallel during migration, compare outputs. Key risk: schema differences between Hive and Delta type inference.

sections.title: Lesson 9: Behavioral Questions for Data Engineers

blocks.content: Technical interviews are necessary but not sufficient. Behavioral questions assess judgment, communication, and collaboration. Use the STAR format: Situation, Task, Action, Result.

Question What They Are Really Asking How to Answer Tell me about a time a pipeline failed in production. Can you handle pressure? Do you have a systematic debugging process? Describe the symptom, your diagnostic steps (Spark UI, logs), the root cause, the fix, and the preventive measure you added. How do you handle disagreements about technical approach? Are you collaborative? Can you defend ideas with data? Give an example where you benchmarked two approaches, shared results with the team, and reached a decision based on evidence. Describe a time you improved pipeline performance. Do you measure before and after? Is your approach systematic? Specific numbers: before (2 hours), diagnosis (skew on key X), fix (salting), after (18 minutes). Show the measurement discipline. How do you ensure data quality? Do you think about quality proactively or react to problems? Describe quality checks, gates, metrics logging, anomaly detection on pipeline health. Reference a specific example. Tell me about a project you are most proud of. What do you value? Can you communicate complex work clearly? Use the capstone: Medallion architecture, SCD Type 2, streaming alerts, quality gates. Explain the architecture to a non-technical interviewer. How do you prioritize when multiple pipelines need attention? Can you make trade-offs under pressure? Business impact first: which consumers are blocked? Then: which fix is fastest? Then: which prevents the most future failures?

Mistake Why It Hurts Better Approach Saying 'I used PySpark' without specifics Generic; could mean anything from df.show() to production platforms Specify: 'I built a Medallion pipeline processing 200M rows/day with SCD Type 2 dimensions on Delta Lake' Not mentioning scale Interviewers cannot assess impact without numbers Always include data volume, row counts, cluster size, latency, or user count Jumping to solutions without diagnosis Signals guessing rather than engineering discipline Always say: 'First I would check the Spark UI to identify the slow stage and look for skew or spill' Ignoring trade-offs Every design decision has trade-offs; pretending otherwise is naive Discuss both sides: 'Broadcast is faster but uses executor memory; with 30 executors and a 200MB table, that is 6GB of replicated memory' Not asking clarifying questions Shows you accept ambiguous requirements without pushback Ask: 'What is the expected data volume? How fresh must the data be? Who are the consumers?' Overcomplicating the answer More complexity is not more impressive Start simple. Add complexity only when the requirements demand it. State this explicitly. Failing to mention testing or monitoring Production engineers think about reliability, not just correctness Always mention: 'I would add quality checks, logging, and alerting to catch regressions' Using only pandas terminology in Spark context Shows you think in single-machine terms Use Spark vocabulary: partitions, shuffle, broadcast, execution plan, DAG, stages, tasks

Compensation varies significantly by geography, company size, and seniority. These ranges are approximate and will shift with market conditions. Use sites like levels.fyi, Glassdoor, and Blind for current data. The numbers below are intended as general orientation, not guarantees.

Level US (Major Tech Hub) US (Other Markets) Europe (Western) Key Differentiator Junior Data Engineer (0-2 yr) $90K-$130K $70K-$100K €45K-€70K Can write PySpark, basic transforms, reads/writes Mid Data Engineer (2-5 yr) $130K-$180K $100K-$150K €65K-€100K Designs pipelines, handles joins/streaming, some tuning Senior Data Engineer (5+ yr) $170K-$250K+ $140K-$200K €90K-€140K Architects platforms, performance engineering, mentors Staff / Principal (8+ yr) $230K-$350K+ $180K-$280K €120K-€180K Org-wide technical leadership, sets standards

Note: These ranges often include base salary only. Total compensation at tech companies includes equity (RSUs/options) and bonus, which can add 20-100% to base at senior levels. Databricks, major cloud providers, and top-tier tech companies typically pay at the higher end.
Negotiation Principles

items: Research first: Know the market range for your level and location before discussing numbers. Use levels.fyi for tech companies.
Negotiate on total compensation, not just base: equity, signing bonus, annual bonus, and benefits all matter.
Leverage competing offers: Having multiple offers gives you data and leverage. Apply broadly.
Quantify your impact: "I built a pipeline that saved 200 hours of manual analyst work per month" translates directly to business value.
Do not accept the first offer: Most companies have negotiation room. A polite counter-request is expected and respected.
Consider the full package: A lower base at a company with strong learning opportunities, good team, and equity upside may be worth more long-term.

sections.title: Lesson 12: What Comes Next — Continuing Your Growth

blocks.content: This course gave you a deep foundation. But the Spark ecosystem evolves, and the data engineering field is expanding. Here is where to go next.
Deepening Spark Skills

Topic Why It Matters Where to Learn Spark Connect (Spark 3.4+) Decouples client from server; enables thin client architecture Spark documentation, Databricks blog Photon Engine Databricks' native vectorized engine replacing Tungsten in some cases Databricks documentation Apache Iceberg / Hudi Alternative table formats competing with Delta Lake Project documentation, comparison articles Unity Catalog deep dive Governance, lineage, access control for production lakehouses Databricks Academy Spark on Kubernetes Container-native Spark deployment, replacing YARN spark.apache.org, Kubernetes docs Cost optimization Spot instances, autoscaling, right-sizing, storage tiering Cloud provider blogs, FinOps resources
Broadening Data Engineering Skills

Skill Why It Complements PySpark Priority dbt (data build tool) SQL-first transformation layer; often used alongside Spark for gold layer High for analytics engineering roles Kafka / Event Streaming Upstream of Spark Streaming; understanding producers and consumers High for streaming-heavy roles Terraform / IaC Infrastructure as code for provisioning Spark clusters and storage High for platform-leaning roles Docker / Kubernetes Containerized deployment of Spark applications and dependencies Medium to High DataOps / CI/CD for Data Automated testing, deployment, and monitoring of data pipelines High for senior roles Data Governance (non-technical) Understanding regulatory requirements, compliance frameworks High for roles at regulated companies Communication and Documentation Explaining technical decisions to non-technical stakeholders Critical at every level, especially senior
Community and Resources

items: Spark documentation (spark.apache.org/docs/latest/) — the authoritative reference. Read the tuning guide, SQL reference, and streaming guide.
Databricks blog (databricks.com/blog) — deeply technical posts on Delta Lake, Spark internals, and performance from the people who build Spark.
Advancing Analytics (YouTube) — PySpark tutorials with engineering depth.
Data Engineering Weekly (newsletter) — industry trends, new tools, and best practices.
Open source contributions — contributing to Spark, Delta Lake, or related projects is the strongest signal of deep understanding.
Local meetups and conferences — Data + AI Summit (Databricks), Spark Summit, local data engineering meetups. Presenting a talk on your capstone project is powerful for visibility.

sections.title: Lesson 13: The Complete Course Skills Map

blocks.content: This table summarizes every skill taught in this course, mapped to the module where you learned it and the interview context where you will use it.

Skill Module Interview Context Why Spark exists (scaling, Hadoop limitations) M1 Conceptual: 'Why not just use a bigger database?' SparkSession, Driver/Executor architecture M2 Conceptual: 'What happens when you start a Spark app?' Lazy evaluation, DAG, transformations vs actions M2 Conceptual: 'Explain lazy evaluation' Parquet, columnar storage, predicate pushdown M2 Conceptual: 'Why Parquet over CSV?' Partitions, repartition vs coalesce M2 Coding/Design: partition strategy questions Catalyst Optimizer, physical vs logical plans M3 Conceptual: 'How does Spark optimize queries?' Shuffle, stages, tasks M3 Conceptual: 'What is shuffle and why is it expensive?' Project Tungsten, code generation M3 Conceptual: 'How does Spark achieve hardware speed?' Spill, OOM diagnosis, broadcast joins, salting M4 Design: 'Pipeline is slow, how do you diagnose?' Execution plan reading M4 Coding: 'Read this plan and identify the bottleneck' 7 join types, 4 physical strategies M5 Coding/Design: join strategy selection Window functions vs self-joins M5 Coding: 'Replace this self-join with a window function' Spark SQL, catalog management, views M6 Coding: SQL interview questions Medallion architecture (bronze/silver/gold) M7 Design: 'How do you structure a data platform?' Data cleaning patterns (dedup, impute, validate) M7 Coding: 'Clean this messy dataset' Complex types (Array, Map, Struct) M8 Coding: 'Parse this nested JSON' UDFs (row, Pandas vectorized), decision framework M9 Conceptual: 'When to use UDFs vs built-in functions?' Structured Streaming, watermarks, triggers M10 Design: 'Build a real-time pipeline' Delta Lake, MERGE, time travel, OPTIMIZE, VACUUM M11 Design: 'Why Delta over raw Parquet?' Testing, quality checks, observability M12 Behavioral: 'How do you ensure data quality?' Airflow, spark-submit, deployment M13 Design: 'How do you orchestrate a daily pipeline?' RBAC, column masking, PII handling, audit M14 Design: 'How do you secure sensitive data?' Window functions (ranking, lag, rolling, sessions) M15 Coding: dedup, top-N, running total, anomaly detection AQE, DPP, bucketing, Spark UI, Z-ORDER M16 Conceptual/Design: advanced performance questions Star schema, SCD Types 1/2/3, grain, surrogates M17 Design: 'Design a data model for X' pyspark.pandas, API migration, index traps M18 Conceptual: 'When to use pandas API on Spark?' End-to-end platform design M19 System design: 'Build a complete data platform' Career strategy, portfolio, behavioral M20 Meta: how to present all of the above
Module 20 Checkpoint — Course Complete
You have completed the entire PySpark course.
You started in Module 1 asking why traditional databases failed. You end in Module 20 preparing to architect production data platforms and demonstrate that capability in interviews.
Here is what you now carry:

items: Distributed systems fundamentals: You understand why data must be partitioned, why shuffle is expensive, and how Spark’s lazy evaluation and Catalyst optimizer transform your code into efficient distributed execution.

Production engineering skills: You can build Medallion architectures, implement SCD Type 2 dimensions, design star schemas, write quality gates, orchestrate with Airflow, and secure data with RBAC and column masking.

Performance engineering discipline: You can diagnose slow jobs using the Spark UI, identify skew and spill, apply broadcast joins, salting, AQE, DPP, bucketing, and Z-ORDER, and measure before and after every change.

The full toolkit: Native PySpark DataFrames for production, Spark SQL for analyst interfaces, Structured Streaming for real-time pipelines, Delta Lake for reliable storage, pyspark.pandas for exploration, and Airflow for orchestration.

Career readiness: A portfolio strategy, resume impact statements, answers to the most common interview questions across conceptual, coding, system design, and behavioral categories, and a clear understanding of the role landscape.

blocks.content: The knowledge is here. The depth is real.
What comes next is yours to build.
